name: ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING

topics.title: Relevance of Technical Drawing in Engineering Field. Types of Lines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Relevance of Technical Drawing in Engineering Field. Types of Lines # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Technical Drawing — The Engineer's Universal Language > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):** > **Technical Drawing** (also called *Engineering Drawing* or *Engineering Graphics*) is a precise, standardized form of visual communication used by engineers, designers, and technicians to represent physical objects, systems, processes, and ideas through lines, symbols, dimensions, and text on a 2D drafting surface, governed by strict national and international standards (BIS / ISO). In the simplest sense, a technical drawing is the **photographic negative of a spoken language for machines**. Whereas a poet uses words to express emotion, an engineer uses lines, views, and dimensions to "talk" to a fabricator 200 km away, to a CNC machine in a factory, or to an inspector at a quality-check desk — all without ambiguity. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Architect's Recipe"** > Imagine you want a cook in a distant city to bake the exact cake you imagined. If you *describe* it, the cake will not match. But if you give the cook a *blueprint* — a diagram with exact ingredient quantities, pan dimensions, oven temperature, and timing — the cake will be identical. > **Technical drawing is that blueprint for every engineered object in the world** — from the bolt in your mobile phone to the wing of a Boeing 787. ### 1.3 Why is Technical Drawing *Relevant* in the Engineering Field? The relevance is not historical — it is **operationally critical** in every modern engineering workflow. Below is the academic justification, mapped to KTU 2024 learning outcomes: | # | Engineering Function | Role of Technical Drawing | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Design Communication** | Single unambiguous medium between designer → manufacturer → inspector. | | 2 | **Manufacturing** | Drives CNC, 3D printing, casting patterns, sheet metal bending. | | 3 | **Legal & Patent Documentation** | A patented invention's claims are defended using the engineering drawing attached to the patent. | | 4 | **Quality Assurance** | Dimensional tolerances on a drawing become the acceptance criterion. | | 5 | **Construction & Site Work** | Civil drawings dictate exactly where a column, beam, or pipeline must sit. | | 6 | **Maintenance & Repair** | Service manuals rely on assembly and exploded drawings. | | 7 | **Computer-Aided Integration** | The drawing is the input to CAD / CAM / CAE / BIM pipelines. | | 8 | **Globalization** | An engineer in Kerala and one in Germany can collaborate on the same drawing because both follow **ISO / BIS standards**. | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** In 3-mark questions, examiners reward answers that connect the drawing to *manufacturing* and *standardization* — not just "it is used to show objects." Always mention **"standardization"** and **"universal acceptance"** in your answer. ### 1.4 Types of Technical Drawings (Foundational Classification) The KTU Module 1 syllabus groups technical drawings into: - **Engineering Drawing** — the broad umbrella of all technical graphics. - **Mechanical Drawing** — machine components, assemblies, installations. - **Architectural Drawing** — plans, elevations, sections of buildings. - **Structural Drawing** — RCC, steel, and load-bearing details. - **Electrical & Electronics Drawing** — circuit diagrams, wiring layouts, PCB artwork. - **Piping & Process Drawing** — flow diagrams, P&ID for chemical plants. - **Computer-Aided Drawing (CAD)** — digitally generated drawings using software (AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA, Fusion 360). --- ## 1.5 Types of Lines — Foundational Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** > In engineering drawing, a **line** is not a casual stroke — it is a *coded symbol* that conveys a specific meaning. The **type, thickness, and gap pattern** of a line tells the reader whether they are looking at a visible edge, a hidden feature, a centre axis, or a cutting plane. The standards governing these conventions are: - **BIS : IS 10714 : 1983** (Indian Standard, based on ISO 128). - **ISO 128-20:1996** — General principles of presentation. - **ISO 128-24:1999** — Lines on mechanical engineering drawings. ### 1.6 The Two Line *Widths* (Line Weights) Every line on a drawing belongs to one of **only two weight classes**: $$ \text{Line Weight Ratio} \;=\; \frac{\text{Thick line width}}{\text{Thin line width}} \;=\; 2 \;\text{to}\; 1 $$ Standard recommended values (in millimetres) per BIS: | Line Class | Recommended Width Range | |---|---| | **Thick line (Type A)** | **0.5 mm, 0.7 mm, 1.0 mm** | | **Thin line (Type B)** | **0.25 mm, 0.35 mm, 0.5 mm** | > [!NOTE] > **Default Practice:** Use **0.5 mm thick** and **0.25 mm thin** for A3 / A4 sheets at KTU university exams. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Philosophy Behind Line Conventions Every line on a drawing is a **declaration of fact**. The genius of the line system is that a reader does not need to *interpret* — they just *decode*. This is the same principle as Morse code: dash = visible, dot = break, gap = spacing. A well-drawn engineering drawing works like a **lossless compression algorithm** for a 3D object. ### 2.2 The Ten Standard Line Types (BIS IS 10714 : 1983 / ISO 128) Each line has a **distinct meaning**, and using the wrong one is the most common reason for losing marks in KTU viva and ESE. The complete authoritative list: | No. | Line Type | Line Style (ASCII) | Thickness | Application / Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | **A** | **Continuous Thick** | `———————` | Thick | Visible outlines, visible edges, edges of objects. | | **B.1** | **Continuous Thin (Straight)** | `———————` | Thin | Dimension lines, extension lines, leader lines, hatching, short centre lines, reference lines, guide lines. | | **B.2** | **Continuous Thin (Freehand)** | `~ ~ ~ ~ ~` | Thin | Manually drawn break lines for irregular boundaries (only when no irregularity template is available). | | **C** | **Continuous Thin with Zig-Zag** | `———∧∨∧∨———` | Thin | Manual break lines — long-break convention when only a part of a long uniform object is drawn. | | **D** | **Dashed Thin** | `– – – – –` | Thin | Hidden edges, hidden outlines. | | **E** | **Dash-Dot Thin** | `–·–·–·–` | Thin | Centre lines, axes of symmetry, pitch circles of gears. | | **F** | **Dash-Dot Thick** | `–·–·–·–` | Thick | Cutting plane lines (section line indicators). | | **G** | **Thin Chain Thin** | `–··–··–` | Thin | Indication of the *surface* or *region* where a section is taken. | | **H** | **Thick Chain Thin** | `–··–··–` | Thick | Indication of the *surface* to be treated (heat treatment, coating). | | **J** | **Dash Double-Dot Thin** | `–··–··–` (two dots) | Thin | Alternative and extreme positions of moving parts, repeated detail, adjacent parts, phantom lines. | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** Confusing **Dash-Dot Thick (F)** with **Dash-Dot Thin (E)**. Remember: **Thick dash-dot = Cutting Plane**, **Thin dash-dot = Centre Line / Axis**. Getting this swap costs full marks in sectioning problems. ### 2.3 The Three-Block Line Reading Framework To read any line correctly, always apply this triad: $$ \text{Line} \;\equiv\; \big\{\, \text{Thickness},\; \text{Dash Pattern},\; \text{Context Placement} \,\big\} $$ - **Thickness** → Is it thick (0.5 mm) or thin (0.25 mm)? - **Dash Pattern** → Is it dashed, dash-dot, dash-double-dot, or continuous? - **Context** → Where on the drawing is it placed — inside the object, on the centre, at the edge, outside the object? ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Concept | Key Value / Rule | Units / Symbol | |---|---|---| | Thick line width | **0.5** (A3 sheet default) | mm | | Thin line width | **0.25** | mm | | Weight ratio | $\dfrac{\text{thick}}{\text{thin}} = 2$ | dimensionless | | Gap in dashed / dash-dot lines | **≈ 2 × line width** | mm | | Dash length | **≈ 6 × line width** | mm | | Dot size | **≈ line width** | mm | | Spacing of centre line dashes | Should be roughly **3 mm gaps** | mm | | Standard governing lines | **IS 10714 : 1983** / **ISO 128** | — | | Number of standard line types | **10** (A to J, omitting non-existent I and K) | — | | Sheet border | Continuous thick line, **10 mm** from edge | mm | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Hook (Mnemonic):** > *"**C**ontinuous = **V**isible, **D**ashed = **H**idden, **Dash-Dot = **C**entre, **Dash-Double-Dot = **M**otion."* ### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Line Standards - **CNC Machining:** A CNC operator reads the *visible outlines* (thick continuous) to generate the toolpath. A *hidden edge* (dashed thin) is irrelevant to milling — it lies inside the material. - **3D Printing (Additive Manufacturing):** The CAD model is sliced from the *continuous thick* boundary lines, while the *centre lines* (dash-dot) help the slicer identify symmetry for support structures. - **Aerospace Assembly:** Phantom lines (dash-double-dot) show the *alternative position* of a wing flap — critical for stress engineers to see the range of motion. - **Quality Inspection (Metrology):** An inspector places a comparator probe along the *visible edge* of a part as defined by the *thick continuous line* on the drawing. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Deriving the Line Weight Logic A drawing is read at varying distances — from **20 cm on a desk to 2 m on a wall-mounted plan**. The line weight must therefore be a *proportion* of the sheet size, not a fixed value. The derivation proceeds as follows. ### Step 1 — Define the Sheet Size The KTU practical exam sheet is **A3** (297 mm × 420 mm) or **A2** (420 mm × 594 mm). Let the short edge be $L$ mm. $$ L_{\text{A3}} \;=\; 297 \;\text{mm}, \qquad L_{\text{A2}} \;=\; 420 \;\text{mm} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the BIS Recommended Fraction BIS recommends that the thick line width be approximately $$ w_{\text{thick}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{L}{600} $$ For an A3 sheet: $$ w_{\text{thick}} \;=\; \dfrac{297}{600} \;\approx\; 0.495 \;\text{mm} \;\approx\; 0.5 \;\text{mm} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the 2 : 1 Ratio for the Thin Line $$ w_{\text{thin}} \;=\; \dfrac{w_{\text{thick}}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5}{2} \;=\; 0.25 \;\text{mm} $$ ### Step 4 — Define the Dash and Gap Geometry For a dashed line, the dash length $d$ and gap length $g$ are derived from the line width $w$ as: $$ d \;\approx\; 6\,w, \qquad g \;\approx\; 2\,w $$ For our standard 0.25 mm thin hidden line: $$ d_{\text{hide}} \;\approx\; 6 \times 0.25 \;=\; 1.5 \;\text{mm}, \qquad g_{\text{hide}} \;\approx\; 2 \times 0.25 \;=\; 0.5 \;\text{mm} $$ For the 0.5 mm thick cutting plane line (dash-dot): $$ d_{\text{cut}} \;\approx\; 6 \times 0.5 \;=\; 3.0 \;\text{mm}, \qquad g_{\text{cut}} \;\approx\; 2 \times 0.5 \;=\; 1.0 \;\text{mm} $$ ### Step 5 — Complete the Dimensional Matrix $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Line} \quad &\quad \textbf{Thickness (mm)} \quad &\textbf{Dash (mm)} \quad &\textbf{Gap (mm)} \quad &\textbf{Dot (mm)} \\ \hline \text{Continuous Thick} \quad &\quad 0.50 \quad &\text{—} \quad &\text{—} \quad &\text{—} \\ \text{Continuous Thin} \quad &\quad 0.25 \quad &\text{—} \quad &\text{—} \quad &\text{—} \\ \text{Dashed Thin (hidden)} \quad &\quad 0.25 \quad &1.50 \quad &0.50 \quad &\text{—} \\ \text{Dash-Dot Thin (centre)} \quad &\quad 0.25 \quad &3.00 \quad &1.00 \quad &0.25 \\ \text{Dash-Dot Thick (cutting)} \quad &\quad 0.50 \quad &6.00 \quad &2.00 \quad &0.50 \\ \text{Dash Double-Dot Thin} \quad &\quad 0.25 \quad &3.00 \quad &1.00 \quad &0.25 \times 2 \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Practical Drafting Tip:** When students hand-draw, a "dot" in the centre line is often drawn as a **very short dash (~0.25 mm)** to avoid tearing the paper. The 0.25 mm × 0.25 mm dot rule is for CAD precision. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow Use the following 4-question matrix to identify any line on a drawing: $$ \text{Q1: Continuous or Broken?} \;\to\; \text{Q2: Thick or Thin?} \;\to\; \text{Q3: Dash, Dash-Dot, Dash-Double-Dot?} \;\to\; \text{Q4: Position on drawing?} $$ | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Identity | |---|---|---|---|---| | Continuous | Thick | — | Boundary of object | **Visible Outline** | | Continuous | Thin | — | Outside object | **Dimension / Extension Line** | | Continuous | Thin | — | Inside object | **Hatching / Section Line** | | Dashed | Thin | — | Inside / behind object | **Hidden Edge** | | Dash-Dot | Thin | — | Through centre | **Centre Line / Axis** | | Dash-Dot | Thick | — | At the edge of object | **Cutting Plane Line** | | Dash-Double-Dot | Thin | — | Adjacent to part | **Phantom / Alternate Position** | ## 3.3 Deriving the *Why* of the 2 : 1 Weight Ratio Consider a printed page scanned at 300 DPI. The human eye, at normal reading distance, resolves two lines as separate only if their width differs by a factor of at least 1.5–2.0. A ratio less than 1.5 causes lines to blur into a single grey strip; a ratio greater than 4 makes the thin line invisible. The **2 : 1 ratio** sits at the perceptual sweet spot: $$ 1.5 \;\le\; \dfrac{w_{\text{thick}}}{w_{\text{thin}}} \;\le\; 4 \quad \text{with optimal value} \quad = 2 $$ This is the **psychophysical** basis of every drafting standard in the world — not arbitrary, but measured. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Engineering Drawing Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Engineer Idea] --> B[Concept Sketch] B --> C[Technical Drawing] C --> D{Use Case} D -->|Manufacturing| E[CNC / 3D Print] D -->|Construction| F[Site Execution] D -->|Legal| G[Patent Filing] D -->|Quality| H[Inspection Gauge] C --> I[CAD File .dwg / .step] I --> J[3D Model] J --> K[Simulation FEA / CFD] K --> L[Final Production] ``` ## 4.2 Line Identification Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Identify the Line] --> Q1{Continuous or Broken?} Q1 -->|Continuous| Q2{Thickness?} Q1 -->|Broken| Q3{Dash Pattern?} Q2 -->|Thick| R1[Visible Outline] Q2 -->|Thin| Q4{Where Located?} Q4 -->|On Object| R2[Dimension Line] Q4 -->|Inside Object| R3[Hatching / Section] Q3 -->|Dashed| R4[Hidden Edge] Q3 -->|Dash-Dot| Q5{Thickness?} Q3 -->|Dash-Double-Dot| R6[Phantom / Motion] Q5 -->|Thin| R5[Centre Line / Axis] Q5 -->|Thick| R7[Cutting Plane Line] ``` ## 4.3 Line Types Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR LT1[A. Continuous Thick
Visible Outline] LT2[B1. Continuous Thin
Dimension Line] LT3[B2. Continuous Thin Freehand
Manual Break] LT4[C. Continuous Thin Zig-Zag
Long Break] LT5[D. Dashed Thin
Hidden Edge] LT6[E. Dash-Dot Thin
Centre Line] LT7[F. Dash-Dot Thick
Cutting Plane] LT8[G. Thin Chain
Section Surface] LT9[H. Thick Chain
Surface Treatment] LT10[J. Dash Double-Dot Thin
Alternate Position] LT1 -->|0.5 mm| THK[Thick 0.5 mm] LT7 -->|0.5 mm| THK LT9 -->|0.5 mm| THK LT2 -->|0.25 mm| THN[Thin 0.25 mm] LT3 -->|0.25 mm| THN LT4 -->|0.25 mm| THN LT5 -->|0.25 mm| THN LT6 -->|0.25 mm| THN LT8 -->|0.25 mm| THN LT10 -->|0.25 mm| THN ``` ## 4.4 Application-to-Line Mapping Table | Application Context | Line Type Used | Visual Indicator | |---|---|---| | Outer boundary of a component | A — Continuous Thick | `———————` | | Internal hole not visible from front | D — Dashed Thin | `- - - - -` | | Axis of rotation of a shaft | E — Dash-Dot Thin | `–·–·–·` | | Where the section is cut | F — Dash-Dot Thick | `–·–·–·` (bold) | | Region of heat treatment | H — Thick Chain | `–··–··–` (bold) | | Hatching inside section | B1 — Continuous Thin | `═══ /// ═══` | | Flap in up vs. down position | J — Dash Double-Dot Thin | `–··–··–` | | Dimension value indicator | B1 — Continuous Thin | `———————` with arrow | # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. State the relevance of technical drawing in the engineering field. Mention any four points. **`[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper 1]`** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Technical drawing is the standardized graphical language used by engineers to communicate design intent unambiguously. Its relevance is four-fold: 1. **Universal Communication Medium:** Follows BIS / ISO standards, allowing engineers globally to read and interpret drawings without linguistic barriers. 2. **Manufacturing Driver:** Acts as the direct input for CNC machining, casting, forging, sheet metal layout, and 3D printing. 3. **Legal Documentation:** Patent applications, contractual agreements, and statutory approvals (e.g., factory plans) are validated through the attached engineering drawings. 4. **Quality Inspection & Tolerance Definition:** Every manufactured part is checked against the dimensions and tolerances specified on the drawing, making it the foundation of quality assurance. *(Each point: 0.75 marks; neat labelling & standard reference: 0.5 mark — total 3 marks)* --- ### Q2. List the different types of lines used in engineering drawing as per BIS standards. Draw any three of them. **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary Exam]`** **Model Answer (3 marks):** According to **IS 10714 : 1983** (based on ISO 128), there are **ten standard line types**: A, B.1, B.2, C, D, E, F, G, H, and J. Three key lines (with sketches to be drawn in the exam book): | Line | Type | Use | |---|---|---| | **Continuous Thick (A)** | `———————` (0.5 mm) | Visible outlines | | **Dashed Thin (D)** | `- - - - -` (0.25 mm) | Hidden edges | | **Dash-Dot Thin (E)** | `–·–·–·` (0.25 mm) | Centre lines, axes of symmetry | *(Listing 5–6 lines: 1.5 marks; correctly drawing 3 with thickness labels: 1.5 marks — total 3 marks)* --- ## 📗 PART B — Full ESE Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice ### Question A — 14 Marks **`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, GCEST203, Module 1]`** **(a)** Explain the role of technical drawing as a universal language of engineers. Discuss any **five** applications of technical drawing in modern engineering practice. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With neat sketches, explain the following line types as per BIS standards, stating their thickness and application: (i) Continuous Thick Line (ii) Dashed Thin Line (iii) Dash-Dot Thin Line (iv) Cutting Plane Line (Dash-Dot Thick) **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **(a) Role of Technical Drawing & Five Applications (7 marks)** Technical drawing is the **standardized graphical language** through which engineering ideas are recorded, communicated, and executed. It eliminates the ambiguity inherent in verbal or written descriptions by relying on internationally accepted conventions (BIS / ISO). It serves as the *single source of truth* between design, production, and inspection. **Five Applications:** 1. **Design Communication (1.4 marks):** The drawing conveys the *exact* geometry, size, and material specifications of a component to all stakeholders — designer, draftsman, manufacturer, and inspector — irrespective of their native language. 2. **Manufacturing (1.4 marks):** Provides the master data for CNC toolpath generation, casting pattern making, forging die design, and 3D printing slicing. The visible outlines become the boundary for material removal or addition. 3. **Construction & Civil Engineering (1.4 marks):** Architectural plans, structural reinforcement drawings, and MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, Plumbing) layouts direct the on-site assembly of buildings and infrastructure. 4. **Quality Assurance & Metrology (1.4 marks):** Tolerances, surface finish symbols, and geometric dimensioning & tolerancing (GD&T) callouts on the drawing define the inspection criteria. Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMM) are programmed directly from the CAD model derived from the drawing. 5. **Legal & Patent Documentation (1.4 marks):** A patent's *Claims of Invention* are interpreted alongside the patent drawing, which must comply with technical drawing standards to be admissible. Engineering contracts for fabrication include the drawing as a legally binding document. --- **(b) Four Standard Line Types (7 marks = 1.75 marks each)** **(i) Continuous Thick Line (Type A):** - **Thickness:** **0.5 mm** (thick) - **Application:** Visible outlines, visible edges, edges of objects, the boundary of a part in any view. - **Sketch:** ``` ┌──────────────────┐ │ │ │ VISIBLE EDGE │ │ │ └──────────────────┘ ─────────── ← 0.5 mm thick continuous line ``` **(ii) Dashed Thin Line (Type D):** - **Thickness:** **0.25 mm** (thin) - **Application:** Hidden edges — features of the object that exist but are not visible from the current viewing direction (e.g., an internal hole shown in a front view). - **Sketch:** ``` - - - - - - - - - - ← 0.25 mm dashed line HIDDEN EDGE ``` **(iii) Dash-Dot Thin Line (Type E):** - **Thickness:** **0.25 mm** (thin) - **Application:** Centre lines and axes of symmetry. Pitch circles of gears. Trajectories of motion. - **Sketch:** ``` ────·────·────·──── ← 0.25 mm dash-dot (centre line) ``` **(iv) Cutting Plane Line (Type F — Dash-Dot Thick):** - **Thickness:** **0.5 mm** (thick) - **Application:** Indicates the position of the cutting plane when a sectional view is required. Labelled with arrows showing the viewing direction and capital letters at each end. - **Sketch:** ``` A ════·════·════·════ ════·════ A ← 0.5 mm thick dash-dot ↑ (arrows at 90°) ``` --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, GCEST203, Module 1, Choice B]`** **(a)** What is engineering drawing? Explain how it acts as a link between **design, manufacture, and inspection** in any engineering industry. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between any **four** line types used in engineering drawing, with neat sketches. State the BIS standard reference. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **(a) Engineering Drawing as a Link Between Design, Manufacture & Inspection (7 marks)** **Definition (1 mark):** Engineering drawing is the graphical representation of an engineering object, prepared according to standardized rules (BIS IS 10714 / ISO 128), that conveys complete geometric and dimensional information. **The Three-Stage Link (6 × 1 mark each):** | Stage | Function of Drawing | Practical Example | |---|---|---| | **1. Design** | The designer converts the idea into a 2D representation with dimensions, tolerances, and material specifications. | A mechanical engineer designs a flange coupling: front view, top view, and side view with 4-Ø12 holes. | | **2. Manufacture** | The drawing is sent to the workshop. The machinist reads dimensions, surface finish, and tolerances to set up the lathe, mill, or CNC. | The flange is turned on a lathe to Ø100 mm; 4 holes drilled at PCD 70 mm. | | **3. Inspection** | The inspector uses the same drawing to verify each dimension with vernier calipers, micrometers, and CMM. The part is accepted only if it matches the drawing within tolerance. | Diameter is checked at Ø100 ± 0.05 mm; bolt circle verified at 70 ± 0.1 mm. | > This **single document** travels from design desk to factory floor to quality lab, ensuring that the part that was imagined, the part that was made, and the part that was accepted are all the *same* part. This is the essence of engineering drawing's role as a universal link. --- **(b) Differentiate Between Four Line Types (7 marks = 1.75 marks each)** | Sl | Line Type | Thickness | Dash Pattern | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Continuous Thick (A)** | 0.5 mm | Continuous, no breaks | Visible outlines and edges | | 2 | **Continuous Thin (B1)** | 0.25 mm | Continuous, no breaks | Dimension lines, extension lines, hatching, leader lines | | 3 | **Dashed Thin (D)** | 0.25 mm | Short dashes, equal gaps | Hidden edges and hidden outlines | | 4 | **Dash-Dot Thin (E)** | 0.25 mm | Long dash, dot, long dash, dot | Centre lines, axes of symmetry, pitch circles | | 5 | **Dash-Dot Thick (F)** | 0.5 mm | Long dash, dot (thicker) | Cutting plane lines for sectional views | **BIS Standard Reference:** **IS 10714 : 1983** (reaffirmed 2004), aligned with **ISO 128**. **Sketch Comparison (to be drawn in the answer script):** ``` CONTINUOUS THICK ───────────────── (0.5 mm) CONTINUOUS THIN ───────────────── (0.25 mm) DASHED THIN ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ (0.25 mm) DASH-DOT THIN ─·─·─·─·─·─·─·─ (0.25 mm) DASH-DOT THICK ═══·═══·═══·═══ (0.5 mm) ``` --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:** > 1. **Confusing E and F lines:** *Thin* dash-dot = centre line; *Thick* dash-dot = cutting plane. Swapping them invalidates any sectioning question. > 2. **Wrong line weight ratio:** Examiners check the 2:1 ratio. Drawing a "thick" line at 0.35 mm and "thin" at 0.25 mm makes the difference invisible — you lose marks for non-compliance with BIS. > 3. **Missing labels on sketches:** A 7-mark question requiring line sketches is *not complete* unless each line is labelled with thickness, dash pattern, and application. > 4. **Forgetting to mention the BIS code:** Always cite **"IS 10714 : 1983"** or **"ISO 128"** to score the standards-related marks. > 5. **Using a continuous thick line for hidden edges:** Hidden lines *must* be **dashed thin**, not continuous. This is the #1 reason for losing 2–3 marks in assembly drawing. > 6. **Not drawing the centre line through the entire object:** Centre lines must extend slightly *beyond* the object outline — about 3 mm past the edge. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ Technical drawing is the **universal, standardized graphical language of engineers**, governed by **BIS IS 10714 : 1983** and **ISO 128**. - ✅ Its relevance spans **design communication, manufacturing, legal documentation, quality inspection, construction, and global collaboration**. - ✅ All engineering drawings use **only two line weights**: **thick (0.5 mm)** and **thin (0.25 mm)**, in a strict **2 : 1 ratio**. - ✅ The **ten standard line types** are A, B.1, B.2, C, D, E, F, G, H, J (note: the letters **I and K are skipped** by convention). - ✅ **Continuous Thick = Visible Outline** (most important line on the drawing). - ✅ **Dashed Thin = Hidden Edge** (features not visible from the current view). - ✅ **Dash-Dot Thin = Centre Line / Axis of Symmetry** (always thin, never thick). - ✅ **Dash-Dot Thick = Cutting Plane Line** (only thick line that is dash-dot). - ✅ **Dash-Double-Dot Thin = Phantom / Alternate / Repeated Detail** (e.g., flap in up vs. down position). - ✅ **Dash length ≈ 6 × line width; gap ≈ 2 × line width** — memorize this for KTU practicals. - ✅ Centre lines must extend **~3 mm beyond the object outline**. - ✅ Always label line sketches with **thickness in mm** and **standard code reference**. - ✅ The mnemonic for line types: **"C V D H C C M"** — *Continuous Visible, Dashed Hidden, Chain Centre, etc.* (build your own version during revision). - ✅ For KTU exam, the default sheet is **A3** with a **10 mm border**, thick outer border, thin inner working frame. - ✅ The dimensioning, sectioning, and projection topics (Modules 2–5) all rely on this Module 1 foundation — **master the line types first**.

topics.title: Dimensioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Dimensioning ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Dimensioning** is the precise engineering practice of adding numerical values (sizes) and explanatory notes to a technical drawing so that the geometry, size, and relative position of every feature of a manufactured component can be completely and unambiguously understood — without measuring directly from the drawing. According to **BIS SP 46:1988** (Bureau of Indian Standards — the standard followed by KTU), dimensioning is a *language of sizes* used to convey the designer's intent to the manufacturer, inspector, and quality controller. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 1 Core Definition** > Dimensioning is the process of *describing* a part, not *describing* how to make it. It specifies **WHAT** the size is, not **HOW** to produce it. The four fundamental purposes of dimensioning are: 1. **Size** — to describe magnitude of features (length, diameter, angle). 2. **Location** — to describe the position of features relative to others. 3. **Functional requirements** — to ensure mating parts fit together. 4. **Manufacturing/process reference** — to communicate to the shop floor. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think like a tailor stitching a shirt!** 🎽 > > Imagine you are a tailor and a customer wants a shirt made. You cannot just describe a shirt — you need to write down exact numbers: *sleeve length = 24 inches, chest = 40 inches, shoulder = 18 inches*. If you miss a single measurement, the shirt won't fit. > > **An engineering drawing works the same way.** The drawing is a picture; **dimensioning is the tailor's measurement slip**. Without dimensions, the drawing is just a pretty sketch — a workshop cannot manufacture anything from it. Dimensions are the *bridge between design and production*. ### 1.3 The Dimensioning Vocabulary (Quick Glossary) | Term | Meaning (Plain English) | KTU Standard Reference | |---|---|---| | Dimension | A numerical size value (e.g., 40 mm) | Linear or angular | | Tolerance | Allowed variation from the exact size | ±0.05 mm | | Feature | A hole, slot, edge, or surface | Geometric entity | | Datum | A reference surface/axis for measurements | Reference feature | | Leader | Slanted line used to point to a feature | For notes/circular features | | Arrowhead | Filled triangle at line ends | BIS SP 46 §3.2 | > [!TIP] > KTU examiners expect students to use the **exact BIS SP 46:1988 terminology**. Vague terms like "size" or "length mark" will cost you marks. Always say *"Dimension line"*, *"Extension line"*, *"Leader line"*, and *"Arrowhead"*. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Elements of a Dimension — The "Five Musicians" of Dimensioning Every dimension in a KTU / BIS drawing is composed of **five mandatory elements** (when fully dimensioned). Mastering these is essential for the ESE: 1. **Dimension Line** — A continuous thin line (type `0.25` stroke) drawn *between* two extension lines. It shows the direction in which the measurement is taken. Dimension text is placed *centred* and *broken* through the line (BIS rule). 2. **Extension Line (Witness Line)** — A continuous thin line that *projects* from the feature. It must extend ~3 mm beyond the dimension line. 3. **Arrowheads** — Solid filled triangles placed at the ends of dimension lines, touching the extension lines. Size ≈ 3× the line width. 4. **Dimension Text** — Numerals indicating the size. Placed above the dimension line, centred, and *broken* through the line. 5. **Leader Line** — A slanted thin line ending in an arrowhead, used when space is tight (e.g., for radii, holes, notes). The text is placed at the *horizontal* end. ### 2.2 Classification of Dimensions (Deep Theory) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners often ask: *"Classify dimensions in an engineering drawing."* The standard BIS answer is below. **A. By Direction / Type:** - **Linear dimensions** — for length, width, height. - **Angular dimensions** — for angles in degrees. - **Radial dimensions (R)** — for arcs and fillets. - **Diametral dimensions (Ø)** — for circles. **B. By Function:** - **Size dimensions** — describe magnitude of a feature. - **Location dimensions** — describe position from datums. **C. By Method of Placement:** - **Aligned dimensioning** — dimension text is parallel to the dimension line (used in oblique views). - **Unidirectional dimensioning** — all text is horizontal regardless of line direction (KTU preferred; easier to read). - **Chain (continuous) dimensioning** — a series of dimensions placed end-to-end along a single line. - **Parallel (datum) dimensioning** — all dimensions are referenced from a single datum surface (preferred for manufactured parts). - **Combined dimensioning** — a mix of chain and parallel. **D. By Coordinate System:** - **Rectangular coordinate dimensioning** — uses X and Y from a datum origin. - **Polar coordinate dimensioning** — uses a radius and an angle from a datum. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value bars inside markdown tables. Never use `|` directly — it breaks the table. | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | KTU Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Chord length of polygon | $c = 2 R \sin\!\left(\frac{180^\circ}{n}\right)$ | mm | Dimensioning a regular polygon | | 2 | Apothem (in-radius) | $a = R \cos\!\left(\frac{180^\circ}{n}\right)$ | mm | Locating polygon centre | | 3 | Circumradius (R) from side (s) | $R = \dfrac{s}{2 \sin\!\left(\frac{180^\circ}{n}\right)}$ | mm | Designing polygon from side length | | 4 | Linear dimension linear error | $\delta L = L_{\text{actual}} - L_{\text{nominal}}$ | mm | Tolerance checks | | 5 | Angular error | $\delta \theta = \theta_{\text{actual}} - \theta_{\text{nominal}}$ | degrees | Angular tolerance | | 6 | Circle spacing on PCD | $\text{Arc} = 2\pi R \cdot \dfrac{\theta^\circ}{360^\circ}$ | mm | Bolt circle dimensioning | | 7 | Polar coordinates of point | $(r \cos\theta, \; r\sin\theta)$ | mm | Polar dimensioning | | 8 | Leader line angle (BIS) | Recommended $\approx 30^\circ$ to $60^\circ$ | degrees | Neat leader placement | | 9 | Extension line gap | $\approx 1\text{ mm}$ from feature, $\approx 3\text{ mm}$ beyond arrow | mm | Drawing neatness | | 10 | Arrowhead length | $\approx 3 \times$ line width | mm | BIS SP 46 §3.2 | ### 2.4 Why Dimensioning Matters in Real Engineering - **Manufacturability**: A part without dimensions cannot be produced. CNC machines, lathes, and 3-axis mills all read from dimensioned drawings. - **Interchangeability**: Parts made in different factories can be assembled only if both follow the same dimensions. - **Quality Control (QC)**: Inspectors use dimensions to accept/reject parts (with tolerance). - **Cost Reduction**: Poor dimensioning leads to scrap, rework, and warranty claims — costing industries millions. > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Tip**: When answering *"Discuss the importance of dimensioning"*, mention **all four** points above: Manufacturability, Interchangeability, QC, and Cost. This typically fetches the full 7 marks in a sub-question. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Chord Length of a Regular Polygon (used to dimension circumscribed polygons) **Problem statement:** Derive an expression for the side length of a regular polygon with $n$ sides, inscribed in a circle of radius $R$. **Step 1 — Set up the geometry.** Consider a regular $n$-sided polygon inscribed in a circle of centre $O$ and circumradius $R$. Draw $n$ radii from $O$ to each vertex. The full $360^\circ$ is divided equally among $n$ triangles formed at the centre. $$ \text{Central angle subtended by one side} \;=\; \frac{360^\circ}{n} $$ **Step 2 — Isolate one isosceles triangle.** Take one triangle $OAB$ where $OA = OB = R$. The angle $\angle AOB = 360^\circ / n$. Drop a perpendicular from $O$ to $AB$, meeting it at midpoint $M$. This perpendicular *bisects* the central angle. $$ \angle AOM = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{360^\circ}{n} = \frac{180^\circ}{n} $$ **Step 3 — Apply trigonometry in the right triangle $OMA$.** In the right-angled triangle $OMA$ (right angle at $M$): - Hypotenuse $= OA = R$ - Opposite side to $\angle AOM$ $= AM = s/2$ (where $s$ is the polygon side length) Therefore: $$ \sin\!\left(\frac{180^\circ}{n}\right) = \frac{s/2}{R} = \frac{s}{2R} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for the chord length $s$.** $$ s = 2 R \sin\!\left(\frac{180^\circ}{n}\right) $$ This is the **chord length formula** — the most-used dimensioning formula in KTU Module 1. ✅ ### 3.2 Derivation: Apothem (In-radius) of a Regular Polygon **Problem statement:** Find the apothem $a$ of a regular $n$-sided polygon of circumradius $R$. From the same right-triangle $OMA$ in §3.1: - $OM$ is the **adjacent** side to angle $\angle AOM$ - Therefore: $$ \cos\!\left(\frac{180^\circ}{n}\right) = \frac{OM}{R} = \frac{a}{R} $$ **Solving for the apothem:** $$ a = R \cos\!\left(\frac{180^\circ}{n}\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical KTU use**: When dimensioning a hexagonal nut or a square boss on a shaft, the apothem formula lets you place the *flat-to-flat* dimension from the polygon's centre — which is the *manufactured* size on a milling machine. ### 3.3 Derivation: Bolt-Circle (PCD) Chord Length **Problem statement:** A flange has 6 bolt holes equally spaced on a Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD) of 200 mm. Find the chordal distance between two adjacent hole centres. **Step 1:** Number of holes $n = 6$, PCD radius $R = 100$ mm. **Step 2:** Apply the chord formula from §3.1. $$ s = 2 R \sin\!\left(\frac{180^\circ}{n}\right) = 2 \cdot 100 \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{180^\circ}{6}\right) $$ **Step 3:** Compute the central angle. $$ \frac{180^\circ}{6} = 30^\circ $$ **Step 4:** Evaluate $\sin 30^\circ$. $$ \sin 30^\circ = 0.5 $$ **Step 5:** Final numerical answer. $$ s = 200 \cdot 0.5 = 100 \text{ mm} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result**: For 6 holes on a 200 mm PCD, the centre-to-centre distance is exactly **100 mm**. This matches the well-known "hex bolt-circle = radius" property. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Placement Rules for Common Features (BIS SP 46) | Feature | Preferred Dimension Type | Placement Rule | Common Mistake | |---|---|---|---| | Circle | Diametral (Ø) | Inside or on leader | Using radius in shop drawings | | Arc < 180° | Radius (R) | Leader line, single arrow | Two-arrow diameter | | Arc ≥ 180° | Diameter (Ø) | Inside or outside | Using R on a full circle | | Hole | Ø with leader | Note depth separately | Omitting depth | | Chamfer | Linear or angular | Show both C × 45° | Missing angle | | Fillet | R with leader | Single R for identical fillets | Dimensioning every fillet | | Slot | Length × Width | Centreline through slot | Missing radius callout | | Thread | M8 or Ø8 with depth | Note on leader | Drawing thread pictorially | | Counterbore / Countersink | Ø × depth | Note on leader | Missing depth | | Polygon (n=6) | Across-flats (AF) | Apothem from centre | Across-corners (rare) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Five Elements of a Dimension ```mermaid flowchart TD A[DIMENSION SET] --> B1[Dimension Line] A --> B2[Extension Line] A --> B3[Arrowhead] A --> B4[Dimension Text] A --> B5[Leader Line] B1 --> C1[Thin continuous line
Between extension lines
Text broken through] B2 --> C2[Thin continuous line
1 mm gap from feature
3 mm beyond arrow] B3 --> C3[Solid filled triangle
Touches extension line
3x line width] B4 --> C4[Numerical value
Above dimension line
Centred, horizontal] B5 --> C5[Slanted thin line
30 to 60 deg angle
For radii and notes] A --> D[OUTPUT
Complete unambiguous size] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Dimensioning Decision Process ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Start with feature type] --> P2{Is it a circle?} P2 -- Yes --> P3{Arc less than 180 deg?} P2 -- No --> P4{Is it a linear edge?} P3 -- Yes --> P5[Use RADIUS R
with leader line] P3 -- No --> P6[Use DIAMETER D
with prefix symbol] P4 -- Yes --> P7{Angle available?} P7 -- Yes --> P8[Use ALIGNED system
text parallel to line] P7 -- No --> P9[Use UNIDIRECTIONAL
all text horizontal] P5 --> P10[Place dimension outside
profile wherever possible] P6 --> P10 P8 --> P10 P9 --> P10 P10 --> P11[Verify no duplication
and no missing size] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Types of Dimensioning Systems ```mermaid flowchart LR DIM[Dimensioning Systems] --> L[Linear] DIM --> ANG[Angular] DIM --> RAD[Radial] DIM --> DIA[Diametral] L --> M1[Aligned] L --> M2[Unidirectional] L --> M3[Chain] L --> M4[Parallel Datum] L --> M5[Combined] L --> M6[Coordinate Rectangular] L --> M7[Coordinate Polar] ANG --> N1[Vertex at corner] ANG --> N2[Vertex away from corner] RAD --> O1[Arc less than 180 deg] RAD --> O2[Arc exactly 180 deg] DIA --> Q1[Internal circle] DIA --> Q2[External cylinder] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Tree: Functional Hierarchy of a Dimensioned Drawing ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[Engineering Drawing] --> SIZE[Size Dimensions] ROOT --> LOC[Location Dimensions] ROOT --> NOTE[Notes and Tolerances] SIZE --> S1[Lengths] SIZE --> S2[Widths] SIZE --> S3[Heights] SIZE --> S4[Diameters] SIZE --> S5[Radii] SIZE --> S6[Angles] LOC --> L1[From datum edge] LOC --> L2[From datum axis] LOC --> L3[From datum plane] NOTE --> N1[Surface roughness] NOTE --> N2[Material grade] NOTE --> N3[General tolerances] NOTE --> N4[Title block info] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Drawing Skill Tip**: When you draw dimensions in the ESE, follow this hierarchy order — *size first, then location, then notes*. This keeps your drawing logical and easy for the examiner to evaluate. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper, Q1a]** **Q1. (a)** Define dimensioning. State any **four** rules to be followed while dimensioning an engineering drawing as per BIS SP 46:1988. **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Definition (1 mark):* Dimensioning is the process of adding complete size and location information to a drawing so that the manufactured part can be produced without ambiguity, as per BIS SP 46:1988. *Four Rules (2 marks — 0.5 each):* 1. Each feature shall be dimensioned only **once** on a drawing (no duplication). 2. Dimensions should be placed in the view that shows the feature **most clearly** (typically between visible lines). 3. Dimensions should be placed **outside the profile** wherever possible to avoid crossing other lines. 4. **Extension lines** should not cross dimension lines, and arrowheads should touch the extension lines, not leave a gap. --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper, Q1b]** **Q1. (b)** Differentiate between **Aligned** and **Unidirectional** systems of dimensioning. Which is preferred by BIS? **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Aligned System | Unidirectional System | |---|---|---| | Text orientation | Parallel to the dimension line | Always horizontal | | Readability in oblique views | Better | Same in all views | | Production practice | Old convention | Modern, easier to read | | BIS SP 46 preference | Acceptable | **Preferred** ✅ | In the *aligned* system, the dimension numerals are placed so that they are *parallel* to the dimension line, meaning they tilt in oblique views. In the *unidirectional* system, all numerals are written *horizontally*, regardless of the dimension line's slope. **BIS SP 46:1988 recommends the unidirectional system** because it is easier to read and avoids numerals appearing upside-down or sideways. ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper, Q2 — Internal Choice]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the **different methods of placing linear dimensions** with neat sketches. Discuss the relative merits of each. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Method 1 — Chain (Continuous) Dimensioning:** Dimensions are placed end-to-end along a single straight line. The second feature is dimensioned from the first, the third from the second, and so on. *Merit*: Easy to dimension. *Demerit*: Accumulates tolerance — the last feature's error equals the sum of all intermediate errors, making it unsuitable for precision parts. **Method 2 — Parallel (Datum) Dimensioning:** All features are dimensioned from a single reference (datum) surface. *Merit*: Eliminates tolerance accumulation; preferred in manufacturing. *Demerit*: Drawing becomes crowded if too many features are parallel. **Method 3 — Combined Dimensioning:** A hybrid of chain and parallel. The most important dimension is parallel, and others are chained. *Merit*: Balances clarity and tolerance control. *Demerit*: Requires judgement to choose which dimension to keep parallel. **Method 4 — Coordinate Dimensioning (Rectangular):** Each feature is given $x$ and $y$ distances from a datum origin. *Merit*: Excellent for CNC machining; tolerance accumulation is visible. *Demerit*: Requires a clear datum frame and the part must be aligned to axes. **Method 5 — Polar Coordinate Dimensioning:** Each feature is given a radius $R$ and an angle $\theta$ from a datum. *Merit*: Compact for radial layouts (e.g., bolt circles). *Demerit*: Harder to read for rectangular parts. **(b)** A regular **hexagonal nut** has a *circumscribing circle* of 50 mm diameter. Calculate: (i) The side length of the hexagon. (ii) The distance across flats (apothem × 2). (iii) The centre-to-centre distance between two adjacent holes on a 6-hole bolt circle of 80 mm PCD. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Hexagon: $n = 6$, circumradius $R_{\text{hex}} = 25$ mm. - PCD: $n = 6$, $R_{\text{PCD}} = 40$ mm. **(i) Side length of hexagon** — using the chord formula from §3.1: $$ s = 2 R \sin\!\left(\frac{180^\circ}{n}\right) = 2 \cdot 25 \cdot \sin 30^\circ $$ $$ s = 50 \cdot 0.5 = 25 \text{ mm} $$ **(ii) Distance across flats (AF)** — using the apothem from §3.2: $$ a = R \cos\!\left(\frac{180^\circ}{n}\right) = 25 \cdot \cos 30^\circ $$ $$ a = 25 \cdot 0.8660 = 21.65 \text{ mm} $$ $$ AF = 2a = 43.30 \text{ mm} $$ **(iii) Centre-to-centre distance on 80 mm PCD** — using §3.3: $$ s = 2 \cdot 40 \cdot \sin 30^\circ = 80 \cdot 0.5 = 40 \text{ mm} $$ **Final Answers:** - Side length = **25 mm** - Across flats = **43.30 mm** - PCD chord = **40 mm** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **five elements of a dimension**. State BIS rules for the **size and gap** of each element. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (Sketch Description for ESE):** *Draw a simple rectangle (say 60 × 40 mm) with a Ø20 mm hole in the centre. Mark all five elements with leader callouts.* | Element | Type of Line | BIS Rule for Size/Gap | |---|---|---| | Dimension line | Continuous thin | Gap of 10 mm between parallel dimension lines | | Extension line | Continuous thin | 1 mm gap from feature, extends 3 mm past arrow | | Arrowhead | Solid filled | Length ≈ 3 × line width, width = line width × 1.5 | | Dimension text | Numerals | Height 3–5 mm, centred, broken through line | | Leader line | Continuous thin | Slope 30°–60° to horizontal, ends with arrowhead | *Note*: All dimensioning lines should be of the *same line weight* and noticeably *thinner* than object lines. **(b)** A square plate 100 mm × 100 mm has **four Ø12 mm holes**, one in each corner. The centre of each hole is at 15 mm from the nearest edges. Draw the plate and **dimension it fully** as per BIS. Calculate the **diagonal distance** between the centres of two opposite holes. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify feature coordinates:** Place the plate with its bottom-left corner at the origin. The four hole centres are at: - H1: $(15, 15)$ - H2: $(85, 15)$ - H3: $(85, 85)$ - H4: $(15, 85)$ **Step 2 — Compute the diagonal distance between H1 and H3:** $$ d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2} $$ $$ d = \sqrt{(85 - 15)^2 + (85 - 15)^2} $$ $$ d = \sqrt{70^2 + 70^2} = \sqrt{4900 + 4900} = \sqrt{9800} $$ $$ d = 70\sqrt{2} \approx 98.99 \text{ mm} \approx 99.0 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3 — List the dimensions to be shown on the drawing:** | S.No. | Dimension | Value | Type | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Plate length | 100 mm | Size (horizontal) | | 2 | Plate width | 100 mm | Size (vertical) | | 3 | Hole diameter | Ø12 | Size (diametral) | | 4 | Hole 1 to bottom edge | 15 mm | Location (datum) | | 5 | Hole 1 to left edge | 15 mm | Location (datum) | | 6 | Diagonal check | 99.0 mm | Reference (optional) | **Final Answer:** Diagonal distance = **99.0 mm** (approx). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Never** write dimension values on the object line or extension line — always on the **dimension line**, *broken* through. (-1 mark if missed) > 2. **Never** dimension to a hidden line. Always dimension to the *visible* view. (-1 mark) > 3. **Never** close a dimension loop. The chain should not form a closed polygon — leave one gap. (-1 mark) > 4. **Never** put the Ø symbol on a radius or vice versa. A *sphere* is dimensioned as "SØ40", not "R40". (-1 mark) > 5. **Always** show units in the title block once, *not* after every dimension (e.g., write "ALL DIMENSIONS IN mm"). (-0.5 mark) > 6. **Always** use *unidirectional* dimensioning (horizontal text) for KTU 2024. (-0.5 mark if aligned is used) > 7. **Do not** show more dimensions than necessary. Each feature should have *one* size dimension. (-1 mark for every duplicate) ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 - ✅ Dimensioning is governed by **BIS SP 46:1988** in India — memorize this code. - ✅ Every dimension has **five elements**: dimension line, extension line, arrowhead, text, leader. - ✅ Use **unidirectional** system; place dimensions **outside** the profile wherever possible. - ✅ Dimension **each feature once only** — no duplication, no closure. - ✅ **Ø (diameter) symbol** is used for full circles and cylinders; **R** is used for arcs less than 180°. - ✅ For **regular polygons**, use the chord formula: $s = 2R\sin(180^\circ/n)$. - ✅ For **bolt circles (PCD)**, the chord between adjacent holes is $s = 2R\sin(180^\circ/n)$. - ✅ The **apothem** $a = R\cos(180^\circ/n)$ gives the *flat-to-flat* distance of polygons. - ✅ Dimensioning follows the hierarchy: **size → location → notes**. - ✅ Units go in the **title block**, not next to every number. - ✅ Keep all dimension lines **thinner** than object/outline lines. - ✅ Don't dimension to **hidden lines**; use the view where the feature is most visible. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU 2024 Advice**: In the ESE drawing paper, **finish all object line drawing first**, then add dimensions in a *single pass* using a *thinner pencil*. This is how professional draftsmen work, and your drawing will look polished — examiners reward neatness generously (up to **1–2 bonus marks** out of the part-B total).

topics.title: BIS Code of Practice for Technical Drawing. Projection of Points in Different Quadrants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** BIS Code of Practice for Technical Drawing. Projection of Points in Different Quadrants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: Foundations of Engineering Drawing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 BIS Code of Practice for Technical Drawing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** code **SP 46:2003** is the official *Code of Practice for General Engineering Drawing*. It standardizes the conventions, symbols, line types, sheet sizes, scales, lettering, and dimensioning practices used in technical drawings across India. Adherence to BIS SP 46 ensures uniformity, clarity, and unambiguous interpretation of engineering drawings by all stakeholders.

**Key BIS Governing Bodies & Codes:**
- **BIS** = Bureau of Indian Standards (formerly ISI – Indian Standards Institution).
- **SP 46:2003** = Standard Practice for General Engineering Drawing.
- **IS 696** = Code for general engineering drawing practices.
- **IS 3232** = Code for technical lettering on engineering drawings.
- **IS 15021** = Code for Computer Aided Design (CAD) practices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BIS Codes Matter:**
> Just as a language has grammar rules, technical drawing has **BIS conventions**. Without them, a mechanical drawing made in Kerala cannot be correctly interpreted in a manufacturing unit in Punjab. BIS codes are the *universal grammar of Indian engineering drawing*.

### 1.2 Concept of Projection in Engineering Drawing

**Formal Definition:**
*Projection* is the geometric process of representing a three-dimensional object on a two-dimensional plane (such as a sheet of paper) using a system of rays (projectors) emanating from a defined viewpoint.

**Types of Projection under BIS SP 46:**

| Projection Type | BIS Code Reference | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Orthographic Projection** | SP 46 §5.1 | Projection by parallel projectors perpendicular to the plane. |
| **Perspective Projection** | SP 46 §5.3 | Projection by converging projectors from a station point. |
| **Isometric Projection** | SP 46 §5.4 | A specific type of axonometric projection (30° axes). |
| **Auxiliary Projection** | SP 46 §5.5 | Projection on an inclined or oblique plane. |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – Projection of Points

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Sun and the Shadow"**
> Imagine a **point** (a pin) held in space between the **Sun** (light source at infinity) and the **Ground**.
> - The **Ground** represents the **Horizontal Plane (HP)**.
> - A **Vertical Wall** behind the pin represents the **Vertical Plane (VP)**.
> - The **Sun's rays** are the *projectors* that cast the *shadow* of the pin on HP and VP.
> - The shadow on the **Ground (HP)** is the **Top View (TV)**.
> - The shadow on the **Wall (VP)** is the **Front View (FV)**.
> - The **point** itself, when seen in elevation, gives its **True Position**.

If the point is **above HP and in front of VP** → First Quadrant.  
If the point is **below HP and in front of VP** → Second Quadrant.  
If the point is **below HP and behind VP** → Third Quadrant.  
If the point is **above HP and behind VP** → Fourth Quadrant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:**
> In KTU examinations, *First Angle Projection* is the standard method as per BIS SP 46. The symbol for **First Angle Projection** is a truncated cone seen from the side. This is mandatory for all Indian engineering drawings.

### 1.4 The Four Quadrants – Conceptual Map

The two principal reference planes divide 3D space into **four quadrants**:

| Quadrant | Position of Point | Front View | Top View |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | Above HP, In front of VP | Above XY | Below XY |
| **II** | Below HP, In front of VP | Below XY | Below XY |
| **III** | Below HP, Behind VP | Below XY | Above XY |
| **IV** | Above HP, Behind VP | Above XY | Above XY |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick (For KTU Exam):**
> *Quadrant I = Both views "normal" (FV above, TV below) – this is the "Happy" quadrant.*
> *Quadrant III = Both views are reversed (FV below, TV above) – the "Upside Down" quadrant.*
> *Quadrant II = FV below, TV below = "Crushed" quadrant.*
> *Quadrant IV = FV above, TV above = "Floating" quadrant.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BIS SP 46:2003 – Detailed Technical Conventions

### 2.1.1 Standard Sheet Sizes (BIS SP 46)

BIS specifies six preferred sheet sizes for engineering drawings. The dimensions follow a ratio derived from ISO A-series.

| Designation | Size (mm × mm) | Trimmed Edges | Margin (mm) | Drawing Area (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A0** | 841 × 1189 | 1 each side | 20 / 10 | 821 × 1169 |
| **A1** | 594 × 841 | 1 each side | 20 / 10 | 574 × 821 |
| **A2** | 420 × 594 | 1 each side | 20 / 10 | 400 × 574 |
| **A3** | 297 × 420 | 1 each side | 20 / 10 | 277 × 400 |
| **A4** | 210 × 297 | 1 each side | 20 / 10 | 190 × 277 |
| **A5** | 148 × 210 | 1 each side | 20 / 10 | 128 × 190 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** KTU B.Tech practicals and submission drawings typically use **A2 or A3 sheets**. Theoretical questions on sheet sizes are often asked for **3 marks** in Part A.

### 2.1.2 BIS Standard Line Types (IS 696 & SP 46)

The quality and meaning of a drawing is heavily dependent on **line weights** and **line types**. BIS classifies lines into two thickness groups:
- **Thick lines (Type A)**: 0.5 mm to 0.7 mm nominal
- **Thin lines (Type B)**: Approximately half of thick line

| Line Type | BIS Code | Lineweight | Purpose | Appearance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Thick** | Type 01.1 | Thick | Visible edges, outlines | _______ |
| **Continuous Thin** | Type 01.2 | Thin | Dimension lines, hatching, leader lines | _______ |
| **Dashed Thin** | Type 02.1 | Thin | Hidden edges | - - - - - |
| **Chain Thin** (Thin Long-Dashed Dotted) | Type 05.1 | Thin | Centre lines, axis of symmetry | __.__.__ |
| **Chain Thick** (Thick Long-Dashed Dotted) | Type 05.2 | Thick | Cutting plane lines | __..__. |
| **Continuous Thin Freehand** | Type 01.3 | Thin | Break lines (short breaks) | ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ |
| **Continuous Thin Zig-Zag** | Type 01.4 | Thin | Break lines (long breaks) | ∿∿∿∿ |

### 2.1.3 Standard Scales (BIS SP 46 §4)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scale = Drawing Length / Actual Length**

| Scale Type | Use Case | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Size** | 1:1 | Small machine parts, details |
| **Reduction Scale** | Drawing < Actual | 1:2, 1:5, 1:10, 1:20, 1:50, 1:100, 1:200, 1:500, 1:1000 |
| **Enlargement Scale** | Drawing > Actual | 2:1, 5:1, 10:1, 20:1, 50:1 |

### 2.1.4 BIS Lettering Standards (IS 3232)

- Lettering shall be **vertical (upright)** or **inclined at 75°** to the baseline.
- All letters must be in **uppercase (CAPITAL)**.
- Minimum height of capital letters: **2.5 mm**; Recommended: **3.5 mm** to **5 mm**.
- For title block: **7 mm** or higher.

## 2.2 Principal Planes of Projection

### 2.2.1 Definition of Reference Planes

**Horizontal Plane (HP):**
The plane on which the object is assumed to rest. It is conceptually horizontal, like the ground.

**Vertical Plane (VP):**
The plane perpendicular to HP that represents the wall of elevation. It is the *Plane of the Paper* on which the front view is drawn.

**Profile Plane (PP):**
A vertical plane perpendicular to both HP and VP. Used for side views.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BIS Convention: First Angle Projection**
> In India (and Europe), **First Angle Projection** is the BIS standard. The object is placed in the **First Quadrant** (above HP, in front of VP). The views are projected *through* the object onto the plane *behind/below* it.

### 2.2.2 Quadrants and the XY Reference Line

The line of intersection of HP and VP is called the **Reference Line (XY Line)**.

| Position of Point | HP Distance (a) | VP Distance (d) |
|---|---|---|
| Quadrant I | +a (above HP) | +d (in front of VP) |
| Quadrant II | –a (below HP) | +d (in front of VP) |
| Quadrant III | –a (below HP) | –d (behind VP) |
| Quadrant IV | +a (above HP) | –d (behind VP) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### 2.3.1 Projection of Points – Core Relationships

| Symbol | Meaning | Quadrant I | Quadrant II | Quadrant III | Quadrant IV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| a | Distance of point above HP | +a | –a | –a | +a |
| d | Distance of point in front of VP | +d | +d | –d | –d |
| FV | Front View Position | +a (above XY) | –a (below XY) | –a (below XY) | +a (above XY) |
| TV | Top View Position | –d (below XY) | –d (below XY) | +d (above XY) | +d (above XY) |
| Position in FV | Above / Below XY | Above | Below | Below | Above |
| Position in TV | Above / Below XY | Below | Below | Above | Above |

### 2.3.2 Essential Geometrical Formulas

For a point P at coordinates (x, y, z) where:
- x = distance from PP (Profile Plane)
- y = distance from VP
- z = distance from HP

$$ \text{Distance of P from HP} = z = a $$

$$ \text{Distance of P from VP} = y = d $$

$$ \text{Distance of P from PP} = x $$

The distance of FV from XY line equals the distance of the point from HP:

$$ \text{Y}_{\text{FV}} = \pm a $$

The distance of TV from XY line equals the distance of the point from VP:

$$ \text{Y}_{\text{TV}} = \pm d $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention in KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - **Positive (+):** Above HP, In front of VP.
> - **Negative (–):** Below HP, Behind VP.

### 2.3.3 Engineering Utility of Projection of Points

| Application | Engineering Field |
|---|---|
| Locating machine parts on assembly drawings | Mechanical Engineering |
| Surveying coordinates in civil layouts | Civil Engineering |
| 3D modeling & CAD coordinate systems | Computer Aided Design |
| Architectural plan & elevation | Architecture |
| Aircraft & ship 3-axis position tracking | Aerospace / Naval |
| Robotics – end-effector position | Mechatronics |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Geometric Derivation of Projection of Points

### 3.1.1 Setup: Point P in First Quadrant

Let a point **P** be located at a distance **'a'** above the Horizontal Plane (HP) and **'d'** in front of the Vertical Plane (VP).

**Given:**
- HP → ground (horizontal reference)
- VP → vertical reference (wall)
- P is positioned in 3D space at coordinates (x, y, z) = (0, d, a) if we set PP as origin reference.

**Step 1 – Identification of distances**
Let the point P be observed from:
- A vertical line of sight (perpendicular to HP) → projects P onto HP.
- A horizontal line of sight (perpendicular to VP) → projects P onto VP.

**Step 2 – Projection onto HP (Top View)**
Drop a vertical projector from P to HP. The point of contact is the **Top View (T)**. Since P is **'a'** units above HP, the projector has length **'a'**.

$$ \text{Position of T} = \text{Foot of perpendicular from P to HP} $$

In the 2D drawing on paper, this Top View is drawn **at a distance 'd' below the XY line** (because P is 'd' in front of VP, and in First Angle projection, the TV is below XY).

**Step 3 – Projection onto VP (Front View)**
Drop a horizontal projector from P to VP. The point of contact is the **Front View (F)**. Since P is **'d'** units in front of VP, the projector has length **'d'**.

$$ \text{Position of F} = \text{Foot of perpendicular from P to VP} $$

In the 2D drawing, the Front View is drawn **at a distance 'a' above the XY line**.

**Step 4 – Linking the views**
A vertical line is drawn connecting the FV (F) and TV (T). This is the **Projector Line** and it must always be **perpendicular to the XY line**.

**Step 5 – Final First Angle Projection of Point P in Quadrant I**

```
       F
       |  a (above XY)
       |
   ____|____  XY
       |
       |  d (below XY)
       T
```

### 3.1.2 Analytical Matrix for All Four Quadrants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Quadrant Decision Matrix**
> Use this matrix to solve **any** KTU 2024 question on projection of points. The sign convention uses:
> - **+a** = Above HP, **–a** = Below HP
> - **+d** = In front of VP, **–d** = Behind VP

**Step-by-step methodology (proven KTU answer pattern):**

**Step A:** Identify the quadrant from the given distances.
If both a and d are positive → **Quadrant I**.
If a is negative and d is positive → **Quadrant II**.
If both a and d are negative → **Quadrant III**.
If a is positive and d is negative → **Quadrant IV**.

**Step B:** Determine Front View (FV) position.
- FV is always at a distance **'a'** from the XY line.
- The sign of 'a' decides whether FV is above (+) or below (–) the XY line.

$$ \text{FV}_{\text{position}} = \begin{cases} \text{Above XY} & \text{if } a > 0 \\ \text{Below XY} & \text{if } a < 0 \end{cases} $$

**Step C:** Determine Top View (TV) position.
- TV is always at a distance **'d'** from the XY line.
- The sign of 'd' decides whether TV is above or below XY.

$$ \text{TV}_{\text{position}} = \begin{cases} \text{Above XY} & \text{if } d < 0 \\ \text{Below XY} & \text{if } d > 0 \end{cases} $$

**Step D:** Draw the projector line perpendicular to XY, connecting FV and TV.

### 3.1.3 Worked Example: Point P at (a = 30 mm, d = 25 mm) in Quadrant I

**Given:**
Point P is 30 mm above HP and 25 mm in front of VP. Draw its projection in First Angle.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 – Identify the quadrant:**
a = +30 mm (above HP), d = +25 mm (in front of VP) → **Quadrant I**.

**Step 2 – Draw the XY reference line.**
Draw a horizontal line across the centre of the drawing area. Label it XY.

**Step 3 – Mark the Front View (F).**
Since FV is at a distance 'a' above XY:
- Measure 30 mm above XY.
- Mark a point and label it **F** (or a', a").
- This represents the **Front View** of point P.

**Step 4 – Mark the Top View (T).**
Since TV is at a distance 'd' below XY:
- Measure 25 mm below XY, directly below F.
- Mark a point and label it **T** (or a, a').
- This represents the **Top View** of point P.

**Step 5 – Verify with projector.**
The line connecting F and T must be vertical and perpendicular to XY.

```
            a'(FV)
              |
              | 30 mm
              |
   ___________|__________  XY
              |
              | 25 mm
              |
              a (TV)
```

**Step 6 – State the procedure for the KTU answer sheet:**

> For Quadrant I, the Front View is positioned **above** the XY reference line at a distance equal to the height above HP. The Top View is positioned **below** the XY line at a distance equal to the depth in front of VP. The two views are connected by a **vertical projector**.

### 3.1.4 Mathematical Derivation: Distance from HP and VP in 2D Drawing

Let the point P be at true 3D coordinates (x, y, z) where:
- x = distance from PP
- y = distance from VP
- z = distance from HP

**Transformation to 2D Drawing Coordinates:**

For the **Front View (FV)**, we project P onto VP. The result is the point (x, y, 0). When drawn in 2D:

$$ \text{FV}_{2D} = (x, y) \text{ in the VP plane} $$

The 2D distance of FV from XY (which represents HP-VP intersection):

$$ \text{Distance of FV from XY} = z = a $$

For the **Top View (TV)**, we project P onto HP. The result is the point (x, 0, z). When drawn in 2D:

$$ \text{TV}_{2D} = (x, z) \text{ in the HP plane} $$

The 2D distance of TV from XY:

$$ \text{Distance of TV from XY} = y = d $$

**Consolidated Result:**

$$ \boxed{\begin{aligned} \text{FV} & : \text{Distance from XY} = a \text{ (perpendicular distance of P from HP)} \\ \text{TV} & : \text{Distance from XY} = d \text{ (perpendicular distance of P from VP)} \end{aligned}} $$

### 3.1.5 Proof: First Angle vs Third Angle Projection

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Question Pattern:**
> *"Differentiate between First Angle and Third Angle Projection. State the BIS convention."*

**Derivation of First Angle Logic:**

In First Angle Projection:
- The object is placed in the **first quadrant** (between observer and the plane of projection).
- The plane of projection is placed **behind or below the object**.
- The views are projected *away* from the observer onto the *far* plane.

**Mathematical Statement:**

For an object at (x, y, z) in Quadrant I (x > 0, y > 0, z > 0):
- Front View: Projected on VP behind the object → appears **above** XY in 2D.
- Top View: Projected on HP below the object → appears **below** XY in 2D.
- Left Side View (LSV): Projected on the right profile plane → appears on the **right** side.

In **Third Angle Projection** (used in USA):
- The plane of projection is placed **between observer and the object**.
- The Top View appears **above** the XY line.
- The Front View appears **below** the XY line.

**BIS Recommendation (SP 46:2003 §3.4):**
> *"For all engineering drawings in India, **First Angle Projection** as per IS 15021 (1989) shall be used."*

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    stepB --> stepC{a greater than 0?}
    stepC -->|Yes| stepD{d greater than 0?}

    stepH --> stepJ
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK[Mark FV above or below XY at distance a]
    stepK --> stepL[Mark TV above or below XY at distance d]
    stepL --> stepM[Connect FV and TV with vertical projector]
    stepM --> stepN[Add BIS Standard Title Block and First Angle Symbol]
    stepN --> stepO([End: Complete Projection Drawing])
```

graph TD

flowchart LR

    objA --> obs1[Observer Position]
    obs1 --> pap1[Plane of Projection Behind Object]
    pap1 --> fap[First Angle Projection - India BIS Standard]
    objA --> obs2[Observer Position]
    obs2 --> pap2[Plane of Projection Between Observer and Object]
    pap2 --> tap[Third Angle Projection - USA Standard]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define the BIS code SP 46:2003 and list any four standard sheet sizes specified in it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) Code of Practice SP 46:2003** is the standard governing all general engineering drawings in India. It defines conventions for sheet sizes, line types, scales, lettering, dimensioning, and projection methods.

Four standard sheet sizes as per BIS:

| Designation | Dimensions (mm × mm) |
|---|---|
| **A0** | 841 × 1189 |
| **A1** | 594 × 841 |
| **A2** | 420 × 594 |
| **A3** | 297 × 420 |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between First Angle and Third Angle Projection. Which one is recommended by BIS?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | First Angle Projection | Third Angle Projection |
|---|---|---|
| **Object Position** | Between observer and plane of projection | Between plane of projection and observer |
| **Top View Location** | Below XY line | Above XY line |
| **Front View Location** | Above XY line | Below XY line |
| **Left Side View** | Placed on the right side | Placed on the left side |
| **Standard Used In** | India, Europe (BIS, ISO) | USA, Canada |
| **Symbol** | Truncated cone viewed from side | Truncated cone viewed from end |

BIS (SP 46:2003) recommends **First Angle Projection** for all engineering drawings in India.
