## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – With Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice – Module 1 (Total 14 Marks)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Projection of Points in Multiple Quadrants**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Pattern]**

**(a)** A point **P** is 30 mm above HP and 25 mm in front of VP. Another point **Q** is 20 mm below HP and 30 mm in front of VP. Draw the projection of the line PQ joining the two points. Mention the true length and true inclinations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A point **R** is 40 mm above HP and 35 mm behind VP. Another point **S** is 25 mm below HP and 20 mm behind VP. Draw the projections of line RS. Find the apparent length, top view length, and front view length. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Projection of points P and Q in Quadrants I and II:**

**Step 1 – Identify Quadrants:**
- P: a = +30 mm (above HP), d = +25 mm (in front of VP) → **Quadrant I**
- Q: a = –20 mm (below HP), d = +30 mm (in front of VP) → **Quadrant II**

**Step 2 – Draw XY line.**
Draw a horizontal line in the centre of the drawing area; label as XY.

**Step 3 – Plot FV and TV of P (Quadrant I):**
- P-FV is 30 mm **above** XY → mark as **p'**.
- P-TV is 25 mm **below** XY, directly below p' → mark as **p**.

**Step 4 – Plot FV and TV of Q (Quadrant II):**
- Q-FV is 20 mm **below** XY → mark as **q'**.
- Q-TV is 30 mm **below** XY, directly below q' → mark as **q**.

**Step 5 – Join the points:**
- In Front View, join **p' and q'** with a straight line.
- In Top View, join **p and q** with a straight line.

**Step 6 – Mark the projected length and angles:**
- The line p'q' (FV length) = **apparent length of FV** = $\sqrt{(\Delta d)^2 + (\Delta a_FV)^2}$
- The line pq (TV length) = **apparent length of TV** = $\sqrt{(\Delta a)^2 + (\Delta d)^2}$

**Final sketch description:**

```
              p' (FV of P) 30 mm above XY
                \
                 \  (Line PQ in FV)
                  \
   ________________\____________ XY
                  /\
                 /  \
                /    \  (Line PQ in TV)
               /      \
              q'------p  (q' is 20 mm below, p is 25 mm below)
              20 mm    25 mm
```

**(b) Projection of points R and S in Quadrants IV and III:**

**Step 1 – Identify Quadrants:**
- R: a = +40 mm (above HP), d = –35 mm (behind VP) → **Quadrant IV**
- S: a = –25 mm (below HP), d = –20 mm (behind VP) → **Quadrant III**

**Step 2 – Plot FV and TV of R (Quadrant IV):**
- R-FV is 40 mm **above** XY → mark as **r'**.
- R-TV is 35 mm **above** XY (since d is negative) → mark as **r** above XY.

**Step 3 – Plot FV and TV of S (Quadrant III):**
- S-FV is 25 mm **below** XY → mark as **s'**.
- S-TV is 20 mm **above** XY → mark as **s** above XY.

**Step 4 – Join the points:**
- In Front View, join **r' and s'**.
- In Top View, join **r and s**.

**Step 5 – Determine projected lengths:**

Apparent length in Front View:

$$ L_{FV} = \sqrt{(d_1 - d_2)^2 + (a_1 - a_2)^2} $$

$$ L_{FV} = \sqrt{(35-20)^2 + (40-(-25))^2} $$

$$ L_{FV} = \sqrt{(15)^2 + (65)^2} = \sqrt{225 + 4225} = \sqrt{4450} $$

$$ L_{FV} \approx 66.7 \text{ mm} $$

Apparent length in Top View:

$$ L_{TV} = \sqrt{(a_1 - a_2)^2 + (d_1 - d_2)^2} $$

$$ L_{TV} = \sqrt{(40+25)^2 + (35-20)^2} = \sqrt{(65)^2 + (15)^2} = \sqrt{4450} \approx 66.7 \text{ mm} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Sign Error:** Many students mark the TV of a Quadrant IV point **below** XY. The correct position is **above** XY because the point is *behind* VP (d < 0).
> 2. **Reversal of FV and TV Positions:** Students often swap the FV and TV. Remember: FV distance from XY = a (HP distance); TV distance from XY = d (VP distance).
> 3. **Missing First Angle Symbol:** The **truncated cone symbol for First Angle Projection** must be drawn in the title block; failure results in **deduction of 1 mark**.
> 4. **Incomplete dimensioning:** All distances 'a' and 'd' must be shown with dimension lines on the projection.
> 5. **Incorrect projector lines:** The projector connecting FV and TV must be **continuous and vertical** (perpendicular to XY), not dashed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): BIS Conventions and Quadrant Application**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **BIS conventions** for the following with neat sketches: **(i)** First Angle Projection symbol, **(ii)** Centre line convention, **(iii)** Hidden line convention, **(iv)** Cutting plane line convention. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A point **A** is 35 mm above HP and 20 mm in front of VP. Another point **B** is 45 mm above HP and 50 mm behind VP. A third point **C** is in the HP such that its top view is 40 mm to the right of the top view of A. Draw the projections of the three points and join them in all views. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) BIS Conventions for Engineering Lines:**

**Step 1 – First Angle Projection Symbol (SP 46 §3.4):**
The First Angle Projection symbol is a **truncated cone (frustum) drawn in 3D** with the smaller end on the right side. The frustum is placed inside a **square frame** divided into four quadrants by the projection plane lines. The frustum is visible from the larger (left) end.

```
    ___________
   |     |     |
   |     |     |
   |  __ |     |
   | /  \|     |
   | \  /|     |
   |  \/ |     |
   |_____|_____|
```

**Step 2 – Centre Line (IS 696):**
A **chain line** consisting of alternating long dashes and short dots, drawn **thin** (Type 05.1). It represents the axis of symmetry or centre of circular features.
Convention: long dash = **20 mm** typical, dot = **1 mm**, gap = **3 mm**.

$$ \text{Centre Line Pattern:}\ \underline{\quad\quad\quad}\cdot\underline{\quad\quad\quad}\cdot\underline{\quad\quad\quad} $$

**Step 3 – Hidden Line Convention:**
A **dashed thin line** (Type 02.1) with even dashes of equal length (approx. **4 mm** dashes, **1 mm** gaps). It represents edges that are **not visible** from the current viewing direction.

$$ \text{Hidden Line Pattern:}\ \text{- - - - - - - - - -} $$

**Step 4 – Cutting Plane Line:**
A **thick chain line** (Type 05.2) showing long dashes of **20 mm** with thick dots and **3 mm** gaps. The cutting plane line is **thicker** than the centre line and is identified at its ends by capital letters and arrows indicating the viewing direction.

$$ \text{Cutting Plane Pattern:}\ \overline{\quad\quad\quad}\boldsymbol{\bullet}\overline{\quad\quad\quad}\boldsymbol{\bullet} $$

**(b) Projection of Three Points A, B, C:**

**Step 1 – Identify Quadrants:**
- A: a = +35 mm, d = +20 mm → **Quadrant I**
- B: a = +45 mm, d = –50 mm → **Quadrant IV**
- C: a = 0 (in HP), d is given by 40 mm to the right of A-TV → **C is in HP**

**Step 2 – Draw XY line and projectors:**

| Point | FV Position | TV Position |
|---|---|---|
| A (Q-I) | 35 mm above XY | 20 mm below XY |
| B (Q-IV) | 45 mm above XY | 50 mm above XY (since d is negative) |
| C (in HP) | On the XY line | 40 mm to the right of A-TV, on XY |

**Step 3 – Mark the points and label:**
- A-FV → a' (35 mm above XY)
- A-TV → a (20 mm below XY, on left side of drawing)
- B-FV → b' (45 mm above XY, on right side)
- B-TV → b (50 mm above XY)
- C-FV → c' (on XY line)
- C-TV → c (on XY line, 40 mm to the right of a)

**Step 4 – Join the points:**
- In FV: Join a' → c' → b' (a continuous line through a', c', b' since c' is between them or referenced).
- In TV: Join a → c → b.

**Step 5 – Verify and add title block:**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Part B Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing 'right' and 'left':** In First Angle projection, the **right side of an object appears on the right in TV and left in FV**. Students frequently reverse this orientation.
> 2. **Not labelling each view:** Every point must be labelled: **a, a'** (TV and FV of A); **b, b'** (TV and FV of B). The single prime (') denotes the Front View, and the unprimed letter denotes the Top View.
> 3. **Forgetting the cutting plane and centre line distinction:** Both look similar but have different lineweights. The centre line is **thin**; the cutting plane line is **thick** with arrows.
> 4. **Missing the First Angle Symbol:** Always include it in the title block.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **BIS SP 46:2003** is the authoritative code for engineering drawing in India.
- Standard sheet sizes follow ISO A-series: **A0, A1, A2, A3, A4, A5**.
- **First Angle Projection** is the BIS-mandated method. Symbol: truncated cone in a frame.
- BIS line types: **Continuous Thick** (visible edges), **Dashed Thin** (hidden edges), **Chain Thin** (centre lines), **Thick Chain** (cutting planes).
- All lettering must be **uppercase, vertical or 75° inclined**, minimum **2.5 mm** height.
- Two principal planes: **HP (Horizontal Plane)** and **VP (Vertical Plane)** intersect along the **XY line**.
- The point divides 3D space into **4 quadrants** based on its position relative to HP and VP.
- **FV distance from XY = distance of point from HP = a**.
- **TV distance from XY = distance of point from VP = d**.
- In **First Angle Projection**: Quadrant I → FV above, TV below; Quadrant III → both views reversed.
- The **projector line** connecting FV and TV is always **vertical and perpendicular to XY**.
- Sign convention: **+a** = above HP, **–a** = below HP, **+d** = in front of VP, **–d** = behind VP.
- Always include the **First Angle Projection symbol**, **title block**, and **dimension lines** for 'a' and 'd' in your KTU answer.
- Apparent length of a line in any view ≠ True length. True length is found only when the line is parallel to the projection plane.
- For any point in **HP**, its **Front View lies on the XY line** (a = 0).
- For any point in **VP**, its **Top View lies on the XY line** (d = 0).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:**
> Before finalising your answer, verify the **quadrant assignment** by checking both the FV and TV positions of every point. A common reason for losing **3 to 5 marks** in a 14-mark question is a single sign error that cascades through the entire solution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Projection of Straight Lines Inclined to One Plane and Inclined to Both Planes. Traces of a Line. Inclination of Lines with Reference Planes. True Length and True Inclinations of Line Inclined to Both the Reference Planes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Projection of Straight Lines Inclined to One Plane and Inclined to Both Planes. Traces of a Line. Inclination of Lines with Reference Planes. True Length and True Inclinations of Line Inclined to Both the Reference Planes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📐 MODULE 1 — PROJECTION OF STRAIGHT LINES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Projection of a Straight Line:** A straight line in three-dimensional space is represented on a **two-dimensional drawing sheet** by projecting it onto two mutually perpendicular reference planes — the **Horizontal Plane (HP)** and the **Vertical Plane (VP)** — using perpendicular projectors (lines of sight). The figure obtained on HP is the **Top View (TV) / Plan**, and the figure on VP is the **Front View (FV) / Elevation**. The line of intersection of HP and VP is the **Reference Line (XY)**.

When a line is **inclined to one plane** but **parallel to the other**, the projection on the parallel plane shows the **True Length (TL)** and the **True Inclination** with the inclined plane. When a line is **inclined to both HP and VP**, the true length and true inclinations must be determined analytically — they are *not* directly visible in the standard orthographic projections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Standard):**
> - **θ (theta)** = True inclination of the line with **HP**
> - **φ (phi)** = True inclination of the line with **VP**
> - **α (alpha)** = Apparent angle seen in the Front View (apparent φ)
> - **β (beta)** = Apparent angle seen in the Top View (apparent θ)
> - **TL** = True Length of the line
> - **HT** = Horizontal Trace — point where the line meets HP
> - **VT** = Vertical Trace — point where the line meets VP

---

### 🌍 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **wooden stick (pencil)** in your hand:

1. **Stick lying flat on a glass table (HP) but tilted away from you (VP)** — This is a line *inclined to VP only*. You can see the stick's true length from the top (looking down at the table), but from the front, you see a foreshortened (shortened) version. This is the case of a line **inclined to one plane**.

2. **Stick held in mid-air, tilted in BOTH directions** — One end closer to the floor, the other end pointing slightly to the left. Now the stick is inclined to *both* HP and VP. Neither the top view nor the front view shows the *true* length. You have to mathematically "rotate" the stick virtually in your mind to find its true length. This is the case of a line **inclined to both planes**.

3. **Stick piercing the table and the wall** — The point where the stick touches the floor (HP) is its **Horizontal Trace (HT)**. The point where the stick touches the wall (VP) is its **Vertical Trace (VT)**. These traces are *hidden* in normal views and must be constructed.

> [!TIP]
> 💡 **Student Tip:** The single most important insight is that the **true length is the hypotenuse** of a right triangle whose other two sides are the *plan length* (Top View length) and the *difference in heights* of the two endpoints. Memorize this — it is the foundation of every KTU problem on this topic.

---

### 📏 Standard Reference Setup (Every KTU Problem Begins Here)

| Reference Element | Symbol | Standard Notation in Problem |
|---|---|---|
| Horizontal Plane | **HP** | Drawn in **Top View** (looking down ↓) |
| Vertical Plane | **VP** | Drawn in **Front View** (looking from front →) |
| Reference Line | **XY** | Intersection of HP and VP |
| Top View | **TV** | Projection on HP — drawn **below XY** |
| Front View | **FV** | Projection on VP — drawn **above XY** |
| Distance above HP | **+y** | Heights of endpoints above XY (in FV) |
| Distance in front of VP | **+x** | Distances from XY (in TV) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used throughout KTU 2024:** End `a` is nearer to the observer in both views; end `b` is farther. In TV, `a' b'` are the top view points. In FV, `a'' b''` are the front view points. The distance `a'b' = a''b''` when the line is parallel to one of the planes.

---

### 🔁 Two Fundamental Cases in KTU Module 1

**Case 1 — Line inclined to ONE plane (and parallel to the other):**
- Inclined to HP only, parallel to VP → Top View shows **True Length** and **True Angle θ**.
- Inclined to VP only, parallel to HP → Front View shows **True Length** and **True Angle φ**.
- Construction: Tilt the elevation in the *parallel* view, then project perpendiculars to the other view.

**Case 2 — Line inclined to BOTH planes (HP and VP):**
- Neither view shows true length.
- Two standard construction methods: **Rotation Method (Rotating Line Method)** and **Trapezoidal Method (Rotating Plane Method)**.
- True length and true inclinations must be **constructed geometrically** using the locus of endpoints.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Geometry — How the Views are Related

When a line `AB` is projected onto HP and VP, the following geometric relationships **always hold** (these are board-exam fundamentals):

- The **end projectors** (a'a'' and b'b'') are **perpendicular to XY** and remain so in *all* cases.
- The Top View `a'b'` and the Front View `a''b''` are **linked by the projectors**.
- The **heights above HP** (y-coordinates) of the endpoints are read from the **Front View** (distances above XY).
- The **distances from VP** (x-coordinates) of the endpoints are read from the **Top View** (distances below XY).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the True Length is hidden when inclined to both planes:** Because in the Front View, the length is shortened by a factor of `cos θ` (due to inclination with HP), and the Top View is shortened by a factor of `cos φ` (due to inclination with VP). The actual length lies "in between" these two shortened projections and is recoverable only by **geometric construction**.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Relationships — The Foundation of Every KTU Problem

Let a line `AB` have:
- **a** at coordinates (x₁, y₁) — distance x₁ from VP, height y₁ above HP
- **b** at coordinates (x₂, y₂) — distance x₂ from VP, height y₂ above HP

Then the three fundamental distances are:

| Distance | Symbol | How Measured | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plan Length** | PL | Length of Top View `a'b'` | Projection of line on HP |
| **Elevation Length** | EL | Length of Front View `a''b''` | Projection of line on VP |
| **True Length** | TL | Actual 3D length of line AB | Actual length of the straight line |

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Memorize This!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following relations are the **single most tested set of equations** in KTU Module 1. Every Part B (14-mark) question on straight lines requires at least 2–3 of these.

### Set A — From Plan View and Heights (used to find TL and θ)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{True Length (TL)} &= \sqrt{(\text{Plan Length})^{2} + (\text{Difference in heights})^{2}} \\
\text{TL} &= \sqrt{(a'b')^{2} + (y_{2} - y_{1})^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\theta &= \frac{\text{Difference in heights}}{\text{True Length}} = \frac{\vert y_{2} - y_{1} \vert}{\text{TL}} \\
\cos\theta &= \frac{\text{Plan Length}}{\text{True Length}} = \frac{a'b'}{\text{TL}} \\
\tan\theta &= \frac{\text{Difference in heights}}{\text{Plan Length}} = \frac{\vert y_{2} - y_{1} \vert}{a'b'}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Set B — From Elevation View and Distances (used to find TL and φ)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{True Length (TL)} &= \sqrt{(\text{Elevation Length})^{2} + (\text{Difference in distances from VP})^{2}} \\
\text{TL} &= \sqrt{(a''b'')^{2} + (x_{2} - x_{1})^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\phi &= \frac{\text{Difference in distances from VP}}{\text{True Length}} = \frac{\vert x_{2} - x_{1} \vert}{\text{TL}} \\
\cos\phi &= \frac{\text{Elevation Length}}{\text{True Length}} = \frac{a''b''}{\text{TL}} \\
\tan\phi &= \frac{\text{Difference in distances from VP}}{\text{Elevation Length}} = \frac{\vert x_{2} - x_{1} \vert}{a''b''}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Set C — Direct Relations Between the Two Views (used for verification)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TL} &= \frac{\text{Plan Length}}{\cos\theta} = \frac{\text{Elevation Length}}{\cos\phi} \\
\text{Elevation Length} &= \text{Plan Length} \times \frac{\cos\phi}{\cos\theta} \\
\text{Plan Length} &= \text{Elevation Length} \times \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\phi}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Set D — Traces of a Line (KTU Favourite — Frequently Asked)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance of HT from } a' \text{ in TV} &= \text{Difference in distances of ends from VP} = \vert x_{2} - x_{1} \vert \\
\text{Distance of VT from } a'' \text{ in FV} &= \text{Difference in heights of ends} = \vert y_{2} - y_{1} \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Locus Concept:** The end `b'` (Top View) moves on a horizontal line parallel to XY when the line is rotated to find true length. The end `b''` (Front View) moves on a vertical line parallel to the projector when the line is rotated to find true length. These are the **two loci** that govern every construction problem.

---

## 2.4 The Trapezoidal Method (Rotating Plane Method) — Step Logic

The **Trapezoidal Method** is the most commonly tested construction technique in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam) for lines inclined to both planes. The logic proceeds in this strict order:

1. **Draw** the initial Top View `a'b'` and Front View `a''b''` from the given coordinates of A and B.
2. **Top View** has length = `a'b'`. Front View has length = `a''b''`.
3. **To find True Length:** From a'' in FV, draw a **horizontal line** (locus of b''). From a' in TV, draw a line inclined at the **elevation angle** (apparent φ) such that the line, when projected, gives the proper length relationship.
4. **Construct the trapezoid** with the two apparent lengths as parallel sides.
5. **Diagonal of the trapezoid = True Length.**
6. **True inclination θ** is the angle the true length makes with the plan length.
7. **True inclination φ** is the angle the true length makes with the elevation length.

> [!TIP]
> 💡 **Why is it called a trapezoid?** When you arrange the Plan Length and Elevation Length as two parallel lines (both horizontal) separated by the *difference in distances from VP* (or *difference in heights*), the resulting four-sided figure is a trapezium/trapezoid. The **diagonal** of this trapezoid is the True Length.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of These Projections

| Engineering Field | Why Projection of Lines Matters |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Layout of sloped roofs, ramps, retaining walls, drainage pipes, and cable-stayed bridges requires true inclinations of structural members. |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Design of inclined pipes, machine tool slideways, IC engine valve push rods, conveyor systems, and crankshafts. |
| **Aerospace Engineering** | Aircraft wing bracing members, fuselage stringers, and landing-gear struts are inclined to multiple planes. |
| **Architecture** | Staircase handrails, inclined glazing panels, sloped ceilings, and roof trusses must be drawn in true length for fabrication. |
| **Surveying** | The horizontal distance and vertical rise of a line on hilly terrain are precisely the Plan Length and Difference in Heights — the foundation of contour mapping. |

> [!NOTE]
> The mathematical relationship `True Length² = Plan Length² + Difference in Heights²` is essentially the **3D distance formula** in disguise, and is identical to the **Pythagorean theorem** in 3D space.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — The Three Core Identities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every step below is **algebraically complete** with no shortcuts. These derivations are the reason KTU 2024 questions test the same five identities again and again — once you understand *why* they are true, you can re-derive them in the exam even if you forget the form.

---

### 🔷 Derivation 1 — True Length from Plan Length and Difference in Heights

**Geometric Setup:** Consider a line `AB` inclined at angle `θ` to the Horizontal Plane. Let us take a vertical plane containing the line `AB`. In this plane, drop perpendiculars from both ends to the Horizontal Plane. Let the perpendiculars from A and B meet HP at points A₁ and B₁ respectively. Then `A₁B₁` is the Plan Length, and the line `AB` is the True Length.

The right triangle `AA₁B` has:
- **Base** `A₁B₁` = Plan Length of the line
- **Vertical side** `BB₁` = Difference in heights of ends above HP
- **Hypotenuse** `AB` = True Length of the line
- **Angle at B₁** = θ (the true inclination with HP)

**Applying the Pythagorean Theorem to right triangle ABB₁:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(AB)^{2} &= (A_{1}B_{1})^{2} + (BB_{1})^{2} \\
(\text{True Length})^{2} &= (\text{Plan Length})^{2} + (\text{Difference in heights})^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the square root of both sides:

$$
\text{True Length (TL)} = \sqrt{(\text{Plan Length})^{2} + (\text{Difference in heights})^{2}}
$$

**Trigonometric extraction from the same right triangle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\theta &= \frac{\text{Opposite side}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{\text{Difference in heights}}{\text{TL}} \\
\cos\theta &= \frac{\text{Adjacent side}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{\text{Plan Length}}{\text{TL}} \\
\tan\theta &= \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{\text{Difference in heights}}{\text{Plan Length}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The algebraic chain `sin²θ + cos²θ = 1` must hold for these. Verify: $\left(\frac{\Delta h}{\text{TL}}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\text{PL}}{\text{TL}}\right)^{2} = \frac{(\Delta h)^{2} + \text{PL}^{2}}{\text{TL}^{2}} = \frac{\text{TL}^{2}}{\text{TL}^{2}} = 1$ ✓

---

### 🔷 Derivation 2 — True Length from Elevation Length and Difference in Distances from VP

**Geometric Setup:** Now consider a line `AB` inclined at angle `φ` to the Vertical Plane. Take a horizontal plane containing the line. Project A and B onto the Vertical Plane. Let these projections be A' and B'. Then `A'B'` is the Elevation Length, and the perpendicular distance from A to the line `A'B'` (measured along a line parallel to HP) equals the **Difference in Distances of ends from VP**.

The right triangle so formed has:
- **Base** `A'B'` = Elevation Length of the line
- **Perpendicular side** = Difference in distances of ends from VP
- **Hypotenuse** `AB` = True Length of the line
- **Angle at A'` = φ (the true inclination with VP)

**Applying the Pythagorean Theorem:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(AB)^{2} &= (A'B')^{2} + (\text{Difference in distances from VP})^{2} \\
(\text{True Length})^{2} &= (\text{Elevation Length})^{2} + (\text{Difference in distances from VP})^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the square root:

$$
\text{True Length (TL)} = \sqrt{(\text{Elevation Length})^{2} + (\text{Difference in distances from VP})^{2}}
$$

**Trigonometric extraction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\phi &= \frac{\text{Difference in distances from VP}}{\text{TL}} \\
\cos\phi &= \frac{\text{Elevation Length}}{\text{TL}} \\
\tan\phi &= \frac{\text{Difference in distances from VP}}{\text{Elevation Length}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 🔷 Derivation 3 — Equivalence of the Two True Lengths

The same line must give the **same TL** whether computed from Set A or Set B. Equating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{\text{PL}^{2} + (\Delta y)^{2}} &= \sqrt{\text{EL}^{2} + (\Delta x)^{2}} \\
\text{PL}^{2} + (\Delta y)^{2} &= \text{EL}^{2} + (\Delta x)^{2} \\
\text{EL}^{2} &= \text{PL}^{2} + (\Delta y)^{2} - (\Delta x)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting `PL = \text{TL} \cos\theta` and `\Delta y = \text{TL} \sin\theta` into the right side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EL}^{2} &= \text{TL}^{2}\cos^{2}\theta + \text{TL}^{2}\sin^{2}\theta - (\Delta x)^{2} \\
\text{EL}^{2} &= \text{TL}^{2}(\cos^{2}\theta + \sin^{2}\theta) - (\Delta x)^{2} \\
\text{EL}^{2} &= \text{TL}^{2} - (\Delta x)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

But from Set B, `\text{TL}^{2} = \text{EL}^{2} + (\Delta x)^{2}`, which gives `\text{TL}^{2} - (\Delta x)^{2} = \text{EL}^{2}`. The identity is **self-consistent** ✓.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight (KTU):** The result `\text{TL}^{2} = \text{PL}^{2} + \text{EL}^{2} - (\Delta x)^{2} - (\Delta y)^{2} + (\Delta x)^{2} + (\Delta y)^{2}` simplifies trivially because the coordinate origin is arbitrary. The KTU trick is to **always compute TL twice** — once from PL and once from EL — and verify they are equal. This serves as a **self-check** that you have extracted the elevations and distances correctly from the given problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Trace Distances

### 🔷 Horizontal Trace (HT) — Where the Line Meets HP

The Horizontal Trace is the point `H` on the line where the height above HP is **zero**. The distance from `a'` to the Top View of `H` (call it `h'`) equals the **distance the line's end B is *behind* A in terms of distance from VP**, scaled by the trigonometric ratio of inclination.

**Geometric Proof (similar triangles):** Consider the elevation view. As we travel from A toward B along the line, the height changes from `y₁` to `y₂`. The trace H lies at height `y₁ = 0` (i.e., we travel a *negative* height `y₁` from A). By similar triangles in the trapezoidal construction:

$$
\frac{\text{Distance of HT from } a'}{\text{Plan Length}} = \frac{\text{Height of A above HP}}{\text{Difference in heights}}
$$

But for a point *on* the line where the height is `y₁` (so `H` is at height 0, meaning the height-change from A to H is `y₁` itself, with sign):

$$
\text{Distance of HT from } a' \text{ in TV} = \frac{y_{1}}{\tan\theta} = \frac{y_{1} \cdot \text{Plan Length}}{y_{2} - y_{1}}
$$

For the **simpler KTU case** where A lies *on* HP (i.e., `y₁ = 0`) and the line then rises to B at height `y₂`:

$$
\text{Distance of HT from } a' \text{ in TV} = 0
$$

And in this case HT coincides with `a'`. The more useful general formula is:

$$
\text{HT distance (from a') in TV} = \frac{x_{1}}{\tan\theta_{\text{in plan}}}
$$

where `\theta_{\text{in plan}}` is the apparent angle in the plan view (β) — this is the angle that `a'b'` makes with XY.

> [!TIP]
> 💡 **Board-Exam Shortcut:** When A is on HP (y₁ = 0), the HT coincides with `a'`, so the top view of the line is `a'(=HT) — b'`. The line from `a'` to `b'` extended does NOT give HT — HT is exactly at `a'`. This is the case most often given in KTU problems.

---

### 🔷 Vertical Trace (VT) — Where the Line Meets VP

By symmetric reasoning, the Vertical Trace is the point `V` where the distance from VP is **zero**. By identical similar-triangle reasoning:

$$
\text{Distance of VT from } a'' \text{ in FV} = \frac{y_{1}}{\tan\phi_{\text{in elevation}}}
$$

For the **standard KTU case** where A lies *on* VP (i.e., `x₁ = 0`):

$$
\text{Distance of VT from } a'' \text{ in FV} = 0
$$

i.e., the VT coincides with `a''`. The Front View becomes `a''(=VT) — b''`.

---

## 3.3 📊 Analytical Matrix — Quick Computation Reference

| Given Quantity | How to Find | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Plan Length (a'b') | Directly from TV | Measure in drawing or use coordinates |
| Elevation Length (a''b'') | Directly from FV | Measure in drawing or use coordinates |
| Difference in heights | From FV | $\vert y_{2} - y_{1} \vert$ |
| Difference in distances from VP | From TV | $\vert x_{2} - x_{1} \vert$ |
| **True Length (TL)** | From PL and heights | $\text{TL} = \sqrt{\text{PL}^{2} + (\Delta y)^{2}}$ |
| **True Length (TL)** | From EL and distances | $\text{TL} = \sqrt{\text{EL}^{2} + (\Delta x)^{2}}$ |
| **True inclination θ with HP** | From PL and heights | $\tan\theta = \Delta y / \text{PL}$ |
| **True inclination φ with VP** | From EL and distances | $\tan\phi = \Delta x / \text{EL}$ |
| **Apparent angle β (in TV)** | From elevations and PL | $\tan\beta = \Delta y / \text{PL}$ — same as θ! |
| **Apparent angle α (in FV)** | From distances and EL | $\tan\alpha = \Delta x / \text{EL}$ — same as φ! |
| **HT position in TV** | From elevations and apparent angle | $\text{HT}_x = y_{1} / \tan\beta$ |
| **VT position in FV** | From distances and apparent angle | $\text{VT}_y = x_{1} / \tan\alpha$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Surprise Fact (often asked as a trick question in KTU):** The apparent angle in the Top View (β) is **always equal to** the true inclination with HP (θ), and the apparent angle in the Front View (α) is **always equal to** the true inclination with VP (φ). This is because the angle the line makes with HP is *visible* in the top view as the angle the projected line makes with XY, and the apparent angle in the plan view is a faithful representation of the true inclination θ. The same holds for α and φ in the elevation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A line AB, 75 mm long, is inclined at 30° to HP and 45° to VP. End A is 20 mm above HP and 30 mm in front of VP. End B is in the first quadrant. Draw the projections and find the apparent angles.

**Solution Sketch (Step-by-Step):**

- Given: TL = 75 mm, θ = 30°, φ = 45°, A = (30, 20) mm.
- Plan Length: PL = TL × cos θ = 75 × cos 30° = 75 × 0.866 = **64.95 mm**.
- Elevation Length: EL = TL × cos φ = 75 × cos 45° = 75 × 0.707 = **53.03 mm**.
- Difference in heights (B above A): Δy = TL × sin θ = 75 × sin 30° = 75 × 0.5 = **37.5 mm**. So B is at height = 20 + 37.5 = **57.5 mm**.
- Difference in distances from VP (B from VP): Δx = TL × sin φ = 75 × sin 45° = 75 × 0.707 = **53.03 mm**. So B is at distance = 30 + 53.03 = **83.03 mm** in front of VP.

**Verification (TL must equal 75):**
$$
\text{TL} = \sqrt{\text{PL}^{2} + \Delta y^{2}} = \sqrt{64.95^{2} + 37.5^{2}} = \sqrt{4218.5 + 1406.25} = \sqrt{5624.75} \approx 75.00 \text{ mm} \checkmark
$$

$$
\text{TL} = \sqrt{\text{EL}^{2} + \Delta x^{2}} = \sqrt{53.03^{2} + 53.03^{2}} = \sqrt{2812.18 + 2812.18} = \sqrt{5624.36} \approx 75.00 \text{ mm} \checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> 💡 **The two-TL-check** is the single best safeguard against KTU valuation marks being lost on computation errors. Always verify in the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — The Complete Projection Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete decision flow** for solving any KTU Module 1 problem on projection of straight lines, from data interpretation to final answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([KTU Problem Statement Given]) --> nodeReadData[Read End A and End B coordinates and inclinations]
    nodeReadData --> nodeIdentify{Is line parallel to one of HP or VP?}
    nodeIdentify -- Yes, parallel to VP --> nodeCaseA[Case A: True length visible in Top View, angle = theta]
    nodeIdentify -- Yes, parallel to HP --> nodeCaseB[Case B: True length visible in Front View, angle = phi]
    nodeIdentify -- Inclined to BOTH planes --> nodeCaseC[Case C: True length hidden, use Trapezoidal Method]
    nodeCaseA --> nodeDrawA[Draw FV as inclined line at theta, then project TV as horizontal line of same length]
    nodeCaseB --> nodeDrawB[Draw TV as inclined line at phi, then project FV as horizontal line of same length]
    nodeCaseC --> nodeStep1[Step 1: Draw initial FV a double prime b double prime and TV a prime b prime from coordinates]
    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2[Step 2: Construct trapezoid with PL and EL as parallel horizontal sides]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3[Step 3: Diagonal of trapezoid equals True Length]
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4[Step 4: Angle of TL with PL gives true theta]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5[Step 5: Angle of TL with EL gives true phi]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6[Step 6: Find Horizontal Trace by extending line in TV to intersect XY]
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7[Step 7: Find Vertical Trace by extending line in FV to intersect XY]
    nodeStep7 --> nodeStep8[Step 8: Project traces back to other view using perpendiculars to XY]
    nodeDrawA --> nodeFinal[Mark all dimensions, label true inclinations, write answer in box]
    nodeDrawB --> nodeFinal
    nodeStep8 --> nodeFinal
    nodeFinal --> nodeDone([Solution Complete and Verified])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each box is a discrete construction step. In the KTU exam, you must *execute* each step with a **ruler, set-square, and protractor** on the drawing sheet. The "locus" concept (where a point moves during rotation) is embedded in Steps 2–5.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — The Two-View Coordinate System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node3D[3D Line AB in Space] --> nodeProjVP[Project perpendicular to VP]
    node3D --> nodeProjHP[Project perpendicular to HP]
    nodeProjVP --> nodeFV[Front View on VP: a double prime b double prime]
    nodeProjHP --> nodeTV[Top View on HP: a prime b prime]
    nodeFV --> nodeXYref[Vertical distance above XY = Heights above HP]
    nodeTV --> nodeXYref2[Vertical distance below XY = Distances from VP]
    nodeXYref --> nodeStore[Heights y1 and y2 stored in FV]
    nodeXYref2 --> nodeStore2[Distances x1 and x2 stored in TV]
    nodeStore --> nodeCompute[Compute TL, theta, phi using formulas]
    nodeStore2 --> nodeCompute
    nodeCompute --> nodeOutput[Output: TL, theta, phi, HT, VT]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Relationship Between Plan Length, Elevation Length, and True Length

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTL[True Length TL of line AB] --> nodeDecomp1[Component along HP = Plan Length = TL cos theta]
    nodeTL --> nodeDecomp2[Component along VP = Elevation Length = TL cos phi]
    nodeTL --> nodeDecomp3[Vertical component = TL sin theta = Difference in heights]
    nodeTL --> nodeDecomp4[Horizontal component perpendicular to VP = TL sin phi = Difference in distances from VP]
    nodeDecomp1 --> nodeRel1[Plan Length visible in Top View]
    nodeDecomp2 --> nodeRel2[Elevation Length visible in Front View]
    nodeDecomp3 --> nodeRel3[Heights read from Front View]
    nodeDecomp4 --> nodeRel4[Distances read from Top View]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Table — Which Method to Use

| Problem Type | Recommended Method | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Line inclined to one plane only, parallel to the other | Direct tilt + project method | ⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) |
| Line inclined to both planes, find TL and inclinations | Trapezoidal Method (Rotating Plane) | ⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) |
| Line inclined to both planes, find TL and inclinations (alt) | Rotation Method (Rotating Line) | ⭐⭐ (High) |
| Find traces of a line inclined to both planes | Locus extension + projection | ⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) |
| Given trace positions, find line | Reverse construction from HT/VT | ⭐⭐ (High) |
| Find apparent angles only | Direct measurement in views | ⭐ (Moderate) |

---

## 4.5 Construction Procedure — Rotating Line Method (RL Method) Summary

1. **Initial projection** of `a'b'` (TV) and `a''b''` (FV) from given coordinates.
2. **Rotate `a'b'` in TV** about `a'` until it is **parallel to XY**. The rotated position is `a' b'₁`.
3. **Project `b'₁` upward** to intersect the locus of `b''` (a horizontal line through `b''`). This gives the rotated FV `a'' b''₁`.
4. **`a''b''₁` is the True Length**, and the angle it makes with XY is the **true inclination φ with VP**.
5. **Rotate `a'b'` back to its original position.** Then rotate `a'b'` *further* about `a'` until it is **perpendicular to XY** (or until the locus of `b''` is intersected appropriately). The angle this rotated TV makes with XY is the **true inclination θ with HP**.
6. The length `a''b''₁` in step 4 gives the True Length; the angle traced in step 5 gives θ.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** Most solved textbook problems and KTU past papers use the **Trapezoidal Method** because it is faster and easier to draw on paper. The Rotating Line Method is conceptually cleaner and is preferred in **CAD-based** solutions (AutoCAD, SolidWorks).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question A1
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the terms (i) True Length, (ii) Apparent Angle, and (iii) Trace of a straight line as applied to engineering drawing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

- **(i) True Length (TL):** The actual length of the straight line in three-dimensional space, as opposed to its foreshortened length in any of the orthographic projections (Top View or Front View). When the line is inclined to both reference planes, the true length is not directly visible in either view and must be obtained by construction.
- **(ii) Apparent Angle:** The angle that a foreshortened projection of a line makes with the reference line XY. The apparent angle in the Front View is denoted **α** (alpha) and equals the true inclination with VP, **φ**. The apparent angle in the Top View is denoted **β** (beta) and equals the true inclination with HP, **θ**.
- **(iii) Trace of a Line:** The point where the straight line, when extended, intersects a reference plane. The **Horizontal Trace (HT)** is where the line meets the Horizontal Plane (HP); the **Vertical Trace (VT)** is where it meets the Vertical Plane (VP).

---

### ❓ Question A2
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**A line AB of length 80 mm is inclined at 40° to HP. Its end A is 15 mm above HP and 25 mm in front of VP. Draw the front view of the line when it is parallel to VP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- Given: TL = 80 mm, θ = 40°, A = (25, 15).
- Since the line is **parallel to VP** and inclined to HP, the **Front View shows the True Length** and the **True Inclination θ**.
- Plan Length: `PL = TL \cos\theta = 80 \times \cos 40° = 80 \times 0.766 = 61.28 \text{ mm}`.
- Difference in heights: `\Delta y = TL \sin\theta = 80 \times \sin 40° = 80 \times 0.643 = 51.42 \text{ mm}`.
- Height of B above HP: `y₂ = 15 + 51.42 = 66.42 \text{ mm}`.
- **Construction:** Mark `a''` 15 mm above XY, 25 mm to the left of the right margin (or as per convention). Through `a''`, draw a line at **40° to XY**, length **80 mm** (True Length). This is the Front View `a''b''`. The Top View is a horizontal line of length 80 mm below XY (since line is parallel to VP).
- **Answer:** FV is 80 mm long inclined at 40° to XY, with end A 15 mm above XY.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 📝 Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

A line PQ, **90 mm long**, is inclined at **35° to HP** and **50° to VP**. End P is **20 mm above HP** and **15 mm in front of VP**. End Q is in the **first quadrant** and is at a greater distance from both HP and VP than P.

**(a) Draw the Front View and Top View of the line. Find the apparent angles. (7 marks)**

**(b) Determine the True Length, True Inclinations, and the apparent angles using the Trapezoidal Method. Also locate the traces. (7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Mark XY line.** Draw the reference line horizontally, dividing the drawing area.

**Step 2: Fix end P position.**
- In FV: mark `p''` at 20 mm above XY.
- In TV: mark `p'` at 15 mm below XY.
- Project `p'` upward and `p''` downward so that `p'p''` is a single vertical line (the projector).

**Step 3: Compute Plan Length and Elevation Length.**
- Plan Length: `PL = TL \cos\theta = 90 \times \cos 35° = 90 \times 0.819 = 73.73 \text{ mm}`.
- Elevation Length: `EL = TL \cos\phi = 90 \times \cos 50° = 90 \times 0.643 = 57.86 \text{ mm}`.
- Difference in heights: `\Delta y = TL \sin\theta = 90 \times \sin 35° = 90 \times 0.574 = 51.62 \text{ mm}`.
- Difference in distances from VP: `\Delta x = TL \sin\phi = 90 \times \sin 50° = 90 \times 0.766 = 68.97 \text{ mm}`.

**Step 4: Compute Q coordinates.**
- `q''` is at height: `20 + 51.62 = 71.62 \text{ mm}` above XY.
- `q'` is at distance: `15 + 68.97 = 83.97 \text{ mm}` below XY.

**Step 5: Draw the views.**
- In TV: connect `p'` (at 15 mm below XY) to `q'` (at 83.97 mm below XY) such that horizontal distance between projectors = 73.73 mm. So `p'q'` = 73.73 mm.
- In FV: connect `p''` (at 20 mm above XY) to `q''` (at 71.62 mm above XY) such that horizontal distance between projectors = 57.86 mm. So `p''q''` = 57.86 mm.

**Step 6: Find apparent angles.**
- In TV: angle of `p'q'` with XY = `\tan^{-1}(51.62 / 73.73) = \tan^{-1}(0.700) = 35.0°`. This is the **apparent angle β** (and equals true θ, as expected).
- In FV: angle of `p''q''` with XY = `\tan^{-1}(68.97 / 57.86) = \tan^{-1}(1.192) = 50.0°`. This is the **apparent angle α** (and equals true φ, as expected).

**Answer for (a):** Plan Length = 73.73 mm; Elevation Length = 57.86 mm; Apparent angle β = 35°; Apparent angle α = 50°.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Construct the Trapezoid.**

Draw a horizontal baseline. On it, mark off:
- `AB = 73.73 \text{ mm}` (Plan Length)
- `CD = 57.86 \text{ mm}` (Elevation Length)
- The two segments are separated by the **difference in distances from VP** = 68.97 mm at one end and the **difference in heights** = 51.62 mm at the other end.

A cleaner approach: Mark `A` at origin. Mark `B` at 73.73 mm. From `A`, erect a perpendicular of 51.62 mm (the difference in heights). From `B`, draw a horizontal line. From `A`, draw a slanted line such that the perpendicular distance to the horizontal line equals the appropriate projection. The trapezoid has `AB` and `CD` as parallel sides, and the slanted side has length equal to the True Length.

**Step 2: Identify the True Length diagonal.**

The diagonal of the trapezoid connecting `A` to `C` (or `B` to `D`, depending on orientation) is the **True Length** = 90 mm. Verify by measurement: `TL = \sqrt{73.73^{2} + 51.62^{2}} = \sqrt{5436.0 + 2664.6} = \sqrt{8100.6} \approx 90.0 \text{ mm} \checkmark`.

**Step 3: Determine True Inclinations.**

- The angle that the True Length diagonal makes with the Plan Length is **θ = 35°**.
- The angle that the True Length diagonal makes with the Elevation Length is **φ = 50°**.

**Step 4: Locate the Horizontal Trace (HT).**

The HT is the point where the line PQ, when extended, meets the HP (where height = 0).
- In the FV, locate the point on `p''q''` (or its extension) that lies **on XY** (height = 0). Call this point `h''`.
- Project `h''` downward to the TV to get `h'`. The point `h'` is the **HT**.

Mathematically: `\text{Distance of HT from } p' \text{ in TV} = 15 \times (\text{PL} / \Delta y) \times \tan\theta` — but easier: extend `p''q''` in FV backward (since `p''` is at height 20 mm, and HT must be at height 0, the line must be extended *downward* from `p''` beyond it).

**Step 5: Locate the Vertical Trace (VT).**

The VT is the point where the line meets the VP (where distance from VP = 0).
- In the TV, locate the point on `p'q'` (or its extension) that lies **on XY** (distance from VP = 0). Call this point `v'`.
- Project `v'` upward to the FV to get `v''`. The point `v''` is the **VT**.

**Answer for (b):** TL = 90 mm; True θ = 35°; True φ = 50°; HT and VT located as constructed.

---

### 📝 Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

A line segment RS, **100 mm long**, has its end R **on HP** and **30 mm in front of VP**. The end S is **40 mm above HP** and the line is inclined at **25° to VP**.

**(a) Draw the projections of the line and find the apparent angles. (7 marks)**

**(b) Using the Rotation Method, determine the True Length and True Inclination of the line with HP. Also locate the traces. (7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Given:** TL = 100 mm, R on HP (y₁ = 0), R is 30 mm in front of VP (x₁ = 30), S is 40 mm above HP (y₂ = 40), φ = 25°.

**Step 1: Compute coordinates of S.**
- Elevation Length: `EL = TL \cos\phi = 100 \times \cos 25° = 100 \times 0.906 = 90.63 \text{ mm}`.
- Difference in distances from VP: `\Delta x = TL \sin\phi = 100 \times \sin 25° = 100 \times 0.423 = 42.26 \text{ mm}`.
- Distance of S from VP: `x₂ = 30 + 42.26 = 72.26 \text{ mm}`.
- Verification: `\Delta y = y₂ - y₁ = 40 - 0 = 40 \text{ mm}`.
- Plan Length: `PL = \sqrt{TL^{2} - \Delta y^{2}} = \sqrt{10000 - 1600} = \sqrt{8400} = 91.65 \text{ mm}`.
- True inclination with HP: `\theta = \sin^{-1}(\Delta y / TL) = \sin^{-1}(40 / 100) = \sin^{-1}(0.4) = 23.58°`.

**Step 2: Draw views.**
- In TV: mark `r'` at 30 mm below XY, `s'` at 72.26 mm below XY, separated horizontally by 91.65 mm.
- In FV: mark `r''` *on XY* (since R is on HP, y₁ = 0), `s''` at 40 mm above XY, separated horizontally by 90.63 mm.
- The projectors `r'r''` and `s's''` are vertical lines perpendicular to XY.

**Step 3: Apparent angles.**
- In TV: apparent angle β = `\tan^{-1}(40 / 91.65) = \tan^{-1}(0.4364) = 23.58°` (equals true θ).
- In FV: apparent angle α = `\tan^{-1}(42.26 / 90.63) = \tan^{-1}(0.4663) = 25.0°` (equals true φ).

**Answer for (a):** PL = 91.65 mm, EL = 90.63 mm, Apparent β = 23.58°, Apparent α = 25°.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Construct the FV (already drawn).** `r''` is on XY, `s''` is 40 mm above XY, `r''s'' = 90.63 mm` and inclined at 25° to XY.

**Step 2: Rotate TV to be parallel to XY.** In TV, rotate `r's'` about `r'` until it is parallel to XY. The new position `r's'₁` has the same length (91.65 mm).

**Step 3: Project `s'₁` upward to meet the horizontal locus through `s''`.** This gives `s''₁` in FV. The line `r''s''₁` is the **True Length** of the line.

- Length: `TL = 100 \text{ mm}`.
- Angle of `r''s''₁` with XY = **true inclination φ with VP = 25°** (by construction).

**Step 4: Find true θ.** Rotate the TV about `r'` back to its original position. The angle that the original `r's'` makes with XY in TV is **true θ = 23.58°** (since apparent β = true θ when the FV and TV are correctly drawn).

**Step 5: Locate the Horizontal Trace (HT).**
- Since R is on HP, **HT coincides with `r' = r''`** in the drawing.
- In TV, `r'` is the HT.

**Step 6: Locate the Vertical Trace (VT).**
- In TV, extend `r's'` backward (toward XY) until it meets XY. Call this point `v'`.
- Project `v'` upward to the FV. Call this `v''`. This is the **VT**.

**Mathematical verification:** `v'` is on XY, at distance 30 mm to the left of `r'`'s projector (i.e., at the same x-coordinate as `r'`'s projector line). The VT is the point where the line crosses the VP.

**Answer for (b):** TL = 100 mm, true θ = 23.58°, true φ = 25°, HT at `r'`, VT at `v'v''` as constructed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add or subtract coordinates correctly:** If end A is 20 mm *above* HP and end B is 10 mm *below* HP, the difference in heights is **30 mm**, not 10 mm. Always use `|y₂ − y₁|`, never just `y₂`.
>
> 2. **Confusing TL with PL or EL:** Students frequently report the Plan Length as the True Length. The True Length is **always the largest** of the three and equals `TL = PL / cos θ = EL / cos φ`.
>
> 3. **Mixing up true and apparent angles:** In the Top View, the angle of `a'b'` with XY is the **apparent angle β**, but it numerically equals the true θ. The same for α and φ. Do not label β and θ as different values in your answer script.
>
> 4. **Drawing the projector line in the wrong direction:** Projectors are *always* vertical (perpendicular to XY) and *always* connect the same physical point in TV to its corresponding point in FV. If `a'` is to the right of `b'`, then `a''` is to the right of `b''` in FV — the projectors never cross each other.
>
> 5. **Not extending the line to find traces:** Many students stop the line at `a'b'` and forget to extend it to meet XY. **Traces require extension of the line beyond the endpoints.** Use dashed lines for the extensions.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to write the unit (mm):** Every dimension must carry the unit. KTU examiners *will* deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.
>
> 7. **Drawing the FV above XY and TV below XY in reverse:** The standard convention in KTU is FV **above** XY, TV **below** XY. Reversing this loses presentation marks.
>
> 8. **Not showing construction lines:** In the Trapezoidal Method, the auxiliary construction lines (the trapezoid, the loci, the diagonal) **must be drawn as thin lines** and labeled. Skipping them costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ The two reference planes are **HP** (Horizontal Plane) and **VP** (Vertical Plane); they intersect along the **XY reference line**.
- ✅ The **Top View (TV)** is the projection on HP, drawn **below XY**. The **Front View (FV)** is the projection on VP, drawn **above XY**.
- ✅ **Projectors** are always vertical lines perpendicular to XY, connecting the same physical point in both views.
- ✅ **θ (theta)** = true inclination with HP, visible as the apparent angle in the **Top View (β)**. **φ (phi)** = true inclination with VP, visible as the apparent angle in the **Front View (α)**.
- ✅ **Plan Length (PL)** is the length of the Top View. **Elevation Length (EL)** is the length of the Front View. **True Length (TL)** is the actual 3D length.
- ✅ The fundamental identity: `TL² = PL² + (Δy)² = EL² + (Δx)²`. Compute TL *twice* for self-verification.
- ✅ The Trapezoidal Method gives TL as the **diagonal of a trapezoid** whose parallel sides are PL and EL.
- ✅ The Rotation Method gives TL as the **projection obtained after rotating the TV parallel to XY**.
- ✅ The **Horizontal Trace (HT)** is where the extended line meets HP (height = 0). The **Vertical Trace (VT)** is where the extended line meets VP (distance from VP = 0).
- ✅ When a line is **parallel to one plane**, that plane's view shows the **True Length and True Inclination** with the *other* plane.
- ✅ When a line is **inclined to both planes**, neither view shows the true length — it must be **constructed** using the Trapezoidal or Rotation Method.
- ✅ **Heights** of endpoints are always read from the **Front View** (distances above XY). **Distances from VP** are always read from the **Top View** (distances below XY).
- ✅ The KTU 2024 standard for problem data: line is given as TL, θ, φ, and one endpoint's coordinates; the other endpoint is in the first quadrant (or specified).
- ✅ The line connecting the centers of the projections of a line in both views — the locus — is **always parallel to XY** for the rotating end.
- ✅ **Apparent angles are always equal to their corresponding true inclinations** — β = θ and α = φ. This is a useful sanity check in every problem.

> [!NOTE]
> 🏆 **Final Exam Mantra (KTU 2024):** "Compute the three lengths (PL, EL, TL) using the three formulas. The TL is the diagonal of the trapezoid formed by PL and EL. The angles of this diagonal with PL and EL are θ and φ respectively. Locate the traces by extending the views to meet XY. Always double-check the units and the quadrant."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Projection of Simple Solids Such as Triangular
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Projection of Simple Solids Such as Triangular

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Simple Solid" in Engineering Graphics?

A **simple solid** (also called a *polyhedron* or *regular geometric solid*) is a three-dimensional object bounded entirely by flat polygonal faces. In the context of KTU Module 2, simple solids are restricted to those whose **base is a regular polygon** (triangle, square, pentagon, hexagon, etc.) and whose lateral faces converge either to a flat top (prism) or to a single apex (pyramid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> *"A simple solid is a three-dimensional geometric figure having a regular polygonal base, with either a flat parallel top (prism) or a pointed apex (pyramid), and whose axis is defined as the line joining the centre of the base to the centre of the top face, or the line joining the centre of the base to the apex."*

### 1.2 Two Principal Families of Simple Solids

| Family | Defining Feature | Triangular Variant | Number of Faces | Number of Edges | Number of Vertices |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Prisms** | Two parallel polygonal bases connected by **rectangular** lateral faces | **Triangular Prism** | **5** | **9** | **6** |
| **Pyramids** | One polygonal base, lateral faces are **triangular** and meet at a single apex | **Triangular Pyramid (Tetrahedron)** | **4** | **6** | **4** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Euler's Polyhedron Formula (always satisfied by convex simple solids):**
> $$F + V - E = 2$$
> Verification for Triangular Prism: $5 + 6 - 9 = 2$ ✓
> Verification for Triangular Pyramid: $4 + 4 - 6 = 2$ ✓

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are holding a **closed cardboard gift box** that happens to have a triangular cross-section (this is a triangular prism — like a Toblerone chocolate bar). Now imagine a **pointed paper tent** resting on a triangular floor mat (this is a triangular pyramid — like the roof of a small hut with a triangular floor plan).

- The Toblerone is a *prism* because cutting it horizontally at any height gives you the **same triangle**.
- The tent is a *pyramid* because the horizontal cross-section keeps getting **smaller and smaller** until it pinches to the apex.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for the Exam Hall:**
> **P**yramid → **P**ointed (all lateral faces are triangles meeting at one apex)
> **P**rism → **P**arallel top (a translated copy of the base)

## 1.4 Why "Projection" of Solids Matters

In engineering practice, every manufactured component (a wedge, a roof truss, a tapered shaft, a beam cross-section) is a *solid*. Before fabrication, the engineer must communicate the **3D shape unambiguously on 2D paper** using orthographic projection. Hence, drawing the **front view (elevation)** and **top view (plan)** of a solid is a foundational skill tested in Part B of the KTU ESE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:**
> The board examiner expects students to project solids in the following three standard orientations:
> 1. **Axis parallel to both HP and VP** (resting on base on HP)
> 2. **Axis inclined to one reference plane and parallel to the other**
> 3. **Axis inclined to both HP and VP** (final orientation — needs auxiliary projection)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometry of a Triangular Solid

### 2.1.1 Triangular Prism (Construction Lines)

A regular triangular prism of base-side $a$ and height $h$ has the following geometric centres and lines:

- **Base centroid $G$** = point where all three medians of the triangular base intersect.
- **Axis** = the line passing through the centroids of the top and bottom triangular bases; this line is **perpendicular to both bases** and has length equal to $h$.
- **Lateral edges** = the three lines joining corresponding vertices of the two bases; each has length $= h$.

### 2.1.2 Triangular Pyramid / Tetrahedron

- **Base centroid $G$** = same definition as above.
- **Axis** = the line joining base centroid $G$ to the apex $O$.
- **Lateral edges** = the three lines joining the apex $O$ to the three base vertices; for a *regular* tetrahedron, all three lateral edges are **equal** and make the same angle with the base.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **regular tetrahedron** with base side $a$, the height $h$ (i.e., length of the axis from $G$ to $O$) is:
> $$h = a\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}}$$

## 2.2 The Three Standard Projection Orientations (KTU Pattern)

Every simple solid problem in the KTU ESE is one of these three cases:

| Case | Axis Orientation | Resting Condition | Views Drawn Directly | Special Construction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Case 1** | Axis ⊥ to HP, ∥ to VP | Base on HP, axis vertical | Front view, Top view | None — simple rectangle + triangle |
| **Case 2** | Axis inclined to HP, ∥ to VP | Tilted on a lateral edge / base corner | Front view, Top view | **Auxiliary front view is drawn first**, then tilted to required angle |
| **Case 3** | Axis inclined to BOTH HP and VP | Freely suspended or resting on a corner | Top view + Front view + **Auxiliary view** | Two-step rotation using auxiliary projection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Heuristic:**
> - If the problem states *"axis inclined at $\theta^\circ$ to HP"* → **Case 2** (only the top view needs to be redrawn with the axis tilted).
> - If the problem states *"axis inclined at $\theta^\circ$ to HP and $\phi^\circ$ to VP"* → **Case 3** (an auxiliary view is mandatory).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are derived from the **rotation method** and the **auxiliary projection method**, which are the two KTU-approved construction techniques. They are routinely tested as **3-mark short derivations**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True length of axis (TL) | $\text{TL} = \text{length of axis in the position it appears in true size}$ | — | mm |
| 2 | Apparent angle in Top View (AV_top) | $\tan(\text{AV\_top}) = \dfrac{h}{a}\tan(\theta)$ | $h$=height, $a$=base side, $\theta$=inclination to HP | degrees |
| 3 | Apparent angle in Front View (AV_front) | $\sin(\text{AV\_front}) = \sin(\alpha)\cos(\phi)$ | $\alpha$=inclination to VP, $\phi$=inclination to HP | degrees |
| 4 | Final top-view length of axis | $L_t = \text{TL} \cdot \cos(\phi)$ | $L_t$=projected length on HP | mm |
| 5 | Final front-view length of axis | $L_f = \text{TL} \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ | $L_f$=projected length on VP | mm |
| 6 | Top-view inclination of a lateral edge | $\theta_e = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h \tan\theta}{R}\right)$ | $R$=circumradius of base | degrees |
| 7 | Tetrahedron height (regular) | $h = a\sqrt{2/3}$ | $a$=base edge | mm |
| 8 | Circumradius of equilateral triangle | $R = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $a$=base side | mm |
| 9 | Inradius of equilateral triangle | $r = \dfrac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | $a$=base side | mm |
| 10 | Distance from base side to centroid (in 2D base view) | $d_c = \dfrac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | $a$=base side | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule Followed:**
> Absolute-value and division bars in the table above are written as `\dfrac`, `\arctan`, `\sqrt` to prevent markdown table corruption. Do **not** write `|x|` inside a KTU answer-sheet table.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Triangular Solids

| Industry | Triangular Prism Used As | Triangular Pyramid Used As |
|---|---|---|
| **Civil / Structural** | Triangular roof truss, gable beam | Pyramidal roof cap, foundation pier |
| **Mechanical** | Wedge key, dovetail slider | Tool bit tip, conical indenter (approx.) |
| **Architecture** | Glass prism skylight, atrium | Pyramid skylight (Louvre, Egypt models) |
| **Manufacturing** | Triangular guide rail | Tapered locating pin |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Connection:** The Great Pyramid of Giza is essentially a square pyramid — but Egyptian architects built *smaller* triangular pyramids as capstone "pyramidia." Engineers still use triangular pyramids today as **flow distributors** in hoppers and silos.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Apparent Angle of a Tilted Axis

This is the **single most-tested derivation** in KTU ESE for this module. The board examiner awards full **7 marks** only if every step is shown.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A triangular prism of base side $a$ and height $h$ is resting on its base on the HP, with its axis vertical. It is then tilted such that its axis makes an angle $\theta$ with the HP, while remaining parallel to the VP. We must find the **apparent angle of the axis in the top view**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Construction Logic

**Step 1 — Initial Position (Axis Vertical, Parallel to VP):**
The axis $O_1 O_2$ has true length equal to $h$. In the front view, it appears as a vertical line of length $h$ (since the axis is parallel to VP). In the top view, the base appears as an equilateral triangle of side $a$, and the axis projects as a single point at the centroid $G$ of the base.

**Step 2 — Tilt the Axis:**
The prism is tilted forward about a base corner (say corner $A$) until the axis $O_1 O_2$ makes an angle $\theta$ with the HP. Because the axis is constrained to remain parallel to the VP, the top view of the axis must still appear as a **straight line** (not shortened), but now inclined at some apparent angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal reference line $XY$.

**Step 3 — Identify the Right Triangle in the Top View:**
In the top view, consider the right triangle formed by:
- The projection of the tilted axis (hypotenuse)
- The horizontal displacement of the top base from the bottom base
- The vertical drop of the top base

The **horizontal displacement** of the centroid (top base) from the centroid (bottom base) is the distance through which the top base has shifted laterally.

**Step 4 — Express the Horizontal Shift Geometrically:**
When the axis is tilted by angle $\theta$ with HP, the vertical drop of the top of the axis is $h \sin\theta$, and the horizontal component of the axis length is $h \cos\theta$. This horizontal component, however, is **not** the same as the apparent length of the axis in the top view because the axis is rotated in 3D space, not flattened.

**Step 5 — Apply the Auxiliary Projection Principle:**
To obtain the true apparent angle in the top view, we project the tilted front view **perpendicularly downward** onto the HP. The new top view's axis length $L_t$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_t &= h \cos\theta \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The vertical separation (in the top view) between the projections of the two base centroids is the **vertical drop** of the top base, which equals $h \sin\theta$.

**Step 6 — Form the Right Triangle and Compute the Apparent Angle:**
In the top view, the tilted axis now forms a right triangle whose:
- Horizontal leg = $L_t = h \cos\theta$
- Vertical leg = $h \sin\theta$
- Hypotenuse = apparent length of axis in top view

The apparent angle $\alpha$ of the axis in the top view (measured from the horizontal $XY$ line) satisfies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\alpha) &= \frac{\text{Vertical leg}}{\text{Horizontal leg}} \\
\tan(\alpha) &= \frac{h \sin\theta}{h \cos\theta} \\
\tan(\alpha) &= \tan\theta \\
\therefore \alpha &= \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** When the axis is tilted in a plane parallel to the VP, the apparent angle of the axis in the top view is **numerically equal to the true inclination** $\theta$ of the axis with the HP. This is why Case-2 problems are the *easiest* in the exam — only a single rotation is required.

### 3.1.3 Verification with a Numerical Example

Let $a = 30$ mm, $h = 50$ mm, $\theta = 30^\circ$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{True TL of axis} &= h = 50 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Vertical drop} &= 50 \sin 30^\circ = 50 \times 0.5 = 25 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Horizontal shift} &= 50 \cos 30^\circ = 50 \times 0.866 = 43.30 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Apparent angle in top view} &= \arctan\!\left(\frac{25}{43.30}\right) = \arctan(0.5774) = 30^\circ \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The result confirms the derivation: $\alpha = \theta = 30^\circ$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Apparent Length of Lateral Edge of a Triangular Pyramid

### 3.2.1 Setup
A regular triangular pyramid (tetrahedron) of base side $a$ is resting on its base on the HP. It is tilted so that its axis is inclined at $\theta$ to the HP and remains parallel to the VP. We must find the apparent length of a lateral edge (say $OA$, where $O$ is the apex and $A$ is a base vertex) in the top view.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Compute the True Lateral Edge Length $L$ (TL of Edge):**
For a regular tetrahedron, all four edges are equal. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — True Length of the Axis:**
From the formula sheet (Section 2.3, row 7):

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= a\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — True Angle Between Lateral Edge and Axis:**
The lateral edge $OA$ makes an angle $\beta$ with the axis. By geometry of the regular tetrahedron, the foot of the perpendicular from the apex to the base is at the centroid $G$, and the distance $GA$ is the circumradius $R$ of the equilateral triangle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the angle $\beta$ between the lateral edge $OA$ and the axis $OG$ satisfies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin(\beta) &= \frac{R}{L} = \frac{a/\sqrt{3}}{a} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\
\beta &= \arcsin\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \approx 35.26^\circ \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apparent Angle in the Tilted Position:**
When the axis is tilted by $\theta$ to the HP (and stays parallel to VP), the lateral edge $OA$ rotates in 3D space. The new angle that the lateral edge makes with the HP is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \beta - \theta \quad \text{(if the edge tilts downward)} \\
\gamma &= \beta + \theta \quad \text{(if the edge tilts upward)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The choice between $\pm$ depends on which base vertex the pyramid is pivoted on. KTU convention: pivot on the **lowest** vertex in the top view, so the formula is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \beta - \theta \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apparent Length in the Top View:**
Project the lateral edge perpendicularly onto the HP. Its apparent length $L_t$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_t &= L \cos(\gamma) \\
L_t &= a \cos(\beta - \theta) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Verification:**
Let $a = 40$ mm, $\theta = 20^\circ$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 40 \text{ mm} \\
\beta &= 35.26^\circ \\
\gamma &= 35.26^\circ - 20^\circ = 15.26^\circ \\
L_t &= 40 \cos(15.26^\circ) = 40 \times 0.9648 = 38.59 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Result: The lateral edge $OA$ appears as a line of length **38.59 mm** in the final top view, drawn at the appropriate angle relative to the $XY$ line.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU Construction Steps (Sequential)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **exam-hall algorithm**. Print this on a separate sheet and use it to solve any Case-2 or Case-3 problem in under 20 minutes.

| Step | Action | Pencil Pressure | Marks Allotted (KTU) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Draw the **assumed position**: axis vertical, base on HP, with true dimensions | Light | 1 |
| 2 | Project the top view from the front view (using 30°-60° projectors) | Light | 1 |
| 3 | Locate the **apparent angle** in the top view (for Case 2) or in the auxiliary view (for Case 3) | Light | 1 |
| 4 | **Redraw** the top view with the axis tilted at the required angle, keeping the base line stationary | Medium | 2 |
| 5 | Re-project the new front view from the redrawn top view | Medium | 2 |
| 6 | For Case 3: draw an **auxiliary view** to make the axis parallel to the new VP, then tilt the auxiliary view to the given inclination to VP | Medium | 3 |
| 7 | Mark the **hidden edges** with short dashes; mark the **visible edges** with thick continuous lines | Heavy | 2 |
| 8 | Add the **dimensions** in mm, the title, and the scale (1:1 is the KTU default) | Heavy | 1 |
| 9 | Add the **part name**, the **projection symbol** (1st angle or 3rd angle), and your **registration number** | Heavy | 1 |

> [!TIP]
> **Time Management Tip:** In a 14-mark Part B question, you have roughly 25 minutes. Spend no more than 3 minutes on the assumed position, 7 minutes on the tilted top view, 10 minutes on the final front view + dimensioning, and 5 minutes on the auxiliary view (if Case 3).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Universal Algorithm for Solid Projection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read the Problem Statement] --> B{Identify the Solid Type}
    B -->|Triangular Prism| C[Draw Base as Equilateral Triangle of Side a]
    B -->|Triangular Pyramid| D[Draw Base as Equilateral Triangle of Side a and Mark Apex O above Centroid G]
    C --> E[Draw Front View: Rectangle a x h]
    D --> F[Draw Front View: Two Slanting Lines from Apex to Base Vertices]
    E --> G[Project Top View: Triangle with Axis as a Point]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Identify the Case}
    H -->|Case 1: Axis Vertical| I[Done - Label Dimensions]
    H -->|Case 2: Axis Inclined to HP only| J[Redraw Top View with Axis Tilted by theta]
    H -->|Case 3: Axis Inclined to Both| K[Draw Auxiliary View First]
    J --> L[Re-project Final Front View]
    K --> M[Tilt Auxiliary View by phi to VP]
    M --> L
    L --> N[Add Hidden Lines as Dashes]
    N --> O[Dimension and Title Block]
    O --> P[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Projection Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        A1[Solid Type Identifier] --> A2[Dimension Parser: a, h, theta, phi]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[Geometric Construction Engine]
        B1[Assumed Position Generator] --> B2[Base Layout Engine]
        B2 --> B3[Axis Tilt Calculator]
        B3 --> B4[True Length Resolver]
        B4 --> B5[Apparent Angle Computer]
        B5 --> B6[Auxiliary Projection Module]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Module]
        C1[Front View Renderer] --> C2[Top View Renderer]
        C2 --> C3[Auxiliary View Renderer]
        C3 --> C4[Hidden Line Detector]
        C4 --> C5[Dimensioning and Title Block]
    end

    A2 --> B1
    B6 --> C1
    C5 --> D1[Final KTU Sheet]
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Top View of a Tilted Triangular Pyramid (Case 2)

```
        Reference line XY
   ─────────────────────────────────────────────
                  o  ← G' (Top base centroid)
                 /|
                / |
               /  |   h·sinθ  (vertical drop in top view)
              /   |
             /    |
            o ─── o ← A (Base vertex on which the pyramid is tilted)
            A    B,C (other base vertices)
                 |
   ─────────────────────────────────────────────
       ←  h·cosθ  →
        (horizontal shift of apex)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above ASCII schematic is a **plan-view** (top view) of the tilted triangular pyramid. The axis of the pyramid goes from the base centroid $G$ (now at $A$) to the tilted apex position $G'$, inclined at $\theta$ to the horizontal $XY$ reference line.
