<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**Q:** *Define a "simple solid" as per KTU Engineering Graphics conventions. Distinguish between a prism and a pyramid with the specific example of a triangular solid.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **simple solid** is a three-dimensional geometric figure bounded entirely by flat polygonal faces, with a regular polygonal base and either a flat parallel top or a single pointed apex. **(1 mark)**

**Distinction applied to a triangular solid: (2 marks)**

| Feature | Triangular Prism | Triangular Pyramid |
|---|---|---|
| Top face | A second triangle parallel to the base, joined by 3 rectangles | A single apex (no top face) |
| Lateral faces | 3 rectangles | 3 triangles meeting at the apex |
| Number of faces | 5 | 4 |
| Axis | Perpendicular to both bases, length = height $h$ | From base centroid $G$ to apex $O$ |
| Horizontal cross-section | Identical triangle at every height | Progressively smaller similar triangle |

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
**Q:** *What is the difference between "True Length" and "Apparent Length" of an edge in the projection of solids? Why is this distinction important when a solid is tilted?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **True Length (TL):** The actual measured length of an edge in 3D space. For example, the axis of a regular triangular prism of height $h$ has true length $= h$. **(1 mark)**
- **Apparent Length (AL):** The length of the projection of that same edge onto a 2D reference plane (HP or VP). When the edge is inclined to the reference plane, the apparent length is **shorter** than the true length. **(1 mark)**
- **Why it matters (1 mark):** When a solid is tilted, the edges no longer project in their true lengths. The KTU construction method requires the student to **first draw the assumed position (where the edge is in true length), then redraw the tilted view using the apparent length** as the projected dimension. Failing to make this distinction leads to a 2-mark deduction in the board exam.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part B question offers **internal choice**. Below, Question A and Question B are independent alternatives — the student answers either one in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
A triangular prism of base side **30 mm** and height of axis **50 mm** rests on its base on the Horizontal Plane (HP) such that one of its rectangular faces is parallel to the Vertical Plane (VP). It is then tilted such that the axis appears inclined at **30° to the HP** in the top view. Draw the projections of the solid in this new tilted position. Use **First Angle Projection**.

#### Part (a) — Draw the assumed position and the tilted top view (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Assumed Position (3 marks):**
   - Draw the $XY$ reference line in the middle of the sheet.
   - Above the $XY$ line, draw an equilateral triangle of side $a = 30$ mm with one side **perpendicular** to $XY$ (this places the prism with one rectangular face parallel to VP). Label the corners as $A$ (left), $B$ (right), $C$ (top).
   - Mark the centroid $G$ of the triangle.
   - Project the corners down to $XY$ using 30°-60° projectors, then up by $h = 50$ mm on the lower half (Front View region).
   - Draw the top triangle $A'B'C'$ parallel to the base triangle, directly above it at a vertical distance of 50 mm. Connect the corresponding corners to form the three rectangular lateral faces.

2. **Tilted Top View (4 marks):**
   - In the original top view, the axis is just the centroid point $G$. Redraw the base triangle $A_1 B_1 C_1$ below the $XY$ line, in the Top View region, keeping corner $A_1$ in the same vertical line as $A$.
   - From $A_1$, draw a line inclined at **30° to the horizontal** ($XY$-parallel line). Mark a point $G_1$ on this line at a distance of $L_t = h \cos 30^\circ = 50 \times 0.866 = 43.30$ mm from $A_1$.
   - With $G_1$ as the new centroid, construct a new equilateral triangle $A_1 B_2 C_2$ of side 30 mm such that $A_1$ is one vertex, and the other two vertices $B_2, C_2$ are positioned so that the triangle is the translated copy of the base, rotated so that the line $A_1 G_1$ becomes the new "vertical" of the tilted prism.

#### Part (b) — Draw the final front view and add dimensions (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Final Front View (5 marks):**
   - Project the new top-view corners $A_1, B_2, C_2$ upward using 30°-60° projectors that cross the $XY$ line.
   - The corner $A_1$ projects to a point at the **same height** as the original base in the front view (since the prism pivots on corner $A$, $A$ does not move vertically).
   - The corners $B_2$ and $C_2$ project to points at a **higher level** in the front view, determined by the new top-view positions.
   - Draw the top triangle of the prism in the front view, joining the projected points.
   - Connect the corresponding corners to draw the three visible rectangular lateral faces (one will be a parallelogram in the tilted view).

2. **Hidden Line Convention (1 mark):**
   - The two lateral edges at the back of the prism (corresponding to $B$ and $C$ in the original view) are **visible** from the front; the edge at $A$ is **hidden** if the prism is tilted away from the viewer. Mark hidden edges with short dashes.

3. **Dimensioning and Title Block (1 mark):**
   - Dimension the base side as **30 mm**, the axis length as **50 mm (TL)** and the apparent axis length as **43.30 mm**, the inclination as **30°**, and the title as *"Projections of a Triangular Prism — Axis Inclined at 30° to HP."*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
A triangular pyramid (regular tetrahedron) of base side **40 mm** rests on its base on the HP with one base side perpendicular to the VP. The axis is then inclined at **30° to the HP** while remaining parallel to the VP. Draw the front view, top view, and the true shape of the base in the auxiliary plane. Use **First Angle Projection**.

#### Part (a) — Draw the assumed position and the tilted top view (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Assumed Position (3 marks):**
   - Draw the $XY$ reference line.
   - In the top view (above $XY$): draw an equilateral triangle of side $a = 40$ mm with one side perpendicular to $XY$. Label the vertices $A$ (left), $B$ (right), $C$ (front). Mark the centroid $G$.
   - In the front view (below $XY$): mark the axis as a vertical line of length $h = a\sqrt{2/3} = 40 \times 0.8165 = 32.66$ mm, with the apex $O$ at the top. Connect $O$ to the projections of $A, B, C$ to form two visible triangular lateral faces and one hidden face (corresponding to edge $CA$).

2. **Tilted Top View (4 marks):**
   - Redraw the base triangle below the $XY$ line in the top view region (this is the new tilted top view base). Pivot the pyramid on corner $A$.
   - From $A$, draw a line at 30° to the horizontal. Mark the new centroid $G_1$ at distance $L_t = h \cos 30^\circ = 32.66 \times 0.866 = 28.28$ mm from $A$.
   - Construct the new base triangle with $A$ fixed and centroid at $G_1$. Mark the other two vertices as $B_1$ and $C_1$.

#### Part (b) — Draw the final front view and the auxiliary view (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Final Front View (4 marks):**
   - Project the new top-view vertices $A, B_1, C_1$ down to the front view region using 30°-60° projectors.
   - Vertex $A$ projects to the same level as the original base vertex $A$ (since the pyramid pivots on $A$).
   - The apex $O_1$ projects to a new position determined by the tilted top view.
   - Connect the projected apex $O_1$ to the projected base vertices to form the final front view, which now shows the axis tilted at 30° to the horizontal.

2. **Auxiliary View for True Shape of Base (2 marks):**
   - Draw an auxiliary reference line $X_1 Y_1$ perpendicular to the projectors from the top view.
   - Project the base triangle onto $X_1 Y_1$ at its true size (40 mm side), labelled $A_2 B_2 C_2$. This is the **true shape of the base**.

3. **Dimensioning and Title Block (1 mark):**
   - Dimension the base side **40 mm**, the axis TL **32.66 mm**, the apparent axis length **28.28 mm**, the inclination **30°**, and the title as *"Projections of a Regular Tetrahedron — Axis Inclined at 30° to HP."*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Module 2 (Based on KTU Valuation Patterns 2022–2024):**
>
> 1. **Skipping the Assumed Position (–3 marks):** The "assumed position" is the foundation. The board examiner deducts 2–3 marks if the student draws the tilted view directly without showing how it was obtained.
> 2. **Using the True Length Instead of the Apparent Length (–2 marks):** When the axis is tilted, the front view's axis length is the **apparent** length, not the true height. Using $h$ instead of $h\cos\theta$ is a frequent error.
> 3. **Wrong Hidden-Line Convention (–1 mark):** In First Angle Projection (Indian/European/ISO standard used in KTU), the top view is placed **below** the front view, and the right-side view is placed on the **left**. Students confuse this with Third Angle (American) projection and lose 1 mark on the title block.
> 4. **Missing the Centroid Marking (–1 mark):** For triangular pyramids, the apex is directly above the centroid. Forgetting to compute the centroid location (using the inradius or circumradius formula) leads to a wrongly-placed apex, costing 1 mark.
> 5. **No Dimensioning or Title Block (–1 mark):** Even a perfect drawing loses 1 mark if dimensions and the title block are missing. Always include: base side, axis length (TL and apparent), inclination angle, and the part name.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Exam Hall (Print This!)**

- [ ] **Simple Solid** = 3D shape with flat polygonal faces; satisfies Euler's formula $F + V - E = 2$.
- [ ] **Triangular Prism** has **5 faces, 9 edges, 6 vertices**; axis is perpendicular to both triangular bases.
- [ ] **Triangular Pyramid (Tetrahedron)** has **4 faces, 6 edges, 4 vertices**; axis joins base centroid $G$ to apex $O$.
- [ ] For a **regular tetrahedron** of base side $a$: axis height $h = a\sqrt{2/3}$, circumradius $R = a/\sqrt{3}$, inradius $r = a/(2\sqrt{3})$.
- [ ] **Case 1** = axis vertical; **Case 2** = axis inclined to HP only; **Case 3** = axis inclined to both HP and VP.
- [ ] When the axis is tilted by $\theta$ to HP (Case 2), the **apparent angle in the top view** equals $\theta$ itself.
- [ ] **Apparent length of axis in top view** = $h \cos\theta$ (not $h$).
- [ ] **Apparent length of axis in front view** = $h \cos\alpha$, where $\alpha$ is the inclination to VP.
- [ ] Always **draw the assumed position first** (3 marks reserved in KTU marking scheme).
- [ ] Use **30°–60° projectors** (not 45°) for transferring points between views, as per KTU convention.
- [ ] **First Angle Projection** is the KTU default: Top view BELOW front view, Right view to the LEFT, Left view to the RIGHT.
- [ ] **Hidden edges** = short dashes; **Visible edges** = thick continuous lines; **Centre lines** = long dash–short dash.
- [ ] Title block must contain: Part name, Student name, Roll number, Scale (1:1 default), and the projection symbol (a truncated cone with a truncated frustum).
- [ ] **Always show the true shape of the base** using an auxiliary view if the problem asks for "projections of the solid in the tilted position" — this is a 2-mark bonus.
- [ ] **Time management:** 14-mark Part B = 3 min assumed position + 5 min tilted top view + 8 min final front view + 3 min dimensioning + 5 min auxiliary (if Case 3) = 24–25 min total.
- [ ] **Common mistake to avoid:** Drawing the tilted prism as a *parallelogram* in the top view when the base is supposed to remain an *equilateral triangle* — the base is a rigid equilateral triangle, and only the **centroid shifts**.
- [ ] **Mnemonic for base centroid location in an equilateral triangle:** *The centroid divides each median in the ratio 2:1, with the longer part on the vertex side.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam-Hall Mantra:**
> *"Assumed position first → Tilt the top view → Re-project the front view → Mark hidden lines → Dimension → Title block."*
> Follow these six steps in order, and you will secure **at least 12 out of 14 marks** in any Part B question on triangular solids.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rectangle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** A **Rectangle** is a quadrilateral (four-sided closed polygon) whose opposite sides are **equal** and **parallel**, and all four interior angles are **right angles (90°)**. In Euclidean plane geometry, a rectangle is a special case of a parallelogram, inheriting all the properties of a parallelogram along with the additional constraint of perpendicular adjacent sides.

Mathematically, a rectangle with vertices $A$, $B$, $C$, and $D$ satisfies:

$$AB \parallel CD \quad \text{and} \quad AD \parallel BC$$
$$AB = CD \quad \text{and} \quad AD = BC$$
$$\angle A = \angle B = \angle C = \angle D = 90°$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **sheet of A4 paper** lying flat on a desk, or a **blackboard** mounted on a wall. These everyday objects are practical examples of rectangles. 

- They have **four straight edges**.
- The edges that face each other (top-bottom, left-right) are exactly the **same length**.
- Every corner is a **perfect square corner** (right angle), unlike a tilted parallelogram where corners look "slanted."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A rectangle is essentially a "squarish" shape that has been stretched or compressed along one direction. If you stretch the horizontal sides while keeping the vertical sides the same, the square becomes a rectangle. If you keep both sides equal, the rectangle collapses into a **square** — meaning the **square is a special case of a rectangle** where adjacent sides are equal.

## 1.3 Standard Geometric Properties

The fundamental properties of a rectangle, essential for KTU examinations, are:

1. **Opposite sides are equal and parallel.**
2. **All four interior angles are right angles ($90°$).**
3. **The sum of adjacent angles is $180°$** (co-interior / consecutive angles).
4. **The sum of all interior angles is $360°$** (true for any quadrilateral).
5. **The diagonals are equal in length** ($d_1 = d_2$).
6. **The diagonals bisect each other** at the point of intersection.
7. **The diagonals divide the rectangle into two pairs of congruent right-angled triangles.**
8. **The rectangle possesses two lines of symmetry** (one through each pair of midpoints of opposite sides).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Dimensions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a rectangle of length $L$ and width $W$ (with $L \geq W$):
> - The standard unit is in **millimeters (mm)** for engineering drawings, as per **BIS SP 46:1988** drafting standards adopted in KTU curriculum.
> - Length ($L$) is always the **longer** side, and Breadth/Width ($W$) is the **shorter** side.
> - The aspect ratio of an A4 sheet is approximately $1 : \sqrt{2} \approx 1 : 1.414$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theorem Breakdown

The geometric construction of a rectangle rests on **three foundational conditions**. Any one of these, when satisfied, uniquely defines a rectangle:

### Condition 1: The Right-Angle & Parallel-Side Condition
- Draw two **perpendicular intersecting lines** at a point $O$.
- Mark off length $L$ along one direction (say horizontal) and width $W$ along the perpendicular direction (say vertical).
- Complete the rectangle by drawing lines **parallel** to the existing sides through the marked endpoints.

### Condition 2: The Diagonal Condition
A rectangle can be constructed if we know:
- The **length of its diagonal ($d$)** and
- The **length of one side (either $L$ or $W$)**.

The other side can be computed using the **Pythagorean Theorem**.

### Condition 3: The Area & Perimeter Condition
A rectangle can also be constructed if we know:
- The **area ($A$)** and one side, or
- The **perimeter ($P$)** and one side, or
- The **perimeter ($P$)** and the **area ($A$)** (this requires solving a quadratic equation).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **SI / Engineering Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Perimeter ($P$)** | $P = 2(L + W)$ | Sum of all four sides | **mm** |
| **Area ($A$)** | $A = L \times W$ | Region enclosed by rectangle | **mm²** |
| **Diagonal ($d$)** | $d = \sqrt{L^2 + W^2}$ | Length of one diagonal (Pythagoras) | **mm** |
| **Other side from $d$ and $L$** | $W = \sqrt{d^2 - L^2}$ | Width if diagonal and length known | **mm** |
| **Other side from $d$ and $W$** | $L = \sqrt{d^2 - W^2}$ | Length if diagonal and width known | **mm** |
| **Length from $A$ and $W$** | $L = A / W$ | Length if area and width known | **mm** |
| **Width from $A$ and $L$** | $W = A / L$ | Width if area and length known | **mm** |
| **Sum of sides** | $L + W = P/2$ | Half-perimeter identity | **mm** |
| **Length from $A$ and $P$** | $L = \frac{P}{4} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{P}{4}\right)^2 - A}$ | Roots of quadratic $L^2 - (P/2)L + A = 0$ | **mm** |
| **Aspect Ratio (Golden)** | $L : W = \phi : 1 = 1.618 : 1$ | Golden Rectangle proportion | **dimensionless** |
| **Number of diagonals** | $2$ | Diagonals $AC$ and $BD$ | — |
| **Interior angle** | $90°$ | Each of the four angles | **degrees** |
| **Sum of interior angles** | $(n-2) \times 180° = 360°$ | For $n=4$ sides | **degrees** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** All absolute value and "such that" symbols in formulas use vertical bars that may resemble table separators. In actual KTU answer scripts, students are advised to use parentheses (e.g., $\sqrt{L^2 + W^2}$) or write "given by" to avoid confusion during manual valuation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The rectangle is the **most prevalent geometric shape in engineering design**. Its utility spans:

1. **Civil Engineering:** Building floor plans, rooms, doors, windows, brick layouts, foundation footings, and plotting land parcels on site plans.
2. **Mechanical Engineering:** Rectangular machine bases, mounting plates, gasket designs, sheet metal developments, and chassis fabrication.
3. **Architecture:** Standardized A-series paper sizes (A0, A1, A2, A3, A4) all maintain the **$\sqrt{2}$ aspect ratio** so that halving a sheet produces a similar rectangle.
4. **Electronics:** Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs), chip packaging, and LCD/OLED display panels are predominantly rectangular.
5. **Computer Graphics:** Screens, image bitmaps, and viewport coordinates are all defined on rectangular grids.

The **Golden Rectangle** (ratio $1 : 1.618$) appears in classical architecture (Parthenon), product design, and the **Fibonacci sequence** in nature (nautilus shells, sunflower seeds).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Diagonal of a Rectangle using Pythagorean Theorem

Let a rectangle $ABCD$ have:
- Length $AB = CD = L$
- Width $BC = AD = W$
- Diagonal $AC = d$ and $BD = d$

**Step 1:** Consider triangle $ABC$, formed by the diagonal $AC$ splitting the rectangle.

**Step 2:** Since $\angle B = 90°$ (rectangle property), triangle $ABC$ is a **right-angled triangle** with:
- Hypotenuse $= AC = d$
- Base $= BC = W$
- Perpendicular $= AB = L$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Pythagorean Theorem**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(AC)^2 &= (AB)^2 + (BC)^2 \\
d^2 &= L^2 + W^2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Take the positive square root (length is always positive):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \sqrt{L^2 + W^2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** The diagonal of the rectangle is given by $d = \sqrt{L^2 + W^2}$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Length and Width from Perimeter and Area

**Given:** Perimeter $P$ and Area $A$ of a rectangle.

**Find:** Length $L$ and Width $W$.

**Step 1:** Write the perimeter equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 2(L + W) \\
\Rightarrow L + W &= \frac{P}{2} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Write the area equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= L \times W \quad \text{...(Equation 2)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** From Equation 1, express $W$ in terms of $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{P}{2} - L \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into Equation 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= L \left( \frac{P}{2} - L \right) \\
A &= \frac{P}{2} L - L^2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange into standard quadratic form ($ax^2 + bx + c = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^2 - \frac{P}{2} L + A &= 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Apply the quadratic formula with $a = 1$, $b = -\frac{P}{2}$, $c = A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{-(-\frac{P}{2}) \pm \sqrt{(-\frac{P}{2})^2 - 4(1)(A)}}{2(1)} \\
L &= \frac{\frac{P}{2} \pm \sqrt{\frac{P^2}{4} - 4A}}{2} \\
L &= \frac{P}{4} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{P}{4}\right)^2 - A} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** The two roots correspond to $L$ and $W$. Take $L$ as the larger root:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{P}{4} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{P}{4}\right)^2 - A} \\
W &= \frac{P}{4} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{P}{4}\right)^2 - A} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation: Area of the Largest Rectangle Inscribed in a Circle

**Given:** A circle of radius $R$. Find the dimensions of the largest rectangle that can be inscribed.

**Step 1:** When a rectangle is inscribed in a circle, its **diagonal equals the diameter** of the circle.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 2R \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Using the Pythagorean relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^2 + W^2 &= (2R)^2 = 4R^2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The area is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= L \times W \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Maximum area occurs when $L = W$ (the rectangle is a square). Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^2 + L^2 &= 4R^2 \\
2L^2 &= 4R^2 \\
L^2 &= 2R^2 \\
L &= R\sqrt{2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** The maximum area is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\max} &= L \times L = L^2 = 2R^2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Construction Conditions

| **Given Quantities** | **Method to Find Other Sides** | **Number of Solutions** | **Validity Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L$ and $W$ | Direct construction | **Unique** | Always valid |
| $L$ and $d$ | $W = \sqrt{d^2 - L^2}$ | **Unique** | $d > L$ (strict) |
| $W$ and $d$ | $L = \sqrt{d^2 - W^2}$ | **Unique** | $d > W$ (strict) |
| $A$ and $L$ | $W = A / L$ | **Unique** | $A > 0$ and $L > 0$ |
| $A$ and $W$ | $L = A / W$ | **Unique** | $A > 0$ and $W > 0$ |
| $P$ and $L$ | $W = \frac{P}{2} - L$ | **Unique** | $P > 2L$ (strict) |
| $P$ and $A$ | Quadratic: $L^2 - \frac{P}{2}L + A = 0$ | **Two** (or **one** if degenerate, or **zero** if impossible) | $\left(\frac{P}{4}\right)^2 \geq A$ |
| $d$ only | Underdetermined — infinite rectangles | **Infinite** | $L \in (0, d)$ |
| $A$ only | Underdetermined — infinite rectangles | **Infinite** | $W = A/L$ for any $L > 0$ |
| $P$ only | Underdetermined — infinite rectangles | **Infinite** | $L \in (0, P/2)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Standard Rectangle Geometry (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Rectangle ABCD"] --> nodeB["Side AB parallel to CD"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Side AD parallel to BC"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Angle A equals 90 degrees"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Angle B equals 90 degrees"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["Angle C equals 90 degrees"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["Angle D equals 90 degrees"]
    nodeA --> nodeH["Diagonal AC"]
    nodeA --> nodeI["Diagonal BD"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["AC equals BD"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["AC equals sqrt of L squared plus W squared"]
    nodeB --> nodeL["AB equals CD equals L"]
    nodeC --> nodeM["AD equals BC equals W"]
    nodeA --> nodeN["Area equals L times W"]
    nodeA --> nodeO["Perimeter equals 2 times L plus W"]
    nodeA --> nodeP["Special case Square when L equals W"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["All sides equal and all angles 90"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Construction Decision Tree for Rectangle Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start: Rectangle Construction Problem"] --> q1{"What is given?"}
    q1 -- "Length L and Width W" --> path1["Draw perpendicular lines and complete rectangle directly"]
    q1 -- "Diagonal d and Side L" --> path2["Compute W = sqrt of d squared minus L squared, then construct"]
    q1 -- "Diagonal d and Side W" --> path3["Compute L = sqrt of d squared minus W squared, then construct"]
    q1 -- "Area A and Side L" --> path4["Compute W = A divided by L, then construct"]
    q1 -- "Perimeter P and Side L" --> path5["Compute W = P divided by 2 minus L, then construct"]
    q1 -- "Perimeter P and Area A" --> path6["Solve quadratic L squared minus P over 2 times L plus A equals 0, then construct"]
    path2 --> drawS["Standard Construction Steps"]
    path3 --> drawS
    path4 --> drawS
    path5 --> drawS
    path6 --> drawS
    path1 --> drawS
    drawS --> step1["Step 1: Draw horizontal base line of length L"]
    drawS --> step2["Step 2: Erect perpendiculars at both ends of length W"]
    drawS --> step3["Step 3: Join the top endpoints to complete rectangle"]
    drawS --> step4["Step 4: Label all vertices and dimensions"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Rectangle Hierarchy in Quadrilateral Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    quad["Quadrilateral: Four-sided polygon"] --> para["Parallelogram: Opposite sides parallel"]
    quad --> trap["Trapezium: One pair of parallel sides"]
    quad --> kite["Kite: Two pairs of adjacent equal sides"]
    para --> rect["Rectangle: Parallelogram with all right angles"]
    para --> rhom["Rhombus: Parallelogram with all sides equal"]
    rect --> sq["Square: Rectangle with all sides equal"]
    rhom --> sq
    rect --> goldR["Golden Rectangle: Ratio L to W equals 1.618 to 1"]
    sq --> goldR
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Inscribed and Circumscribed Circles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rectC["Rectangle ABCD with length L and width W"] --> qC{"Type of Circle Relation"}
    qC -- "Inscribed circle" --> caseA["Circle touches all four sides internally"]
    caseA --> cA1["Inscribed circle exists only when L equals W"]
    cA1 --> cA2["Inscribed circle becomes Square case"]
    cA1 --> cA3["Inscribed circle radius r equals L divided by 2"]
    qC -- "Circumscribed circle" --> caseB["Circle passes through all four vertices"]
    caseB --> cB1["Circumscribed circle always exists for any rectangle"]
    cB1 --> cB2["Center is intersection of diagonals"]
    cB1 --> cB3["Radius R equals d divided by 2 equals sqrt of L squared plus W squared divided by 2"]
    cB1 --> cB4["Diagonal of rectangle equals diameter of circumscribed circle"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Many students incorrectly believe a circle can always be inscribed in a rectangle. The correct statement is: *a circle can be inscribed in a rectangle only if the rectangle is a square.* A circle can **always be circumscribed** about any rectangle, since the diagonals are equal and bisect each other.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: Define a rectangle. State any three properties of a rectangle.**

**Model Answer:**

A rectangle is a quadrilateral in which **opposite sides are equal and parallel** and all four interior angles are **right angles ($90°$)**.

**Three Properties:**

1. The diagonals of a rectangle are **equal in length** and they **bisect each other** at the point of intersection.
2. The opposite sides of a rectangle are **parallel and equal** in length.
3. Each interior angle of a rectangle measures exactly **$90°$**, and the sum of all four interior angles is $360°$.

**Supplementary Points (for full marks):**
- The diagonals divide the rectangle into two pairs of **congruent right-angled triangles**.
- A rectangle possesses **two lines of symmetry**, each passing through the midpoints of a pair of opposite sides.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: A rectangle has length 12 cm and breadth 9 cm. Find its diagonal and area.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** Length $L = 12$ cm, Breadth $W = 9$ cm.

**Step 1: Diagonal Calculation using Pythagoras**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \sqrt{L^2 + W^2} \\
d &= \sqrt{(12)^2 + (9)^2} \\
d &= \sqrt{144 + 81} \\
d &= \sqrt{225} \\
d &= 15 \text{ cm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Area Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= L \times W \\
A &= 12 \times 9 \\
A &= 108 \text{ cm}^2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Diagonal $d = 15$ cm; Area $A = 108$ cm².

---

## PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q (a): Construct a rectangle of length 80 mm and breadth 50 mm. Also, draw the two diagonals and measure the diagonal length. (7 Marks)**

**Model Step-by-Step Construction Procedure:**

**Step 1:** Draw a horizontal line segment $AB$ of length **80 mm** using a ruler. This represents the length of the rectangle.

**Step 2:** At point $A$, erect a perpendicular using a set-square ($90°$ angle) or a protractor.

**Step 3:** On the perpendicular at $A$, mark off $AD = 50$ mm. Point $D$ is now located.

**Step 4:** At point $B$, similarly erect a perpendicular to $AB$ (parallel to $AD$).

**Step 5:** On the perpendicular at $B$, mark off $BC = 50$ mm. Point $C$ is now located.

**Step 6:** Join points $D$ and $C$ with a straight line. Verify that $DC = 80$ mm (parallel to $AB$).

**Step 7:** Draw diagonal $AC$ and diagonal $BD$ using a ruler. They should intersect at the center.

**Step 8:** Measure diagonal $AC$ (or $BD$) with a ruler. The measured length should be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \sqrt{80^2 + 50^2} \\
d &= \sqrt{6400 + 2500} \\
d &= \sqrt{8900} \\
d &= 94.34 \text{ mm (approximately)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9:** Dimension the drawing as per **BIS conventions**: length on the bottom edge, breadth on the left edge, and diagonal labeled inside.

**Step 10:** Title the drawing: *"Rectangle 80 mm × 50 mm."*

---

**Q (b): A rectangle has a diagonal of 75 mm and a length of 60 mm. Construct the rectangle and find its breadth. (7 Marks)**

**Model Step-by-Step Solution and Construction:**

**Step 1: Calculate the Breadth using Pythagoras**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \sqrt{d^2 - L^2} \\
W &= \sqrt{(75)^2 - (60)^2} \\
W &= \sqrt{5625 - 3600} \\
W &= \sqrt{2025} \\
W &= 45 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Construction**

- Draw a horizontal line $AB$ of length **60 mm**.
- At $A$, erect a perpendicular and mark $AD = 45$ mm.
- At $B$, erect a perpendicular and mark $BC = 45$ mm.
- Join $DC$. Verify that $DC = 60$ mm.
- Draw the diagonal $AC$ (or $BD$) and confirm its length is 75 mm using a ruler.

**Final Answer:** Breadth $W = 45$ mm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q (a): The perimeter of a rectangle is 140 mm and its area is 1200 mm². Construct the rectangle. (7 Marks)**

**Model Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Set up the equations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(L + W) &= 140 \Rightarrow L + W = 70 \quad \text{...(1)} \\
L \times W &= 1200 \quad \text{...(2)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Form the quadratic equation**

From (1): $W = 70 - L$. Substituting into (2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(70 - L) &= 1200 \\
70L - L^2 &= 1200 \\
L^2 - 70L + 1200 &= 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Solve using the quadratic formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{70 \pm \sqrt{70^2 - 4(1)(1200)}}{2} \\
L &= \frac{70 \pm \sqrt{4900 - 4800}}{2} \\
L &= \frac{70 \pm \sqrt{100}}{2} \\
L &= \frac{70 \pm 10}{2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Obtain the two roots**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= \frac{70 + 10}{2} = 40 \text{ mm} \\
L_2 &= \frac{70 - 10}{2} = 30 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Determine corresponding breadths**

If $L = 40$ mm, then $W = 70 - 40 = 30$ mm.  
If $L = 30$ mm, then $W = 70 - 30 = 40$ mm. (Same rectangle, just relabeled.)

**Step 6: Construct the rectangle**

- Draw $AB = 40$ mm.
- Erect perpendiculars and mark $AD = BC = 30$ mm.
- Join $DC = 40$ mm.

**Final Answer:** Length $L = 40$ mm, Breadth $W = 30$ mm.

---

**Q (b): Construct a rectangle of area 1500 mm² having a diagonal of 65 mm. (7 Marks)**

**Model Step-by-Step Construction Procedure (Geometric Method):**

**Step 1:** Draw a horizontal base line of length 65 mm. Label endpoints $P$ and $Q$. This represents the diagonal of the rectangle.

**Step 2:** Find the midpoint $O$ of $PQ$ by bisection (using compass arcs from $P$ and $Q$).

**Step 3:** With $O$ as center and radius $OP = 32.5$ mm, draw a circle. This circle is the **circumscribed circle** of the rectangle (the rectangle's vertices all lie on this circle).

**Step 4:** To find the rectangle's vertices, we use the constraint that $L \times W = 1500$ mm². The diagonal is split by $O$ into two equal halves. Let the half-diagonal be $r = 32.5$ mm. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^2 + W^2 &= (2r)^2 = 4r^2 = 4225 \\
L \times W &= 1500 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** From the above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(L + W)^2 &= L^2 + 2LW + W^2 = 4225 + 3000 = 7225 \\
L + W &= 85 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Solving: $L$ and $W$ are roots of $x^2 - 85x + 1500 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= \frac{85 \pm \sqrt{7225 - 6000}}{2} = \frac{85 \pm \sqrt{1225}}{2} = \frac{85 \pm 35}{2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

So $L = 60$ mm, $W = 25$ mm.

**Step 7:** From the midpoint $O$ on the diagonal, mark the half-sides:
- On a perpendicular to $PQ$ through $O$, mark half-sides or use compass to swing arcs of length 60 mm and 25 mm at appropriate orientations to find vertex $A$ on the circle.
- Similarly locate $B$, $C$, $D$ on the circle such that $AB = 60$, $BC = 25$, with all angles $90°$.

**Step 8:** Verify by measuring sides and checking $60 \times 25 = 1500$ mm² and diagonal $= \sqrt{60^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 625} = \sqrt{4225} = 65$ mm. ✓

**Final Answer:** Length $L = 60$ mm, Breadth $W = 25$ mm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to construct with proper instruments:** A rectangle construction question demands a **compass, divider, and set-square**, not freehand drawing. Freehand sketches attract **partial marking only**.
> 2. **Missing dimension lines and BIS arrows:** Every construction must be **dimensioned** with proper extension lines, dimension lines, and arrowheads as per **BIS SP 46:1988**. Omitting dimensions leads to a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing inscribing and circumscribing circles:** Many students incorrectly state that a circle can be inscribed in any rectangle. The **correct** statement is: a circle is inscribed only in a **square**; a circle is circumscribed about **any** rectangle.
> 4. **Wrong sign in the quadratic formula:** When solving for $L$ and $W$ from $P$ and $A$, students often miss the $\pm$ and write only one root. The full solution requires **both roots**, even if they are symmetric.
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Always write the final answer with units (e.g., **mm** for linear dimensions, **mm²** for areas). KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
> 6. **Not verifying the diagonal measurement:** After construction, always measure the diagonal with a ruler and **compare it with the calculated value**. Failing to verify suggests a construction error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] A **rectangle** is a parallelogram with all four right angles.
- [ ] A **square** is a special case of a rectangle where $L = W$.
- [ ] The opposite sides of a rectangle are **equal and parallel**.
- [ ] All four interior angles of a rectangle are exactly **$90°$**.
- [ ] The sum of interior angles of any rectangle is **$360°$**.
- [ ] The two **diagonals of a rectangle are equal** in length and **bisect each other**.
- [ ] The diagonal is given by the **Pythagorean Theorem**: $d = \sqrt{L^2 + W^2}$.
- [ ] The **perimeter** is $P = 2(L + W)$.
- [ ] The **area** is $A = L \times W$.
- [ ] A circle can be **circumscribed** about any rectangle (radius = $d/2$).
- [ ] A circle can be **inscribed** in a rectangle **only if the rectangle is a square**.
- [ ] The **Golden Rectangle** has the ratio $L : W = 1.618 : 1$ and is widely used in classical architecture.
- [ ] Construction of a rectangle requires a **ruler, set-square ($90°$), and divider** for accurate perpendiculars.
- [ ] All KTU 2024 drawings must conform to **BIS SP 46:1988** drafting conventions.
- [ ] Construction problems demand **step-by-step procedures** with arcs and construction lines visible in the answer script.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Square
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Square

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Geometric Definition

In the context of **KTU GCEST203 — Engineering Graphics and Computer Aided Drawing**, a **Square** is defined as a **regular quadrilateral (a regular polygon with four sides)** that lies in a single plane. By definition, a square is a closed planar figure bounded by four equal straight sides and four equal interior angles, each measuring **90°**.

In the language of the KTU 2024 syllabus, it is a special case of the family of regular polygons (triangle, square, pentagon, hexagon, etc.) and serves as the **fundamental building block** for understanding symmetry, orthographic projection concepts, and tessellation patterns used in engineering drawing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> A square is the simplest regular polygon after the equilateral triangle. In Module 2, students must learn to construct a square under **various given geometric conditions** (e.g., given a side, given a diagonal, inscribed in a circle, on a given line). Mastery of this topic directly leads into the construction of **regular polygons** using general methods (general method, radial line method, etc.).

## 1.2 Geometric Properties of a Square

For a square with side length $a$ and diagonal $d$:

1. **All four sides are equal**: $\overline{AB} = \overline{BC} = \overline{CD} = \overline{DA} = a$
2. **All four interior angles are right angles**: $\angle A = \angle B = \angle C = \angle D = 90°$
3. **Both diagonals are equal in length**: $\overline{AC} = \overline{BD} = d$
4. **The diagonals bisect each other** at the centre $O$.
5. **The diagonals bisect the interior angles** (each diagonal cuts a 90° angle into two 45° angles).
6. **The diagonals are perpendicular to each other** at the centre: $\overline{AC} \perp \overline{BD}$.
7. **The square has 4 lines of symmetry** (2 through midpoints of opposite sides, 2 along the diagonals).
8. **The square has rotational symmetry of order 4** (looks the same after rotation by 90°, 180°, 270°, 360°).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight**
> The 45°/45°/90° triangle that forms when a diagonal is drawn is one of the most frequently used triangles in engineering graphics. Every square diagonal creates **two congruent isosceles right triangles**, and this is the geometric reason a square's diagonal equals $a\sqrt{2}$.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **perfectly cut slice of bread from a sandwich** before any spreading — the top surface is a square. Or think of a **single tile on your kitchen floor** — that tile is square. Even the **face of a die (singular of dice)** used in board games is a square (the face that shows the number 1, 4, 9, etc.).

Now, engineering-wise, think of a **photo frame**, a **window pane**, or the **plan view of a room** — these are all squares (or rectangles, which are "stretched squares"). When a civil or mechanical engineer draws a **plan of a square column footing** or a **square machine base plate**, they are essentially constructing a square in the drawing sheet. The construction techniques you learn in this topic will be reused hundreds of times in your engineering career.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following constants are required while constructing or solving problems on squares:

| Quantity | Symbol / Value |
|---|---|
| Number of sides of a square | $n = 4$ |
| Interior angle of a square | $90°$ |
| Exterior angle of a square | $90°$ |
| Central angle subtended by each side at the centre | $360°/4 = 90°$ |
| Diagonal-to-side ratio | $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142$ |
| Side-to-diagonal ratio | $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **diagonal of a square is $\sqrt{2}$ times its side** — this is a direct consequence of the Pythagoras theorem applied to the right isosceles triangle formed by two adjacent sides and the diagonal.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Square is a "Regular Polygon"

A **regular polygon** is a polygon that is both:
- **Equilateral** (all sides equal), AND
- **Equiangular** (all interior angles equal).

A square satisfies **both** conditions simultaneously, hence it is a **regular polygon of order 4** (i.e., 4 sides).

For any regular polygon with $n$ sides:
- Each **interior angle** $= \dfrac{(n-2) \times 180°}{n}$
- Each **exterior angle** $= \dfrac{360°}{n}$
- Each **central angle** $= \dfrac{360°}{n}$

For a square, $n = 4$:
- Interior angle $= \dfrac{(4-2) \times 180°}{4} = 90°$
- Exterior angle $= 360°/4 = 90°$
- Central angle $= 360°/4 = 90°$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the central angle concept important?**
> When constructing a square (or any regular polygon) **inscribed in a circle**, the vertices lie on the circle, and the centre of the circle is also the centre of the polygon. The angle subtended at the centre by each side equals the exterior angle. This is the foundation of the **"Radial Line Method"** and **"Concentric Circle Method"** of polygon construction taught later in this module.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Square Construction Methods

The various methods of constructing a square in engineering graphics are not arbitrary — each one corresponds to a different **given datum** (a piece of starting information). The KTU syllabus emphasizes that an engineering draughtsman must be able to construct the required figure starting from **whichever single piece of information is available** in a real engineering drawing problem.

The five classical starting data for a square are:
1. **Given a side** (the side length is known)
2. **Given a diagonal** (only the diagonal length is known)
3. **Inscribed in a given circle** (the circumscribing circle is known)
4. **Circumscribing a given circle** (the inscribed circle is known)
5. **On a given straight line as one side** (the line is fixed in position and orientation)

In every method, the **end result** is a closed quadrilateral with four equal sides meeting at right angles. The difference lies in the **starting information** and the **geometric principle** invoked (Pythagoras, circle theorems, parallel-line construction, etc.).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for Square

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master These Before the Exam**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Side of a square | $a$ | mm / cm (given or chosen) |
| 2 | Diagonal in terms of side | $d = a\sqrt{2}$ | Always positive |
| 3 | Side in terms of diagonal | $a = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{d\sqrt{2}}{2}$ | Rationalized form preferred |
| 4 | Perimeter | $P = 4a$ | Sum of all four sides |
| 5 | Area | $A = a^{2} = \dfrac{d^{2}}{2}$ | Square units |
| 6 | Inradius (circle inscribed in square) | $r = \dfrac{a}{2}$ | Touches all 4 sides |
| 7 | Circumradius (circle circumscribing square) | $R = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{d}{2}$ | Passes through all 4 vertices |
| 8 | Ratio $R/r$ | $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142$ | Independent of size |
| 9 | Length of the diagonal of a face of a cube of side $a$ | $a\sqrt{2}$ | Extension to 3D |
| 10 | Body diagonal of a cube of side $a$ | $a\sqrt{3}$ | Extension to 3D |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Conflict Avoidance**
> Throughout these notes we use the standard convention: $a$ = side length, $d$ = diagonal length, $r$ = inradius, $R$ = circumradius. In KTU question papers, do not confuse $d$ (the side of a square drawn in oblique projection) with $d$ (the true length of the diagonal) — always re-read the question carefully.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The square is not just a classroom exercise. It is the geometric primitive upon which the following are built:

- **Architecture & Civil Engineering**: Square floor plans, square column footings, square tiles, square windows.
- **Mechanical Engineering**: Square flanges, square nuts and bolt heads, square keyways, square base plates.
- **Electrical Engineering**: Square integrated circuit packages, square PCB pads.
- **Computer Graphics & CAD**: The square is the basis of the **pixel**, the **voxel face**, and the **2D bitmap**. Every raster image in AutoCAD or any CAD package is internally stored as a grid of tiny squares.
- **Manufacturing**: Square bars, square tubing, square section beams.

Thus, learning to construct a square in the drawing sheet is a foundational skill that translates directly to **CAD modeling** in tools like AutoCAD, SolidWorks, and CATIA.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Constructions & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive step-by-step construction procedures** for the five standard cases of square construction, as required by the KTU 2024 scheme.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Convention**
> In every construction, we will clearly distinguish between:
> - **Given** — what is supplied in the problem.
> - **Required** — what must be produced.
> - **Principle** — the geometric theorem on which the construction is based.
> - **Steps** — the sequential drafting instructions.

---

## 3.1 Case 1: Construction of a Square Given One Side (The Most Fundamental Case)

**Given:** A straight line segment $\overline{AB}$ of length $a$.
**Required:** A square $\overline{ABCD}$ on side $\overline{AB}$.
**Principle Used:** Perpendicular construction at a point on a line; transfer of length using compass.

### Step-by-Step Procedure:

1. **Draw the given side**: Draw a horizontal line segment $\overline{AB}$ of the given length $a$ using a ruler.
2. **Construct a perpendicular at A**: Place the compass point at $A$. With a radius greater than half of $\overline{AB}$, draw two small arcs — one above and one below the line — cutting $\overline{AB}$ at two points. From those two points, draw equal arcs that intersect above the line. The line from $A$ through that intersection is the perpendicular at $A$. Let this perpendicular be ray $AX$.
3. **Transfer the length $a$ onto the perpendicular**: With the compass point at $A$ and radius equal to $a$ (i.e., the length of $\overline{AB}$), draw an arc cutting ray $AX$ at point $D$.
4. **Draw a parallel line through D**: Through $D$, draw a line parallel to $\overline{AB}$. (This can be done using the same perpendicular-and-arc method at $D$, or by sliding a set square.)
5. **Transfer the length $a$ onto the parallel line**: With the compass point at $B$ and radius equal to $a$, draw an arc cutting the parallel line through $D$ at point $C$.
6. **Close the figure**: Join $C$ to $D$. The closed figure $\overline{ABCD}$ is the required square.
7. **Verification (KTU marking scheme)**: Measure all four sides with the ruler — they should all equal $a$. Measure all four angles with a protractor — they should all be $90°$. Measure the diagonals $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BD}$ — they should both equal $a\sqrt{2}$.

### Mathematical Justification (Why it works):

Since $\overline{AD} \perp \overline{AB}$ and $\overline{AD} = a$ (transferred compass length), and $\overline{BC} \perp \overline{AB}$ and $\overline{BC} = a$ (transferred compass length), and $\overline{DC} \parallel \overline{AB}$ with $\overline{DC} = a$ (by construction), the quadrilateral $\overline{ABCD}$ has:
- Two pairs of parallel sides (so it is a parallelogram),
- One right angle (so it is a rectangle),
- Two adjacent equal sides (so it is a square).

$$\text{Therefore: } \overline{ABCD} \text{ is a square of side } a.$$

### Dimensioning as per KTU Norms:

- Mark the side length $a$ on one side (say $\overline{AB}$).
- Mark the diagonal $a\sqrt{2}$ on one diagonal (say $\overline{AC}$).
- All dimensions are in mm.
- Use a continuous (chain) dimension line for the side and a leader line for the diagonal.

---

## 3.2 Case 2: Construction of a Square Given the Diagonal

**Given:** A straight line segment $\overline{AC}$ of length $d$.
**Required:** A square $\overline{ABCD}$ whose diagonal is $\overline{AC}$.
**Principle Used:** The diagonals of a square bisect each other at right angles; the centre is the midpoint of the diagonal.

### Step-by-Step Procedure:

1. **Draw the given diagonal**: Draw line segment $\overline{AC}$ of length $d$ horizontally.
2. **Find the midpoint O of the diagonal**: Construct the perpendicular bisector of $\overline{AC}$. The point where the perpendicular bisector cuts $\overline{AC}$ is the midpoint $O$.
3. **Draw a circle with AC as diameter**: With $O$ as centre and radius $\overline{OA} = \overline{OC} = d/2$, draw a circle. This is the **circumscribed circle** of the required square.
4. **Draw the perpendicular to AC at O**: Extend the perpendicular bisector of $\overline{AC}$ (which was already drawn in Step 2). This perpendicular line passes through $O$ and is perpendicular to $\overline{AC}$.
5. **Mark points B and D**: Let the perpendicular bisector (extended both upward and downward) intersect the circle at points $B$ and $D$. By construction, $\overline{OB} = \overline{OD} = d/2$, and $\overline{OB} \perp \overline{OA}$.
6. **Join the vertices**: Join $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ in order. The resulting figure is the required square.
7. **Verification**: Measure all four sides — they should all equal $d/\sqrt{2}$. Measure the diagonals — they should be equal to $d$ (the given value) and intersect at right angles at $O$.

### Mathematical Justification (Why it works):

By Thales' theorem, since $\overline{AC}$ is a diameter of the circle, any angle inscribed in the semicircle (such as $\angle ABC$) is a right angle. Therefore $\angle ABC = 90°$. Similarly, $\angle ADC = 90°$. The diagonals $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BD}$ bisect each other at $O$ and are perpendicular (since $\overline{BD}$ was drawn perpendicular to $\overline{AC}$ at $O$). A parallelogram with equal diagonals that bisect at right angles is a **rhombus**, and a rhombus with one right angle is a **square**.

$$\boxed{\text{Side of square} = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{d\sqrt{2}}{2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Case 3: Square Inscribed in a Given Circle

**Given:** A circle of radius $R$ with centre $O$.
**Required:** A square $\overline{ABCD}$ inscribed in the circle (all four vertices on the circle).
**Principle Used:** The vertices of an inscribed regular polygon divide the circle into $n$ equal arcs, and the central angles are equal.

### Step-by-Step Procedure:

1. **Draw the given circle** with centre $O$ and radius $R$.
2. **Draw a horizontal diameter** $\overline{XX'}$ through $O$. Let the right end be $A$ and the left end be $C$ (so $\overline{AC}$ is a diameter, and $\overline{AC} = 2R$).
3. **Construct the perpendicular diameter** through $O$. This is the vertical line through $O$, perpendicular to $\overline{AC}$. Let the upper intersection with the circle be $B$ and the lower be $D$.
4. **Join the four points**: Join $A \to B \to C \to D$ in cyclic order.
5. **The closed figure ABCD is the required inscribed square.**

### Mathematical Justification:

The central angles $\angle AOB = \angle BOC = \angle COD = \angle DOA = 90°$ (since they are quadrants of the circle). The vertices are equally spaced on the circle, so chords $\overline{AB} = \overline{BC} = \overline{CD} = \overline{DA}$. The diameter of the circle is the diagonal of the square:

$$d = 2R \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \dfrac{2R}{\sqrt{2}} = R\sqrt{2}$$

---

## 3.4 Case 4: Square Circumscribing a Given Circle (Circle Inscribed in Square)

**Given:** A circle of radius $r$ with centre $O$.
**Required:** A square $\overline{ABCD}$ that circumscribes the circle (all four sides tangent to the circle).
**Principle Used:** The radius drawn to the point of tangency is perpendicular to the tangent line.

### Step-by-Step Procedure:

1. **Draw the given circle** with centre $O$ and radius $r$.
2. **Draw two perpendicular diameters** $\overline{XX'}$ (horizontal) and $\overline{YY'}$ (vertical) through $O$. They divide the circle into 4 quadrants.
3. **Draw tangents at the four cardinal points**: At the four points where the diameters meet the circle (the "north, south, east, west" points), draw tangents perpendicular to the respective radii.
4. **The four tangent lines** intersect to form a square $\overline{ABCD}$ that circumscribes the circle.

### Mathematical Justification:

The four tangent lines are at distances $r$ from $O$ along four mutually perpendicular directions, forming a closed figure with all sides at perpendicular distances $r$ from the centre. The side length of the circumscribing square equals the diameter of the circle:

$$a = 2r$$

---

## 3.5 Case 5: Square on a Given Straight Line as One Side (where the line's position and orientation are fixed)

**Given:** A straight line $L$ (no specific length) on which one side of the square must lie.
**Required:** Construct any square having one of its sides on line $L$.

### Step-by-Step Procedure:

1. **Mark any two points P and Q on the line L** such that $\overline{PQ}$ is the desired side length of the square. (This is the "free" choice — the line $L$ only fixes the orientation, not the size, unless the problem specifies a size.)
2. **Construct perpendiculars** at $P$ and at $Q$ to the line $L$.
3. **On both perpendiculars**, mark off equal lengths equal to $\overline{PQ}$ to obtain points $R$ and $S$ on the same side of $L$.
4. **Join R to S**. $\overline{PQSR}$ is the required square.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: If the problem says "construct a square on a given line as one side", check whether a side length is also given. If only the line is given, any square with that line as a side is acceptable. If a side length is also given, restrict $\overline{PQ}$ to that length.

---

## 3.6 Summary Construction Matrix

| Case | Given | Key Construction Tool | Side $a$ in terms of given | Number of Construction Steps |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Side $a$ | Perpendicular + length transfer | $a$ | 6 |
| 2 | Diagonal $d$ | Perpendicular bisector + circle | $d/\sqrt{2}$ | 6 |
| 3 | Circumscribed circle (radius $R$) | Two perpendicular diameters | $R\sqrt{2}$ | 5 |
| 4 | Inscribed circle (radius $r$) | Two perpendicular diameters + tangents | $2r$ | 4 |
| 5 | Line as side (orientation fixed) | Perpendiculars at endpoints | Free choice | 4 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides **block-level functional architecture diagrams** (Mermaid) that visualize the construction logic, the relationship between a square and its dual circle (inscribed/circumscribed), and the workflow for the five construction cases.

## 4.1 Square-Circle Geometric Relationship (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Square ABCD with side a"] --> B["Four sides of equal length a"]
    A --> C["Four right angles 90 deg"]
    A --> D["Two diagonals of length a times sqrt 2"]
    A --> E["Centre O is intersection of diagonals"]
    E --> F["Circumscribed circle: radius R equals a times sqrt 2 divided by 2"]
    E --> G["Inscribed circle: radius r equals a divided by 2"]
    F --> H["R equals sqrt 2 times r"]
    G --> H
    D --> I["Diagonals bisect at O and are perpendicular"]
    C --> I
```

## 4.2 Construction Workflow: Five Cases of Square Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start["START: Identify the GIVEN datum"] --> q1{"What is given?"}
    q1 -->|"Side length a"| case1["Case 1: Construct perpendicular at A, transfer length a"]
    q1 -->|"Diagonal length d"| case2["Case 2: Draw circle on AC as diameter, find perpendicular"]
    q1 -->|"Circumscribed circle radius R"| case3["Case 3: Draw two perpendicular diameters, join ends"]
    q1 -->|"Inscribed circle radius r"| case4["Case 4: Draw tangents at four cardinal points of circle"]
    q1 -->|"Straight line L as side"| case5["Case 5: Mark endpoints on L, erect perpendiculars"]
    case1 --> verify["VERIFY: Check sides equal, angles 90 deg"]
    case2 --> verify
    case3 --> verify
    case4 --> verify
    case5 --> verify
    verify --> endnode["END: Required Square ABCD obtained"]
```

## 4.3 Property Dependency Map of a Square

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    def["Definition: Regular polygon with 4 sides"] --> p1["Property: All sides equal to a"]
    def --> p2["Property: All interior angles 90 deg"]
    def --> p3["Property: Diagonals equal to a times sqrt 2"]
    def --> p4["Property: Diagonals bisect at right angles at centre O"]
    p1 --> app1["Application: Tile floor, square column footing"]
    p2 --> app2["Application: Right-angle bracket, window frame"]
    p3 --> app3["Application: Diagonal bracing, isometric projection"]
    p4 --> app4["Application: Centre of mass, square inscribing circle"]
```

## 4.4 Construction Step Sequence: Case 1 (Square from a Given Side)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["Step 1: Draw side AB of length a"] --> s2["Step 2: Construct perpendicular AX at point A"]
    s2 --> s3["Step 3: With centre A and radius a, mark D on ray AX"]
    s3 --> s4["Step 4: Through D, draw line parallel to AB"]
    s4 --> s5["Step 5: With centre B and radius a, mark C on parallel line"]
    s5 --> s6["Step 6: Join C to D to close the square"]
    s6 --> s7["Step 7: Dimension and verify all sides and angles"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams**
> These Mermaid block diagrams are designed to show the **logical flow** of the construction process and the **inter-dependencies** between geometric properties of a square. In an actual KTU exam, you will of course draw the **physical figure** on the drawing sheet with a pencil, ruler, compass, and protractor — the Mermaid diagrams here are for **conceptual clarity** of the construction logic, not as a substitute for the actual drawing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern (Part A)**
> Part A questions test **Recall and Understanding** (Bloom Level 1 & 2). Answers must be concise (typically 1–3 sentences plus a small sketch where applicable).

### Question A1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**"Define a square. List any four geometric properties of a square."**

**Model Answer:**

A square is a **regular quadrilateral** — a closed planar figure bounded by **four equal straight sides** meeting at **four right angles**.

Four geometric properties:
1. All four sides are equal in length ($AB = BC = CD = DA$).
2. All four interior angles are $90°$.
3. The two diagonals are equal in length and bisect each other at the centre.
4. The diagonals intersect each other at right angles ($90°$).

> [!NOTE]
> For full marks (3), students should mention the definition plus **at least four** properties. A small sketch with labelled sides and a diagonal will earn **partial extra credit** for presentation.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**"The diagonal of a square is 70 mm. Construct the square and dimension it as per BIS standards. State the formula used."**

**Model Answer:**

**Formula used:**

$$a = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{70}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{70 \times \sqrt{2}}{2} = 35\sqrt{2} \approx 49.497 \text{ mm}$$

**Construction steps (concise version):**
1. Draw diagonal $\overline{AC} = 70$ mm.
2. Construct the perpendicular bisector of $\overline{AC}$; let the midpoint be $O$.
3. With $O$ as centre and radius $35$ mm, draw a circle on $\overline{AC}$ as diameter.
4. The perpendicular bisector meets the circle at points $B$ and $D$.
5. Join $A \to B \to C \to D$ to obtain the square.

**Dimensions to mark:** Side $\approx 49.5$ mm on one side; diagonal $70$ mm on $\overline{AC}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (Part B)**
> Part B questions test **Application, Analysis, and Synthesis** (Bloom Levels 3, 4, 5). Each Part B question has two sub-parts: **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Construct a square $\overline{ABCD}$ of side **50 mm**. Draw both diagonals and measure their length. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A square is inscribed in a circle of diameter **80 mm**. Construct the figure and find the side of the square. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Given:** Side of square = $a = 50$ mm.

**Construction steps:**
1. Draw $\overline{AB} = 50$ mm horizontal.
2. Construct a perpendicular $\overline{AX}$ at $A$ using compass arcs.
3. With centre $A$ and radius $50$ mm, cut $\overline{AX}$ at $D$. So $\overline{AD} = 50$ mm.
4. Through $D$, draw a line parallel to $\overline{AB}$.
5. With centre $B$ and radius $50$ mm, cut the parallel line at $C$. So $\overline{BC} = 50$ mm.
6. Join $C$ and $D$. The square $\overline{ABCD}$ is formed.
7. Draw diagonals $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BD}$. They intersect at $O$ (the centre).

**Length of diagonal (theoretical):**

$$d = a\sqrt{2} = 50\sqrt{2} \approx 70.71 \text{ mm}$$

**Length of diagonal (measured from drawing):** should be approximately $70.7$ mm.

**Dimensions to mark:** $50$ mm on side $\overline{AB}$, and $70.7$ mm on diagonal $\overline{AC}$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** Diameter of circumscribing circle = $80$ mm, so radius $R = 40$ mm.

**Principle:** The diameter of the circumscribing circle is the **diagonal** of the inscribed square.

**Calculation of side:**

$$d = 80 \text{ mm}, \quad a = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{80}{\sqrt{2}} = 40\sqrt{2} \approx 56.57 \text{ mm}$$

**Construction steps:**
1. Draw a circle with centre $O$ and radius $40$ mm.
2. Draw a horizontal diameter $\overline{AC}$. (So $\overline{AC} = 80$ mm.)
3. Draw the perpendicular diameter $\overline{BD}$ through $O$.
4. Join $A \to B \to C \to D$ in cyclic order. This is the inscribed square.
5. Measure side $\overline{AB}$ — it should be approximately $56.57$ mm.

**Dimensions to mark:** Diameter $80$ mm on $\overline{AC}$, side $56.57$ mm on $\overline{AB}$, and radius $R = 40$ mm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Construct a square given one diagonal of length **60 mm**. Write the construction procedure and state the formula for the side. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A circle of radius **30 mm** is inscribed in a square. Draw the square circumscribing the circle and determine its side. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Given:** Diagonal $d = 60$ mm.

**Formula for side:**

$$a = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{2}} = 30\sqrt{2} \approx 42.43 \text{ mm}$$

**Construction steps:**
1. Draw diagonal $\overline{AC} = 60$ mm horizontally.
2. Construct the perpendicular bisector of $\overline{AC}$ to find the midpoint $O$.
3. With $O$ as centre and radius $30$ mm, draw a circle on $\overline{AC}$ as diameter.
4. Extend the perpendicular bisector to meet the circle at $B$ (above) and $D$ (below).
5. Join $A \to B \to C \to D$ to complete the square.
6. Verify: $\overline{AB} = \overline{BC} = \overline{CD} = \overline{DA} \approx 42.43$ mm; $\overline{AC} = \overline{BD} = 60$ mm.

**Dimensions to mark:** $60$ mm on diagonal $\overline{AC}$ and $42.43$ mm on side $\overline{AB}$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** Inscribed circle radius $r = 30$ mm.

**Formula for side:** The side of a square circumscribing a circle of radius $r$ is $a = 2r$.

$$a = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ mm}$$

**Construction steps:**
1. Draw a circle with centre $O$ and radius $30$ mm.
2. Draw two perpendicular diameters $\overline{XX'}$ and $\overline{YY'}$ through $O$. These meet the circle at four points $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ (the "N, S, E, W" points).
3. At each of these four points, draw a tangent line perpendicular to the corresponding radius.
4. The four tangents intersect to form the square $\overline{ABCD}$.
5. Measure $\overline{AB}$ — should be exactly $60$ mm.

**Dimensions to mark:** $60$ mm on side $\overline{AB}$ and $30$ mm as the radius (with an "R" label or "r30" notation).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (and How to Avoid Them)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to construct the perpendicular bisector carefully in Case 2 (Square from Diagonal).** Many students simply guess the position of $B$ and $D$ instead of using the perpendicular bisector of the diagonal. This results in a non-square rhombus. **Always use the compass** to find the perpendicular bisector.
>
> 2. **Confusing inscribed and circumscribed.** "Square inscribed in a circle" means the square's **vertices** touch the circle. "Square circumscribing a circle" means the square's **sides** touch the circle. Read the question twice.
>
> 3. **Using $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.4$ instead of $1.414$ in final answers.** The difference of $0.014 \times 50 = 0.7$ mm in a $50$ mm side can lose **1 mark** for "incorrect dimensional accuracy".
>
> 4. **Not dimensioning both the side and the diagonal.** KTU examiners want to see both key dimensions marked. Marking only the side loses partial credit.
>
> 5. **Drawing construction lines in heavy/thick strokes.** Construction lines (arcs, perpendicular bisectors, tangents) must be drawn in **thin, light, dashed or faint continuous strokes**. Only the final square is drawn in a **thick, dark, continuous stroke**.
>
> 6. **Not writing the construction procedure** alongside the drawing. In KTU Part B questions, the written procedure carries **2–3 marks** separately from the drawing. Skipping it is a guaranteed loss.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the formula sheet entry** at the start of the answer. Always write the formulas ($d = a\sqrt{2}$ or $a = d/\sqrt{2}$) at the top of the construction page before beginning to draw.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Square Construction (Module 2, GCEST203)**

- **Definition**: A square is a regular quadrilateral with 4 equal sides and 4 right angles.
- **Key formula**: $d = a\sqrt{2}$, equivalently $a = d/\sqrt{2} = d\sqrt{2}/2$.
- **Perimeter**: $P = 4a$.
- **Area**: $A = a^2 = d^2/2$.
- **Inscribed circle radius** (circle inside square): $r = a/2$.
- **Circumscribed circle radius** (circle outside square, through vertices): $R = a\sqrt{2}/2 = d/2$.
- **Central angle** of square: $90°$ (one quarter of $360°$).
- **Five construction cases** to master:
  - Case 1: Given side — use perpendicular + length transfer.
  - Case 2: Given diagonal — use perpendicular bisector + Thales' circle.
  - Case 3: Inscribed in circle — use two perpendicular diameters.
  - Case 4: Circumscribing a circle — use two perpendicular diameters + tangents.
  - Case 5: On a given line as side — erect perpendiculars at the endpoints.
- **Always** write the construction procedure (3–6 steps) on the answer sheet.
- **Always** dimension both the side and the diagonal.
- **Always** use thin lines for construction arcs and thick lines for the final figure.
- **Diagonal = $a\sqrt{2}$** is the most frequently used relationship in KTU problems — memorize it.
- The square is the **first** regular polygon studied in Module 2; mastery here makes pentagon, hexagon, and octagon constructions much easier.
- The $45°/45°/90°$ triangle (formed by drawing a square's diagonal) is a **building block** for later topics in isometric projection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pentagonal and Hexagonal Prisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pentagonal and Hexagonal Prisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **prism** is a polyhedron generated by translating a plane polygon (the base) parallel to itself along a straight line (the axis) that does not lie in the plane of the base. The lateral surface is composed of a series of parallelograms (rectangles when the axis is perpendicular to the base).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box**
> - A **Pentagonal Prism** is a prism whose base is a regular pentagon — comprising **2 pentagonal faces** and **5 rectangular lateral faces**, with a total of **7 faces, 15 edges, and 10 vertices**.
> - A **Hexagonal Prism** is a prism whose base is a regular hexagon — comprising **2 hexagonal faces** and **6 rectangular lateral faces**, with a total of **8 faces, 18 edges, and 12 vertices**.

In **Engineering Graphics (GCEST203)**, the typical KTU board problem requires drawing the **orthographic projections** (Top View, Front View, and Side View using first-angle projection) of a pentagonal or hexagonal prism resting on the **Horizontal Plane (HP)** on one of its base edges, with the base inclined at a standard **30°** to the HP and the axis perpendicular to the **Vertical Plane (VP)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long **hexagonal pencil** (like a carpenter's pencil or a thick crayon). The end you see is a hexagon, and the body stretches back as six identical rectangular faces. That is exactly a hexagonal prism! Now take that pencil and **chop one of its ends off obliquely** so it stands on a sharpened edge instead of the flat hexagonal face — that is what KTU means by *“resting on a base edge on HP, with the base inclined at 30° to HP.”*

A **pentagonal prism** is similar but has 5 sides instead of 6 — think of a slice of a **pentagon-shaped cake** with a tall icing layer on top, or a portion of a **fortification tower** in old castle architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Both prisms are the foundation for understanding inclined solids in Module 2. They are the natural next step after pyramids and cones, and they frequently appear in the 14-mark Part-B section of the university ESE.

### 1.3 Standard KTU Convention & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Problem Data (Highlighted as Bold)**
> - **Side of pentagonal/hexagonal base (a)** = **30 mm**
> - **Height of prism (h)** = **50 mm to 65 mm** (typically **60 mm**)
> - **Angle of inclination of base to HP** = **30°** (when resting on a base edge)
> - **Projection method** = **First Angle Projection** (Indian/British Standard **IS 696**)
> - **Resting condition** = Solid rests on **HP on one of its base edges** such that the base is inclined at 30° to HP, and the axis is **perpendicular to VP** (i.e., appears as a point in the side view).

The standard KTU approach for a pentagonal or hexagonal prism follows a **two-stage projection**:
1. **Stage 1 (Initial Position):** Prism rests on its base on HP with axis perpendicular to HP. Draw three views.
2. **Stage 2 (Final Position):** Tilt the prism so it rests on one base edge on HP, with the base inclined at 30° to HP. Redraw the views using the auxiliary/rotation method.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

#### Stage 1: Initial Position (Prism Standing on its Base on HP)
- **Position:** Axis is vertical, perpendicular to HP. Base is parallel to HP.
- **Top View (TV):** A regular pentagon or hexagon (true shape) of side `a = 30 mm`.
- **Front View (FV):** A rectangle of width = length of base edge projection across, and height = `h = 60 mm`. The base edge appears as a **horizontal line** on the XY line.
- **Side View (SV):** A rectangle (if pentagon) or a rectangle with width = apothem × 2 (if hexagon), height = `h = 60 mm`.

#### Stage 2: Final Position (Prism Resting on a Base Edge on HP)
- **Tilt the TV** such that the resting edge (say edge `a-b` for the pentagon, or one of the outer edges for the hexagon) remains on the XY line, and the rest of the pentagon/hexagon is inclined at 30° to the XY line.
- **New Front View:** The base edge stays on XY, and the opposite vertex/edge is lifted up. Project vertically upward from the new TV to find the apparent height of the prism's top.
- **New Side View:** The axis appears as a line inclined at 30° (or as per the actual geometry) — for true inclination of 30° in the new position, additional projection lines are drawn.

#### 2.2 The Critical 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why draw Stage 1 first?** Because it gives the **true length** of edges and the **true shape** of the base. Without the initial top view, you cannot compute the projections of the inclined position accurately.
- **Why use the resting edge on XY?** Because the problem says the solid rests on a base edge. The edge is in contact with HP, so it must lie on the XY reference line in the top view.
- **Why does the front view get shorter?** When the base is inclined, the apparent height of the prism in the FV is reduced from `h` to `h × cos(30°)` plus the vertical rise of the inclined base.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Apothem of regular pentagon | $a_{p} = \dfrac{a}{2}\tan(54°)$ or $\dfrac{a}{2}\cot(36°)$ | mm | Distance from center to mid-edge |
| 2 | Diagonal of regular pentagon | $D_{p} = a \cdot \phi$ where $\phi = \dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ | mm | Golden ratio |
| 3 | Circumradius of regular pentagon | $R_{p} = \dfrac{a}{2\sin(36°)}$ | mm | Distance from center to vertex |
| 4 | Apothem of regular hexagon | $a_{h} = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}$ | mm | Equals circumradius |
| 5 | Long diagonal of regular hexagon | $D_{h} = 2a$ | mm | Twice the side |
| 6 | Apparent height in final FV (when base inclined at $\theta$) | $h' = h \cos\theta + d_{\text{rise}}$ | mm | $d_{\text{rise}}$ = vertical lift of farthest edge |
| 7 | Vertical lift of opposite edge (pentagon) | $\Delta h_{p} = a_{p} \cdot \sin\theta$ | mm | Apothem × sin(inclination) |
| 8 | Vertical lift of opposite edge (hexagon) | $\Delta h_{h} = a_{h} \cdot \sin\theta$ | mm | Apothem × sin(inclination) |
| 9 | Number of faces (pentagon prism) | $F = 7$ | — | Euler: $V - E + F = 2$ |
| 10 | Number of faces (hexagon prism) | $F = 8$ | — | Euler: $V - E + F = 2$ |
| 11 | Surface area of regular pentagonal prism | $A_{p} = 5ah + \dfrac{5a^{2}}{4}\cot(36°)$ | mm² | Lateral + 2 bases |
| 12 | Surface area of regular hexagonal prism | $A_{h} = 6ah + 3\sqrt{3}\,a^{2}$ | mm² | Lateral + 2 bases |
| 13 | Volume of pentagonal prism | $V_{p} = \dfrac{5a^{2}}{4}\cot(36°) \cdot h$ | mm³ | Base area × height |
| 14 | Volume of hexagonal prism | $V_{h} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}\,a^{2} \cdot h$ | mm³ | Base area × height |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Prisms:**
> Pentagonal and hexagonal prisms are the **backbone of mechanical fastening** (hex nuts, hex bolts, pencil lead casings), **civil construction** (hexagonal columns in classical architecture, pentagonal fortification towers), **optical engineering** (hexagonal lens arrays), and **crystallography** (hexagonal close-packed unit cells). The projection skills you learn here are directly used in **sheet metal drafting, CNC tool-path design, and 3D CAD modelling** (SolidWorks, AutoCAD, CATIA).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Apothem for a Regular Pentagon

The regular pentagon is divided into 5 isosceles triangles from its center. Each triangle has a vertex angle of $72°$, and the perpendicular from the center bisects this into two right triangles, each with angle $36°$ at the center.

Let $a$ be the side length and $a_{p}$ be the apothem. Then:

$$
\tan(36°) = \dfrac{a/2}{a_{p}}
$$

Solving for $a_{p}$:

$$
a_{p} = \dfrac{a/2}{\tan(36°)} = \dfrac{a}{2}\cot(36°)
$$

Substituting the numerical value $\cot(36°) \approx 1.3764$:

$$
a_{p} \approx 0.6882 \, a
$$

For KTU standard problem with $a = 30$ mm:

$$
a_{p} = \dfrac{30}{2} \cdot \cot(36°) = 15 \times 1.3764 = 20.65 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Logic Note:** The apothem is the perpendicular distance from the center of the regular polygon to the midpoint of any side. In a regular pentagon, it is needed to find the *shortest* distance from center to edge (used in volume) versus the *longest* distance to vertex (used in diagonal computations).

### 3.2 Derivation of Apothem for a Regular Hexagon

The regular hexagon is divided into 6 equilateral triangles. The apothem is the height of one such triangle.

For an equilateral triangle of side $a$:

$$
\text{height} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \, a
$$

Therefore:

$$
a_{h} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \, a
$$

For KTU standard problem with $a = 30$ mm:

$$
a_{h} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 30 = 15\sqrt{3} \approx 25.98 \text{ mm}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Apparent Height in the Inclined Position

When the pentagonal/hexagonal prism rests on a base edge on HP with the base inclined at angle $\theta$ to HP, the front view is a rectangle whose height is *not* the true height `h`. Instead, the top corners of the prism are projected from the inclined top view.

Let:
- `h` = true height of the prism (axis length)
- $\theta$ = angle of inclination of the base to HP = **30°**
- $a_{p}$ or $a_{h}$ = apothem of base
- $d$ = perpendicular distance from the resting edge to the *opposite* edge (this is the apothem for a pentagon/hexagon resting on an edge)

The vertical rise of the opposite edge above HP is:

$$
\Delta h = d \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

For a pentagon resting on one of its edges, the apothem from that edge to the opposite vertex (which lies in the center plane) is $a_{p}$. So:

$$
\Delta h_{p} = a_{p} \cdot \sin(\theta) = 20.65 \times \sin(30°) = 20.65 \times 0.5 = 10.325 \text{ mm}
$$

For a hexagon resting on one of its outer edges, the apothem from the center of that edge to the parallel opposite edge is also $a_{h}$:

$$
