\Delta h_{h} = a_{h} \cdot \sin(\theta) = 25.98 \times \sin(30°) = 25.98 \times 0.5 = 12.99 \text{ mm}
$$

The total apparent height of the prism in the final Front View is then:

$$
h_{\text{FV}} = h + \Delta h = h + a_{p} \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

For the pentagonal prism with $h = 60$ mm:

$$
h_{\text{FV}} = 60 + 10.325 = 70.325 \text{ mm}
$$

For the hexagonal prism with $h = 60$ mm:

$$
h_{\text{FV}} = 60 + 12.99 = 72.99 \text{ mm}
$$

### 3.4 The Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Construction Procedure

The following matrix shows the **complete KTU procedure** for drawing the projections of a hexagonal prism resting on a base edge on HP with the base inclined at 30° to HP and the axis perpendicular to VP.

| Step No. | Action | What you draw | Key value/line |
|----------|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **1** | Draw reference line XY. | Horizontal reference line | XY line at the top of FV |
| **2** | Draw initial TV: a regular hexagon. | Hexagon with all 6 sides of 30 mm | Side $a = 30$ mm |
| **3** | Label the hexagon vertices. | Mark `a, b, c, d, e, f` going around | Center `o` is mid-point |
| **4** | Project initial FV from TV. | Rectangle of width = long diagonal = $2a = 60$ mm, height = $h = 60$ mm | Base on XY line |
| **5** | Project initial SV from TV and FV. | Rectangle of width = $a\sqrt{3} \approx 51.96$ mm, height = $h = 60$ mm | All views in Stage 1 |
| **6** | Select the resting edge, e.g., `a-b`. | The edge must be on the perimeter of the hexagon | Edge length 30 mm |
| **7** | Redraw the hexagon with `a-b` on the XY line, tilted at 30°. | New TV with base inclined at 30° to XY | Use compass / protractor |
| **8** | Project new FV from new TV. | New rectangle with top edge inclined | Project vertically upward |
| **9** | Project new SV from new TV and FV. | New side view showing the prism tilted | Axis appears inclined |
| **10** | Add dimensions, title block, and section hatching if asked. | Dimension lines and notes | Final answer |

### 3.5 Derivation of the Pentagon Diagonal using the Golden Ratio

The diagonal-to-side ratio of a regular pentagon is famously the **Golden Ratio** $\phi$:

$$
D_{p} = \phi \cdot a, \quad \text{where} \quad \phi = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2}
$$

Algebraic proof sketch: Let the diagonal be $D$ and side be $a$. The intersecting diagonals form a smaller regular pentagon with side $D - a$. By similar triangles:

$$
\dfrac{D}{a} = \dfrac{a}{D - a}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
D(D - a) = a^{2}
$$

Expanding:

$$
D^{2} - aD - a^{2} = 0
$$

Solving the quadratic in $D$:

$$
D = \dfrac{a \pm \sqrt{a^{2} + 4a^{2}}}{2} = \dfrac{a(1 \pm \sqrt{5})}{2}
$$

Taking the positive root:

$$
D = a \cdot \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} = a \cdot \phi \approx 1.618 \, a
$$

> **Logic Note:** This is the same ratio that appears in the **Parthenon, the Great Pyramids, sunflower seed patterns, and modern stock-market Fibonacci retracements** — a beautiful example of how a simple geometry problem connects to deep mathematical truth. KTU examiners love to set a 3-mark Part-A question asking "Define the golden ratio in the context of a pentagonal prism."

### 3.6 KTU Numerical: Volume of a Hexagonal Prism

Given: $a = 30$ mm, $h = 60$ mm.

Base area of regular hexagon:

$$
A_{\text{base}} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \, a^{2}
$$

Substituting $a = 30$:

$$
A_{\text{base}} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 30^{2} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 900 = 1350\sqrt{3} \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
A_{\text{base}} \approx 1350 \times 1.732 = 2338.21 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

Volume:

$$
V = A_{\text{base}} \cdot h = 2338.21 \times 60 = 140,292.6 \text{ mm}^{3} \approx 140.29 \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

### 3.7 KTU Numerical: Volume of a Pentagonal Prism

Given: $a = 30$ mm, $h = 60$ mm.

Base area of regular pentagon:

$$
A_{\text{base}} = \dfrac{5a^{2}}{4} \cot(36°)
$$

Substituting $a = 30$:

$$
A_{\text{base}} = \dfrac{5 \times 900}{4} \times 1.3764 = 1125 \times 1.3764 = 1548.45 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

Volume:

$$
V = A_{\text{base}} \cdot h = 1548.45 \times 60 = 92,907 \text{ mm}^{3} \approx 92.91 \text{ cm}^{3}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to the student:** Engineering Graphics requires precise physical drawings (top view, front view, side view with proper dimensioning, hidden lines, and sectioning). Since Mermaid cannot render such orthographic projections, the diagram below presents the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that maps the *decision logic* of constructing these projections step by step. Use this as a mental checklist while you draw on your answer sheet.

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Construction Logic for Pentagonal / Hexagonal Prism Projections

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read KTU Problem Statement] --> B{Identify Solid Type}
    B -- Pentagonal --> C[Set side a = 30 mm and height h = 60 mm]
    B -- Hexagonal --> D[Set side a = 30 mm and height h = 60 mm]
    C --> E[Draw Stage 1: True Shape Top View on XY]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Mark Center O and Label Vertices a b c d e f]
    F --> G[Project Stage 1 Front View: Rectangle Width 2a Height h]
    G --> H[Project Stage 1 Side View: Rectangle Width a sqrt 3 Height h]
    H --> I{Identify Resting Edge from Problem}
    I -- Edge ab --> J[Redraw Top View Tilted at 30 Degrees]
    I -- Other Edge --> J
    J --> K[Keep Resting Edge on XY Line]
    K --> L[Project New Front View Vertically Upward]
    L --> M[Project New Side View Horizontally]
    M --> N[Add Hidden Lines for Back Edges]
    N --> O[Add Visible Lines for Front Edges]
    O --> P[Dimension All Views with mm Units]
    P --> Q[Write Title Block and Student Details]
    Q --> R[End: Final KTU Answer Sheet Ready]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Component Identification of a Hexagonal Prism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Faces
        F1[Top Hexagonal Base]
        F2[Bottom Hexagonal Base]
        F3[Lateral Face 1]
        F4[Lateral Face 2]
        F5[Lateral Face 3]
        F6[Lateral Face 4]
        F7[Lateral Face 5]
        F8[Lateral Face 6]
    end
    subgraph Vertices
        V1[Vertex A]
        V2[Vertex B]
        V3[Vertex C]
        V4[Vertex D]
        V5[Vertex E]
        V6[Vertex F]
        V7[Vertex A Prime]
        V8[Vertex B Prime]
        V9[Vertex C Prime]
        V10[Vertex D Prime]
        V11[Vertex E Prime]
        V12[Vertex F Prime]
    end
    subgraph KeyDimensions
        K1[Side a = 30 mm]
        K2[Apothem ap = 25.98 mm]
        K3[Long Diagonal D = 60 mm]
        K4[Height h = 60 mm]
    end
    K1 --> F1
    K2 --> F1
    K3 --> F1
    K4 --> Faces
```

### 4.3 Visual Summary of Stage 1 vs Stage 2 (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitialPosition
    InitialPosition: Stage 1 — Base on HP, Axis Vertical
    InitialPosition --> InclinedPosition
    InclinedPosition: Stage 2 — Resting on Base Edge, Base Inclined 30 deg
    InclinedPosition --> ProjectedTV
    ProjectedTV: Tilted Top View in Stage 2
    ProjectedTV --> ProjectedFV
    ProjectedFV: Trapezoidal / Inclined Rectangle Front View
    ProjectedFV --> ProjectedSV
    ProjectedSV: Side View with Tilted Axis
    ProjectedSV --> FinalAnswer
    FinalAnswer: KTU Answer with Dimensions and Hidden Lines
    FinalAnswer --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drawing Tip from KTU Toppers:** Always draw the **Stage 1 projections in faint/thin lines** (construction lines). Draw the **Stage 2 (final) projections in dark/thick continuous lines** as per BIS conventions. **Hidden edges** (those behind the visible faces) must be drawn as **short-dashed lines**, not as continuous lines. This single distinction carries **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define a prism. How is a regular pentagonal prism different from a regular hexagonal prism in terms of number of faces, edges, and vertices?**

**Model Answer:**

A **prism** is a polyhedron with two parallel and congruent polygonal bases joined by rectangular lateral faces, generated by translating a base polygon parallel to itself along a straight axis perpendicular to the base.

| Feature | Pentagonal Prism | Hexagonal Prism |
|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| Number of Faces | **7** (2 pentagons + 5 rectangles) | **8** (2 hexagons + 6 rectangles) |
| Number of Edges | **15** (5 + 5 + 5) | **18** (6 + 6 + 6) |
| Number of Vertices | **10** (5 + 5) | **12** (6 + 6) |

Euler's formula check: $V - E + F = 2$ in both cases. For the pentagonal prism: $10 - 15 + 7 = 2$ ✓. For the hexagonal prism: $12 - 18 + 8 = 2$ ✓.

> **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **State the formula for the volume and total surface area of a regular hexagonal prism of side `a` and height `h`.**

**Model Answer:**

**Volume:** The base is a regular hexagon of area $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}a^{2}$, so:

$$
V = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \, a^{2} \, h
$$

**Total Surface Area:** Lateral area (6 rectangles of size $a \times h$) + 2 hexagonal bases:

$$
A_{\text{TSA}} = 6ah + 2 \times \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}a^{2} = 6ah + 3\sqrt{3}\,a^{2}
$$

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`
> A pentagonal prism of side of base **30 mm** and height of axis **60 mm** rests on one of its base edges on the HP such that the base is inclined at **30°** to the HP. The axis is perpendicular to the VP. Draw the **Top View, Front View, and Side View** using first-angle projection.

#### Sub-question (a) — 7 Marks: Draw the Top View and Front View in both initial and final positions.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Draw the XY reference line** horizontally at the top of the drawing area.
2. **Stage 1 – Initial Position (Base on HP, Axis vertical):**
   - Draw a **regular pentagon** of side 30 mm below the XY line. This is the Top View (true shape).
   - Label the vertices as `a, b, c, d, e`, and the center as `o`.
   - Compute the apothem: $a_{p} = 15 \cot(36°) \approx 20.65$ mm. This is the distance from `o` to the midpoint of edge `ab`.
   - **Project the Front View** above the XY line as a rectangle: width = distance from `b` to `e` in the TV (i.e., long diagonal = $a \cdot \phi = 30 \times 1.618 = 48.54$ mm), height = 60 mm. The base edge lies on the XY line.
3. **Stage 2 – Final Position (Resting on edge `ab`, base inclined at 30° to HP):**
   - Redraw the pentagon such that edge `ab` lies on the XY line, and the rest of the pentagon is inclined at 30° to XY (i.e., rotate the pentagon around edge `ab` by 30° into HP).
   - Use a protractor/compass: draw a line from the midpoint of `ab` at angle 30° to XY, and locate the new positions of vertices `c, d, e` such that the pentagon's geometry is preserved.
   - **Project the new Front View** vertically from the new TV: it is a rectangle whose base coincides with the inclined top edge (projected from `c-d-e`) and whose top edge is the inclined version of the top base.
   - The vertical rise of vertex `e` (or whichever vertex is farthest from `ab`) is $20.65 \times \sin(30°) = 10.325$ mm.
4. **Hidden Lines:** Draw the edges of the back faces (`c-d` and `d-e` if `ab` is in front) as short-dashed hidden lines.

#### Sub-question (b) — 7 Marks: Draw the Side View and dimension both stages.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

5. **Initial Side View (Stage 1):** Project horizontally to the right of the Front View. The width is the apothem-related distance, and height is 60 mm. For a pentagon with one edge `ab` horizontal, the side view width is $a_{p} \times 2 = 41.3$ mm. Draw as a rectangle.
6. **Final Side View (Stage 2):** Project the new TV horizontally to the right of the new FV. The side view shows the prism tilted, with the axis inclined at 30° to the vertical.
7. **Dimensioning:** Use BIS conventions. Show:
   - Side of base = 30 mm (in TV).
   - Height of axis = 60 mm (in FV).
   - Angle of inclination = 30° (in FV).
   - Apothem = 20.65 mm (in TV).
8. **Title Block:** Course code (GCEST203), problem title, name, roll number, scale, date.

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]`
> A hexagonal prism of side of base **30 mm** and height **55 mm** rests on one of its rectangular faces on the HP with the axis inclined at **45°** to the HP. The axis is parallel to the VP. Draw the **Top View, Front View, and Side View** using first-angle projection. Also calculate the **total surface area** of the prism.

#### Sub-question (a) — 7 Marks: Orthographic projections.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Stage 1 (Resting on hexagonal base on HP, axis vertical):**
   - Draw a regular hexagon of side 30 mm in the TV. Label vertices `a, b, c, d, e, f`.
   - Apothem $a_{h} = 15\sqrt{3} \approx 25.98$ mm.
   - Long diagonal $D = 2a = 60$ mm.
   - Project FV as a rectangle of width 60 mm, height 55 mm.
   - Project SV as a rectangle of width $a\sqrt{3} \approx 51.96$ mm, height 55 mm.

2. **Stage 2 (Resting on rectangular face on HP, axis inclined at 45° to HP):**
   - Identify the rectangular face on which the prism rests. Suppose it is the face between edges `ab` (bottom) and `a'b'` (top).
   - In the new TV, the width of this face is 30 mm (side of hexagon), and the apparent length is 55 mm.
   - Tilt the TV such that the rectangular face lies on the XY line and the rest of the hexagon is rotated by 45° around this face.
   - The new FV will have the axis inclined at 45° to the XY line.

3. **Final Side View:** Project horizontally. The axis appears at 45° inclination in the SV.

#### Sub-question (b) — 7 Marks: Total Surface Area calculation.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Lateral surface area (6 rectangular faces):

$$
A_{\text{lat}} = 6 \times a \times h = 6 \times 30 \times 55 = 9,900 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

Two hexagonal base areas:

$$
A_{\text{base}} = 2 \times \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times a^{2} = 3\sqrt{3} \times 30^{2} = 2700\sqrt{3} \approx 4676.5 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

Total surface area:

$$
A_{\text{TSA}} = A_{\text{lat}} + A_{\text{base}} = 9,900 + 4,676.5 = 14,576.5 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

Converting:

$$
A_{\text{TSA}} \approx 145.77 \text{ cm}^{2}
$$

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones (Examiner's Eye View):**
> 1. **Missing Hidden Lines (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often forget to draw the back edges as short-dashed lines. In a 14-mark question, missing hidden lines can cost you nearly a full sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing First vs Third Angle Projection (–2 marks):** KTU strictly uses **First Angle Projection**. In first angle, the **Top View is below the XY line** and the **Side View (from the left) is to the right of the Front View**. Reversing this convention will lose marks immediately.
> 3. **Not Redrawing Stage 1 in Faint Lines (–1 mark):** The Stage 1 projections are *construction* lines, not the final answer. Drawing them in heavy lines makes the diagram look messy and confuses the examiner.
> 4. **Wrong Angle of Inclination in the Final TV (–2 marks):** The 30° angle must be measured from the XY line to the line joining the resting edge's midpoint to the opposite vertex. Many students measure it from the wrong reference and end up with a totally different shape.
> 5. **Omitting the XY Reference Line in the Side View (–1 mark):** The XY line is the **focal point** of the entire projection. Every view must reference it.
> 6. **Skipping Dimensioning (–1 to –2 marks):** Even if your drawing is correct, missing dimensions (side, height, angle) loses you easy marks.
> 7. **Calculation Errors in Volume/TSA (–1 mark per wrong sub-part):** Always double-check your apothem and golden ratio computations.

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- A **pentagonal prism** has **7 faces, 15 edges, 10 vertices**; a **hexagonal prism** has **8 faces, 18 edges, 12 vertices**.
- Always verify with **Euler's formula**: $V - E + F = 2$.
- KTU standard problem data: **side = 30 mm**, **height = 50–65 mm**, **inclination = 30°** to HP (when resting on a base edge).
- Use **First Angle Projection** (BIS IS 696). TV below XY, FV above XY, Left SV on the right side of FV.
- The **apothem** of a regular pentagon is $\dfrac{a}{2}\cot(36°) \approx 0.688a$.
- The **apothem** of a regular hexagon is $\dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866a$.
- The **golden ratio** $\phi = \dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ governs pentagon diagonals.
- For a hexagon, the apothem equals the circumradius.
- **Stage 1** must be drawn in faint construction lines; **Stage 2** in dark, thick continuous lines.
- **Hidden edges** = short-dashed lines; **Visible edges** = thick continuous lines; **Construction lines** = thin faint lines; **Center lines** = long-dash short-dash pattern.
- When the base is inclined at 30°, the **vertical rise** of the farthest edge = apothem × $\sin(30°)$ = apothem × 0.5.
- **Total Surface Area** of a hexagonal prism = $6ah + 3\sqrt{3}\,a^{2}$.
- **Volume** of a hexagonal prism = $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}\,a^{2}\,h$.
- **Volume** of a pentagonal prism = $\dfrac{5a^{2}}{4}\cot(36°) \cdot h$.
- In a hexagon, the **long diagonal = 2a** (passes through center).
- In a pentagon, the **long diagonal = $\phi a$** and does **not** pass through center in a simple way.
- Always include a **title block** with course code, problem number, name, scale, and date.
- Practical applications: **hex nuts, hex bolts, hexagonal pencils, optical lens arrays, fortification columns, pencil lead casings.**
- For a 14-mark question, the **standard split is 7 + 7 marks**, typically one sub-question on the drawing and one on a calculation.
- Examiner's biggest pet peeve: **Missing hidden lines** and **wrong projection angle convention**. Avoid these and you will score well.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pyramids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pyramids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **PYRAMID (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *pyramid* is a polyhedron formed by joining a polygonal base (regular or irregular) to an **apex** (a single point not lying in the plane of the base) using straight line segments called **lateral edges**. The lateral surface consists of triangular faces (one for each side of the base). The line joining the apex to the centre of the base is called the **axis** of the pyramid.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Engineering Graphics syllabus, pyramids are studied as a category of **right regular solids** whose orthographic projections (Front View – FV, Top View – TV, and Side View) and **auxiliary projections** must be constructed using the principles of *First-Angle Projection* and *Projection of Solids on Principal Planes*.

## 1.2 Component Terminology (Mandatory Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Base** | The polygonal face on which the pyramid rests (triangle, square, pentagon, hexagon…). |
| **Apex / Vertex** | The single top point (denoted `o` or `v`) where all lateral edges meet. |
| **Axis** | The imaginary line joining the apex to the geometric centre of the base. |
| **Lateral Edges** | Straight lines joining the apex to each corner of the base (e.g., `oa, ob, oc…`). |
| **Lateral Faces** | Triangular faces formed by two adjacent lateral edges and a side of the base. |
| **Slant Edge** | The true length of a lateral edge (in its inclined position). |
| **Slant Height** | The true length of the line on a lateral face drawn from the apex perpendicular to a base side. |
| **Right Pyramid** | Axis is perpendicular to the base. |
| **Regular Pyramid** | A right pyramid whose base is a regular polygon. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Tent and the Monument**
> Think of a **campaign tent pitched in a desert**: the ground forms a square or rectangular *base*, and the cloth rises to a single pole at the top — that pole-cap is the *apex*. The ropes that hold the tent in place represent the *lateral edges*, and the triangular flaps of cloth are the *lateral faces*. Now scale this image up to the size of the **Great Pyramid of Giza** — the geometric logic is identical; only the precision of the base polygon changes. In KTU problems, the "tent" is usually a clean geometric solid (square, pentagonal, hexagonal base) that we must draw on paper using the language of orthographic projection.

## 1.4 Classification Used in KTU Board Exams

1. **Triangular Pyramid (Tetrahedron)** — 3-sided base.
2. **Square Pyramid** — 4-sided base.
3. **Pentagonal Pyramid** — 5-sided base.
4. **Hexagonal Pyramid** — 6-sided base (the most frequently asked in KTU).
5. **Frustum of a Pyramid** — The portion remaining after the top is cut parallel to the base (a frequent follow-up topic in Module 2).

The base diameter / side length and the **height (axis length, `h`)** are the two standard inputs KTU provides in a 14-mark problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Geometry of a Regular Right Pyramid

A **right regular pyramid** is the standard KTU assumption. Let the base be a regular polygon of `n` sides, each of side length `a`. Let the axis (height) be `h`.

### 2.1.1 Construction Logic (Why each step works)

1. The base is drawn as a regular polygon because the lateral edges must be **equal in length** — this symmetry is what makes the solid *regular*.
2. The axis is drawn **perpendicular** to the base. This means in the Top View (which is the view looking down the axis), the apex projects at the **geometric centre** of the base polygon.
3. In the Front View, the base appears as a straight line (because the base is parallel to the Horizontal Plane, HP), and the apex appears at a perpendicular distance `h` above this line.

## 2.2 Standard Resting Positions in KTU Problems

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** A solid in a projection problem is always assumed to be in the *First Quadrant* of the reference planes, resting on HP (with the base on HP) unless the problem specifies otherwise.

| Resting Position | Top View (TV) | Front View (FV) | When Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Base on HP, Axis vertical** | True-shape regular polygon (apex at centre) | Base as a straight line; apex at height `h` above | Default (most KTU problems) |
| **Base on HP, Axis inclined to HP** | Polygon is foreshortened; apex not at centre | Slant lines showing tilt | When axis tilt angle `θ` is given |
| **Lateral face on HP** | Triangle visible; apex on reference line `XY` | Two slant edges; axis inclined to VP | Section-plane problems |
| **Base on VP** | Base as line; apex on `XY` | True-shape polygon | Auxiliary-view problems |

## 2.3 Slant Edge and Slant Height — The Two Key True-Length Lines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slant Edge (`L`) and Slant Height (`l`)** are the only true-length lines that must be located for almost every KTU pyramid problem. Knowing their value unlocks **truncation**, **sectioning**, and **development** questions.

Let:
- `a` = side length of the base polygon
- `n` = number of sides
- `R` = circumradius of the base (distance from centre to a vertex)
- `r` = apothem of the base (distance from centre to the midpoint of a side)
- `h` = axis / height of the pyramid

Then the relationships are:

$$
R = \frac{a}{2 \sin\!\left(\dfrac{180^\circ}{n}\right)}
$$

$$
r = \frac{a}{2 \tan\!\left(\dfrac{180^\circ}{n}\right)}
$$

$$
\text{Slant Edge } L = \sqrt{h^{2} + R^{2}}
$$

$$
\text{Slant Height } l = \sqrt{h^{2} + r^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas?** Each lateral face is an **isosceles triangle**. The slant edge is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are the axis `h` and the base-radius `R`. The slant height is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are the axis `h` and the apothem `r`.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Print-Friendly Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every** formula tested in KTU 2024 ESE Module 2 for pyramids. Memorise it before attempting any 14-mark numerical.

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit (SI) |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Base perimeter | `P` | $P = n \cdot a$ | mm |
| Apothem of base | `r` | $r = \dfrac{a}{2 \tan(180^\circ/n)}$ | mm |
| Circumradius of base | `R` | $R = \dfrac{a}{2 \sin(180^\circ/n)}$ | mm |
| Base area | `A_b` | $A_b = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot P \cdot r$ | mm² |
| Lateral surface area | `A_L` | $A_L = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot P \cdot l$ | mm² |
| Total surface area | `A_T` | $A_T = A_b + A_L$ | mm² |
| Slant edge | `L` | $L = \sqrt{h^{2} + R^{2}}$ | mm |
| Slant height | `l` | $l = \sqrt{h^{2} + r^{2}}$ | mm |
| Axis / Height | `h` | $h$ — given directly in KTU question | mm |
| Volume | `V` | $V = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot A_b \cdot h$ | mm³ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Architecture & Civil Engineering:** Pyramidal roofs, transmission towers, and cooling-tower crowns are essentially frustums of pyramids.
2. **Mechanical Engineering:** Hopper bottoms of storage silos and conical hoppers use the geometry of a truncated pyramid.
3. **Computer Graphics & CAD:** Every 3D mesh in software like *AutoCAD, SolidWorks, Blender* is internally stored as a collection of triangular facets — the same triangular faces of a pyramid.
4. **Surveying:** Volume of earthwork in pyramidal embankments is calculated using $V = \dfrac{1}{3} A_b h$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation of Slant Edge and Slant Height

The derivations below are the most-tested analytical transitions in KTU's 14-mark problems. Every step is shown explicitly.

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Slant Edge `L`

Consider a **right regular hexagonal pyramid** of side `a` and axis `h`, resting on HP with the base on HP.

**Step 1 — Locate the base geometry.**
For a regular hexagon, the central angle subtended at the centre by one side is $360^\circ/6 = 60^\circ$.

**Step 2 — Compute the circumradius `R`.**
Join the centre `O` of the hexagon to a corner, say `A`. The triangle `OAB` is isosceles with apex angle $60^\circ$, hence equilateral. Therefore:

$$
R = a
$$

**Step 3 — Form the right triangle.**
The lateral edge `OA` is the slant edge `L`. In the right triangle formed by the axis (vertical), the circumradius (horizontal, in TV), and the slant edge (hypotenuse):

$$
L^{2} = h^{2} + R^{2}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad L = \sqrt{h^{2} + R^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU-style).**
Let `a = 30 mm`, `h = 60 mm`. For a hexagonal pyramid:

$$
L = \sqrt{60^{2} + 30^{2}} = \sqrt{3600 + 900} = \sqrt{4500} \approx 67.08 \text{ mm}
$$

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Slant Height `l`

**Step 1 — Compute the apothem `r` of the hexagon.**
For a regular hexagon, the apothem equals the circumradius times $\cos(30^\circ)$:

$$
r = R \cdot \cos(30^\circ) = a \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the right triangle.**
The slant height is the perpendicular distance from the apex to the midpoint of a base edge. The right triangle has legs `h` (vertical) and `r` (horizontal) with hypotenuse `l`:

$$
l^{2} = h^{2} + r^{2}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad l = \sqrt{h^{2} + r^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example (KTU-style).**
Using `a = 30 mm`, `h = 60 mm`:

$$
r = 30 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 15\sqrt{3} \approx 25.98 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
l = \sqrt{60^{2} + (15\sqrt{3})^{2}} = \sqrt{3600 + 675} = \sqrt{4275} \approx 65.38 \text{ mm}
$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of True Length using Rotation Method

When the pyramid's axis is **tilted** to HP, the lateral edge in the front view appears *foreshortened*. The true length is found by the **rotation method**:

**Step 1** — In TV, keep one end of the edge fixed (say the centre `o`) and rotate the other end to make the line parallel to `XY`.

**Step 2** — Project the rotated point vertically up to the FV. The new line in the FV now equals the true length of the edge.

The mathematical justification: rotation preserves the length of a line because every point on the line traces a circle of constant radius around the fixed end.

## 3.2 Procedural Algorithm — Projection of a Right Regular Pyramid (Base on HP, Axis Vertical)

This is the **default KTU procedure** worth 14 marks. The table below is the examiner's grading matrix — every row carries weight.

| Step | Action | Marks Weight (KTU) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Assume unit polygon side. Construct the regular polygon in the Top View (true shape). | 2 |
| 2 | Mark the centre `o` of the polygon. Locate the apex projection `o'` coincident with `o`. | 1 |
| 3 | Name all base corners `a, b, c, d, e, f` anticlockwise from the front-most corner. | 1 |
| 4 | Draw the `XY` reference line below the TV by a vertical distance equal to the axis height `h`. | 1 |
| 5 | Project the base corners vertically up to the `XY` line to form the base-line in the FV. | 1 |
| 6 | Mark the apex `o₁'` at a height `h` above the midpoint of the base-line. | 1 |
| 7 | Join the apex `o₁'` to all base-corner points in the FV with slant lines (these are the **visible lateral edges**). | 2 |
| 8 | Show the **axis** as a vertical centre-line with `+ +` marks for centre line convention. | 1 |
| 9 | Add the **hidden** edges (dashed) — for a hexagonal/square pyramid, the edges behind the visible ones are drawn as short dashed lines. | 2 |
| 10 | Dimension the drawing (base side `a`, axis `h`) and add the title block. | 2 |
| **Total** | | **14** |

## 3.3 Case Analysis — Tilting the Pyramid's Axis

When a KTU problem asks *"the pyramid is tilted such that its axis makes 30° with HP"*, the procedure is:

1. **Draw the base in TV** as before (true shape, since the base remains on HP).
2. **Draw the initial FV** with axis vertical and apex at height `h`.
3. **In the FV**, rotate the entire pyramid about a point on the base (usually the midpoint of a side) until the axis makes the required angle `θ` with `XY`. The new apex position is `o'₂`.
4. **Re-project the new apex position** vertically down to the TV. The new TV of the apex is no longer at the centre of the polygon — it is shifted.
5. **Join the new TV apex to every base corner** in the TV to obtain the new top view.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to *re-project* the apex back to the TV after tilting. This costs 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question.

## 3.4 Section of a Pyramid — Finding the True Shape

A **section plane** cuts the pyramid producing a polygon. To find the **true shape** of the section:

1. Mark where the section plane intersects each lateral edge in the FV (call these `1', 2', 3'…`).
2. Project these intersection points vertically down to the corresponding edges in the TV (`1, 2, 3…`).
3. The polygon `1–2–3…` in the TV is the *apparent* section.
4. To find the **true shape**, draw an auxiliary plane parallel to the section plane. Project the section points onto a new view taken on this auxiliary plane. The resulting polygon is the true shape.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are rendered using **Mermaid** block-flow syntax. They map the *workflow logic* of pyramid projection — a method adopted in modern KTU smart-evaluation rubrics where the process steps are awarded partial marks even if the final drawing has a minor inaccuracy.

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Workflow for Projecting a Right Regular Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: KTU Question] --> B[Read inputs: base side a, height h, base type n-gon]
    B --> C[Compute R and r using sine and tangent formulas]
    C --> D[Draw regular n-gon as Top View on HP]
    D --> E[Mark centre o of polygon]
    E --> F[Place TV apex coincident with centre o]
    F --> G[Project base corners to XY line for Front View]
    G --> H[Locate apex o-prime at height h above base line]
    H --> I[Join o-prime to all base corner projections in FV]
    I --> J[Add axis as vertical centre line with tick marks]
    J --> K[Identify and draw hidden edges as dashed lines]
    K --> L[Dimension: mark a, h, add title block]
    L --> M[Final Projection Drawing Complete]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Decision Flow — Choosing the True-Length Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Need True Length of Slant Edge] --> Q{Is the axis vertical?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Use L = sqrt of h squared plus R squared directly]
    Q -- No --> S{Axis inclined or pyramid tilted?}
    S -- Axis inclined only --> T[Use Rotation Method in TV: rotate point to XY parallel]
    S -- Pyramid tilted on base --> U[Use Auxiliary Inclined Plane Method]
    T --> V[Project rotated point to FV to obtain true length]
    U --> V
    R --> W[True Length Obtained]
    V --> W
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Section-Plane Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Section Plane Cuts Pyramid] --> INT[Intersection Points on Lateral Edges]
    INT --> FV[Mark in Front View]
    FV --> PROJ[Project Vertically Down to Top View]
    PROJ --> APP[Apparent Section in TV]
    APP --> AUX[Auxiliary Plane Parallel to Section Plane]
    AUX --> TRUE[True Shape of Section]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Layout of a Hexagonal Pyramid (Top View + Front View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TV[Top View on HP]
        direction TB
        HEX[Regular Hexagon: corners a b c d e f around centre o]
    end
    subgraph FV[Front View above XY]
        direction TB
        BASE[Base line a-prime b-prime coincident with XY]
        APEX[Apex o-prime at height h above base line]
        EDGES[Slant edges o-prime-a-prime, o-prime-b-prime, o-prime-c-prime, etc.]
        AXIS[Vertical axis line o to o-prime with centre-line marks]
    end
    TV -. projection lines .-> FV
```

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU answer sheet, the **Top View** is always placed *below* the `XY` reference line, and the **Front View** is placed *above* the `XY` line. The projection lines (vertical, thin, continuous) connect the base corners of both views. This convention is enforced strictly and is the first thing the examiner checks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern**

### Q1. Define a right regular pyramid. State the formulae for slant edge and slant height.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A pyramid is a polyhedron having a polygonal base and an apex. A **right regular pyramid** has its axis perpendicular to the base, and the base is a regular polygon.

For a base of `n` sides, side `a`, axis `h`:

- Slant edge $L = \sqrt{h^{2} + R^{2}}$ where $R$ is the circumradius.
- Slant height $l = \sqrt{h^{2} + r^{2}}$ where $r$ is the apothem.

(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two formulae.)

---

### Q2. A hexagonal pyramid of base side 25 mm and height 50 mm rests on HP with a side of the base on the ground. Name the views and state which lateral edges are visible in the front view.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Top View (TV):** True-shape regular hexagon, apex at centre.
- **Front View (FV):** Base shown as a single horizontal line (because the base is on HP and a side is parallel to VP). The two slant edges meeting at the front-most corners of the base are **visible**; the back ones are hidden (drawn dashed).
- The two **end corner lateral edges** are the only true-length slant edges in the FV; the others are foreshortened.

(1 mark for naming views, 1 mark for visible edge identification, 1 mark for true-length statement.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**A square pyramid of base side 30 mm and axis height 50 mm rests on HP on its base with all sides of the base equally inclined to VP. Draw the orthographic projections. Find the true length of the lateral edge using the rotation method.**

#### Part (a) — Orthographic Projections [7 marks]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Top View (TV):** Draw a square of side 30 mm with sides equally inclined to the vertical centre line (this gives all sides equally inclined to VP). Label the corners `a, b, c, d` from the front. Mark the centre `o` and place the apex `o'` coincident with `o`.
2. **Reference line XY:** Draw a horizontal line `XY` 50 mm below the TV.
3. **Front View (FV):** Project corners `a, b, c, d` vertically up to `XY`. They appear as a single line segment on `XY` because the base is in the HP plane. Mark the apex `o₁'` at 50 mm above the midpoint of this base line.
4. **Slant edges:** Join `o₁'` to `a₁', b₁', c₁', d₁'`. The two outer edges (`o₁'a₁'` and `o₁'d₁'`) are visible. The two inner edges (`o₁'b₁'` and `o₁'c₁'`) are hidden — draw them as dashed lines.
5. **Axis:** Draw a vertical centre line from `o` (TV) to `o₁'` (FV) with `+ +` convention marks.
6. **Dimensions:** Mark base side `30 mm` and axis `50 mm`. Add title block.

**Mark distribution (a):**
- TV construction: **2 marks**
- FV construction: **2 marks**
- Hidden/visible edge convention: **2 marks**
- Dimensioning and neatness: **1 mark**

#### Part (b) — True Length using Rotation Method [7 marks]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. In the TV, identify the lateral edge `oa` (or any one edge).
2. Keep point `o` (centre) fixed. Rotate point `a` about `o` in the TV until the line `oa` becomes **parallel to XY**. The rotated position of `a` is `a₁`.
3. Project `a₁` vertically up to the corresponding slant edge in the FV. The new line in the FV from `o₁'` to `a₁'` now has the **true length of the slant edge**.
4. Measure: $L = \sqrt{50^{2} + (15\sqrt{2})^{2}} = \sqrt{2500 + 450} = \sqrt{2950} \approx 54.13$ mm.
5. Write the value on the drawing with a label: `True Length = 54.13 mm`.

**Mark distribution (b):**
- Rotation step correctly drawn in TV: **3 marks**
- Re-projection to FV: **2 marks**
- Correct measurement / numerical label: **2 marks**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**A pentagonal pyramid of base side 30 mm and axis 60 mm rests on HP on its base, with one side of the base parallel to VP. It is cut by a section plane perpendicular to VP, inclined at 45° to HP, and passing through a point on the axis at 25 mm from the base. Draw the projections and find the true shape of the section.**

#### Part (a) — Initial Projections and Section Lines [7 marks]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **TV:** Draw a regular pentagon of side 30 mm with one side parallel to the horizontal (i.e., parallel to `XY`). Label corners `a, b, c, d, e`. Centre `o` carries the apex.
2. **FV:** Project corners up. The base appears as a horizontal line. Apex `o₁'` is 60 mm above the midpoint.
3. **Section plane:** In the FV, draw a line inclined at 45° to `XY` passing through a point 25 mm above the base on the axis. Mark its intersection with the five slant edges in the FV as `1', 2', 3', 4', 5'`.
4. **Project section points to TV:** Drop vertical lines from `1', 2', 3', 4', 5'` to find `1, 2, 3, 4, 5` on the corresponding slant edges in the TV. Join them to form the **apparent section** (a pentagon in the TV).
5. **Identify visible / hidden portions:** The portion of the pyramid above the section plane is removed. The lower portion is the frustum-with-slope.

**Mark distribution (a):** TV: 2, FV: 2, Section line: 2, Visible/hidden: 1.

#### Part (b) — True Shape of Section [7 marks]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. The section plane is inclined at 45° to HP. To see the true shape, we need an **auxiliary view** looking *perpendicular* to the section plane.
2. Draw an auxiliary reference line `X₁Y₁` parallel to the section plane (inclined at 45° to XY).
3. Project the section points `1', 2', 3', 4', 5'` from the FV *perpendicular* to `X₁Y₁` (i.e., along lines at 90° to the section plane).
4. Measure the distances of points `1, 2, 3, 4, 5` in the TV from the `XY` line and transfer them to the auxiliary view by setting off these heights from `X₁Y₁`.
5. Join the projected points in the auxiliary view. The resulting closed polygon is the **true shape** of the section (a regular pentagon, since the section is parallel to the base, distorted only by the perspective — here, the section is parallel to the base, so the true shape is in the auxiliary view).
6. Dimension and label as `True Shape of Section`.

> [!NOTE]
> If the section plane were **parallel to the base**, the apparent section in the TV would itself be the true shape, and no auxiliary view would be needed. The 45° tilt in this problem is what necessitates the auxiliary projection.

**Mark distribution (b):** Auxiliary line setup: 2, Projection: 2, True shape polygon: 2, Labelling: 1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the centre-line convention:** Drawing the axis as a normal continuous line instead of a chain-thin line with `+ + +` ticks costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Missing hidden lines:** When the pyramid is tilted, the back lateral edges must be drawn as short dashes. Missing them costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing slant edge with slant height:** A common 1-mark deduction. Remember: *slant edge* joins apex to a **vertex** of the base; *slant height* joins apex to the **midpoint of a base side**.
> 4. **Re-projection skip:** After tilting the pyramid, students draw the new FV but forget to redraw the TV by re-projecting the apex. This costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **Title block omission:** A drawing without `Name, Roll No, Scale, Date` loses **1 mark** outright.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise the night before the KTU exam.**

- A **pyramid** = polygonal base + apex joined by lateral edges.
- A **right regular pyramid** has its axis perpendicular to a **regular** base polygon.
- Default KTU position: **base on HP, axis vertical, sides equally inclined to VP** for square and hexagonal pyramids.
- Default pentagonal / triangular pyramids: **one base side on HP, parallel to VP**.
- Key quantities: base side `a`, axis `h`, circumradius `R`, apothem `r`, slant edge `L`, slant height `l`.
- **Critical formulas:**
  - $R = \dfrac{a}{2\sin(180^\circ/n)}$
  - $r = \dfrac{a}{2\tan(180^\circ/n)}$
  - $L = \sqrt{h^2 + R^2}$
  - $l = \sqrt{h^2 + r^2}$
- **Top View** is the true shape of the base; **Front View** shows the base as a line and the apex at height `h`.
- **Slant edges meeting at the front corners** of the base are **visible** in the FV; others are **hidden** (dashed).
- **Tilting the axis:** rotate the apex in the FV first, then re-project to TV.
- **Rotation method for true length:** keep one end fixed in TV, rotate the other end parallel to `XY`, then re-project to FV.
- **Section plane procedure:** mark intersection on each edge in FV → drop to TV → if section plane is inclined, use **auxiliary view** for the true shape.
- **Hidden lines** are short dashes; **centre lines** are chain-thin with `+ + +` ticks; **visible edges** are continuous thick lines.
- **Dimensioning is compulsory** — always show `a` and `h` on the projection.
- **Always include the title block** at the bottom-right corner of the sheet.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cone, Cylinder, and Tetrahedron. Projection of Solids in Simple Position Including Profile View. Projection of Solids with Axis Inclined to One of the Reference Planes and with Axis Inclined to Both Reference Planes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Solids of Elementary Geometry

In Engineering Graphics, the study of **solids** is fundamental to understanding how three-dimensional objects are represented on two-dimensional paper. The solids covered in this module are the three most commonly encountered primitive solids in engineering practice: the **Cone**, the **Cylinder**, and the **Tetrahedron**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> A **solid** is a three-dimensional geometric figure that occupies space and has length, breadth, and thickness. In engineering drawing, solids are represented by their *orthographic projections* on the three principal reference planes — the **Horizontal Plane (HP)**, the **Vertical Plane (VP)**, and the **Profile Plane (PP)**.

---

## 1.2 Definitions of the Three Primitives

### 1.2.1 The Cone

A **Cone** is a solid of revolution generated by rotating a right-angled triangle about one of its perpendicular sides. It has a circular base and tapers smoothly to a single point called the **apex**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology of a Cone:**
> - **Apex (A):** The pointed tip of the cone.
> - **Base (B):** The flat circular face of the cone.
> - **Axis:** The straight line joining the apex to the centre of the base. It is perpendicular to the base.
> - **Generator (Slant Edge):** The straight line from any point on the base perimeter to the apex.
> - **Base Radius (r):** The radius of the circular base.
> - **Height (h):** The perpendicular distance from the base to the apex.
> - **Slant Height (l):** The length of the generator; $l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$.

**Intuitive Analogy (Cone):**
Imagine a **party hat** 🎉 or an **ice-cream cone** 🍦. The pointed tip is the apex, the round opening is the base, and the slanted surface is generated by the straight line we call the generator. When we look at it from the side, we see a triangle; from the top, we see a circle.

### 1.2.2 The Cylinder

A **Cylinder** is a solid of revolution generated by translating a straight line (generator) parallel to a fixed line (axis) along a closed curve (the base). The cylinder has two parallel circular ends joined by a curved surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology of a Cylinder:**
> - **Top Base & Bottom Base:** Two parallel circular faces.
> - **Axis:** The straight line joining the centres of the two bases. It is perpendicular to both bases.
> - **Generator:** A straight line on the curved surface, parallel to the axis, joining corresponding points on the two base perimeters.
> - **Base Radius (r):** The radius of either base circle.
> - **Height (h):** The perpendicular distance between the two bases.

**Intuitive Analogy (Cylinder):**
Think of a **beverage can** 🥤, a **battery cell** 🔋, or a **water pipe**. When viewed from the side, you see a rectangle; from the top or bottom, you see a circle. All the slanting lines you might see are actually *vertical generators* in the side view.

### 1.2.3 The Tetrahedron

A **Tetrahedron** is the simplest of all regular polyhedra. It is a solid bounded by **four equilateral triangular faces**, having **four vertices** and **six equal edges**. It is also called a **triangular pyramid** whose base is also an equilateral triangle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology of a Tetrahedron (Regular):**
> - **Vertices:** 4 in number.
> - **Edges:** 6 in number, all of equal length ($a$).
> - **Faces:** 4 in number, all equilateral triangles.
> - **Apex (A):** The vertex opposite the base.
> - **Base (BCD):** An equilateral triangle.
> - **Axis:** The line joining the apex to the centroid of the base. It is perpendicular to the base.
> - **Height (h):** $h = a\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{3}}\cdot\sqrt{2}$.

**Intuitive Analogy (Tetrahedron):**
Picture a **four-sided die (d4)** 🎲 or a **pyramid of Egypt** but with a triangular base. Every face is a perfect triangle, and every edge is of equal length. It is the *3D version of an equilateral triangle*.

---

## 1.3 Concept of "Position" of a Solid Relative to Reference Planes

The "position" of a solid in the First-Angle Projection system is defined by the **orientation of its axis** with respect to the three principal reference planes — **HP, VP, and PP**.

| Position Type | Description | KTU 2024 Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Position** | Axis perpendicular to **one** reference plane and parallel to the other two. | Foundation level. |
| **Inclined to One Plane** | Axis inclined to **one** reference plane and perpendicular to the other. | Intermediate level. |
| **Inclined to Both Planes** | Axis inclined to **both** HP and VP (true inclined position). | Advanced / Part B level. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Profile View (PV or Side View):** This is the orthographic projection obtained on the **Profile Plane (PP)**, which is the plane perpendicular to both HP and VP. The profile view reveals the depth of the object — its thickness measured perpendicular to both front and top views. In first-angle projection, the profile view is placed on the **right-hand side** of the front view.

**Intuitive Analogy (Reference Planes & Profile View):**
Imagine the object is sitting on a glass table (HP) facing a glass wall (VP). You can see:
- The **Front View** through the wall (you see the wall view).
- The **Top View** through the table from above (you see the table view).
- The **Profile View** from the side — like watching a person walk past you sideways; you see only the *thickness* and *height* combined.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Principles of Orthographic Projection of Solids

The procedure for projecting any solid follows a rigorous three-step logic established by KTU Board patterns.

### 2.1.1 The Three Foundational Steps (Universal Procedure)

**Step 1 — Assume the Solid is Enclosed in a Bounding Box (Imaginary Rectangular Box).**
The solid is imagined to be placed inside an invisible rectangular box whose faces are parallel to the reference planes. The corners of this box help in defining the outer extents of the projection.

**Step 2 — Identify Key Reference Points (Generators, Corners, Apex, Centre).**
- For **Cone & Cylinder**: identify the apex, the centre of the base, the two extreme generators (Left Generator & Right Generator — denoted **LG** and **RG**), and the front & back points of the base circle (**F** and **B**).
- For **Tetrahedron**: identify all four vertices (A, B, C, D) and the centroid of the base triangle.

**Step 3 — Project Each Key Point Perpendicularly onto the Reference Planes.**
Each point is projected vertically downward to obtain the Top View (on HP) and horizontally toward the viewer to obtain the Front View (on VP). Profile points are projected sideways onto the PP.

---

## 2.2 Projection in Simple Position

In **Simple Position**, the axis is perpendicular to **one** reference plane and parallel to the other two.

### 2.2.1 Cone in Simple Position (Axis Perpendicular to HP — Cone Resting on Base)

- **Front View (FV):** An isosceles triangle of base = $2r$ and height = $h$.
- **Top View (TV):** A circle of radius $r$, with the centre labelled as the axis point.
- **Profile View (PV):** An isosceles triangle identical to the Front View but with its base showing the *depth* (which equals the diameter of the base, $2r$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** When the axis is vertical, the *true shape* of the base (circle) appears in the Top View. The front and profile views appear as triangles of the same dimensions. The generator length shown in FV and PV is the **True Length (TL)** of the generator, equal to $\sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$.

### 2.2.2 Cylinder in Simple Position (Axis Perpendicular to HP — Cylinder Resting on Base)

- **Front View (FV):** A rectangle of width $2r$ and height $h$.
- **Top View (TV):** A circle of radius $r$ (the true shape of the top base).
- **Profile View (PV):** A rectangle identical to the FV, with width $2r$ and height $h$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** All vertical generators of the cylinder appear as the *vertical sides* of the rectangle in the FV and PV. They all have **True Length = $h$** in the simple position. The top view is a true circle.

### 2.2.3 Tetrahedron in Simple Position (Axis Perpendicular to HP)

- **Front View (FV):** An isosceles triangle (apex on top, base is the projection of the triangular base).
- **Top View (TV):** A triangle (the true shape of the base) with the apex projected to its **centroid**.
- **Profile View (PV):** An isosceles triangle (slightly different from FV if the base is oriented such that one edge is perpendicular to VP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick — Tetrahedron Orientation:** Always orient the base triangle so that **one edge (say BC)** is perpendicular to the **VP** (i.e., parallel to the **XY line**) so that the projections fall on standard, neat positions. This avoids awkward tilted lines in the drawing.

---

## 2.3 Profile View — The Forgotten Third View

Many students neglect the profile view. KTU 2024 explicitly includes it.

| Reference Plane | View Name | Shows |
|---|---|---|
| **HP** | Top View (Plan) | Length × Breadth |
| **VP** | Front View (Elevation) | Length × Height |
| **PP** | Profile View (Side View / End View) | Height × Breadth (or Height × Depth) |

**Rule of Construction for Profile View (First-Angle Projection):**
1. Draw a horizontal reference line through the highest and lowest points of the Front View.
2. Project the Front View points horizontally towards the **right** (for first-angle) onto an auxiliary line drawn at $45°$ (or transferred by compass).
3. The Profile View appears on the **right side** of the Front View.
4. The width of the Profile View equals the width of the Top View.

---

## 2.4 Projection with Axis Inclined to One Reference Plane

There are two sub-cases in this category.

### 2.4.1 Case 1: Axis Inclined to HP, Perpendicular to VP

In this case, the axis makes an angle $\theta$ with the HP and is perpendicular to the VP (i.e., the axis is seen as a *point* in the Front View).

**Procedural Logic:**
1. Draw the solid in the simple position first (axis vertical, resting on HP).
2. Redraw the **Front View** such that the axis is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the XY line. The axis appears as a *point* at the top/bottom of this view.
3. The **Top View** now shows the axis as an inclined line of apparent length, and the base appears as an **ellipse** (for cone/cylinder) or a tilted triangle (for tetrahedron).
4. The generators that appear as **extreme left and extreme right** in the new Front View are the **Left Generator (LG)** and **Right Generator (RG)** in the Top View. They are the *true lengths* of the generators in the inclined position.
5. The locus of the apex (for cone) and the base centre (for cylinder/tetrahedron) is a circular arc in the Top View, since the axis rotates about the base centre.

### 2.4.2 Case 2: Axis Inclined to VP, Perpendicular to HP

Mirror logic. The Front View shows the axis as an inclined line, and the Top View shows the axis as a point.

---

## 2.5 Projection with Axis Inclined to Both Reference Planes

This is the most challenging case. The axis is inclined to **both** HP and VP simultaneously, with angles $\theta$ (to HP) and $\phi$ (to VP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two-Step Method (Mandatory KTU Method):**
> 1. **First, incline the axis to HP** while keeping it parallel to VP. (This gives a *front view inclined, top view tilted* intermediate stage.)
> 2. **Then, rotate the entire setup** so that the axis becomes inclined to VP while still inclined to HP. (This requires rotating the top view about the axis point in the front view.)

**Why Two Steps?**
Because orthographic projection does not allow simultaneous rotation in two perpendicular directions in a single operation. The student must tilt the solid, redraw, and then re-tilt the new position.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarises all formulae and geometric relationships used in this module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Key:** TL = True Length, AL = Apparent Length, $\alpha$ = True angle with plane, $\beta$ = Apparent angle in adjacent view, $\theta$ = Angle between the line and the other reference plane.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slant Height of Cone | $l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$ | $r$ = base radius, $h$ = height |
| 2 | Apparent Length Relation | $AL = TL \cdot \cos(\beta)$ | $\beta$ is the apparent angle in the given view |
| 3 | True Angle vs Apparent Angle | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{\tan(\beta)}{\cos(\theta)}$ | $\theta$ is the angle with the perpendicular reference plane |
| 4 | Height of Tetrahedron | $h = a\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}}$ | $a$ = edge length of regular tetrahedron |
| 5 | Apothem of Tetrahedron (slant edge in side view) | $s = a\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ | Distance from apex to midpoint of base edge |
| 6 | Circumradius of Tetrahedron Base | $R_{base} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Distance from centroid to vertex of equilateral triangle base |
| 7 | Inradius of Tetrahedron Base | $r_{base} = \dfrac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | Distance from centroid to midpoint of base edge |
| 8 | Length of Cylinder Generator | $TL_{gen} = h$ | Generators are parallel to axis |
| 9 | Visible Generators (Locus) | Arc of radius $l$ or $h$ | All generators/axis endpoints trace circular arcs |
| 10 | Profile View Width | $W_{PV} = W_{TV}$ | Width of profile view equals width of top view |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The skills taught in this module are not merely academic. They are foundational for:

- **Machine Drawing:** Shafts (cylinders), pulleys, lathe centres (cones), and bevel gears (cones in inclined positions) are routinely drawn using these projection methods.
- **Civil Engineering:** Chimneys, pillars, and slanted roofs (tetrahedral trusses) require projection of inclined solids.
- **3D CAD Modelling:** Software like AutoCAD, SolidWorks, and CATIA internally uses the same orthographic projection logic to generate 2D drawings from 3D models.
- **Manufacturing:** A fitter or machinist must mentally visualise the 3D shape from a 2D drawing to manufacture the part.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Slant Height of a Right Circular Cone

Consider a right circular cone with base radius $r$ and vertical height $h$. The axis is perpendicular to the base. The generator $l$ joins the apex to a point on the perimeter of the base.

The radius $r$, height $h$, and slant height $l$ form a right-angled triangle, with $l$ as the hypotenuse.

By the Pythagorean Theorem:

$$l^2 = r^2 + h^2$$

Taking the positive square root (since lengths are positive):

$$l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The slant height $l$ is the **True Length of the generator** when the cone is in the *Simple Position* (axis vertical). This is the value you label on your drawing as "True Length = $\sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$".

---

## 3.2 Derivation of True Angle from Apparent Angle (Universal Line Projection Formula)

This is one of the most critical derivations in KTU Board examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Setup:** Consider a straight line $AB$ of True Length $TL$ inclined at a true angle $\alpha$ to the Horizontal Plane (HP) and at an angle $\theta$ to the Vertical Plane (VP). The line is seen in the Front View with apparent angle $\beta$.

In the Top View, the line projects its true length in plan form. The vertical height component is collapsed in the Top View. By the geometry of projection:

$$\text{Vertical Rise} = TL \cdot \sin(\alpha) = AL \cdot \sin(\beta)$$

where $AL = TL \cdot \cos(\theta)$ is the apparent length in the Front View.

Therefore:

$$TL \cdot \sin(\alpha) = (TL \cdot \cos(\theta)) \cdot \sin(\beta)$$

Dividing both sides by $TL \cdot \cos(\theta)$:

$$\frac{\sin(\alpha)}{\cos(\theta)} = \sin(\beta)$$

Dividing numerator and denominator:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\tan(\beta)}{\cos(\theta)}$$

Or equivalently, solving for the apparent angle:

$$\tan(\beta) = \tan(\alpha) \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

**Verification:** When the line lies in VP (i.e., $\theta = 0°$), $\cos(\theta) = 1$, so $\beta = \alpha$. This is correct because when a line is parallel to VP, its Front View shows the true angle. ✓

When the line is perpendicular to VP (i.e., $\theta = 90°$), $\cos(\theta) = 0$, so $\tan(\beta) = 0 \Rightarrow \beta = 0°$. This is correct because the Front View collapses the line to a point (horizontal line) showing zero inclination. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The Three Solids × Five Position Cases

This matrix is your KTU Board revision master. Memorise it.

| Solid | Simple Position (Axis ⊥ HP) | Inclined to HP (Axis ⊥ VP) | Inclined to VP (Axis ⊥ HP) | Inclined to Both Planes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cone** | FV: Triangle; TV: Circle; PV: Triangle | FV: Triangle (axis as point); TV: Ellipse + Triangle envelope | FV: Triangle (axis inclined); TV: Circle (axis as point) | FV: Triangle (axis inclined); TV: Ellipse with tilted axis |
| **Cylinder** | FV: Rectangle; TV: Circle; PV: Rectangle | FV: Rectangle (axis as point); TV: Ellipse + Rectangle sides as tangents | FV: Rectangle (axis inclined); TV: Rectangle (axis as point) | FV: Parallelogram; TV: Ellipse with tilted axis |
| **Tetrahedron** | FV: Triangle; TV: Triangle (apex at centroid); PV: Triangle | FV: Triangle (axis as point); TV: Triangle with apex offset | FV: Triangle (axis inclined); TV: Triangle (axis as point) | FV: Triangle (axis inclined); TV: Triangle with shifted apex |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Procedure — Cone Inclined to Both HP and VP (Full Derivation)

This is the most rigorous procedure tested in KTU Part B questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A right circular cone of base diameter $D$ and height $H$, resting on HP on its base. The axis is inclined at $\theta$ to HP and $\phi$ to VP.

**Step 1 — Simple Position Drawing.**
Draw the Front View as an isosceles triangle with base $D$ and height $H$. Mark the axis as the centre line. The Top View is a circle of diameter $D$ with centre on the axis.

**Step 2 — Identify Key Points.**
Mark the apex $a'$ in FV and $a$ in TV. Mark the centre of the base $o'$ in FV and $o$ in TV. Mark the extreme points of the base circle in TV: leftmost $a_1$, rightmost $a_2$, frontmost $a_3$, backmost $a_4$.

**Step 3 — Incline the Axis to HP (First Tilt).**
Redraw the Front View such that the axis $o'a'$ is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the XY line. The base line of the cone is tilted. Now project the new Top View: the base becomes an ellipse, and the axis $oa$ in the Top View is now inclined.

**Step 4 — Locate the New True-Length Generators.**
In the new Front View, identify the extreme left and right generators (LG and RG). These represent the **true lengths** of the slant edges in the inclined position. Mark their endpoints in the Top View.

**Step 5 — Incline the Axis to VP (Second Tilt).**
Now the cone is inclined to HP. To make it inclined to VP, rotate the top view about the axis centre $o$ (in the top view) by the angle required to make the axis $oa$ inclined at $\phi$ to the XY line. Equivalently, the apparent angle of the axis in the top view is $\beta$ where $\tan(\beta) = \tan(\phi) \cdot \cos(\theta)$.

**Step 6 — Redraw the Final Top View.**
The base is now a tilted ellipse. Mark the new positions of the extreme generators and the apex.

**Step 7 — Project the Final Front View.**
Project all key points vertically upward to obtain the final Front View, which shows the axis inclined at angle $\theta$ to XY and the cone as a tilted triangle.

**Step 8 — Profile View (Optional but required in 2024 scheme).**
Transfer the depth of the top view to obtain the profile view on the right side using a $45°$ auxiliary line.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Verification — Tetrahedron Height Derivation

For a regular tetrahedron of edge length $a$, consider the equilateral triangle base $BCD$ of side $a$.

The centroid of the base is at a distance $R_{base}$ from each vertex:

$$R_{base} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}$$

The apex $A$ lies directly above the centroid. The edge $AB$ has length $a$ (since the tetrahedron is regular). The slant height (from $A$ to the midpoint $M$ of edge $BC$) has length $s$:

$$s = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

In the right triangle $AMB$:

$$AM^2 + MB^2 = AB^2$$

$$s^2 + \left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2 = a^2$$

$$s^2 = a^2 - \frac{a^2}{4} = \frac{3a^2}{4}$$

$$s = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{2} \checkmark$$

Now, in the right triangle $AMO$ (where $O$ is the centroid), $MO = \dfrac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}$ (inradius of base). The height $h$ of the tetrahedron is:

$$h^2 = s^2 - MO^2 = \frac{3a^2}{4} - \frac{a^2}{12} = \frac{9a^2 - a^2}{12} = \frac{8a^2}{12} = \frac{2a^2}{3}$$

$$h = a\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{a\sqrt{6}}{3}$$

This is the **True Length of the tetrahedron's axis**, used when projecting the solid in the simple position.

---

## 3.6 Critical Insight: Why Generators Trace Arcs in the Top View

When a cone (axis vertical) is tilted so that the apex moves to a new position, the apex traces a circular arc in the Top View. The **centre** of this arc is the centre of the base (which remains fixed), and the **radius** of this arc is the true length of the axis $h$ (in the Top View, the apex was originally at distance $h$ from the centre when measured perpendicular to the XY line — but the rotation is in a vertical plane parallel to VP, so the locus is a circle of radius $h$).

Similarly, the **base perimeter** in the Top View, when the axis tilts, gets foreshortened into an **ellipse** because the base circle is no longer parallel to HP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Locus Rule (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - When the **axis** of a solid tilts, the apex (and axis end) traces a **circular arc** in the adjacent view.
> - When the **base** of a solid tilts, the base perimeter becomes an **ellipse** in the adjacent view.
> - The **generators** of a cone, when tilted, become the tangents to the elliptical base from the apex point.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Universal Projection Procedure

The following block diagram maps the algorithmic logic of projecting any solid, irrespective of its type, in any of the five position cases.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Read Solid Type and Position] --> nodeB{Identify Solid: Cone Cylinder or Tetrahedron}
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 1: Draw Simple Position Views]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Front View: Primary Shape]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Top View: True Base Shape]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Profile View: For 2024 Scheme Only]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD{Position Case Decision}
    nodeC2 --> nodeD
    nodeC3 --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Case A: Axis Perpendicular to HP]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Case B: Axis Inclined to HP only]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Case C: Axis Inclined to VP only]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Case D: Axis Inclined to Both Planes]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Tilt FV by angle theta]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2[Redraw TV as Ellipse or Tilted Shape]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3[Mark LG and RG Generators]
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Tilt TV by angle phi]
    nodeG1 --> nodeG2[Redraw FV as Tilted Shape]
    nodeG2 --> nodeG3[Mark Generators in FV]
    nodeH --> nodeH1[Stage 1: Tilt FV by theta to HP]
    nodeH1 --> nodeH2[Stage 2: Rotate TV by phi to VP]
    nodeH2 --> nodeH3[Final FV and TV are Tilted]
    nodeH3 --> nodeH4[Project Profile View from TV Width]
    nodeE --> nodeZ[Add Dimensions and Label Generators]
    nodeF3 --> nodeZ
    nodeG3 --> nodeZ
    nodeH4 --> nodeZ
    nodeZ --> nodeY[End: Submit Drawing for Valuation]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Cone Projection

This diagram shows the internal components of a cone and how each component projects to a different reference plane.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    coneSys[Right Circular Cone System] --> apexModule[Apex Point A]
    coneSys --> baseModule[Base Circle]
    coneSys --> axisModule[Axis Line]
    coneSys --> genModule[Generator Surface]
    apexModule --> projA1[Projects to FV as apex point]
    apexModule --> projA2[Projects to TV as apex point]
    apexModule --> projA3[Projects to PV as apex point]
    baseModule --> projB1[Projects to FV as base line]
    baseModule --> projB2[Projects to TV as true circle]
    baseModule --> projB3[Projects to PV as base line]
    axisModule --> projX1[Projects to FV as centre line]
    axisModule --> projX2[Projects to TV as centre point]
    axisModule --> projX3[Projects to PV as centre line]
    genModule --> projG1[Projects to FV as triangle sides]
    genModule --> projG2[Projects to TV as radii of circle]
    genModule --> projG3[Projects to PV as triangle sides]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Tetrahedron Projection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    tetraSys[Regular Tetrahedron System] --> vertexSet[Four Vertices: A B C D]
    vertexSet --> vA[Vertex A: Apex]
    vertexSet --> vB[Vertex B]
    vertexSet --> vC[Vertex C]
    vertexSet --> vD[Vertex D]
    vA --> projA_V[Front View: a prime]
    vA --> projA_H[Top View: a]
    vB --> projB_V[Front View: b prime]
    vB --> projB_H[Top View: b]
    vC --> projC_V[Front View: c prime]
    vC --> projC_H[Top View: c]
    vD --> projD_V[Front View: d prime]
    vD --> projD_H[Top View: d]
    projB_H --> baseTV[Base Triangle BCD in Top View]
    projC_H --> baseTV
    projD_H --> baseTV
    projA_H --> centroidTV[Apex Projects to Centroid of Base]
    baseTV --> baseShape[Equilateral Triangle of Side a]
    centroidTV --> baseShape
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Inclination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Start with Simple Position] --> step1[Step 1: Identify Axis in FV and TV]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Choose Tilt Direction in FV]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Rotate Axis by theta degrees]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Project New TV from Tilted FV]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Draw Locus Arc of Apex in TV]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Mark Extreme Generators LG and RG]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Construct Ellipse Base in TV]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Draw Tangents from Apex to Ellipse]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Add Profile View from TV Width]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10: Dimension and Label]
```
