# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions test definitions, terminology, and direct conceptual recall. Answers should be 3–5 lines with a labelled sketch where applicable.

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a regular tetrahedron. Mention the number of faces, vertices, and edges it possesses. Sketch its front view and top view when it rests on one of its faces on the HP with one edge of the base parallel to the VP.**

**Model Answer:**

A **regular tetrahedron** is a solid bounded by **four equilateral triangular faces**, where all six edges are of equal length.

- Number of **Faces** = **4**
- Number of **Vertices** = **4**
- Number of **Edges** = **6**

**Sketch Description (to be drawn on answer sheet):**
- The **Top View** is an **equilateral triangle** representing the true shape of the base on HP. One edge is drawn parallel to the XY line.
- The **Front View** is an **isosceles triangle**. The apex is positioned at the centre (centroid) of the base edge midpoint in the projection.
- The **height** of the front view is $h = \dfrac{a\sqrt{6}}{3}$ where $a$ is the edge length.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: A cone of base diameter 50 mm and height 60 mm rests on its base on the HP. Draw its front view and top view. Also calculate the true length of its generator.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** Base diameter $D = 50$ mm, so radius $r = 25$ mm. Height $h = 60$ mm.

**True Length of Generator:**

$$l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{(25)^2 + (60)^2}$$

$$l = \sqrt{625 + 3600} = \sqrt{4225}$$

$$l = 65 \text{ mm}$$

**Drawing Description (to be drawn on answer sheet):**
- **Front View:** Isosceles triangle with base $50$ mm and height $60$ mm.
- **Top View:** Circle of diameter $50$ mm with centre on the axis.
- The axis is drawn as a centre line; dimensions are labelled on the drawing.
- The **generator length = 65 mm** is written as a note on the drawing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern (Module 2 Internal Choice):** Students must answer ONE question out of TWO, with each question having sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Full marks require **construction lines, dimensioning, and labelling** of generators and angles.

---

### PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A cylinder of base diameter 40 mm and height 60 mm rests on its base on the HP. It is then inclined to the HP such that its axis makes an angle of 30° with the HP. Draw its projections when the axis is perpendicular to the VP. Also draw the profile view on the right of the front view.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Simple Position + Initial Tilt Setup

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Draw the XY reference line** across the middle of the sheet.

2. **Simple Position (Stage 1):**
   - **Front View (FV):** Draw a rectangle of width $40$ mm and height $60$ mm such that the base rests on the XY line.
   - Mark the top corners as $a'_1$ and $a'_2$ (top of the cylinder generators).
   - Mark the bottom corners as $o'$ (centre of bottom base) and the two base points $b'_1, b'_2$.
   - **Top View (TV):** Below the FV, draw a circle of diameter $40$ mm. The centre is $o$, and the extreme points of the circle are $a_1, a_2$ (left and right) and $a_3, a_4$ (front and back).

3. **Initial Tilt (Stage 2 — Axis Inclined to HP):**
   - Redraw the **FV** above and to the right of the original FV (or below, depending on the chosen side) such that the new axis $o'a'$ makes an angle of **30° with the XY line**.
   - Mark the new top points $a'_1, a'_2$ and bottom points.
   - Project the new **TV**: draw the locus of the top centre (circular arc of radius $60$ mm in TV). The base is now an **ellipse** of major axis $40$ mm and minor axis $40 \cos 30° = 34.64$ mm (since the base circle is now tilted at 60° to the HP).

4. **Mark Generators:**
   - In the tilted FV, the left extreme generator is LG and the right extreme generator is RG. These have the **true length** = $60 / \cos 30° \approx 69.28$ mm (apparent length, since the cylinder is tilted).
   - Locate LG and RG in the new TV by projecting down from the FV.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Completion + Profile View

5. **Complete the Top View:**
   - Draw the **tangent lines** from the top centre to the ellipse — these are the LG and RG in TV.
   - Mark visible (V) and hidden (H) portions of the bottom base.

6. **Profile View (Right Side):**
   - Draw a $45°$ auxiliary line at the right end of the FV.
   - Project horizontally from the FV (top and bottom points) onto the $45°$ line, then project downward.
   - Transfer the **width of the TV** (= 40 mm) onto the profile view.
   - The profile view appears as a **rectangle of width 40 mm and height 60 mm** (since the axis is perpendicular to VP, the profile view is identical to the simple-position rectangle).

7. **Dimensioning:**
   - Label $\theta = 30°$ at the axis.
   - Dimension the base diameter and height.
   - Write the title: "Cylinder — Axis Inclined to HP at 30° — Axis ⊥ VP".

---

### PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A cone of base diameter 50 mm and height 65 mm rests on its base on the HP. It is then so oriented that its axis is inclined at 30° to the HP and 40° to the VP. Draw its projections. Also draw the profile view.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Two-Stage Inclination Procedure

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Simple Position (Stage 1):**
   - **FV:** Isosceles triangle, base = $50$ mm, height = $65$ mm. Apex at top: $a'$. Base centre: $o'$.
   - **TV:** Circle of diameter $50$ mm, centre $o$. Extreme generators marked: LG and RG.

2. **First Tilt (Stage 2 — Axis Inclined to HP at 30°):**
   - Redraw the **FV** to the right. Axis $o'a'$ inclined at 30° to XY.
   - The base is now tilted. Mark new apex $a_1'$ and new base centre $o_1'$.
   - **Project new TV:** The base is an **ellipse** with major axis $50$ mm and minor axis $50 \cos 60° = 25$ mm (since the base is tilted 60° to HP).
   - The apex $a_1$ is at a distance of $65$ mm (slant length) from $o_1$ along the inclined axis.
   - Mark LG and RG generators by joining apex to the extreme points of the ellipse.

3. **Second Tilt (Stage 3 — Axis Inclined to VP at 40°):**
   - The axis in the new TV is inclined at some angle to the XY line.
   - **Rotate the TV** about the axis centre $o_1$ (in TV) such that the axis becomes inclined at the **apparent angle** $\beta$ to the XY line, where:
   
   $$\tan(\beta) = \tan(40°) \cdot \cos(30°) = 0.8391 \cdot 0.8660 = 0.7266$$
   
   $$\beta = \arctan(0.7266) \approx 36°$$
   
   - Mark the new apex position $a_2$ in TV. The new ellipse base is tilted accordingly.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Final Projections + Profile View

4. **Final Front View (Project from Final TV):**
   - Project all key points of the final TV vertically upward to obtain the final FV.
   - The axis in FV is inclined at 30° to XY (unchanged by the second tilt, because the second tilt is about an axis parallel to XY).
   - The cone in the final FV is a **tilted isosceles triangle** with the base inclined.

5. **Profile View:**
   - Use the $45°$ auxiliary line method.
   - Width of profile view = width of final TV.
   - Height of profile view = height of final FV.
   - The profile view shows the cone as a triangle with its apex and base oriented according to the combined inclination.

6. **Dimensioning and Labelling:**
   - $\theta = 30°$ (with HP), $\phi = 40°$ (with VP), and the apparent angle $\beta$ in the top view.
   - Slant height $l = \sqrt{25^2 + 65^2} = \sqrt{625 + 4225} = \sqrt{4850} \approx 69.64$ mm.
   - All generators LG, RG, and the front and back generators (FG, BG) labelled.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Profile View (–3 to –5 marks):** The KTU 2024 scheme *explicitly* includes the profile view. Drawing only FV + TV will lead to significant mark deduction. Always include the PV when the problem says "draw the profile view on the right of the front view".
> 
> 2. **Missing Construction Lines (–2 marks):** Examiners reward the *process*. Always show:
>    - The locus arcs of the apex (and base centre, if the base tilts).
>    - The construction of the ellipse for the tilted base.
>    - The 45° auxiliary line for the profile view transfer.
>    - The XY reference line and projection lines.
> 
> 3. **Confusing LG and RG Positions (–1 to –2 marks):** The **Left Generator (LG)** and **Right Generator (RG)** must be correctly identified. LG is on the left side of the apex in FV, and RG is on the right. They are *not* simply the two sides of the triangle in the simple position.
> 
> 4. **Wrong Apparent Angle Calculation (–3 marks):** In the two-stage inclination, students often mistakenly use $\phi$ instead of $\beta$ in the second tilt. Always use the **apparent angle** $\beta$ (where $\tan(\beta) = \tan(\phi) \cdot \cos(\theta)$) when tilting the top view.
> 
> 5. **Not Dimensioning or Labelling (–1 to –2 marks):** Every drawing must have:
>    - Base diameter and height marked.
>    - Inclination angles $\theta$ and $\phi$ marked.
>    - Title (e.g., "Cone — Axis Inclined to Both HP and VP").
> 
> 6. **Mixing Up First-Angle and Third-Angle Projection (–5 marks):** KTU uses **First-Angle Projection** (PP on the right, TP below FV). Do not use Third-Angle conventions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as a last-minute revision aid before the exam.**

- [ ] A **Cone** has 1 base (circular), 1 apex, 1 axis, and a curved lateral surface. Slant height $l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$.
- [ ] A **Cylinder** has 2 parallel circular bases, 1 axis, and a curved lateral surface. Generators are all parallel to the axis and have length $h$.
- [ ] A **Tetrahedron** has 4 faces, 4 vertices, 6 edges, all of equal length $a$. Height $h = a\sqrt{2/3}$.
- [ ] In the **Simple Position** (axis vertical, ⊥ HP): the base appears as a true circle in TV, and the solid appears as a triangle/rectangle in FV and PV.
- [ ] The **Profile View** is drawn on the **right** of the FV in first-angle projection. It is obtained using a **45° auxiliary line**.
- [ ] When the **axis is inclined to HP only** (and ⊥ VP): FV shows the axis as a point; TV shows the axis as an inclined line, and the base becomes an ellipse.
- [ ] When the **axis is inclined to VP only** (and ⊥ HP): TV shows the axis as a point; FV shows the axis as an inclined line, and the base becomes an ellipse in FV.
- [ ] When the **axis is inclined to both HP and VP**: a **two-stage procedure** is used — first tilt to HP, then tilt to VP. Use the apparent angle formula $\tan(\beta) = \tan(\phi) \cdot \cos(\theta)$ for the second tilt.
- [ ] The **apex** (or moving end of the axis) traces a **circular arc** in the adjacent view, with radius = axis length, centre at the fixed end of the axis.
- [ ] The **base** of a cone/cylinder, when tilted, becomes an **ellipse** in the adjacent view. Major axis = original diameter, minor axis = $D \cos(\text{tilt angle})$.
- [ ] The **extreme generators** (LG and RG) are tangent to the elliptical base in the Top View and connect to the apex. They have the **true length of the generator** in the inclined position.
- [ ] **Visible** portions of a solid are marked with **continuous thick lines**; **hidden** portions are marked with **dashed lines**.
- [ ] **KTU uses First-Angle Projection**: FV (top-left), TV (below FV), PV (right of FV).
- [ ] Always **dimension** the drawing: base diameter, height, slant length, inclination angles $\theta$ and $\phi$.
- [ ] Always write a **title block** below the drawing identifying the solid, its dimensions, and the position/orientation.
- [ ] The **true length of the generator** of a cone remains constant in all positions: $l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$. The generator's *apparent* length in a view changes with inclination.
- [ ] The **generators of a cylinder** are *all parallel* to the axis — they all have the *same length* as the axis.
- [ ] In a **tetrahedron**, the apex projects to the **centroid** of the base triangle in the Top View (when the base is in HP).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sections of Tetrahedron
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sections of Tetrahedron # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tetrahedron (Engineering Graphics Definition):** > A **tetrahedron** is a regular polyhedron (specifically a triangular pyramid) bounded by **four congruent equilateral triangular faces**, four vertices, and six equal edges. In Engineering Graphics (KTU GCEST203), a tetrahedron is conventionally positioned with one of its faces lying flat on the **Horizontal Plane (HP)** and the opposite vertex resting above on the **Vertical Plane (VP)**, making it a 3D solid of uniform geometry. A **section of a tetrahedron** is defined as the 2D figure obtained when the solid is **intersected by a plane** (called the *cutting plane* or *section plane*). The resulting 2D closed curve or polygon is known as the **sectional shape** (or **section line** on the solid's surface). When this section line is projected onto a plane parallel to the cutting plane, the resulting projection is called the **True Shape (T.S.)** of the section. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a perfectly cut diamond or a pyramid-shaped candle 🕯️. If you slice the candle horizontally near the bottom, you get a small triangle. If you slice it vertically, you get a trapezoidal cut. The **outline of the cut surface** is what engineering graphics calls a "section." For a tetrahedron — which is a pyramid with a triangular base — every cut produces a polygon (triangle or quadrilateral), never a curved line, because all faces are flat. > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter in engineering?** > Civil, mechanical, and architectural engineers constantly slice solids to study **load-bearing intersections**, **roof geometries**, and **mould/jig profiles**. The true shape of a section is critical for manufacturing sheet-metal parts, designing hopper outlets, and analyzing stress distribution in pyramidal structural members. ## 1.3 Standard Geometric Properties of a Regular Tetrahedron | Property | Value | Notation | |----------|-------|----------| | Number of faces | **4** (equilateral triangles) | F | | Number of edges | **6** (all equal) | E | | Number of vertices | **4** | V | | Edge length | Let edge = **a** (mm) | a | | Base side (in HP) | a | — | | Slant edge projection length | a | — | | Apex above base centroid | h = √(2/3)·a ≈ 0.8165·a | h | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Keyword Map:** Always memorize "4 faces, 4 vertices, 6 equal edges." Writing "pyramid with triangular base" is acceptable; writing "triangle" alone is **not**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Section Problem The graphical construction of a tetrahedron's section follows a deterministic 6-step logic: 1. **Orientation of solid:** Place the base in HP with one edge parallel to VP (to make the front view a triangle resting on its base). 2. **Definition of section plane (SP):** Represented as a straight line in the front view (a horizontal line indicates a plane parallel to HP; a vertical line indicates a plane perpendicular to HP; an inclined line indicates an inclined plane). 3. **Identification of cutting points:** Mark where the section line crosses the visible edges of the solid in the front view. 4. **Projection to top view:** Drop perpendicular projectors from each cutting point to the corresponding edge in the top view, locating the section points on the top view. 5. **Joining section points:** Connect the points in the top view in sequence (the order is dictated by which faces the section plane intersects). 6. **Construction of true shape (T.S.):** Project the section points onto an auxiliary plane parallel to the section plane, and join them to obtain the true shape. ## 2.2 Types of Section Planes (KTU High-Yield Classification) | S.No. | Section Plane Type | Visual Cue in Front View | Section Shape Produced | True Shape (T.S.) Geometry | |-------|--------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------------------| | 1 | **Parallel to HP** (horizontal) | Horizontal line in FV | Triangle (smaller, similar to base) | Equilateral triangle | | 2 | **Perpendicular to HP and Inclined to VP** | Inclined line in FV | Trapezium | Triangle (in general) | | 3 | **Perpendicular to VP and Inclined to HP** | Vertical line in FV | Triangle | Triangle (similar to base) | | 4 | **Inclined to both HP and VP** | Inclined line in FV | Quadrilateral | Triangle or Quadrilateral | | 5 | **Passing through apex** | Line from apex in FV | Triangle (degenerate small triangle at apex) | Triangle | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table lists **every formula** you may legitimately need for tetrahedron section problems. No invented constants are used. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Height of tetrahedron } (H) &= \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot a \\[4pt] \text{Slant edge length} &= a \\[4pt] \text{Slant height (apothem of triangular face)} &= \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{2} \\[4pt] \text{Area of one triangular face} &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\,a^{2} \\[4pt] \text{Total surface area} &= \sqrt{3}\,a^{2} \\[4pt] \text{Volume} &= \frac{a^{3}}{6\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.1179\,a^{3} \\[4pt] \text{True Shape Area Ratio (for SP parallel to HP at height h from base)} &= \left(\frac{H - h}{H}\right)^{2} \cdot \text{Base Area} \end{aligned} $$ > **Avoid Markdown Pipe Pitfall:** All absolute value notations in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` style separators, never the `|` character, to preserve table syntax integrity. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Sheet-metal work (Development):** Tetrahedral hopper outlets in chemical plants — the true shape of the cut determines the unrolled sheet metal pattern. - **Architecture:** Pyramidal skylights and roof intersections require true-shape sections for fabrication of glazing panels. - **Tooling:** Jigs and fixtures for welding pyramidal sub-assemblies rely on the exact geometry of oblique cuts. - **CAD/CAM:** Parametric solid modeling in SolidWorks/Fusion 360 uses these same construction principles algorithmically. # 3. Step-by-Step Graphical Construction Procedure (Analytical Matrix) > [!NOTE] > In Engineering Graphics, "derivations" take the form of step-by-step **graphical construction procedures**. The following matrix exhaustively details the construction of (a) the tetrahedron, (b) the sectional top view, and (c) the true shape. ## 3.1 Construction of a Tetrahedron (Base in HP, One Base Edge Parallel to VP) **Step 1 — Draw the base (Top View):** Construct an equilateral triangle `abc` of side `a` such that side `ab` is parallel to the XY line. This is the **Top View (T.V.)** of the base. **Step 2 — Locate the centroid O:** The centroid of the equilateral triangle is found by drawing medians. The apex `o` of the tetrahedron in front view lies directly above this centroid. $$ O = \left(\frac{x_{a}+x_{b}+x_{c}}{3},\;\frac{y_{a}+y_{b}+y_{c}}{3}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Draw the Front View:** Project all four points down/up to the **Front View (F.V.)**. Draw the base line `a'(b')c'` in F.V. on the XY reference line. From `o'`, draw a vertical projector upward. Mark the height H = √(2/3)·a along this projector; this is the apex `o'`. **Step 4 — Complete the Front View:** Join `o'–a'`, `o'–b'`, `o'–c'`. The front view is a triangle with apex `o'` above a horizontal base. ## 3.2 Cutting-Plane Procedure (General Inclined Section) **Step 5 — Define the section line (F.V.):** Draw the section plane in F.V. as a straight line, e.g., inclined at angle θ. Label the points where it cuts the slant edges `o'–a'`, `o'–b'`, `o'–c'` as `1'`, `2'`, `3'` respectively (only the points on visible edges are needed for the visible section). **Step 6 — Project to Top View:** From each section point (e.g., `1'`, `2'`, `3'`), draw **vertical projectors** downward. Where each projector intersects the corresponding slant edge in the top view (`o–a`, `o–b`, `o–c`), mark the point (`1`, `2`, `3`). **Step 7 — Join section points in Top View:** Connect `1–2–3–1` (in cyclic order around the solid) using straight lines. This closed polygon is the **sectional Top View**. **Step 8 — Construct the True Shape (Auxiliary Plane Method):** - Draw a new reference line `X₁Y₁` parallel to the section line in F.V. - Project all section points `1'`, `2'`, `3'` perpendicular to `X₁Y₁` (i.e., parallel to projectors of the auxiliary plane). - Transfer distances from the top view (or from the F.V. using compass) onto these new projectors. - Join the points. The resulting polygon is the **True Shape (T.S.)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mark-Carrying Logic:** The True Shape must be drawn on a *separate* auxiliary view, not in the original F.V. or T.V. Examiners deduct 2 marks if it is drawn on the F.V. itself. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Exhaustive) **Problem:** A regular tetrahedron of edge **50 mm** has its base on HP with one edge parallel to VP. It is cut by a section plane inclined at **45° to HP** and passing through the mid-point of the axis. Draw the sectional top view and the true shape of the section. Find the area of the true shape. ### Given Data $$ a = 50 \text{ mm}, \quad H = \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}} \cdot 50 = 40.82 \text{ mm}, \quad \theta = 45^{\circ} $$ ### Step 1: Construct the base in Top View $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Base side } a &= 50 \text{ mm} \\ \text{Base vertices: } A(25,\,0),\; B(75,\,0),\; C(50,\,43.30) \end{aligned} $$ (The base is positioned so that edge AB is parallel to XY, with the centroid at x = 50 mm.) ### Step 2: Locate centroid O and front view base $$ O_{x} = 50 \text{ mm},\quad O_{y} = 14.43 \text{ mm (centroid height of base triangle)} $$ The base in F.V. becomes a line `a'(b')c'` of length 50 mm on the XY line. ### Step 3: Locate apex $$ o' = (50,\; 14.43 + 40.82) = (50,\; 55.25) \text{ in F.V.} $$ ### Step 4: Section Plane Geometry The section plane passes through the midpoint of axis. Midpoint of `o'–o` projected = centroid location in F.V., at height = `14.43 mm` above XY (i.e., on the base line). A 45° inclined line in F.V. starting from this midpoint will cut all three slant edges. By symmetry (centroid on axis), the three cut points `1'`, `2'`, `3'` lie on slant edges `o'–a'`, `o'–b'`, `o'–c'` at equal heights from the base. ### Step 5: True Shape Area Calculation For a 45° plane through the centroid of a regular tetrahedron, the section is a **regular triangle** (by 3-fold rotational symmetry). The three section points form an equilateral triangle whose side can be computed. Distance from apex to centroid along slant edge: $$ d_{\text{slant}} = \sqrt{\left(\tfrac{a}{2}\right)^{2} + H^{2}} = \sqrt{25^{2} + 40.82^{2}} \approx 47.86 \text{ mm} $$ Midpoint of slant edge from apex: $$ \frac{d_{\text{slant}}}{2} \approx 23.93 \text{ mm from apex} $$ The three midpoints of the three slant edges (from apex) form an equilateral triangle of side: $$ L = \frac{a}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 35.36 \text{ mm} $$ > (This result is derivable from the geometry of a regular tetrahedron: the triangle connecting the midpoints of the three lateral edges from the apex is similar to the base, scaled by 1/√2.) ### Step 6: True Shape Area $$ \begin{aligned} A_{\text{T.S.}} &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\,L^{2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot (35.36)^{2} \\[4pt] &= 0.4330 \cdot 1250.33 \\[4pt] &\approx 541.27 \text{ mm}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7: Summary Answer $$ \boxed{\text{True Shape} = \text{Equilateral triangle of side } \approx 35.36 \text{ mm},\; \text{Area} \approx 541.3 \text{ mm}^{2}} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Procedural Flow) > [!NOTE] > A physical drawing of a tetrahedron requires CAD software. The following Mermaid diagram captures the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the construction procedure, which is what examiners test in viva and theory sections. ## 4.1 Construction Procedure Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Step 1: Draw base triangle in Top View
Equilateral triangle abc, side = a, ab parallel to XY"] B["Step 2: Locate centroid O of base
Intersection of medians"] C["Step 3: Project to Front View
Base line on XY, Apex o' at height H = sqrt 2/3 times a"] D["Step 4: Complete Front View
Join o' to a', b', c'"] E["Step 5: Draw Section Plane in F.V.
As straight line at given angle"] F["Step 6: Mark Cutting Points in F.V.
Label as 1 prime, 2 prime, 3 prime"] G["Step 7: Project Cut Points to T.V.
Vertical projectors to slant edges"] H["Step 8: Join Section Points in T.V.
Closed polygon = Sectional Top View"] I["Step 9: Draw Auxiliary Reference Line X1Y1
Parallel to section line in F.V."] J["Step 10: Project to Auxiliary Plane
Transfer distances from T.V."] K["Step 11: Join to obtain True Shape
Final closed polygon = T.S."] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J --> K style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000 style B fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000 style C fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B45F06,color:#000 style D fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B45F06,color:#000 style E fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C00000,color:#000 style F fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C00000,color:#000 style G fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#548235,color:#000 style H fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#548235,color:#000 style I fill:#D9E1F2,stroke:#2E75B6,color:#000 style J fill:#D9E1F2,stroke:#2E75B6,color:#000 style K fill:#FFE699,stroke:#BF8F00,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Section Type Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD SP["Section Plane Orientation"] SP --> P1{"Parallel to HP?"} P1 -->|Yes| T1["Section = Triangle similar to base
True Shape = Equilateral Triangle"] P1 -->|No| P2{"Perpendicular to HP
and Inclined to VP?"} P2 -->|Yes| T2["Section = Trapezium
True Shape = Triangle"] P2 -->|No| P3{"Inclined to both
HP and VP?"} P3 -->|Yes| T3["Section = Quadrilateral
True Shape = Triangle or Quadrilateral"] P3 -->|No| T4["Section = Triangle
True Shape = Triangle"] style SP fill:#FFD966,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000 style T1 fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#385723,color:#000 style T2 fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#385723,color:#000 style T3 fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#385723,color:#000 style T4 fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#385723,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Reference Schematic (ASCII Orthographic Layout) ``` APEX o' /\ / \ / \ / o' \ ← Front View (FV) /________\ / | 1' | \ / |_____| \ ← Section Plane (inclined) / | 2' | \ /____|_____|_____\ | | | o | ← Top View (TV) |_____| X1Y1 | 1''| ──────|─────| ← True Shape (Aux Plane) \ 3''| \ 2'' \___| ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **Q: Define a tetrahedron. State the number of faces, edges, and vertices it has.** **Model Answer:** A tetrahedron is a regular triangular pyramid bounded by four congruent equilateral triangular faces. It has: - **4 faces** (all equilateral triangles), - **6 edges** (all equal in length), - **4 vertices** (3 base vertices + 1 apex). When placed with one face on the HP and one base edge parallel to VP, its front view is a triangle resting on the XY line, and its top view is an equilateral triangle. **(3 marks)** ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Q: What is a "true shape" of a section? Why is it drawn on an auxiliary plane?** **Model Answer:** The **true shape (T.S.)** of a section is the actual shape and size of the cut surface as it would appear when viewed perpendicular (i.e., normal) to the cutting plane. Because the section plane is generally inclined to both HP and VP, its projection in either the front view or top view is **foreshortened** and not the actual size. To obtain the true size, the section plane is made parallel to a new auxiliary projection plane, and the section points are projected onto it. This is why the true shape is always drawn on an **auxiliary plane** (X₁Y₁ reference line parallel to the section line in the front view). **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **A regular tetrahedron of edge 50 mm has its base on the HP with one base edge parallel to the VP. It is cut by a section plane inclined at 45° to the HP, passing through a point on the axis 12 mm above the base. Draw the sectional top view and develop the true shape of the section. Also find the area of the true shape.** **Sub-part (a) [7 marks]: Draw the sectional top view and front view of the cut tetrahedron.** **Model Solution Steps:** 1. **Top View (T.V.):** Draw an equilateral triangle `abc` of side 50 mm with `ab` parallel to XY. Mark the centroid `o`. 2. **Front View (F.V.):** Project to get base line `a'(b')c'`. Locate apex `o'` at height `H = √(2/3) × 50 = 40.82 mm` above centroid. 3. **Section Line:** In F.V., draw a line inclined at 45° to XY, passing through the point 12 mm above base on the axis (i.e., 12 mm above the base line `a'(b')c'`). 4. **Cutting Points:** Mark where this inclined line crosses the three slant edges `o'–a'`, `o'–b'`, `o'–c'`. Label as `1'`, `2'`, `3'`. 5. **Project to T.V.:** Drop vertical projectors from `1'`, `2'`, `3'` to the corresponding edges in T.V. Mark intersection points as `1`, `2`, `3`. 6. **Join in T.V.:** Connect `1–2–3–1` in correct cyclic order to get the **sectional top view** (this is a **triangle** by symmetry for a plane through the axis). **Sub-part (b) [7 marks]: Draw the true shape of the section and compute its area.** **Model Solution Steps:** 1. **Auxiliary Reference Line:** Draw `X₁Y₁` parallel to the section line in F.V. 2. **Project Section Points:** From `1'`, `2'`, `3'`, project perpendicular to `X₁Y₁`. 3. **Transfer Distances:** From the T.V., transfer the perpendicular distances of points `1`, `2`, `3` from the XY line onto the new projectors (using compass). 4. **True Shape:** Mark as `1''`, `2''`, `3''`. Join to obtain the **equilateral triangle T.S.** **Area Calculation:** Since the section plane passes through the axis at 45° inclination through a point 12 mm above base, by symmetry the true shape is an equilateral triangle. Using distance measurements from the constructed figure: $$ L \approx 35 \text{ mm (measured directly from drawing)} $$ $$ \text{Area} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times 35^{2} = 0.4330 \times 1225 \approx 530.4 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ > **Note for students:** In the KTU exam, accept values within ±2 mm of measured distances. Always **show the construction arcs** when transferring distances to the auxiliary plane — examiners award process marks for this. --- ### Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **A regular tetrahedron of edge 40 mm rests on one of its faces on the HP such that the base edge is inclined at 30° to the VP. It is cut by a horizontal section plane at a height of 25 mm above the base. Draw the sectional top view, front view, and the true shape of the section. Also calculate the area of the true shape.** **Sub-part (a) [7 marks]: Construction of the tetrahedron and sectional views.** **Model Solution Steps:** 1. **T.V. Base:** Draw an equilateral triangle `abc` of side 40 mm, then rotate it so that edge `ab` makes 30° with XY line. This is the **Top View**. 2. **F.V. Base:** Project to get the base line in F.V. 3. **Apex Location:** Locate apex `o'` at height `H = √(2/3) × 40 = 32.65 mm` above the centroid of the F.V. base line. 4. **Section Plane:** A horizontal section plane at 25 mm above base is drawn in F.V. as a **horizontal line** at height 25 mm from the XY reference. 5. **Cutting Points:** This horizontal line cuts all three slant edges at points `1'`, `2'`, `3'` (visible points on the F.V. since all slant edges are visible from the front). 6. **Project to T.V.:** Drop vertical projectors from `1'`, `2'`, `3'` to corresponding edges in T.V. — points `1`, `2`, `3`. 7. **Join in T.V.:** Connect `1–2–3–1` to form a **triangle** (the section shape, similar to the base but smaller). **Sub-part (b) [7 marks]: True shape construction and area.** **Model Solution Steps:** 1. Since the section plane is **horizontal** (parallel to HP), its true shape is **already visible in the top view as the triangle 1–2–3** — no auxiliary projection is needed in this special case. 2. **Measure the side length** of triangle 1–2–3 from the drawing (or compute it): $$ \frac{L_{\text{section}}}{L_{\text{base}}} = \frac{H - h}{H} = \frac{32.65 - 25}{32.65} = \frac{7.65}{32.65} \approx 0.2343 $$ $$ L_{\text{section}} = 0.2343 \times 40 \approx 9.37 \text{ mm} $$ 3. **Area of True Shape:** $$ A_{\text{T.S.}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times (9.37)^{2} = 0.4330 \times 87.80 \approx 38.02 \text{ mm}^{2} $$ 4. **Mark on drawing:** Label the triangle as "TRUE SHAPE" and indicate its computed area in the title block. > **Alternative approach for (b):** If a student incorrectly draws the true shape on an auxiliary plane, **deduct 1 mark** but award the area if computed correctly (the logic is right, just the drawing location is wrong). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losses** > > 1. **Forgetting to project section points to the T.V. (–2 marks):** The most common error. The cutting points are visible in the F.V. but MUST be projected down to find their location on the top view. > 2. **Drawing the true shape in the F.V. itself (–2 marks):** The true shape MUST be on a separate auxiliary view. The F.V. is not parallel to the section plane in general. > 3. **Wrong cyclic order of joining (–1 to –2 marks):** Section points must be joined in the order of faces they lie on, not in arbitrary order. Crossing lines = severe deduction. > 4. **Not labeling the true shape (–1 mark):** Always write "TRUE SHAPE" and dimensions on the final answer. > 5. **Incomplete dimensioning (–1 mark):** All edges of the T.S. must be dimensioned. Examiners expect at least 2 dimensions. > 6. **Missing title block / scale (–1 mark):** Standard KTU drawing sheet format requires both. > 7. **Using edge length incorrectly (–2 marks):** The 40 mm or 50 mm is the EDGE length, not the base side. Some students treat it as base diagonal, leading to scaling errors. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision — 60-Second Summary** - ☐ A tetrahedron has **4 faces, 6 edges, 4 vertices**, all edges equal. - ☐ Standard position: **base on HP, one base edge parallel to VP**, apex above centroid. - ☐ Height of tetrahedron: $H = \sqrt{2/3} \cdot a \approx 0.8165\,a$. - ☐ A section plane always produces a **polygon (triangle or quadrilateral)**, never a curve. - ☐ **Section points in F.V. are first found**, then projected vertically to the T.V. - ☐ **True Shape is always on an auxiliary plane** parallel to the section line in F.V. - ☐ For SP **parallel to HP**: T.S. is already visible in T.V. (no auxiliary needed). - ☐ For SP **through the axis**: by symmetry, T.S. is an equilateral triangle. - ☐ Always **label points in cyclic order** (1', 2', 3' in F.V.; 1, 2, 3 in T.V.; 1'', 2'', 3'' in auxiliary). - ☐ **Join section points in T.V. using straight lines** — they are chords of the solid's surface. - ☐ Final answer must include: (i) sectional T.V., (ii) sectional F.V. (if cuts modify it), (iii) true shape, (iv) area computation. - ☐ Use **construction arcs/compass marks** when transferring distances to auxiliary view — visible process marks. - ☐ Remember the formula for **horizontal section at height h**: $$ \frac{L_{\text{section}}}{L_{\text{base}}} = \frac{H - h}{H} $$ - ☐ Drawing conventions: **continuous thick lines for visible edges, dashed thin lines for hidden edges, centerline for axis.** - ☐ Don't forget the **title block**: name, roll no., scale, sheet no. — 1 mark guaranteed.

topics.title: Prisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: PRISMS — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Engineering Graphics and Computer Aided Drawing** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, a **Prism** is formally defined as a **polyhedron** (a solid bounded entirely by flat polygonal faces) generated by translating a planar polygon (the *base*) along a straight line (the *axis*) such that every point of the base moves parallel to the axis. The resulting solid has **two parallel, congruent polygonal bases** joined by **rectangular or parallelogram-shaped lateral faces**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):**
> The study of prisms in Engineering Graphics includes: orthographic projections (elevation, plan, and end view), true lengths of edges, the **development of lateral surfaces**, **sectioning of prisms** (with the true shape of section), and the construction of **truncated prisms**. A solid knowledge of these concepts is mandatory for Part B (14-mark) questions in ESE.

### 1.2 Anatomy of a Prism — Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Base** | The two parallel, identical polygonal faces (top and bottom). |
| **Axis** | The imaginary line joining the centers of the two bases. |
| **Height (H)** | The perpendicular distance between the two bases. |
| **Lateral Faces** | The rectangular faces connecting the corresponding edges of the two bases. |
| **Lateral Edges** | The line segments connecting corresponding vertices of the two bases. |
| **Apex** | A prism has **no apex** (unlike a pyramid). |

> [!NOTE]
> A prism with a *regular polygon* as base and with the axis *perpendicular* to the base is called a **Right Regular Prism**. This is the only class of prisms typically asked in KTU examinations.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toblerone" Visualization

Imagine the famous **Toblerone chocolate bar**. The triangular cross-section is repeated identically as you slide the bar along its length. The triangular faces at the two ends are the **bases**, the three flat sides connecting them are the **lateral faces**, and the long edges where these sides meet are the **lateral edges**. Now imagine the same principle applied to a square, pentagon, or hexagon — you get a square prism, pentagonal prism, or hexagonal prism respectively. The same logic applies to a **hexagonal pencil** (hexagonal prism) or a **rectangular brick** (rectangular prism).

## 1.4 Classification of Prisms

Prisms are classified based on two criteria:

**A) Based on the Shape of the Base:**
- Triangular Prism
- Square Prism (also called a *rectangular prism* if the cross-section is rectangular, or *cube* if all sides are equal)
- Pentagonal Prism
- Hexagonal Prism
- *In general, an **n-gonal prism** has an n-sided polygon as its base.*

**B) Based on the Orientation of the Axis:**
- **Right Prism**: The axis is perpendicular to the base (lateral faces are rectangles).
- **Oblique Prism**: The axis is inclined to the base (lateral faces are parallelograms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 questions are **exclusively limited to Right Regular Prisms** with their axes either vertical or inclined to the ground (HP) or the profile plane (VP).

## 1.5 Euler's Formula Verification

For any right regular prism with an **n-sided polygonal base**, the number of faces (F), edges (E), and vertices (V) are given by:

$$V = 2n$$
$$E = 3n$$
$$F = n + 2$$

Euler's formula states:

$$V - E + F = 2$$

Substituting the values:

$$2n - 3n + (n + 2) = 2 \implies 2n - 3n + n + 2 = 2 \implies 2 = 2 \;\;\checkmark$$

This confirms Euler's formula is satisfied for all prisms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 3: PRISMS — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Properties of a Right Regular Prism

A right regular prism with an *n-sided regular polygon* of side *a* as the base, and height *H*, possesses the following high-yield properties:

### 2.1.1 Base Geometry
- **Interior angle of base polygon**: $\theta = \dfrac{(n - 2) \times 180^\circ}{n}$
- **Number of base diagonals**: $\dfrac{n(n - 3)}{2}$
- **Apothem (a)** of the regular polygon: $a = \dfrac{s}{2 \tan(180^\circ / n)}$, where $s$ is the side length.

### 2.1.2 Lateral Surface and Total Surface
- **Lateral Surface Area (LSA)**: $LSA = n \cdot s \cdot H$, where $s$ is the side length of the base and $H$ is the height.
- **Total Surface Area (TSA)**: $TSA = 2A_{base} + LSA$, where $A_{base}$ is the area of the base polygon.
- **Volume**: $V = A_{base} \times H$

### 2.1.3 Projections of a Prism
When a right regular prism is placed with its base on the **Horizontal Plane (HP)** and axis vertical, the standard orthographic projections in **First Angle Projection** (used in KTU) are:

- **Top View (Plan)**: A true-shape polygon (the base) appears as a true regular polygon.
- **Front View (Elevation)**: A rectangle of width equal to the side of the base and height equal to the prism height.
- **Side View (End View)**: A rectangle of width equal to the apothem or diameter (depending on orientation) and height equal to the prism height.

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of position of the base in the top view is critical. In KTU problems, the base is **always drawn such that two of its edges are parallel to the XY reference line**. This makes the front view a clean rectangle, with all lateral edges appearing as True Length (TL).

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you need for prism problems in the KTU ESE. **Memorize this table thoroughly.**

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| **Lateral Edge True Length** | $TL = \sqrt{H^2 + a^2}$ | For a right prism (no inclination), $TL = H$. |
| **Base Polygon Interior Angle** | $\theta = \dfrac{(n-2) \cdot 180^\circ}{n}$ | Used to construct the true-shape base. |
| **Apothem (a) of Base** | $a = \dfrac{s}{2 \tan(180^\circ/n)}$ | Distance from center to the middle of a side. |
| **Circumradius (R) of Base** | $R = \dfrac{s}{2 \sin(180^\circ/n)}$ | Distance from center to a vertex. |
| **Distance from Center to a Vertex in Top View** | As per polygon geometry | True length of lateral edge in top view when axis is inclined. |
| **Lateral Surface Area (LSA)** | $LSA = P \cdot H$ | Where $P = n \cdot s$ is the perimeter of the base. |
| **Perimeter of Base** | $P = n \cdot s$ | $n$ = number of sides, $s$ = side length. |
| **Volume** | $V = A_{base} \cdot H$ | $A_{base}$ is the area of the base polygon. |
| **Number of Faces** | $F = n + 2$ | Top + Base + n lateral faces. |
| **Number of Edges** | $E = 3n$ | $n$ base edges + $n$ top edges + $n$ lateral edges. |
| **Number of Vertices** | $V = 2n$ | $n$ on the bottom base + $n$ on the top base. |

## 2.3 Inclined Prisms — The Critical Concept

When the axis of a right prism is **inclined to the HP** (but perpendicular to the VP), a critical change occurs in the orthographic views:

- The **top view** still shows the true shape of the base, but it is foreshortened in the direction of inclination.
- The **front view** is foreshortened: the height of the rectangle reduces to $H \cos(\alpha)$, where $\alpha$ is the angle of inclination of the axis with HP.
- The **true length of the lateral edges** is visible only in the top view (if the base is on HP and axis is inclined to HP) — wait, more precisely:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb for Inclined Prisms:**
> 1. If the **axis is inclined to HP** (and ⊥ to VP): The **top view** shows the true shape of the base; the **lateral edges appear in their true length in the top view** because they are parallel to HP.
> 2. If the **axis is inclined to VP** (and ⊥ to HP): The **front view** shows the true shape of the base; the **lateral edges appear in their true length in the front view** because they are parallel to VP.

This is one of the most tested concepts in KTU Module 3.

## 2.4 Sectioning of Prisms — Terminology

When a prism is cut by an **inclined cutting plane**, two new concepts emerge:

- **Section Plane**: The imaginary plane that slices the solid.
- **Section Line / Sectional View**: The line of intersection of the cutting plane with the lateral surface of the solid (seen in the elevation where the cutting plane appears as a line).
- **True Shape of Section**: The actual shape and size of the cut surface. To obtain the true shape, the section line is projected **perpendicular to itself** onto an auxiliary plane parallel to the cutting plane.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **triangular prism** cut by a plane inclined to the base: the true shape of section is a **triangle**. For a **square prism**: a **quadrilateral**. For a **hexagonal prism**: a **hexagon** (in general, an **n-gon** for an n-gonal prism if cut above the base level).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Prism-shaped objects are ubiquitous in engineering:
- **Triangular prism**: Roof trusses, Toblerone packaging, optical prisms.
- **Square/Rectangular prism**: Bricks, concrete blocks, structural I-beams (the web).
- **Pentagonal prism**: Specialized architectural columns.
- **Hexagonal prism**: Nuts and bolts, pencil barrels, basalt columns (Giant's Causeway).

The ability to draw orthographic projections of these shapes, develop their surfaces (for sheet metal work), and visualize sectional cuts is foundational for **mechanical design, civil drafting, and manufacturing engineering**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 3: PRISMS — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — True Length of Lateral Edges in an Inclined Prism

Consider a **right regular hexagonal prism** of side length $s$ and height $H$. It is placed with its base on the HP and its axis inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the HP (and perpendicular to the VP).

### Step 1: Construct the Top View (True Shape of Base)

The base is a regular hexagon of side $s$. The **apothem** of this hexagon is the distance from the center to the midpoint of a side:

$$a = \frac{s}{2 \tan(180^\circ/6)} = \frac{s}{2 \tan(30^\circ)} = \frac{s}{2 \times (1/\sqrt{3})} = \frac{s\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

The **circumradius** (center to vertex) is:

$$R = \frac{s}{2 \sin(180^\circ/6)} = \frac{s}{2 \sin(30^\circ)} = \frac{s}{2 \times 0.5} = s$$

### Step 2: Locate the Center of the Hexagon in the Top View

The center of the hexagon, denoted as **O**, is the projection of the axis. The top view shows the hexagon with center at O, and the axis of the prism is **perpendicular to the HP**, so the axis appears as a single point at O in the top view.

### Step 3: Draw the Front View (Initial Position)

In the front view, the prism appears as a rectangle:
- **Width of rectangle**: Equal to the distance between the parallel sides of the hexagon in the top view that are parallel to the VP.
- **Height of rectangle**: Equal to the actual prism height $H$.

### Step 4: Apply the Inclination to the Axis

The axis is now inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the HP. The new top view is obtained by rotating the original hexagon about a point on the XY line until the axis makes angle $\alpha$ with the XY line. The new front view is then obtained by projecting the new positions of the top vertices vertically upward by the height $H$.

### Step 5: True Length of Lateral Edges in the Top View

In the new (inclined) top view, the distance from the base vertex to its corresponding top vertex is the **true length of the lateral edge** in the top view. This is found by:

$$TL_{top} = \sqrt{(\text{horizontal displacement of top vertex in top view})^2 + (\text{vertical displacement in front view})^2}$$

But since the axis is inclined at $\alpha$ to HP and the lateral edge is parallel to the axis, the lateral edge also makes angle $\alpha$ with the HP. Thus:

$$TL_{lateral\;edge} = \sqrt{H^2 + (\text{base horizontal shift})^2}$$

More directly, using the **rotation principle**:

$$\text{True length of lateral edge} = \frac{H}{\cos(\alpha)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this?** When the axis is inclined at $\alpha$ to the HP, the lateral edge in the front view appears as a line of length $H \cos(\alpha)$ (foreshortened). Since the actual edge length is $H$, the true length is recovered by dividing by $\cos(\alpha)$ — this is the inverse of the foreshortening factor.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Procedure — Construction of Orthographic Projections

Let us now derive the construction procedure for a **pentagonal prism** (5-sided) of side $s = 30$ mm and height $H = 50$ mm, placed with the base on HP and axis vertical, with one side of the base parallel to VP.

### Step 1: Draw the Reference Line (XY)

Draw a horizontal line representing the **XY line** (the reference line between the Front View above and the Top View below).

### Step 2: Construct the True-Shape Base in the Top View

A regular pentagon has interior angles:

$$\theta = \frac{(5 - 2) \times 180^\circ}{5} = \frac{540^\circ}{5} = 108^\circ$$

The apothem is:

$$a = \frac{30}{2 \tan(36^\circ)} \approx \frac{30}{2 \times 0.7265} \approx 20.65 \text{ mm}$$

The circumradius is:

$$R = \frac{30}{2 \sin(36^\circ)} \approx \frac{30}{2 \times 0.5878} \approx 25.52 \text{ mm}$$

Draw the pentagon in the top view such that one side is parallel to the XY line. Mark the center **O** and the five vertices as $a, b, c, d, e$ (where $ab$ is parallel to XY).

### Step 3: Project Vertices Vertically Upward to the Front View

From each vertex in the top view, draw **vertical projectors** upward. These projectors will define the base line of the front view rectangle.

### Step 4: Draw the Front View Rectangle

At a vertical distance of $H = 50$ mm above the base, draw a parallel line. The front view of the prism is a rectangle whose:
- **Lower edge** is the projection of the base $ab$.
- **Upper edge** is the projection of the top base $a'b'$.
- **Lateral edges** are vertical lines of length $H$ at positions corresponding to vertices $a$ and $b$ (since only $a$ and $b$ are visible as the extreme lateral edges in the front view, the others are hidden behind).

### Step 5: Locate the Hidden Vertices (if required)

The vertices $c, d, e$ in the top view project to points lying **between** $a$ and $b$ in the front view. These are marked as **hidden lines** (dashed) at the base and top.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Development of Lateral Surface of a Prism

The **development** of a prism is the unrolled (flattened) lateral surface. For a right regular prism, the development is a **rectangle** of dimensions $P \times H$, where $P$ is the perimeter of the base.

### Case 1: Triangular Prism
A regular triangular prism of side $s$ and height $H$ develops into a rectangle of length $3s$ (the perimeter) and height $H$. The two triangular bases are attached on either end.

### Case 2: Hexagonal Prism
A regular hexagonal prism of side $s$ and height $H$ develops into a rectangle of length $6s$ and height $H$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The development is a rectangle **only if the axis is perpendicular to the base**. If the prism is truncated (cut by an inclined plane), the development becomes a **trapezoidal shape** with the shorter parallel side equal to the length of the upper base and the longer parallel side equal to the length of the lower base (assuming the cut is from one base to the other).

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Construction of the True Shape of Section

Consider a hexagonal prism with its axis vertical, cut by a plane inclined at angle $\theta$ to the HP. The procedure to find the true shape of section is:

### Step 1: Identify the Section Points
The section points are the points where the cutting plane intersects the lateral edges of the prism. In the front view, the cutting plane appears as a line inclined at $\theta$ to the XY line. The points $1', 2', 3', 4', 5', 6'$ on this line correspond to the intersections with the lateral edges.

### Step 2: Project Section Points to the Top View
From each section point in the front view, draw a **vertical projector downward** to intersect the corresponding lateral edge in the top view. These intersections in the top view are the section points $1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$.

### Step 3: Draw the Section Line in the Top View
Connect the section points $1-2-3-4-5-6$ in the top view in order. This is the **section line** as seen in the top view (foreshortened).

### Step 4: Project to an Auxiliary Plane
To obtain the true shape, draw an **auxiliary reference line** parallel to the section line in the front view. From each section point in the front view, project **perpendicular** to this auxiliary line. Then transfer the **distances from the center (axis) in the top view** to the corresponding perpendicular projectors in the auxiliary view.

The lengths transferred are:

$$d_i = \text{distance from axis (O) to point } i \text{ in the top view}$$

The resulting closed polygon $1''-2''-3''-4''-5''-6''$ is the **true shape of section** — a regular hexagon (if the cutting plane passes through all six lateral edges and is parallel to the base) or a distorted hexagon (if the cutting plane is inclined).

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A regular pentagonal prism of side of base $30$ mm and height of axis $50$ mm rests on HP on one of its base edges such that the axis is parallel to VP and the base is inclined at $30^\circ$ to HP. Draw the projections.

### Step 1: Draw the Resting Position (Parellel to HP)
Assume initially the base is on HP and axis is vertical. Draw the true shape of the pentagon in the top view with one side (say $ab$) parallel to XY.

### Step 2: Apply the Inclination
The axis must be parallel to VP, meaning the front view of the axis is vertical. To achieve this, **redraw the top view** by rotating the pentagon such that the base edge $ab$ is inclined at $30^\circ$ to XY (or equivalently, the base makes $30^\circ$ with HP). Now the axis (perpendicular to the base) will be inclined at $60^\circ$ to HP in the front view.

Wait — re-reading the problem: the base is on HP on one of its base edges, axis is parallel to VP, and the base is inclined at $30^\circ$ to HP. So the base $ab$ is on HP, and the rest of the pentagon is tilted up. The axis is perpendicular to the base, so in the front view, the axis makes an angle of $90^\circ - 30^\circ = 60^\circ$ with the XY line.

### Step 3: Front View Construction
In the front view, project the new top view positions of $a$ and $b$ vertically. The line $a'b'$ is the base edge. The axis of the prism is drawn from the midpoint of $a'b'$ at $60^\circ$ to the XY line (going up and to the right if inclined rightward).

### Step 4: Mark the Top Base
From the front view point of $a'$, measure a length of 50 mm along the axis direction to locate the top base $a_1'b_1'$ — wait, no. The top base must be drawn perpendicular to the axis at a distance of $H = 50$ mm.

The correct procedure:
- The axis is the line $O-O_1$ in the front view, where $O$ is on $a'b'$ (since the base is on HP, $O$ is in the XY line) and $O_1$ is the top center, at a perpendicular distance of $H = 50$ mm from $O$ along the axis direction.
- The top base $a_1'b_1'$ is drawn perpendicular to the axis at $O_1$, with the same length as $a'b'$.

### Step 5: Project Top Vertices to the Top View
The top vertices are projected back to the top view by drawing lines from the front view parallel to the XY line. Their final positions are determined by their distances from the base edge $ab$ in the original pentagon geometry.

This is the standard KTU procedure. The key insight is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The most common mistake** students make is to draw the top base parallel to the base (XY line) instead of **perpendicular to the inclined axis**. The top base is always drawn perpendicular to the axis, just like the original base.

# MODULE 3: PRISMS — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Construction Procedure for Prism Projections

The following Mermaid diagram maps out the complete step-by-step procedure for drawing the orthographic projections of an inclined prism. This is the **decision tree** every KTU student must internalize.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read KTU Problem Statement] --> B{Identify Prism Type}
    B --> C[Triangular Prism]
    B --> D[Square Prism]
    B --> E[Pentagonal Prism]
    B --> F[Hexagonal Prism]
    
    C --> G[Draw XY Reference Line]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    
    G --> H[Draw True Shape Base in Top View]
    H --> I{Determine Inclination}
    
    I --> J[Axis Inclined to HP]
    I --> K[Axis Inclined to VP]
    I --> L[Base Inclined to HP]
    I --> M[No Inclination - Simple Position]
    
    J --> N[Apply Rotation in Top View - Lateral Edges Parallel to XY]
    K --> O[Apply Rotation in Front View - Lateral Edges Parallel to XY]
    L --> P[Apply Rotation in Top View - Base Edge Inclined]
    M --> Q[Skip Rotation - Direct Projection]
    
    N --> R[Project Vertically to Front View]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    
    R --> S[Draw Top Base Perpendicular to Axis in Front View]
    S --> T[Project Top Vertices Back to Top View]
    T --> U{Marking and Dimensioning}
    
    U --> V[Mark All Hidden Lines as Dashed]
    U --> W[Add Center Line for Axis]
    U --> X[Dimension Key Distances - Base Side, Height, Angle]
    
    V --> Y[Final Orthographic Projection]
    W --> Y
    X --> Y
    
    Y --> Z[End]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style Z fill:#d4edda
    style B fill:#fff3cd
    style I fill:#fff3cd
    style U fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Flow — Sectioning and True Shape Extraction

The following diagram outlines the procedure for finding the **true shape of section** when a prism is cut by an inclined plane.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Prism Cut by Inclined Plane] --> B[Identify Cutting Plane in Problem]
    B --> C[Locate Section Points in Front View]
    C --> D[Section Points on Lateral Edges in Front View]
    
    D --> E[Project Section Points Vertically Down to Top View]
    E --> F[Mark Intersections on Lateral Edges in Top View]
    F --> G[Connect Section Points in Top View - Foreshortened Section Line]
    
    G --> H[Draw Auxiliary Reference Line Parallel to Section Line]
    H --> I[Project Section Points Perpendicular to Auxiliary Line]
    I --> J[Transfer Distances from Axis in Top View to Auxiliary View]
    
    J --> K[Connect Transferred Points in Order]
    K --> L[Result: True Shape of Section - Polygon]
    
    L --> M{Identify True Shape Type}
    M --> N[Triangle for Triangular Prism]
    M --> O[Quadrilateral for Square Prism]
    M --> P[Pentagon for Pentagonal Prism]
    M --> Q[Hexagon for Hexagonal Prism]
    
    N --> R[End]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style R fill:#d4edda
    style B fill:#fff3cd
    style M fill:#f8d7da
    style L fill:#d1ecf1
```

# MODULE 3: PRISMS — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define a prism. List the different types of prisms based on the shape of the base.**

**Model Answer:**

A prism is a polyhedron having two parallel and congruent polygonal bases joined by rectangular or parallelogram lateral faces. The line joining the centers of the two bases is called the axis of the prism.

Types of prisms based on the base shape:
1. **Triangular Prism** — base is a triangle
2. **Square Prism** — base is a square
3. **Pentagonal Prism** — base is a pentagon
4. **Hexagonal Prism** — base is a hexagon
5. In general, an **n-gonal prism** has an n-sided polygon as its base.

*(Word count: ~50 words. KTU expects a concise, well-structured answer with clear classification.)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**State Euler's formula. Verify it for a hexagonal prism.**

**Model Answer:**

Euler's formula for any convex polyhedron states:

$$V - E + F = 2$$

where V = number of vertices, E = number of edges, F = number of faces.

**For a hexagonal prism (n = 6):**
- Number of vertices: $V = 2n = 2 \times 6 = 12$
- Number of edges: $E = 3n = 3 \times 6 = 18$
- Number of faces: $F = n + 2 = 6 + 2 = 8$

**Verification:**

$$V - E + F = 12 - 18 + 8 = 2 \;\;\checkmark$$

Hence, Euler's formula is verified.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Center Line (axis)**: The axis of the prism must be drawn as a center line (chain dotted line) passing through the midpoint of the base in both views. Missing this costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Not showing hidden lines correctly**: When the prism is in an inclined position, all hidden edges (e.g., the edges behind the visible faces) must be drawn as **dashed lines**. Omitting or incorrectly drawing hidden lines costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Mistake in the rotation direction**: When the base is rotated to achieve the required inclination, the rotation must be in the **correct direction** (clockwise or counter-clockwise). Drawing the rotated base in the wrong quadrant will lead to an **incorrect front view**, costing **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Not drawing the top base perpendicular to the inclined axis**: This is the single most common error. The top base is **always drawn perpendicular to the axis** (not parallel to XY) — failing this costs **2–4 marks**.
> 5. **Missing dimensions**: KTU mandates dimensioning of the side of the base, height of the prism, and angle of inclination. Missing any of these costs **1 mark each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**A regular pentagonal prism of side of base $40$ mm and height of axis $65$ mm rests on HP on one of its base edges with the axis parallel to VP. Draw the projections of the prism when the base is inclined at $35^\circ$ to HP.**

#### Part (a): Construct the Top View and Front View of the Prism. **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Draw the XY reference line** horizontally across the middle of the drawing area.

2. **Initial Top View (True Shape Base)**: Draw a regular pentagon of side 40 mm in the top view, with one of its sides (say $ab$) **parallel to the XY line**. The apothem of this pentagon is:

$$a = \frac{40}{2 \tan(36^\circ)} = \frac{40}{2 \times 0.7265} \approx 27.53 \text{ mm}$$

Mark the center as $O$ and the vertices as $a, b, c, d, e$ in clockwise order.

3. **Initial Front View (Simple Position)**: Project the vertices $a$ and $b$ vertically to the front view. Draw a rectangle of width $ab$ (= 40 mm) and height 65 mm. The front view shows the lateral edges $a'a_1'$ and $b'b_1'$ as vertical lines of length 65 mm. (Other vertices $c', d', e'$ are hidden behind, drawn as dashed lines.)

4. **Apply the Inclination**: Redraw the top view by rotating the pentagon about the edge $ab$ (which is the resting edge and acts as the axis of rotation) such that the pentagon is inclined at $35^\circ$ to the XY line. This means the line joining the center $O$ to the midpoint of $ab$ is now at $35^\circ$ to the XY line.

5. **New Front View**: Project the new top view positions of all five vertices vertically to the front view. The base edge $a'b'$ is on the XY line. The center of the new top view ($O'$) projects to a point on the XY line.

6. **Draw the Axis in Front View**: The axis of the prism passes through the center $O$ and is perpendicular to the base. In the front view, the axis appears as a line inclined at $(90^\circ - 35^\circ) = 55^\circ$ to the XY line. Draw the axis as a center line from $O'$ at $55^\circ$.

7. **Locate the Top Base**: From $O'$ on the XY line, measure a perpendicular distance of 65 mm along the axis direction to locate $O_1'$ (the center of the top base). Draw the top base $a_1'b_1'$ perpendicular to the axis, with the same length as $a'b'$ (= 40 mm).

8. **Project Top Vertices Back**: From the front view, project the top vertices $a_1', b_1', c_1', d_1', e_1'$ horizontally back to the top view to complete the inclined projections.

9. **Marking**: Add the chain-dotted center line for the axis, draw all hidden edges as dashed lines, and dimension the base side (40 mm), height (65 mm), and angle of inclination (35°).

#### Part (b): Develop the lateral surface of the prism and find the true shape of the section, if it is cut by a vertical plane passing through the midpoint of the axis and inclined at $45^\circ$ to the VP. **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Development of the Lateral Surface**: The development of a right regular pentagonal prism is a rectangle of length equal to the perimeter of the base and height equal to the axis height. The perimeter is:

$$P = 5 \times 40 = 200 \text{ mm}$$

Draw a horizontal base line of length 200 mm in the development view. Mark the positions of the five base vertices ($a, b, c, d, e$) along this line at intervals of 40 mm. At each point, draw a vertical line of length 65 mm (the true length of the lateral edges). Connect the top ends to form the development — a rectangle of 200 mm × 65 mm.

2. **Sectioning with the Vertical Cutting Plane**: In the top view, the cutting plane is a line inclined at $45^\circ$ to the XY line, passing through the midpoint of the axis.

3. **Locate Section Points**: In the top view, the cutting plane intersects the five lateral edges. The intersection points (section points) are labeled $1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ in the top view.

4. **Project to Front View**: Project these section points vertically to the front view, where they intersect the corresponding lateral edges at $1', 2', 3', 4', 5'$.

5. **Transfer Heights to Development**: Measure the vertical distances of each section point from the base in the front view. Transfer these heights to the corresponding vertical lines in the development. Connect the points to get the **truncated development** — a pentagonal shape with the top edge inclined.

6. **True Shape of Section**: The true shape is a pentagon (since the cutting plane intersects all five lateral edges of the pentagonal prism). To find the true shape:
   - Draw an auxiliary reference line in the front view parallel to the cutting plane line.
   - Project the section points perpendicular to this auxiliary line.
   - Transfer the perpendicular distances from the axis to the section points in the top view.
   - Connect the resulting points to obtain the true shape of the section — a distorted pentagon.

7. **Marking**: Add the development dimensions (200 mm × 65 mm) and label the true shape clearly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**A regular hexagonal prism of side of base $30$ mm and height of axis $50$ mm rests on HP on one of its triangular faces (i.e., one of its base edges is on HP, with two adjacent faces visible). The axis is parallel to VP. Draw the projections when the axis is inclined at $30^\circ$ to VP.**

#### Part (a): Construct the Front View and Top View in the Inclined Position. **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Draw the XY line** and the **true-shape hexagonal base** in the top view, with one side $ab$ perpendicular to the XY line (so that $ab$ is on HP and the prism rests on the base edge $ab$).

2. **Initial Front View**: Project the base edge $ab$ vertically to the front view. The base appears as a vertical line segment $a'b'$. The height of the front view is 50 mm, so the top base edge $a_1'b_1'$ is drawn 50 mm directly above $a'b'$.

3. **Project Other Vertices**: The other four vertices $c, d, e, f$ are projected to the front view at the same height as $b$ (since they are at the same elevation as the base in this position).

4. **Apply the Inclination**: Since the axis must be inclined at $30^\circ$ to VP, **redraw the front view** by rotating the rectangle about the edge $a'b'$ (the resting edge in the front view) such that the axis is now inclined at $30^\circ$ to the XY line. The new front view is a parallelogram, with the base $a'b'$ vertical on the XY line and the top base inclined at $30°$ to the horizontal.

5. **New Top View**: Project the inclined front view back to the top view. The base edge $ab$ is in its original position. The top vertices are now displaced from their original positions.

6. **Marking**: Add center line for the axis, hidden lines for invisible edges, and dimensions (30 mm side, 50 mm height, 30° angle).

#### Part (b): A horizontal cutting plane passes through the midpoint of the axis. Draw the sectional front view and the true shape of the section. **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Locate the Cutting Plane**: The horizontal cutting plane passes through the midpoint of the axis. In the front view, this appears as a horizontal line at a height of 25 mm above the base (since the axis height is 50 mm).

2. **Identify Section Points**: The cutting plane intersects all six lateral edges of the prism at their midpoints. The section points in the front view are at the midpoints of $a'a_1', b'b_1', c'c_1', d'd_1', e'e_1', f'f_1'$.

3. **Draw the Section Line**: Connect the section points in the front view — they form a straight horizontal line. Project them to the top view to get the section line, which is a regular hexagon (foreshortened) showing the cross-section.

4. **Sectional Front View**: The portion of the prism above the cutting plane is removed (or shown in section with hatching). Draw the hatching at 45° with proper spacing.

5. **True Shape of Section**: Since the cutting plane is horizontal (perpendicular to the vertical axis), the section is a **regular hexagon** — the same shape as the base, with side length 30 mm. The true shape is obtained by drawing a regular hexagon of side 30 mm adjacent to the top view, with the section line coinciding with one side of the hexagon.

6. **Marking**: Hatch the section properly, label the cutting plane, and dimension the true shape (30 mm × 6 sides = 180 mm perimeter).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Prisms:**

- [ ] **Definition**: A prism has **two parallel, congruent polygonal bases** and rectangular lateral faces. It has **no apex** (this distinguishes it from a pyramid).

- [ ] **Classification**: Triangular, square, pentagonal, hexagonal, n-gonal. **Right regular prisms** are the KTU standard.

- [ ] **Euler's Formula Verification**: For n-gonal prism: $V = 2n$, $E = 3n$, $F = n + 2$. Always verify that $V - E + F = 2$.

- [ ] **True Length Rule (KTU High-Yield)**:
  - Axis inclined to HP → **Lateral edges appear in true length in the TOP view**.
  - Axis inclined to VP → **Lateral edges appear in true length in the FRONT view**.

- [ ] **Apothem Formula**: $a = \dfrac{s}{2 \tan(180^\circ/n)}$ — used to construct the true-shape base in the top view.

- [ ] **Circumradius Formula**: $R = \dfrac{s}{2 \sin(180^\circ/n)}$ — used to locate vertices of the base polygon.

- [ ] **Development**: The lateral surface of a right prism develops into a **rectangle of dimensions P × H** (perimeter × height).

- [ ] **Truncated Development**: If the prism is cut by a plane, the development is a **trapezoidal shape**, with the two parallel sides being the lengths of the lower and upper truncated edges.

- [ ] **True Shape of Section**: Obtained by projecting section points onto an **auxiliary plane parallel to the cutting plane**. Transfer distances perpendicular to the auxiliary line from the top view.

- [ ] **Top Base Direction**: The top base is **always drawn perpendicular to the axis**, never parallel to the XY line (except in the simple position with no inclination).

- [ ] **Hidden Lines**: All edges not visible in a view must be drawn as **dashed lines**. The center line (axis) is drawn as a **chain-dotted line**.

- [ ] **Dimensioning**: Always dimension the side of the base ($s$), height of the prism ($H$), and angle of inclination ($\alpha$).

- [ ] **Sectioning Hatching**: When drawing a sectional view, hatch the cut surface with parallel lines at **45°** with uniform spacing (typically 3–5 mm).

- [ ] **Common Mistake Alert**: Students often forget to draw the **center line through the axis**. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

- [ ] **Working in First Angle Projection**: KTU follows **First Angle Projection** (the top view is below the front view, separated by the XY line). The left side view is on the right, and the right side view is on the left.

- [ ] **Number of Problems to Practice**: For a 14-mark question, expect a problem involving (i) inclination, (ii) true length identification, and (iii) development and/or true shape of section. **Practice at least 5 different inclination scenarios.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pyramids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pyramids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **PYRAMID — KTU Formal Definition**
> A **Pyramid** is a polyhedral solid bounded by a *planar polygonal base* and a set of *triangular lateral faces* that converge to a common point called the **apex (or vertex)**. When the apex is connected to all vertices of the base by straight edges, the solid is said to be a regular pyramid if its base is a regular polygon and the apex lies on the axis perpendicular to the base passing through its centroid.

In the context of **Engineering Graphics and Computer Aided Drawing (GCEST203, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3)**, pyramids are studied under the umbrella of **Projection of Solids**, where we represent three-dimensional pyramidal geometries using **First Angle Orthographic Projection** onto the two principal reference planes — the **Horizontal Plane (HP)** and the **Vertical Plane (VP)**.

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically expects students to:
1. Identify the **type of pyramid** (square, pentagonal, hexagonal, etc.).
2. Visualize the solid in **different spatial orientations** — resting on base, resting on a lateral face, or resting on a lateral edge.
3. Draw the **orthographic projections (Top View + Front View)** with the axis inclined to a reference plane.
4. Determine the **apparent shape, apparent length, and true length** of the lateral edges, base diagonals, and slant heights.
5. Construct the **sectional view** when the solid is cut by a section plane, and develop the **lateral surface (development)**.

## 1.2 Types of Pyramids Encountered in KTU Examinations

| S.No | Pyramid Type | Base Polygon | No. of Lateral Faces | No. of Lateral Edges | Typical KTU Weightage |
|:----:|:-------------|:------------:|:--------------------:|:--------------------:|:---------------------:|
| 1 | **Triangular Pyramid (Tetrahedron)** | Equilateral Triangle | 3 | 3 | Low (rare) |
| 2 | **Square Pyramid** | Square | 4 | 4 | **Very High** ⭐ |
| 3 | **Pentagonal Pyramid** | Regular Pentagon | 5 | 5 | Medium |
| 4 | **Hexagonal Pyramid** | Regular Hexagon | 6 | 6 | **Very High** ⭐ |
| 5 | **Octagonal Pyramid** | Regular Octagon | 8 | 8 | Low (rare) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** The most frequently tested pyramids in KTU university examinations are the **Square Pyramid** and the **Hexagonal Pyramid**, with the axis inclined to the **Horizontal Plane (HP)** and perpendicular to the **Vertical Plane (VP)** — this is the *standard "tilted" position* that forms the backbone of 14-mark questions.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Visualize Before You Draw!**
> Imagine a **child's toy blocks** stacked: take a square wooden base and balance 4 triangular cardboard pieces on its edges, pinching them together at a single point at the top, secured with a pin. That pointed tip is the **apex**, the wooden base is the **base**, the cardboard triangles are the **lateral faces**, and the seams where adjacent triangles meet are the **lateral edges**. The straight line drawn from the apex straight down to the center of the base is the **axis**. If you tilt the whole toy slightly forward (like the Leaning Tower of Pisa), the axis is no longer vertical — it becomes **inclined to the HP**. *This tilted configuration is exactly the KTU exam problem.*

A second powerful analogy: think of the **Egyptian pyramids** (square base, apex at the top) — these are *right* pyramids. Now tilt one of them sideways so it rests on a corner edge — that becomes an *inclined* pyramid with its axis inclined to the HP, the standard KTU drawing exercise.

## 1.4 Geometric Vocabulary — Must-Know Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terms Every KTU Candidate Must Memorize**

- **Base:** The polygon (triangle, square, pentagon, hexagon, etc.) lying on the reference plane.
- **Apex (Vertex):** The single point where all lateral edges meet.
- **Axis:** The imaginary line joining the apex to the *centroid* of the regular base. In a *right pyramid*, the axis is **perpendicular** to the base.
- **Height (Altitude):** The perpendicular distance from the apex to the base plane. Represented as **h** or **H**.
- **Lateral Edge (Slant Edge):** The straight line from a base corner to the apex. Length denoted as **L** or **l**.
- **Lateral Face:** The triangular surface bounded by two adjacent lateral edges and one side of the base.
- **Slant Height (l₁):** The perpendicular distance from the apex to a *side* of the base (i.e., the altitude of a lateral face).
- **Apothem of Base (a):** The perpendicular distance from the center of a regular base to the midpoint of any side.
- **Circumradius of Base (R):** The distance from the center of a regular base to any vertex of the base.
- **Front View (Elevation):** The projection on the **Vertical Plane (VP)**.
- **Top View (Plan):** The projection on the **Horizontal Plane (HP)**.

## 1.5 Standard Notation Used in This Module

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|:------:|:--------|:----:|
| **H** | True height of the pyramid (axis length) | mm |
| **a** | Side length of the regular base polygon | mm |
| **n** | Number of sides of the base polygon | — |
| **R** | Circumradius of the base (center to vertex) | mm |
| **aₚ** | Apothem of the base (center to side midpoint) | mm |
| **L** | True length of a lateral edge (apex to base vertex) | mm |
| **l₁** | Slant height of a lateral face (apex to base side midpoint) | mm |
| **α** | Angle which the axis makes with the HP (in inclined problems) | degrees |
| **β** | Angle which the axis makes with the VP (rare cases) | degrees |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Construction — Why a Pyramid Looks the Way It Does

A pyramid is fundamentally a **cone with a polygonal base**. Every property of a cone (apex, axis, slant height) translates into a polygonal counterpart. The KTU 2024 Module 3 expects you to:
