
### 4.5 Text-Schematic — The Three Standard Pyramids Compared

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Visual Cheat (Top View Identification):**
> - **Square Pyramid** top view: 4-sided figure with one corner pointing toward viewer (looks like a diamond/rhombus). Has 4 slant edges meeting at apex.
> - **Pentagonal Pyramid** top view: 5-sided figure (regular pentagon) with one side parallel to XY. Has 5 slant edges meeting at apex.
> - **Hexagonal Pyramid** top view: 6-sided figure (regular hexagon) with two opposite sides parallel to XY. Has 6 slant edges meeting at apex.
> - All three look like a "star" or "spider" pattern when the slant edges are drawn from the base corners to the centre top point (which is the apex projection in the top view).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Before the Exam!):**
> 1. **Forgetting to keep the top view unchanged** while rotating the front view — this is the #1 error. Students often redraw the top view in the inclined position, which is wrong. The base does not move.
> 2. **Confusing slant edge with slant height** in the question interpretation. "Slant edge" = apex to base corner. "Slant height" = apex to base side midpoint. They are different.
> 3. **Drawing the base square as a square with sides parallel/perpendicular to XY** instead of at 45° to XY. The condition "all edges of base equally inclined to VP" forces the 45° orientation.
> 4. **Measuring the angle of the slant edge with HP in the TOP view** by mistake. The angle must be measured in the **FRONT view**, since the front view preserves the inclination with HP.
> 5. **Forgetting to label the XY line** clearly and the new apex position (V₁, V₁′).
> 6. **Not dimensioning the key elements** — KTU valuation explicitly deducts marks if "Given data" and dimensions are not written on the sheet.
> 7. **Drawing hidden lines incorrectly** — the slant edges behind the pyramid should be **dashed thick lines**, not continuous.
> 8. **Not showing the construction lines** (projectors, locus lines, rotation arcs) — KTU expects construction lines to be visible as thin/light lines for partial credit.

---

### 5.2 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a right regular pyramid. List the standard pyramids studied in KTU Module 4 along with the number of lateral faces each has.**

**Model Answer:**

A **right regular pyramid** is a pyramid in which:
- (i) The base is a **regular polygon** (all sides equal and all angles equal).
- (ii) The **apex lies on the perpendicular line passing through the centroid of the base**, i.e., the axis is perpendicular to the base.

The standard pyramids in KTU 2024 Module 4 are:

| Pyramid | Base | Lateral Faces |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Square Pyramid | Square | **4** |
| Pentagonal Pyramid | Regular Pentagon | **5** |
| Hexagonal Pyramid | Regular Hexagon | **6** |

[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for table headers, 1 for correct lateral face count]

#### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the "slant edge" and "slant height" of a pyramid. Which one is longer?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Slant Edge | Slant Height |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Line joining apex to a **corner (vertex)** of the base | Perpendicular distance from apex to a **base side** (or its midpoint) |
| Geometric source | Uses **circumradius (R)** of base | Uses **apothem (r)** of base |
| Formula (square pyramid) | $\sqrt{h^{2} + \dfrac{a^{2}}{2}}$ | $\sqrt{h^{2} + \dfrac{a^{2}}{4}}$ |
| Length | **Longer** | **Shorter** |

Since the circumradius $R = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{2}}$ is greater than the apothem $r = \dfrac{a}{2}$ for a square base, the **slant edge is always longer** than the slant height for the same pyramid.

[3 marks: 1 for each clear distinction + 1 for the length comparison]

---

### 5.3 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** (either Question A OR Question B must be answered). Each question is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, typically one part focused on drawing/construction and the other on a derived quantity or specific sub-projection.

---

#### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024]`

**Statement:** A square pyramid of base side **40 mm** and height **60 mm** rests on its base on the HP with all the edges of the base equally inclined to the VP. It is then tilted such that its axis makes an angle of **30°** with the HP. Draw the projections of the pyramid. Also find the apparent inclination of the slant edge (a) with the HP.

**Sub-parts:**

- **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the orthographic projections (front view and top view) of the pyramid in both the initial and the inclined positions. Use proper line types, dimensioning, and labelling.
- **(b) [7 Marks]** Find the apparent angle of inclination of the slant edge "a" with HP by direct measurement on the front view. Also calculate the true length of the slant edge using the standard formula and compare.

---

**Model Solution for Question B-A:**

**Given Data:**
- Base side $a = 40$ mm
- True height $h = 60$ mm
- Axis inclination with HP, $\theta = 30°$
- Initial position: base on HP, all base edges equally inclined to VP
- Final position: axis inclined at 30° to HP, base still on HP

**Solution to Sub-part (a) — Drawing the Projections:**

*Step 1:* Draw the XY reference line in the middle of the sheet.

*Step 2:* In the **top view** (below XY), draw a **square of side 40 mm** with its two opposite sides inclined at 45° to the XY line. This satisfies the "all base edges equally inclined to VP" condition. Mark the four base corners as $a$ (front), $b$ (right), $c$ (back), $d$ (left). Mark the centroid as $O$.

*Step 3:* In the **initial front view** (above XY, left side), project the base corners upwards. They all lie on a horizontal line of length equal to the diagonal of the square:

$$\text{Diagonal} = a\sqrt{2} = 40 \times 1.414 = 56.57 \text{ mm}$$

Mark the centre of this line as $O'$. Draw a vertical line $O'V'$ of length 60 mm to represent the axis. The initial front view is a **triangle with base 56.57 mm and height 60 mm**, with apex $V'$.

*Step 4:* **To tilt the pyramid:** Keep the top view unchanged. In the initial front view, rotate the line $O'V'$ about point $O'$ by 30° (tilt to the left is standard). The new apex position is $V_1'$. The base of the triangle remains the same line, and the corner positions shift slightly to $a_1', b_1', c_1', d_1'$.

*Step 5:* **Project the new top view:** Drop vertical projectors from $V_1'$ and all base corners. Projectors from $a_1', c_1'$ coincide with the original $a, c$ (no shift). Projectors from $b_1', d_1'$ meet the original $b, d$ at new points $b_1, d_1$. The new apex $V_1$ lies on the XY line at the foot of the projector from $V_1'$.

*Step 6:* Connect $V_1$ to $a, b_1, c, d_1$ to get the **four new slant edges** in the new top view. The **new front view** is the triangle $a_1' V_1' c_1'$ (with base corners $b_1', d_1'$ on the base line).

*Step 7:* Draw all construction lines (projectors, locus of $V_1$ on XY) as thin lines. Make the final visible outlines as **continuous thick lines** and any hidden edges as **dashed thick lines**. Add all dimensions and labels.

**Solution to Sub-part (b) — Finding the Slant Edge Angle:**

*Part (i) — True length of slant edge (theoretical calculation):*

For a square pyramid, the slant edge $VE$ is given by:

$$VE = \sqrt{h^{2} + \dfrac{a^{2}}{2}}$$

Substituting $a = 40$ mm and $h = 60$ mm:

$$VE = \sqrt{60^{2} + \dfrac{40^{2}}{2}} = \sqrt{3600 + 800} = \sqrt{4400} \approx 66.33 \text{ mm}$$

*Part (ii) — Apparent inclination in front view (graphical measurement):*

- In the new front view, identify the slant edge "a", which is the line $V_1' a_1'$.
- Use a protractor to measure the angle this line makes with the XY line.
- The measured angle (typically around 40° to 50° for this problem configuration) is the **apparent inclination of slant edge (a) with HP**.
- The student should label this angle on the drawing as "Angle of slant edge (a) with HP = __°".

*Part (iii) — Comparison note for full marks:*

The true length 66.33 mm is the **3D diagonal length** of the slant edge. The apparent length in the front view is shorter (around 50-55 mm when measured) because the projection foreshortens the edge. The student should explicitly state this in the answer.

**[7 + 7 = 14 marks]**

---

#### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Dec 2024]`

**Statement:** A **pentagonal pyramid** of base side **30 mm** and height **50 mm** rests on its base on the HP with one of the base edges parallel to the VP and the base surface perpendicular to the VP. It is then tilted so that the axis makes **45°** with the HP. Draw the projections. Find the apparent angle of the slant edge with HP.

**Sub-parts:**

- **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw both the initial and inclined projections of the pentagonal pyramid with full construction details.
- **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the true length of the slant edge and the slant height, and measure the apparent angle of the slant edge with HP from the front view.

---

**Model Solution for Question B-B:**

**Given Data:**
- Base side $a = 30$ mm
- True height $h = 50$ mm
- Tilt angle $\theta = 45°$
- Initial: base on HP, **one base edge parallel to VP** (this is a different initial condition from Question B-A)
- Required: apparent angle of slant edge with HP

**Solution to Sub-part (a) — Drawing the Projections:**

*Step 1:* Draw the XY reference line.

*Step 2:* In the **top view**, draw a **regular pentagon of side 30 mm** with one side parallel to the XY line. This is the initial position. The construction of the regular pentagon uses the standard technique:
- Draw a horizontal line of length 30 mm (one side).
- Use the **diagonal of pentagon** = $\phi \times a = 1.618 \times 30 = 48.54$ mm to construct adjacent sides using a compass.
- Mark all five corners and the centroid $O$.

*Step 3:* Project all five base corners upwards. In the **initial front view**, the base appears as a horizontal line of length 30 mm (the side that is parallel to VP, and therefore projects true length in the front view). The other four base corners project onto this same line (they're all at the same height in front view since the base is horizontal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from Square Pyramid:** For a pentagonal/hexagonal pyramid with "one side parallel to VP", the front view of the base is a **straight line of length equal to the base side** (not the diagonal), because the parallel side projects true length.

*Step 4:* Mark the centre of this 30 mm line as $O'$ (foot of axis). Draw a vertical line $O'V'$ of length 50 mm to mark the apex. The initial front view is a triangle of base 30 mm and height 50 mm.

*Step 5:* **Tilt the axis:** Rotate the front view triangle about $O'$ by 45°. New apex $V_1'$. The base of the triangle remains the same 30 mm line.

*Step 6:* **Project new top view:** Drop projectors from $V_1'$ and from the new base corner positions. The new top view's base is the **same pentagon** (the base has not moved). The new apex $V_1$ is on the XY line at the foot of the projector from $V_1'$. Connect $V_1$ to all five base corners to get the five new slant edges.

*Step 7:* Label, dimension, and add line types as per KTU conventions.

**Solution to Sub-part (b) — Calculations:**

*Part (i) — True length of slant edge:*

For a regular pentagonal pyramid, the circumradius is:

$$R = \dfrac{a}{2\sin 36°} = \dfrac{30}{2 \times 0.5878} = \dfrac{30}{1.1756} \approx 25.52 \text{ mm}$$

The true slant edge is:

$$VE = \sqrt{h^{2} + R^{2}} = \sqrt{50^{2} + 25.52^{2}} = \sqrt{2500 + 651.27} = \sqrt{3151.27} \approx 56.14 \text{ mm}$$

*Part (ii) — True length of slant height:*

The apothem of the pentagon is:

$$r = \dfrac{a}{2\tan 36°} = \dfrac{30}{2 \times 0.7265} = \dfrac{30}{1.4531} \approx 20.65 \text{ mm}$$

The true slant height is:

$$L = \sqrt{h^{2} + r^{2}} = \sqrt{50^{2} + 20.65^{2}} = \sqrt{2500 + 426.42} = \sqrt{2926.42} \approx 54.10 \text{ mm}$$

*Part (iii) — Apparent angle with HP:*

Measure the angle that the slant edge (front-most edge $V_1' a_1'$) makes with the XY line in the new front view. This gives the apparent angle (typically around 55°–65° for this configuration).

**[7 + 7 = 14 marks]**

---

### 5.4 KTU 2024 — Frequently Asked Conceptual Questions (1-2 Mark Quick Recall)

| # | Question | One-Line Answer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | What is the name of the line joining the apex to the centroid of the base? | **Axis** of the pyramid. |
| 2 | What is the difference between a right pyramid and an oblique pyramid? | In a right pyramid, the axis is perpendicular to the base; in an oblique pyramid, it is not. |
| 3 | How many lateral faces does a hexagonal pyramid have? | **6** (one for each side of the hexagonal base). |
| 4 | In a square pyramid, what is the length of the diagonal of the base? | $a\sqrt{2}$ where $a$ is the base side. |
| 5 | When the base is on HP and the axis is perpendicular to HP, what shape is the front view of the base? | A **straight line** of length equal to the apparent width of the base in that view direction. |
| 6 | When tilting a pyramid so that the axis is inclined to HP, which view is rotated? | The **front view** is rotated; the top view is kept unchanged. |
| 7 | The angle of the slant edge with HP is measured in which view? | The **front view**. |
| 8 | The true length of the slant edge is measured in which view? | The **front view** of the **initial (resting) position**, where the axis is vertical. |

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points (Read this the night before the exam):**
>
> - [ ] A **right regular pyramid** has a regular polygonal base and an axis perpendicular to the base through its centroid.
> - [ ] The three standard pyramids in KTU Module 4 are: **Square (4 sides)**, **Pentagonal (5 sides)**, **Hexagonal (6 sides)**.
> - [ ] **Slant edge** = apex to **corner** of base. **Slant height** = apex to **midpoint of a base side**.
> - [ ] For a **square pyramid**: Slant edge formula is $\sqrt{h^{2} + \dfrac{a^{2}}{2}}$. Slant height formula is $\sqrt{h^{2} + \dfrac{a^{2}}{4}}$.
> - [ ] For a **hexagonal pyramid**: Slant edge formula is $\sqrt{h^{2} + a^{2}}$. Slant height formula is $\sqrt{h^{2} + \dfrac{3a^{2}}{4}}$.
> - [ ] For a **pentagonal pyramid**: Use $R = \dfrac{a}{2\sin 36°}$ for slant edge and $r = \dfrac{a}{2\tan 36°}$ for slant height.
> - [ ] **Initial position drawing rule:** When base is on HP and all edges equally inclined to VP, draw the base (square/hexagon/pentagon) with its sides (or in pentagon's case, one side) oriented at 45° (or 30°) to XY.
> - [ ] **Inclination drawing rule:** Rotate **front view only**. Keep **top view unchanged** in this step. The base polygon does not move.
> - [ ] **Apparent angle measurement:** Always measure slant edge angle with HP in the **front view** using a protractor.
> - [ ] **Apparent length of axis:** When axis of true length $h$ is tilted at angle $\theta$ to HP, apparent length = $h \cos\theta$.
> - [ ] Always use **continuous thick lines** for visible edges and **dashed thick lines** for hidden edges.
> - [ ] Always show **construction lines** as thin lines and **do not erase** them — they are part of the solution.
> - [ ] Always **label the XY reference line**, the initial and final positions, the apex points ($V, V_1, V', V_1'$), the centroid ($O, O'$), and the base corners ($a, b, c, d$).
> - [ ] Always **write the given data** at the top of the drawing sheet — KTU valuation deducts marks for missing this.
> - [ ] When asked for the **inclination of the slant edge with HP**, present the answer as: *"Apparent angle = __° (measured from front view), True length = __ mm (calculated from formula)."*
> - [ ] **Do NOT confuse** the "slant edge" with the "slant height" when reading the question — re-read the question twice before starting the drawing.
> - [ ] **Hexagonal pyramid trick:** When a regular hexagon has two opposite sides parallel to XY, the front view of the base is a line of length $2a$ (the distance between two parallel sides through the centre), and the top view is a regular hexagon with vertices at the top, bottom, and four side positions.
> - [ ] **Pentagonal pyramid trick:** A regular pentagon cannot be constructed by simple orthogonal lines. Use the **golden ratio diagonal** technique or the inscribed-in-circle technique.
> - [ ] **Square pyramid at 45° trick:** The top view of a square pyramid with all edges equally inclined to VP is a **diamond shape** (rotated square) with the long diagonal perpendicular to XY and the short diagonal parallel to XY.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Pyramids | GCEST203 Engineering Graphics and Computer Aided Drawing | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cone** is a three-dimensional solid (a solid of revolution) generated by revolving a **right-angled triangle** about one of its **perpendicular sides** as an axis. In Engineering Graphics, we typically study the **Right Circular Cone**, which is the surface of revolution produced when a right triangle rotates about its vertical (perpendicular) side.

Geometrically, a cone is a pyramid with a circular base. The base is a circle, and the apex (vertex) lies on the line passing perpendicularly through the center of the base (the axis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A cone is a solid of revolution generated by the revolution of a straight line (the **generator**) with one end fixed at a point (the **apex/vertex**) and the other end tracing a circular path (the **base**).

### 1.2 Key Terminology Used in Cone Projections

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Base (B)** | The circular bottom face of the cone, of radius **r** |
| **Apex / Vertex (V)** | The point at the tip of the cone |
| **Axis (O)** | The imaginary straight line joining the apex to the center of the base; perpendicular to the base |
| **Height (h)** | The perpendicular distance from the apex to the base plane |
| **Slant Height / Generator (l)** | The straight line from the apex to any point on the circumference of the base; **l = √(h² + r²)** |
| **Base Circle** | The circle representing the base when viewed from the top (in the Top View) |
| **Generator Points** | Points on the base circumference (a, b, c, ...) used to generate slant lines in the Front View |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **ice-cream cone** held point-down. The top circular rim is the base (radius r), the pointed bottom is the apex (V), the vertical distance from rim to point is the height (h), and the sloping side surface is the **generator** (l). The slant height is the length of the actual slanted surface — like the long sloping edge you'd trace with your finger from the rim to the tip.

If you "spin" a right triangle around its vertical leg, the hypotenuse sweeps out the lateral surface of the cone. This is why it's called a "solid of revolution."

### 1.4 Types of Cones (KTU 2024 Module 4 Scope)

1. **Right Circular Cone** – Apex lies on the axis perpendicular to the center of the base. (Most common in KTU problems.)
2. **Oblique Cone** – Apex does not lie on the perpendicular axis. (Rarely tested; mainly for definition.)
3. **Truncated Cone / Frustum** – Obtained by cutting off the top of a cone with a plane parallel to the base.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 primarily focuses on the **Right Circular Cone** in various resting positions and the construction of its **sections, development of lateral surface, and frustum projections**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The cone is treated as a **multiview orthographic projection** problem. We visualize the cone in **three principal positions** relative to the Horizontal Plane (HP) and Vertical Plane (VP), and then derive its Front View (Elevation, FV) and Top View (Plan, TV).

**Why study a cone in Engineering Graphics?**
- Real-world objects like storage tanks, hoppers, funnels, traffic cones, and machine parts are cone-shaped.
- The cone is fundamental for understanding **development of surfaces** (used in sheet metal work, pressure vessel fabrication, and boiler design).
- The sectioning of a cone teaches **intersection of solids**, vital for mechanical and civil engineering.

### 2.2 Standard Resting Positions of a Cone (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

**Position 1: Cone resting on HP on its base**
- TV = Circle of radius **r** (base circle visible in true shape).
- FV = Isosceles triangle, base = **2r**, height = **h**, apex V at top.
- Generators appear as the two slant sides of the triangle.

**Position 2: Cone resting on HP on a point on its base circle**
- The cone tilts until a generator touches HP.
- TV becomes an ellipse (for circle) inscribed within a rectangle of width 2r and length **2l** (diameter of circumscribing circle = slant length).
- FV is a triangle inside a rectangle of width 2r and height h.
- This is one of the most frequently asked KTU problems.

**Position 3: Cone resting on HP on its apex (V)**
- Cone is inverted; apex touches HP.
- TV = Circle of radius r (centered offset from V's position).
- FV is a triangle with apex on the XY line.

**Position 4: Cone resting on VP on its base**
- TV = Triangle (height h, base 2r).
- FV = Circle of radius r (visible as base circle).

**Position 5: Cone resting on VP on a generator**
- Similar logic to Position 2 but rotated to the YZ plane.

### 2.3 Important Sectional Views of a Cone

**A. Sectional View when the cutting plane is parallel to the base:**
- The section is a **circle** of reduced radius.
- The cut produces a **frustum** (truncated cone).
- Used to find the height of the truncated portion.

**B. Sectional View when the cutting plane is perpendicular to the base (inclined to axis):**
- The section is an **ellipse** (in true shape) or a **triangle** (in some cases).
- Ellipse major axis = 2 × (radius at section level); minor axis = difference of slant generators.

**C. Sectional View by an Auxiliary Inclined Plane (Tilted Section):**
- True shape of section is obtained by projecting onto an auxiliary plane parallel to the cutting plane.
- Used to find the **true shape of the section** of the cone.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slant Height (l) | $l = \sqrt{h^2 + r^2}$ | Always in **mm** or **cm** |
| 2 | Base Radius (r) | Given or $r = \sqrt{l^2 - h^2}$ | — |
| 3 | Height (h) | Given or $h = \sqrt{l^2 - r^2}$ | — |
| 4 | Lateral (Curved) Surface Area (LSA) | $\text{LSA} = \pi r l$ | — |
| 5 | Total Surface Area (TSA) | $\text{TSA} = \pi r (r + l)$ | — |
| 6 | Volume of Cone | $V = \dfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h$ | — |
| 7 | Volume of Frustum | $V = \dfrac{1}{3} \pi h (R^2 + Rr + r^2)$ | R = big radius, r = small radius, h = frustum height |
| 8 | LSA of Frustum | $\text{LSA} = \pi (R + r) l$ | l = slant height of frustum |
| 9 | Slant Height of Frustum | $l = \sqrt{h^2 + (R - r)^2}$ | — |
| 10 | Arc length of development sector | $2 \pi r$ (= base circumference) | Full development |
| 11 | Sector angle of development | $\theta = \dfrac{r}{l} \times 360°$ | In degrees |
| 12 | Top View diagonal (cone on generator) | $\text{Diagonal} = 2 l$ | Rectangle enclosing ellipse TV |
| 13 | Top View width (cone on generator) | $\text{Width} = 2 r$ | Rectangle enclosing ellipse TV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** The sector angle formula $\theta = \frac{r}{l} \times 360°$ is **THE most tested formula** for cone development questions. The arc length of the developed sector always equals the base circumference $2\pi r$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sheet Metal Work:** Funnels, hoppers, conical roofs, and chimneys are made by developing the lateral surface of a cone into a flat sector.
- **Civil Engineering:** Conical piles of sand, grain, or aggregates — volume calculations are essential.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Nozzles, rocket nose-cones, turbine blade roots.
- **Architecture:** Domed conical structures (e.g., stupas, minarets).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Slant Height (l) of a Cone

We are given a right circular cone with base radius **r** and perpendicular height **h**. The slant height **l** is the line from apex V to a point on the base circumference.

Consider the right triangle formed by:
- Vertical leg: axis of length **h**
- Horizontal leg: radius of base **r**
- Hypotenuse: slant height **l**

By the **Pythagorean Theorem**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l^2 &= h^2 + r^2 \\
\Rightarrow l &= \sqrt{h^2 + r^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This relationship is the foundation for all subsequent cone projection problems. It allows conversion between axis length and generator length.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sector Angle for Development of a Cone

When the lateral (curved) surface of a cone is unrolled onto a flat plane, it forms a **circular sector** (a "pie slice"). The radius of this sector equals the slant height **l** of the cone, and the arc length of the sector equals the **circumference of the base** ($2\pi r$).

**Step-by-step logical development:**

Step 1 — Identify the arc length of the developed sector:
The arc must reproduce the base circumference when rolled back, so:
$$\text{Arc length of sector} = 2\pi r$$

Step 2 — Recall the arc length formula in terms of sector angle $\theta$ (in radians) and radius $l$:
$$\text{Arc length} = l \times \theta$$

Step 3 — Equate the two arc lengths:
$$l \times \theta = 2\pi r$$

Step 4 — Solve for the angle in radians:
$$\theta = \frac{2\pi r}{l}$$

Step 5 — Convert from radians to degrees by multiplying by $\dfrac{180°}{\pi}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta \text{ (in degrees)} &= \frac{2\pi r}{l} \times \frac{180°}{\pi} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 180° \times r}{l} \\
&= \frac{360° \times r}{l}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Formula (KTU Board Standard):**
$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{r}{l} \times 360°}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works:** The lateral surface is a "rolled-up" sector. The straight radial edges of the sector (of length $l$) become the two extreme generators of the cone in the Front View. The arc of the sector, when re-rolled into a circle of radius $r$, forms the base.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Slant Height of a Frustum (l_f)

When a cone of height $H$ and base radius $R$ is cut at a height $h_1$ from the apex (measured along the axis), the resulting frustum has:
- Larger base radius = $R$
- Smaller top radius = $r_1$ (computed by similar triangles)
- Height of frustum = $H - h_1$

By similar triangles (geometry of similar cones):
$$\frac{r_1}{h_1} = \frac{R}{H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_1 = \frac{R \cdot h_1}{H}$$

The slant height of the frustum is the difference of the slant heights of the original and the cut-off (apex) cones:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \sqrt{H^2 + R^2} \quad \text{(slant height of full cone)} \\
l_1 &= \sqrt{h_1^2 + r_1^2} \quad \text{(slant height of small cut-off cone)} \\
l_f &= L - l_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Alternatively, using the frustum dimensions directly (R, r_1, and frustum height $H_f = H - h_1$):
$$\boxed{l_f = \sqrt{(H - h_1)^2 + (R - r_1)^2}}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Numerical Scenarios for Cone

| S.No. | Given Parameters | Find | Key Formula Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | h, r | l (slant height) | $l = \sqrt{h^2 + r^2}$ |
| 2 | h, l | r | $r = \sqrt{l^2 - h^2}$ |
| 3 | r, l | h | $h = \sqrt{l^2 - r^2}$ |
| 4 | r, l | Sector angle $\theta$ for development | $\theta = \frac{r}{l} \times 360°$ |
| 5 | h, r, cut height | Frustum dimensions | Similar triangles + Pythagoras |
| 6 | r, l | LSA | $\pi r l$ |
| 7 | r, l | TSA | $\pi r (r + l)$ |
| 8 | r, h | Volume | $\frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h$ |
| 9 | R, r, h (frustum) | Volume of frustum | $\frac{1}{3}\pi h (R^2 + Rr + r^2)$ |
| 10 | R, r, h (frustum) | LSA of frustum | $\pi(R+r)\sqrt{h^2 + (R-r)^2}$ |

### 3.5 Worked Example: Development of a Cone

**Problem (KTU 2024 Module 4 — typical 14-mark question):**

A right circular cone has a base diameter of **60 mm** and a height of **80 mm**. Draw the development of its lateral surface. State the sector angle in degrees.

**Step 1: Identify the given data**
- Base diameter = 60 mm, so radius $r = 30$ mm
- Height $h = 80$ mm

**Step 2: Calculate the slant height (l)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
l &= \sqrt{h^2 + r^2} \\
&= \sqrt{80^2 + 30^2} \\
&= \sqrt{6400 + 900} \\
&= \sqrt{7300} \\
&\approx 85.44 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the sector angle (θ)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{r}{l} \times 360° \\
&= \frac{30}{85.44} \times 360° \\
&\approx 0.3511 \times 360° \\
&\approx 126.4°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Construct the development**
- Draw a horizontal line and mark point **O** (apex of the developed sector).
- With O as center and radius $l = 85.44$ mm, draw an arc.
- Mark the arc length equal to base circumference: $2 \pi r = 2 \pi (30) = 188.5$ mm.
- The two radii OA and OB bound the sector; angle AOB = 126.4°.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** $\text{Arc length} = l \times \theta_{\text{rad}} = 85.44 \times \left(126.4 \times \frac{\pi}{180}\right) \approx 85.44 \times 2.206 \approx 188.5$ mm ✓ (matches $2\pi r$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flowchart: Cone Projection Workflow (KTU Method)

This Mermaid diagram outlines the systematic step-by-step process for drawing any cone projection problem in KTU board exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read Problem Statement] --> B[Identify Given Data: h, r, l, Resting Position]
    B --> C[Choose Reference Axis XY line on Drawing Sheet]
    C --> D{Determine Resting Position of Cone}
    D -->|On Base on HP| E1[TV = Circle of radius r; FV = Isosceles Triangle of base 2r, height h]
    D -->|On Generator on HP| E2[Construct Rectangle 2r x 2l; Inscribe Ellipse TV; FV inside rectangle]
    D -->|On Apex on HP| E3[Invert Cone; TV = Circle; FV with apex on XY]
    D -->|On VP| E4[Swap TV and FV roles]
    E1 --> F[Label All Generators: V-a, V-b, V-c using 8-Point Circle Division]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G{Is Section or Development Required?}
    G -->|Section of Cone| H1[Identify Cutting Plane Position; Project Points to FV; True Shape via Auxiliary Plane]
    G -->|Development of Cone| H2[Compute l = sqrt h2 + r2; Compute Sector Angle theta = r/l x 360 deg; Draw Sector]
    G -->|Both| H3[Combine Sectional FV with Development]
    H1 --> I[Dimension and Title the Drawing]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[END: Final Cone Projection Sheet]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Components of a Cone Projection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Base Radius r]
        I2[Height h]
        I3[Resting Plane HP or VP]
    end
    subgraph GeometryCore
        G1[Compute Slant Height l]
        G2[Reference XY Line]
        G3[8-Point Circle Division for Generators]
    end
    subgraph Views
        V1[Front View: Triangle]
        V2[Top View: Circle or Ellipse]
    end
    subgraph OutputTasks
        O1[Sectional True Shape]
        O2[Development Sector]
        O3[Frustum Dimensions]
    end
    I1 --> G1
    I2 --> G1
    I3 --> G2
    G1 --> G3
    G2 --> V1
    G2 --> V2
    G3 --> V1
    G3 --> V2
    V1 --> O1
    V1 --> O2
    V1 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Schematic Representation of Cone (True Shape and Development)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is an ASCII schematic of a cone in its **standard position (resting on HP on its base)**. The Front View (FV) and Top View (TV) are shown, with the development of the lateral surface as a sector.

```
        FRONT VIEW (FV)                      TOP VIEW (TV)
                                                
            V (apex)                       a   b   c   d
            /|\                              \ | /     ← circle divided
           / | \                              \|/        into 8 points
          /  |  \                           ___________
         /   |h  \                         (    O      )  ← base circle,
        /    |    \                        (   radius r  )    center O
       /_____|_____\                       (_____________)
      ←—— 2r ——→
       (base)                                  ↓ (unrolled)
                                                  
                 DEVELOPMENT (Lateral Surface)
                 
                          O (apex of sector)
                         /|\
                        / | \
                       /  |  \   ← radius = l (slant height)
                      /   |   \
                     / θ  |    \
                    /     |     \
                   A______|______B
                   
                   Arc A-B = 2πr (= base circumference)
                   Sector angle θ = (r/l) × 360°
```

### 4.4 Key Construction Steps (Verbal Schematic for Drawing)

1. **Draw XY line** horizontally across the middle of the sheet.
2. **Top View (TV):** Draw a circle of radius **r** with center on XY (if cone rests on HP on its base) or above/below XY based on problem.
3. **Divide the base circle** into **8 (or 12) equal parts** by standard angular division (every 45° or 30°). Label points a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h going clockwise from the leftmost point.
4. **Front View (FV):** Project all 8 points vertically from the TV. Mark their heights as a', b', c', d', e', f', g', h' lying on the base line (a horizontal line at distance h below the apex V).
5. **Join V' (apex) to all 8 projected points a', b', ..., h'** to form the visible generators. Only the extreme left (V'a') and extreme right (V'h') are visible solid lines; the rest are interior.
6. **Label the drawing** as per KTU conventions: title, scale, units, student name, etc.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define a **Right Circular Cone**. State any **two** of its standard terms with a neat sketch.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A right circular cone is a solid of revolution obtained by revolving a right-angled triangle about one of its perpendicular sides (the axis). The base is a circle, and the apex lies on the axis perpendicular to the center of the base.

Two key terms:
- **Slant Height (l):** The distance from the apex to any point on the base circumference, given by $l = \sqrt{h^2 + r^2}$.
- **Generator:** A straight line on the lateral surface from the apex to the base circle.

(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two terms = 3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
A cone has base diameter **50 mm** and slant height **65 mm**. Find its **height** and the **sector angle** of its development.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Given: $r = 25$ mm, $l = 65$ mm.
$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \sqrt{l^2 - r^2} = \sqrt{65^2 - 25^2} = \sqrt{4225 - 625} = \sqrt{3600} = 60 \text{ mm} \\
\theta &= \frac{r}{l} \times 360° = \frac{25}{65} \times 360° = \frac{5}{13} \times 360° \approx 138.46°
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark for $h$, 1 mark for formula of $\theta$, 1 mark for final value = 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A cone of base diameter 60 mm and height 70 mm rests on its base on the HP with a generator perpendicular to the VP. Draw the following views to a suitable scale:**
**(a) Front View and Top View of the cone. (7 marks)**
**(b) Develop the lateral surface of the cone and find the sector angle. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Front View and Top View (7 marks)**

*Given:* Diameter = 60 mm, so $r = 30$ mm; $h = 70$ mm.

*Step 1:* Calculate slant height:
$$l = \sqrt{h^2 + r^2} = \sqrt{70^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{4900 + 900} = \sqrt{5800} \approx 76.16 \text{ mm}$$

*Step 2:* Draw the XY reference line.

*Step 3:* Draw the **Top View (TV):** A circle of radius 30 mm with center on the XY line.

*Step 4:* Orient the circle such that one generator is perpendicular to VP. Mark points on the circle: $a$ (front), $b$, $c$ (right extreme), $d$, $e$ (back), $f$, $g$ (left extreme), $h$ — using 8-point division. The point $a$ is the closest generator to the VP (perpendicular to VP).

*Step 5:* Draw the **Front View (FV):** Project points $a', b', \ldots, h'$ vertically. The base of the FV lies on XY (since the cone rests on HP). Draw the base line of length 60 mm (2r) and the apex $V'$ at a height of 70 mm above the center of the base. Connect $V'$ to the extreme generators $a'$ and $g'$ to form the isosceles triangle.

(7 marks: 2 for calculations, 2 for TV, 2 for FV, 1 for dimensions/labels)

**Part (b) — Development of the Lateral Surface (7 marks)**

*Step 1:* Compute the sector angle:
$$\theta = \frac{r}{l} \times 360° = \frac{30}{76.16} \times 360° \approx 141.81°$$

*Step 2:* Draw a horizontal base line. Mark the apex **O** of the sector.

*Step 3:* With O as center and radius $l = 76.16$ mm, draw two radii OA and OB with angle $\angle AOB = 141.81°$.

*Step 4:* With the same radius, draw the arc AB. The arc length must equal $2\pi r = 2\pi(30) \approx 188.5$ mm. **Verification:** Arc length $= l \times \theta_{\text{rad}} = 76.16 \times (141.81 \times \pi/180) \approx 76.16 \times 2.475 \approx 188.5$ mm ✓

*Step 5:* Dimension the sector and label it.

(7 marks: 2 for sector angle, 3 for construction, 2 for labeling)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)

**A cone of base diameter 50 mm and height 60 mm is cut by a horizontal section plane at a height of 25 mm from the base. Draw the following views:**
**(a) Front View, Top View, and the sectional Top View of the truncated cone. (7 marks)**
**(b) Develop the lateral surface of the frustum so obtained. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Front View, Top View, and Sectional Top View (7 marks)**

*Given:* $r = 25$ mm, $h = 60$ mm, cutting plane at $h_1 = 25$ mm from the base.

*Step 1:* Slant height $l = \sqrt{60^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 625} = \sqrt{4225} = 65$ mm.

*Step 2:* Draw the standard Front View (isosceles triangle of base 50 mm and height 60 mm) and Top View (circle of radius 25 mm).

*Step 3:* Mark the cutting plane as a horizontal line at 25 mm above the base in the FV. The intersection with the two slant generators (V'a' and V'g') gives points P' and Q'.

*Step 4:* Project P' and Q' down to the TV. They will lie on the base circle, and the section in the TV is a circle of radius 25 mm (same as base, because the cut is parallel to the base).

*Step 5:* **Sectional Top View:** A circle of radius 25 mm showing the base and the sectioned plane above it. Apply **section hatching** to the portion between the base and the cutting plane (since the upper portion — the small cone — is removed).

(7 marks: 2 for FV/TV, 2 for cutting plane, 2 for sectional TV, 1 for hatching)

**Part (b) — Development of the Frustum (7 marks)**

*Step 1:* Compute dimensions of the frustum:
- Big radius $R = 25$ mm
- Small (top) radius $r_1$ (from similar triangles): The small cone above the cut has height $60 - 25 = 35$ mm, and
$$r_1 = \frac{R}{h} \times h_1 = \frac{25}{60} \times 35 \approx 14.58 \text{ mm}$$

- Slant height of full cone $L = 65$ mm (from Step 1 above)
- Slant height of small cone (cut-off apex): $l_1 = \sqrt{35^2 + 14.58^2} \approx \sqrt{1225 + 212.6} \approx 37.99$ mm
- Slant height of frustum: $l_f = L - l_1 = 65 - 37.99 \approx 27.01$ mm

*Step 2:* Compute the sector angles:
- For full cone: $\theta = \frac{25}{65} \times 360° \approx 138.46°$
- For small cone: $\theta_1 = \frac{14.58}{37.99} \times 360° \approx 138.16°$ (≈ same; a check property of similar cones)

*Step 3:* Draw the **full sector** first: apex O, radius 65 mm, sector angle 138.46°. Mark the arc length (188.5 mm).

*Step 4:* With the **same center O** and radius $l_1 = 37.99$ mm, draw an inner arc. The annular region between the two arcs is the **developed frustum**.

*Step 5:* Dimension and label the development.

(7 marks: 2 for dimension calculation, 3 for sector drawing, 2 for annular frustum)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Cone Projection Problems):**
>
> 1. **Missing the 8-point circle division:** Students often project only 2 generators. KTU requires **at least 8 generator points** ($a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h$) for accuracy in sectioning and development. (−2 marks)
>
> 2. **Wrong slant height formula:** Writing $l = h + r$ or $l = h^2 + r^2$ instead of $l = \sqrt{h^2 + r^2}$. (−1 to −2 marks)
>
> 3. **Confusion between h and l:** Using **height (h)** as the radius of the development sector instead of the **slant height (l)**. The sector's radial length must be **l**, not h. (−2 to −3 marks)
>
> 4. **Sector angle in radians:** Writing the formula in radians and forgetting to convert to degrees (or vice versa). The KTU answer key always expects degrees. (−1 mark)
>
> 5. **No dimensioning / no labeling:** A cone projection without labeled generators (V'a', V'g', etc.) and dimensions loses 1–2 marks in the "neatness" category.
>
> 6. **Not drawing XY line:** The reference line XY is mandatory in KTU board exams. Omitting it = −1 mark.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to verify arc length:** The arc length of the development sector must equal $2\pi r$. Failing to verify (or showing an inconsistent drawing) = up to −2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **Right Circular Cone** is a **solid of revolution** of a right triangle about its vertical leg.
- [ ] The **three principal terms**: base radius **r**, perpendicular height **h**, and slant height / generator **l**.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $l = \sqrt{h^2 + r^2}$ — memorize and be able to rearrange.
- [ ] **Sector angle (development):** $\theta = \dfrac{r}{l} \times 360°$ — the **single most important formula** for KTU cone development problems.
- [ ] **Arc length of developed sector = base circumference** = $2\pi r$. Always verify.
- [ ] **Top View = circle (radius r)** when the cone rests on HP on its base; **ellipse** when resting on a generator.
- [ ] **Front View = isosceles triangle** (base = 2r, height = h) when resting on HP on its base.
- [ ] **Section parallel to base** → **circle** of reduced radius (similar triangle concept: $r_1/h_1 = r/h$).
- [ ] **Section perpendicular to axis** → **ellipse** (true shape obtained via auxiliary projection).
- [ ] **Frustum slant height:** $l_f = L - l_1$ (difference of slant heights), or $l_f = \sqrt{h_f^2 + (R-r)^2}$.
- [ ] **Volume of cone:** $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$ (one-third of cylinder).
- [ ] **Volume of frustum:** $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi h_f(R^2 + Rr + r^2)$.
- [ ] **Lateral surface area of cone:** $\pi r l$ (sector of a circle of radius l and arc length $2\pi r$).
- [ ] Always **divide the base circle into 8 (or 12) parts** before projecting generators — KTU standard.
- [ ] Always include the **XY reference line** and proper **dimensioning** with units.
- [ ] **Common KTU question types:** (1) Cone on base — views; (2) Cone on generator — views; (3) Development of cone; (4) Sectioned cone / frustum views + development; (5) Frustum volume / surface area.
- [ ] **KTU marking weightage (typical 14-mark problem):** 2–3 marks for construction data, 5–6 marks for views, 3–4 marks for development/section, 2 marks for dimensioning and neatness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cylinder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cylinder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 CYLINDER — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Geometric Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Cylinder** is a solid of revolution generated by the revolution of a **straight line (generator)** about a fixed straight line (**axis**) such that the moving line always remains **parallel to the axis** and at a **constant perpendicular distance** from it.

In KTU Engineering Graphics terminology, a cylinder is classified as a **prism with a circular base**. The two ends of the generator trace out two parallel circular ends called the **bases** of the cylinder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Exam Accepted):** A cylinder is a polyhedron-like solid bounded by two parallel circular bases and a continuous curved lateral surface generated by a straight line (generator) moving parallel to itself with one end on each base circle.

## 1.2 Essential Terminology (Must Memorize)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Axis** | The fixed straight line about which the generator revolves |
| **Generator (Element)** | The straight line whose revolution forms the lateral surface |
| **Base** | Either of the two parallel circular ends of the cylinder |
| **Height (h)** | Perpendicular distance between the two circular bases |
| **Radius (r)** | Radius of the circular base |
| **Diameter (d)** | Diameter of the circular base, where $d = 2r$ |

## 1.3 Types of Cylinders (Classification by KTU Board)

1. **Right Circular Cylinder (RCC)** — Axis is perpendicular to the base. Most common in KTU problems.
2. **Oblique Cylinder** — Axis is inclined to the base at an angle other than 90°.
3. **Hollow Cylinder (Pipe/Tube)** — A solid cylinder with a coaxial cylindrical cavity.
4. **Circular Cylinder vs Elliptic Cylinder** — Based on the shape of the base.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Module 4 focuses primarily on the **Right Circular Cylinder** in standard positions (axis parallel, perpendicular, or inclined to reference planes). Hollow cylinders appear in sectioning problems.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Bucket 🪣

Imagine a **straight metallic water bucket** standing upright on a flat floor.

- The **circular top and bottom** of the bucket = the **two bases** of the cylinder.
- The **curved metal wall** of the bucket = the **lateral (curved) surface** generated by the revolution of a vertical line.
- The **central imaginary line** passing through the center of the two circles = the **axis** of the cylinder.
- The **vertical edges** of the metal sheet (which run from top to bottom) = the **generators (elements)**.

If you tilt the bucket sideways, you get an **oblique cylinder** — the base is no longer perpendicular to the axis, but the curved wall is still generated by straight lines parallel to the axis.

If you take a smaller bucket and place it inside the bigger one, you get a **hollow cylinder** (like a PVC pipe).

> [!TIP]
> **Why this analogy works:** It instantly communicates that a cylinder is NOT a sphere or a cone. The defining feature is the **parallel straight-line generators** — every vertical line on the bucket wall is straight and parallel to every other vertical line.

## 1.5 Position in Reference Planes (KTU Convention)

KTU follows **First-Angle Projection** (used in India, Europe, and most of Asia). The three reference planes are:
- **HP** — Horizontal Plane (top view projected onto this)
- **VP** — Vertical Plane (front view projected onto this)
- **PP** — Profile Plane (side view projected onto this)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always draw the **reference line (XY)** between HP and VP. Label every view clearly as **Top View (TV)**, **Front View (FV)**, and **Side View (SV)**. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these labels are missing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔍 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Properties of a Right Circular Cylinder

A right circular cylinder is completely defined by **two parameters**:
- **Radius (r)** of the base
- **Height (h)** of the cylinder

All other properties (surface areas, volume, projections) are derived from these two.

### Key Geometric Relationships

- **Circumference of the base circle:** $C = 2\pi r$
- **Lateral Surface Area (Curved Surface):** $A_{L} = 2\pi r h$
- **Area of each circular base:** $A_{b} = \pi r^{2}$
- **Total Surface Area:** $A_{T} = 2\pi r h + 2\pi r^{2} = 2\pi r (r + h)$
- **Volume:** $V = \pi r^{2} h$
- **Diagonal of the cylinder (corner to opposite corner):** $D = \sqrt{(2r)^{2} + h^{2}} = \sqrt{4r^{2} + h^{2}}$

## 2.2 Principles of Projection of a Cylinder (KTU Board Standard)

The **three standard positions** of a cylinder relative to the reference planes, all of which appear regularly in KTU question papers, are:

### Case 1: Axis Perpendicular to HP and Parallel to VP

- **Top View (TV):** A **circle** of radius $r$ (looking straight down the axis).
- **Front View (FV):** A **rectangle** of width $2r$ and height $h$.
- **Side View (SV):** A **rectangle** of width $2r$ and height $h$.

> This is the most common starting orientation in KTU problems.

### Case 2: Axis Perpendicular to VP and Parallel to HP

- **Top View (TV):** A **rectangle** of width $2r$ and height $h$.
- **Front View (FV):** A **circle** of radius $r$.
- **Side View (SV):** A **rectangle** of width $2r$ and height $h$.

### Case 3: Axis Inclined to HP and Parallel to VP

- **Top View (TV):** An **ellipse** (foreshortened circle) with major axis $2r$ and minor axis $2r \sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle the axis makes with HP.
- **Front View (FV):** A **rectangle** inclined at angle $\theta$ to the XY line, with apparent length $h / \sin\theta$.
- **Side View (SV):** A **rectangle** of width $2r$ and height $h$ (true length, as side view is projected onto PP, which is parallel to VP).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** In a position where the axis is inclined to one plane and parallel to another, the view **perpendicular to the inclined plane** will show the **true shape** or **true length**, while the view **perpendicular to the plane to which the axis is parallel** will show a **foreshortened shape**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| Quantity / Quantity Relationship | Formula | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Circumference of base | $C = 2 \pi r$ | Any cylinder |
| Length of generator in RCC | $L = h$ | Right Circular Cylinder |
| Length of generator in oblique cylinder | $L = h / \cos\theta$ | $\theta$ = angle of axis with base |
| Lateral Surface Area (RCC) | $A_{L} = 2 \pi r h$ | Solid right circular cylinder |
| Total Surface Area (RCC) | $A_{T} = 2 \pi r (r + h)$ | Closed at both ends |
| Volume (RCC) | $V = \pi r^{2} h$ | Solid right circular cylinder |
| Volume of hollow cylinder | $V = \pi (R^{2} - r^{2}) h$ | $R$ = outer radius, $r$ = inner radius |
| Development length (unrolled lateral surface) | $L_{dev} = 2 \pi r$ | Equal to circumference |
| Development height | $H_{dev} = h$ | Height of cylinder |
| True shape of section by plane perpendicular to axis | Circle of radius $r$ | Always identical to base |
| True shape of section by plane inclined to axis | Ellipse with major axis $h / \sin\phi$ | $\phi$ = angle between cutting plane and axis |
| Apparent length of inclined axis in FV | $L_{app} = h / \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = angle of axis with HP |

## 2.4 Sectioning of a Cylinder — Theory of Cutting Planes

A cylinder can be cut by an **auxiliary plane** to reveal its **internal structure**. The type of section produced depends on the **angle of the cutting plane** with respect to the axis.

- **Cutting plane parallel to the axis** → Section is a **rectangle** of width $2r$ and height equal to the cut length.
- **Cutting plane perpendicular to the axis** → Section is a **circle** (true shape = base circle).
- **Cutting plane inclined to the axis at angle $\phi$ (not 90°)** → Section is an **ellipse**. The **major axis** of the ellipse lies along the cut, the **minor axis** is equal to the diameter $2r$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** A cylinder standing on HP with axis vertical is often cut by an **inclined plane** (e.g., inclined at 30° or 45° to HP). Students must find the **true shape of the section**, which is an ellipse.

## 2.5 Development of the Lateral Surface

**Development** means "unrolling" the curved surface of the cylinder into a flat 2D pattern (like unrolling a piece of paper from around a pencil).

- The **length** of the development = circumference of the base = $2\pi r$.
- The **height** of the development = height of the cylinder = $h$.
- The developed shape is a **perfect rectangle** of dimensions $2\pi r \times h$.
- If the cylinder is sectioned by an inclined plane, the development will have a **curved/sloped top edge** corresponding to the cut.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The development of a cylinder is the basis for manufacturing **boiler shells, pressure vessels, storage tanks, chimneys, and pipes**. A sheet metal worker needs the exact 2D pattern to cut and roll the metal into a cylindrical shape.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Field | Application of Cylinder |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Hydraulic cylinders, pistons, engine cylinders, rollers |
| **Civil** | Columns, water tanks, silos, chimneys, drainage pipes |
| **Chemical** | Storage tanks, reaction vessels, distillation columns |
| **Architecture** | Pillars, support columns, ventilation ducts |
| **Aerospace** | Rocket motor casings, fuselage sections, fuel tanks |

# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Length of Generator in an Oblique Cylinder

In a **right circular cylinder**, the generator is perpendicular to the base, so $L = h$. In an **oblique cylinder**, the axis is inclined to the base at angle $\theta$ (where $\theta \neq 90°$). The generator must still be **parallel to the axis** (by the definition of a cylinder), so the generator is also inclined at angle $\theta$ to the base.

**Setup:**
- Let $h$ = perpendicular height of the cylinder (shortest distance between the two base planes).
- Let $L$ = true length of the generator.
- Let $\theta$ = angle between the axis (and therefore the generator) and the base plane.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Consider a cross-section through the axis of the oblique cylinder. We see a rectangle whose:
- Vertical side = $h$ (the perpendicular distance between the two base planes).
- Inclined side = $L$ (the generator).
- The angle between the generator and the base = $\theta$.

From the right triangle formed:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{h}{L}$$

Rearranging to solve for $L$:

$$L = \frac{h}{\cos\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** When $\theta = 90°$ (right circular cylinder), $\cos(90°) = 0$, which would make $L$ infinite. This indicates the formula only applies to oblique cases. For the right circular cylinder, $L = h$ directly.

## 3.2 Derivation: True Shape of a Section Cut by an Inclined Plane

**Setup:**
- A right circular cylinder of radius $r$ and height $h$ stands on HP with its axis vertical.
- A cutting plane is inclined at angle $\phi$ to the axis of the cylinder.
- The section produced is an **ellipse**.

**Step 1 — Identify the section type.**
Since the cutting plane is not perpendicular to the axis ($\phi \neq 90°$) and not parallel to it, the intersection is a **closed curve with major and minor axes of different lengths** — an ellipse.

**Step 2 — Determine the minor axis.**
The cylinder's diameter, measured **perpendicular to the axis**, remains $2r$ regardless of where the cut is made. Therefore, the minor axis of the ellipse equals:

$$b = 2r$$

**Step 3 — Determine the major axis.**
The major axis lies along the line of cut on the cylinder's surface. Its length equals the **length of the cut** along the generator direction, projected.

If the cutting plane intersects the cylinder over a length $s$ along the axis, then the major axis is:

$$a = \frac{s}{\sin\phi}$$

For a cylinder cut from its top to its bottom at angle $\phi$ from the axis (where $\phi$ is small, i.e., a steep cut):

$$a = \frac{h}{\sin\phi}$$

**Step 4 — Express the eccentricity of the ellipse.**
The eccentricity of the elliptical section is:

$$e = \sqrt{1 - \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{4r^{2} \sin^{2}\phi}{h^{2}}}$$

**Step 5 — Summary formula.**
The elliptical section has semi-major axis $a = h / (2 \sin\phi)$ and semi-minor axis $b = r$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Dimensions of the Developed (Unrolled) Lateral Surface

**Step 1 — Establish the principle.**
When a curved surface is unrolled onto a plane, every point on the surface retains its distance from the two reference lines (the base edges). For a cylinder, the unrolled lateral surface is a rectangle.

**Step 2 — Find the length of the development.**
The length of the development equals the distance traveled by a generator as it traces the base circle. For a full revolution:

$$L_{dev} = 2 \pi r$$

**Step 3 — Find the height of the development.**
The height of the development equals the perpendicular height of the cylinder:

$$H_{dev} = h$$

**Step 4 — Construct the development rectangle.**
The development is a rectangle of dimensions:

$$\text{Width} \times \text{Height} = 2 \pi r \times h$$

**Step 5 — Generalization for sectioned cylinders.**
If the top of the cylinder is cut by an inclined plane, the development's top edge is no longer a straight horizontal line. It becomes a **sloped or curved line** whose horizontal projection is $2 \pi r$ and whose vertical variation is determined by the section's shape (e.g., the sine curve for an inclined cut).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — View Generation Rules for a Cylinder

| Axis Orientation | Top View | Front View | Side View |
|---|---|---|---|
| Perpendicular to HP, Parallel to VP | Circle of radius $r$ | Rectangle $2r \times h$ | Rectangle $2r \times h$ |
| Parallel to HP, Perpendicular to VP | Rectangle $2r \times h$ | Circle of radius $r$ | Rectangle $2r \times h$ |
| Inclined $\theta$ to HP, Parallel to VP | Ellipse ($2r \times 2r\sin\theta$) | Inclined rectangle ($2r \times h/\sin\theta$) | Rectangle $2r \times h$ (true length) |
| Inclined to both HP and VP | Ellipse | Inclined rectangle | Inclined rectangle |
| Axis on HP (resting) | Rectangle $2r \times h$ | Rectangle $2r \times h$ | Circle of radius $r$ |

## 3.5 Worked Example: Find Volume and Surface Area

**Given:** A right circular cylinder has radius $r = 7 \text{ cm}$ and height $h = 15 \text{ cm}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the circumference of the base.**

$$C = 2 \pi r = 2 \times \pi \times 7 = 14 \pi \text{ cm}$$

Numerically: $C \approx 43.98 \text{ cm}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the lateral (curved) surface area.**

$$A_{L} = 2 \pi r h = 2 \times \pi \times 7 \times 15 = 210 \pi \text{ cm}^{2}$$

Numerically: $A_{L} \approx 659.73 \text{ cm}^{2}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the area of the two circular bases.**

$$A_{b} = 2 \times \pi r^{2} = 2 \times \pi \times 49 = 98 \pi \text{ cm}^{2}$$

Numerically: $A_{b} \approx 307.88 \text{ cm}^{2}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the total surface area.**

$$A_{T} = A_{L} + A_{b} = 210 \pi + 98 \pi = 308 \pi \text{ cm}^{2}$$

Numerically: $A_{T} \approx 967.61 \text{ cm}^{2}$.

**Step 5 — Compute the volume.**

$$V = \pi r^{2} h = \pi \times 49 \times 15 = 735 \pi \text{ cm}^{3}$$

Numerically: $V \approx 2309.07 \text{ cm}^{3}$.

**Step 6 — Verification using dimensions.**
Each $\pi$-multiplied answer can be re-verified by dividing the numerical value by $\pi \approx 3.14159$. For example, $967.61 / 3.14159 \approx 308$, which matches $A_{T} = 308 \pi$. ✔

## 3.6 Worked Example: True Shape of Section

**Given:** A cylinder of diameter $50 \text{ mm}$ and height $70 \text{ mm}$ stands on HP with axis vertical. A cutting plane inclined at $60°$ to the HP cuts the cylinder, passing through the top edge and the opposite bottom edge.

**Step 1 — Identify the section type.**
The cutting plane is inclined to both the axis and the base. The section is an **ellipse**.

**Step 2 — Compute the minor axis.**
The minor axis equals the diameter of the cylinder:

$$b = 2r = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the major axis using the angle with the HP.**
When the cutting plane is inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the HP, the major axis of the elliptical section is:

$$a = \frac{h}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{70}{\sin(60°)} = \frac{70}{0.8660} \approx 80.83 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — State the true shape.**
True shape: **Ellipse** with major axis $\approx 80.83 \text{ mm}$ and minor axis $= 50 \text{ mm}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Construction Note (for drawing):** To draw this ellipse in the KTU exam, mark the major axis as a horizontal line of length 80.83 mm and the minor axis as a vertical line of length 50 mm at the midpoint. Plot at least 8 points using the **concentric circle method** (draw two circles of radii $a/2$ and $b/2$, project radial lines, and intersect), then join smoothly with a French curve.

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Cylinder Projection Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid cannot render true 3D isometric or physical engineering drawings of solids. The block-level functional architecture below maps out the **decision logic** an examiner expects a student to follow while drawing a cylinder in a given position. This is the workflow KTU examiners test through the marking scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["INPUT: Cylinder Parameters radius r, height h, axis orientation theta"]
    inputB["REFERENCE PLANES: HP, VP, PP with XY line"]
    classify{"AXIS ORIENTATION"}
    case1["CASE 1: Axis Perpendicular to HP, Parallel to VP"]
    case2["CASE 2: Axis Perpendicular to VP, Parallel to HP"]
    case3["CASE 3: Axis Inclined to HP, Parallel to VP"]
    case4["CASE 4: Axis Inclined to Both HP and VP"]
    tv1["TOP VIEW: Circle radius r"]
    fv1["FRONT VIEW: Rectangle 2r by h"]
    sv1["SIDE VIEW: Rectangle 2r by h"]
    tv2["TOP VIEW: Rectangle 2r by h"]
    fv2["FRONT VIEW: Circle radius r"]
    sv2["SIDE VIEW: Rectangle 2r by h"]
    tv3["TOP VIEW: Ellipse 2r by 2r sin theta"]
    fv3["FRONT VIEW: Inclined Rectangle length h by sin theta, true width 2r"]
    sv3["SIDE VIEW: Rectangle 2r by h, TRUE LENGTH"]
    tv4["TOP VIEW: Ellipse foreshortened"]
    fv4["FRONT VIEW: Inclined Rectangle"]
    sv4["SIDE VIEW: Inclined Rectangle"]
    output["OUTPUT: Completed Orthographic Projection Sheet with Section and Development if asked"]
    classDef startEnd fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#c00000,stroke:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    classDef case fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    classDef view fill:#70ad47,stroke:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputBox fill:#7030a0,stroke:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    class inputA,inputB,output startEnd
    class classify decision
    class case1,case2,case3,case4 case
    class tv1,fv1,sv1,tv2,fv2,sv2,tv3,fv3,sv3,tv4,fv4,sv4 view
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Sectioning and Development Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    solid["SOLID CYLINDER ON HP"] --> drawproj["DRAW THREE ORTHOGRAPHIC VIEWS"]
    drawproj --> cutplane["APPLY CUTTING PLANE AT ANGLE phi"]
    cutplane --> secview["SHOW SECTIONAL FRONT VIEW WITH HATCHING"]
    secview --> trueshape{"FIND TRUE SHAPE OF SECTION"}
    trueshape --> minor["MINOR AXIS = 2r always perpendicular to cylinder axis"]
    trueshape --> major["MAJOR AXIS = length of cut along axis divided by sin phi"]
    minor --> ellipse["CONSTRUCT ELLIPSE USING CONCENTRIC CIRCLE METHOD"]
    major --> ellipse
    ellipse --> dev["DEVELOP LATERAL SURFACE"]
    dev --> rect["RECTANGLE width 2 pi r, height h"]
    cutplane --> devshape["IF CUT: TOP EDGE OF DEVELOPMENT BECOMES SLOPED OR CURVED LINE"]
    devshape --> dev
    classDef solidBox fill:#264478,stroke:#264478,color:#ffffff
    classDef procBox fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    classDef decisionBox fill:#c00000,stroke:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputBox fill:#548235,stroke:#548235,color:#ffffff
    class solid,drawproj,cutplane,secview,minor,major,ellipse,dev,rect,devshape procBox
    class trueshape decisionBox
    class solid solidBox
    class rect outputBox
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Graph — Position-to-View Mapping Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["GIVEN POSITION OF CYLINDER"] --> q1{"IS AXIS PERPENDICULAR TO HP"}
    q1 -- YES --> tvcircle["TOP VIEW IS CIRCLE OF RADIUS r"]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{"IS AXIS PERPENDICULAR TO VP"}
    q2 -- YES --> fvcircle["FRONT VIEW IS CIRCLE OF RADIUS r"]

    q3 -- YES --> tve["TOP VIEW IS ELLIPSE"]
    q3 -- NO --> q4["AXIS INCLINED TO BOTH HP AND VP: ALL THREE VIEWS ARE FORESHORTENED"]
    tvcircle --> fvrect["FRONT VIEW IS RECTANGLE OF TRUE HEIGHT h AND WIDTH 2r"]
    fvcircle --> tvrect["TOP VIEW IS RECTANGLE OF TRUE LENGTH h AND WIDTH 2r"]
    tve --> fvinc["FRONT VIEW IS INCLINED RECTANGLE WITH APPARENT LENGTH h by sin theta"]

```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each diamond box represents a **decision** the student must make before drawing. Each rectangle is an **action** (drawing a view). Following these blocks in order is exactly how a KTU topper structures a 14-mark projection question on the answer script.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a cylinder. State any two differences between a right circular cylinder and an oblique cylinder.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A cylinder is a solid bounded by two parallel circular bases and a curved lateral surface generated by a straight line called the **generator**, which moves parallel to a fixed straight line called the **axis** while keeping a constant perpendicular distance from it.

**Differences (2 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Right Circular Cylinder | Oblique Cylinder |
|---|---|
| The axis is perpendicular ($90°$) to the base. | The axis is inclined to the base at an angle other than $90°$. |
| The generator is perpendicular to the base; its length equals the height: $L = h$. | The generator is inclined to the base; its length exceeds the height: $L = h / \cos\theta$. |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the development of a cylinder? State the dimensions of the development of a right circular cylinder of radius $r$ and height $h$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Development Definition (1 mark):**
Development is the process of unrolling the curved lateral surface of a solid onto a flat plane to obtain its **2D surface pattern**, which is used in sheet-metal work and manufacturing.

**Dimensions (2 marks):**
- **Length of development** = Circumference of the base = $2 \pi r$
- **Height of development** = Height of the cylinder = $h$
- The developed shape is a **rectangle** of dimensions $2 \pi r \times h$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Pattern:** Each 14-mark question in Module 4 carries internal choice. The full marks split is **7 + 7** for sub-parts (a) and (b). Students must draw to scale using instruments; freehand sketches receive **0 marks** for the drawing component (only procedure marks awarded).

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A cylinder of diameter $50 \text{ mm}$ and height $70 \text{ mm}$ rests on HP on one of its base circles with the axis vertical. It is cut by a vertical section plane passing through the axis. Draw:**
**(a) The sectional front view and top view when the cutting plane is parallel to VP. (7 marks)**
**(b) The true shape of the section. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Sectional Front View and Top View (7 marks):**

1. Draw the **XY reference line** dividing the sheet into HP (below) and VP (above). *(0.5 mark)*
2. In the **Top View**, draw a circle of diameter $50 \text{ mm}$ (radius $25 \text{ mm}$) centered on the XY line. This represents the top base as seen from above. *(1 mark)*
3. In the **Front View** (above XY), draw a **rectangle** of width $50 \text{ mm}$ (the diameter) and height $70 \text{ mm}$. The two vertical sides of the rectangle are the extreme generators. *(1 mark)*
4. The vertical cutting plane, being parallel to VP and passing through the axis, is represented in the **Top View as a straight line** coinciding with the vertical centerline of the circle. *(0.5 mark)*
5. In the **Front View**, this cutting plane appears as a **vertical line** passing through the middle of the rectangle. *(0.5 mark)*
6. The portion of the cylinder **to the right of the cutting plane is removed** (assume the right half is removed). The remaining left half is the section. *(0.5 mark)*
7. Apply **section hatching (45° lines at 2 mm spacing)** to the cut surface. The section is a **rectangle of width $25 \text{ mm}$ and height $70 \text{ mm}$**. *(1.5 marks)*
8. Project the section back to the Top View as a **rectangle of $25 \text{ mm} \times 70 \text{ mm}$** (with the $70 \text{ mm}$ side horizontal). The Top View is no longer a full circle but a rectangle representing the half-cylinder seen from above. *(1 mark)*
9. Add center lines, dimension lines, and labels (TV, FV). *(0.5 mark)*

**Part (b) — True Shape of the Section (7 marks):**

1. Since the cutting plane is **parallel to the axis** of the cylinder, the section produced is a **rectangle**, not an ellipse. *(1 mark)*
2. **Width of the section** = diameter of the cylinder = $50 \text{ mm}$. *(1 mark)*
3. **Height of the section** = height of the cylinder = $70 \text{ mm}$. *(1 mark)*
4. To draw the true shape, construct a rectangle of $50 \text{ mm} \times 70 \text{ mm}$ on a separate area of the sheet. *(2 marks)*
5. Hatch the rectangle at 45° to indicate the section plane. *(1 mark)*
6. Label the true shape clearly and add dimensions. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Many students wrongly draw an **ellipse** as the true shape, assuming any section of a cylinder is curved. **This loses 4–5 marks.** Remember: when the cutting plane is **parallel to the axis**, the section is a **rectangle**; only when the plane is **inclined to the axis** does the section become an ellipse.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A cylinder of diameter $40 \text{ mm}$ and height $60 \text{ mm}$ is resting on HP on one of its base circles with the axis vertical. A section plane inclined at $45°$ to the HP cuts the cylinder, with the lower end of the cut at the top of the base circle and the higher end at the top of the cylinder. Draw:**
**(a) The front view showing the section, the top view, and the side view. (7 marks)**
**(b) The development of the lateral surface of the cylinder showing the cut. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Three Orthographic Views with Section (7 marks):**

1. Draw the **XY reference line**. Below XY is the Top View area, above is the Front View area. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Top View:** Draw a **circle of diameter $40 \text{ mm}$** (radius $20 \text{ mm}$) with its center on the XY line. *(1 mark)*
3. **Front View:** Draw a **rectangle** of width $40 \text{ mm}$ and height $60 \text{ mm}$ above the XY line. Mark the top and bottom edges. *(1 mark)*
4. **Side View:** Project the Front View to the side; draw a **rectangle** of width $40 \text{ mm}$ and height $60 \text{ mm}$. *(0.5 mark)*
5. Apply the **cutting plane at $45°$ to HP**. In the Front View, the cutting plane appears as a **straight line inclined at $45°$** to the XY line, starting at the bottom-left corner of the rectangle (at the top of the base) and going up to the top-right corner. *(1.5 marks)*
6. **Mark the cutting plane line** with a dash-dot-dot line convention (A–A). *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Hatch the removed portion** at $45°$ with 2 mm spacing. The remaining solid is the lower-left triangular-trapezoidal region. *(1.5 marks)*
8. Project the section's top edge back to the Top View as a **chord cutting the circle** at the appropriate level. The top view now shows the cylinder with one part removed — a circle with a chord-shaped cut. *(0.5 mark)*

**Part (b) — Development of the Lateral Surface (7 marks):**

1. **Divide the top circle** (in the Top View) into **12 equal parts** using the standard method. Mark the points as $a, b, c, d, \ldots, l$ (or $1, 2, 3, \ldots, 12$). *(1 mark)*
2. **Project these points vertically** (parallel to the axis) to the front view. Each point $a, b, c, \ldots$ becomes a generator on the lateral surface, appearing as a vertical line inside the Front View rectangle. *(1 mark)*
3. Mark the **intersection of each generator with the cutting plane** in the Front View. Label these intersection points as $a', b', c', \ldots$ *(1 mark)*
4. **Measure the true length of the cylinder's generators** = $60 \text{ mm}$ (full height). *(0.5 mark)*
5. Draw a **horizontal line** to the right of the Front View. This line will be the **base of the development** (length = circumference = $\pi \times 40 \approx 125.66 \text{ mm}$). *(1 mark)*
6. **Mark off 12 equal divisions** along this horizontal line, each of length $\pi \times 40 / 12 \approx 10.47 \text{ mm}$. These divisions correspond to the points $a, b, c, \ldots$ on the circumference. *(1 mark)*
7. **Erect vertical lines** at each division point to height $60 \text{ mm}$ (the true generator length). On each vertical line, mark the **height** from the base up to the corresponding cutting plane intersection (using the heights measured in the Front View). *(1 mark)*
8. **Join the marked heights with a smooth curve** (using a French curve). This curve is the **trace of the cutting plane** on the developed surface. The development is a rectangle with a curved top edge. *(0.5 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Two common errors cost students up to 4 marks here:
> 1. **Using apparent (foreshortened) heights** instead of true generator lengths. Always measure the heights **parallel to the generator** (i.e., vertically in the Front View) and transfer them as **vertical distances** on the development.
> 2. **Forgetting the base line of the development** — the unrolled circumference line must be drawn first; otherwise the development is meaningless and the examiner deducts 2 marks for "unclear construction."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam — Cylinder (Module 4)**

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- **Cylinder:** Solid with two parallel circular bases, generated by a straight line (generator) moving parallel to a fixed axis.
- **Right Circular Cylinder (RCC):** Axis perpendicular to base, $L = h$.
- **Oblique Cylinder:** Axis inclined to base, $L = h / \cos\theta$.
- **Generator:** Straight line element of the lateral surface, parallel to the axis.
- **Development:** Unrolled 2D pattern of the lateral surface.

### 📐 Must-Know Formulas
- Lateral Surface Area: $A_{L} = 2 \pi r h$
- Total Surface Area: $A_{T} = 2 \pi r (r + h)$
- Volume: $V = \pi r^{2} h$
- Volume of hollow cylinder: $V = \pi (R^{2} - r^{2}) h$
- Length of development: $L_{dev} = 2 \pi r$
- Length of oblique generator: $L = h / \cos\theta$
- Elliptical section major axis: $a = h / \sin\phi$
- Elliptical section minor axis: $b = 2r$

### 🎯 Must-Know View Generation Rules
- **Axis ⊥ HP, ∥ VP** → TV = circle, FV = rectangle, SV = rectangle.
- **Axis ∥ HP, ⊥ VP** → TV = rectangle, FV = circle, SV = rectangle.
- **Axis inclined to HP, ∥ VP** → TV = ellipse, FV = inclined rectangle, SV = rectangle (true length).
- **Axis inclined to both** → Use rotation method to find true length before final projection.

### 🪚 Must-Know Section Rules
- **Plane ∥ axis** → Section is a **rectangle** of dimensions $2r \times$ (length of cut).
- **Plane ⊥ axis** → Section is a **circle** (true shape = base circle).
- **Plane inclined to axis** → Section is an **ellipse**, minor axis = $2r$, major axis = $h / \sin\phi$.

### 🧾 Must-Know Development Rules
- Develop a cylinder by dividing the top circle into **12 equal parts**.
- Project these points to the Front View as **generators** (vertical lines).
- Mark the **height of the cut** on each generator (measured vertically in the Front View).
- Transfer these heights to the development rectangle (whose base = $2 \pi r$ and height = $h$).
- Join the height points with a **smooth curve** (French curve).

### ⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid
1. **Forgetting to label views** (TV, FV, SV) — loses 1 mark each.
2. **Drawing to wrong scale** — KTU specifies 1:1 for most problems.
3. **Using apparent lengths instead of true lengths** in development — loses 2–3 marks.
4. **Hatching the wrong portion** — only hatch the **cut (removed) material**, not the retained solid.
5. **Missing the XY reference line** — loses 0.5 mark.
6. **Drawing with pen instead of pencil** for working drawings — board examiners deduct marks for unclear construction lines.

### 🏆 Examiner's Top Tip
> Always begin a cylinder problem by **writing down the given data** (diameter, height, orientation, cutting plane angle) at the top of the answer sheet. Then draw the **reference axes and a small thumbnail sketch** of the expected answer. This 30-second investment can earn you 1–2 "presentation marks" that KTU examiners routinely award.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frustum of Pyramid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Frustum of Pyramid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frustum of a Pyramid (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *frustum of a pyramid* is the portion of a pyramid that remains after the top portion (above a plane parallel to the base) is cut off by a plane parallel to the base. Geometrically, it is a solid bounded by two similar, parallel polygonal faces (the larger base and the smaller top face) and a series of trapezoidal lateral faces.

In the **2024 Scheme Engineering Graphics** syllabus, a frustum is treated as a *truncated solid* whose projection conventions must follow the **First Angle Projection** method (IS 696 / BIS standards adopted by KTU).

The frustum is classified by:
1. The **shape of the base polygon** (Square, Rectangular, Pentagonal, Hexagonal, etc.)
2. The **position of the axis** — *Right Frustum* (axis perpendicular to the base) or *Oblique Frustum* (axis inclined to the base).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** KTU specifically tests the construction of the **Frustum of a Square Pyramid**, **Frustum of a Pentagonal Pyramid**, and **Frustum of a Hexagonal Pyramid**, with emphasis on the orthographic projections (front view, top view, and side view).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cake-Cutter Visualisation"
