Imagine a tall **square birthday cake** (this is the full pyramid). You slice it **horizontally** at some height between the top and the base. The piece you remove is the *top portion* (a smaller pyramid, called the *apex pyramid*). The piece that remains on the plate is the **frustum**.

| Object | Real-World Analogy | Geometric Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| Square cake | Bottom large square face | Base of Frustum |
| Cutting plane (knife) | Horizontal slice | Truncating plane |
| Top cut face | Top of remaining cake | Top face of Frustum |
| Trapezoidal frosting on the side | Slanted edges | Trapezoidal lateral faces |
| Height of cake left on plate | Distance between cuts | **Height (h)** of frustum |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Trick:** If you know the formula for the full pyramid, you can find the frustum by **subtraction**:
> $$\text{Volume of Frustum} = \text{Volume of Full Pyramid} - \text{Volume of Cut-off Top Pyramid}$$

The "missing top" is called the **complementary pyramid** — it has the *same apex* as the original pyramid but a smaller base. This mental model is **essential** for understanding the derivation in Section 3.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Nomenclature

The following **standard dimensional parameters** are used universally in KTU board problems:

- **Base side length:** denoted **a** (in **mm** or **cm**, typically **40 mm** to **60 mm** in KTU drawing sheets)
- **Top side length:** denoted **b** (always **b < a**)
- **Height of frustum:** denoted **h** (typically **50 mm** to **80 mm**)
- **Slant height of frustum:** denoted **l**
- **Full pyramid height:** denoted **H** (measured from apex to base)
- **Cut-off (top) pyramid height:** denoted **h₁**
- **Total pyramid height relationship:** $H = h + h_1$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU boards use **mm** as the standard unit. Always write units on every dimension line. Missing units is one of the most common reasons for losing **1–2 marks** in the drawing evaluation.

---

### 1.4 Why KTU Tests This Topic

Frustums appear in:
- **Architecture:** Truncated pyramid roofs, sloped ceilings
- **Civil Engineering:** Bridge piers, dam spillways, embankments
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Hopper bottoms, funnel transitions
- **Everyday Life:** Buckets, lampshades, flower pots, paper cups

The ability to **project a 3D frustum into 2D orthographic views** and to **calculate its surface area and volume** forms the foundation for advanced topics like development of surfaces (Module 5) and isometric projections (Module 2/3).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — How a Frustum is Constructed Geometrically

A frustum is a **derived solid**, meaning it is not constructed from scratch but is obtained by *modifying* a parent solid. The construction logic follows a strict geometric sequence:

**Step 1 — Establish the Parent Pyramid**
A pyramid is defined by:
- A polygonal base (n-sided regular polygon)
- An apex point lying above the centroid of the base
- A slant height from apex to the midpoint of a base edge

**Step 2 — Introduce the Truncating Plane**
A plane parallel to the base intersects the pyramid. By the principle of **similarity of sections**, this intersection produces a polygon **similar to the base** (same number of sides, proportional lengths, same angles).

**Step 3 — Define the Frustum**
The frustum is bounded by:
- Two parallel, similar polygonal faces (base and top)
- Lateral trapezoidal faces (the "sliced" portions of the original triangular faces of the pyramid)

**Step 4 — Establish Key Geometric Relations**
By similarity, the ratio of corresponding sides equals the ratio of distances from the apex:
$$\frac{b}{a} = \frac{h_1}{H} = \frac{l_1}{L}$$

Where $l_1$ is the slant height of the cut-off pyramid and $L$ is the slant height of the full pyramid.

---

### 2.2 Why Each Formula Exists — The "Why" Behind the Math

| Formula Component | Geometric Origin | Why It Exists |
|---|---|---|
| $\sqrt{A_1 \cdot A_2}$ term | Similarity of polygons | Captures the "average" cross-sectional area between top and bottom |
| $\frac{h}{3}$ factor | Integration of constant cross-section change | Volume of a pyramid family is always 1/3 × base × height |
| $P_1 + P_2$ factor | Lateral surface unrolling | The lateral surface of a frustum unfolds into a trapezoidal "skirt" |
| $\frac{a-b}{2}$ term | Apothem difference | Horizontal offset between corresponding vertices of top and base |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for Frustum of Pyramid — Memorise Before Exam**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volume of Frustum (General) | $V = \dfrac{h}{3}\left(A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}\right)$ | $h$=height, $A_1, A_2$=base areas | $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{cm}^3$ |
| 2 | Volume — Square Frustum | $V = \dfrac{h}{3}\left(a^2 + b^2 + ab\right)$ | $a, b$ = sides of bottom, top squares | $\text{mm}^3$ |
| 3 | Volume — Hexagonal Frustum | $V = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{h}{3}\left(a^2 + b^2 + ab\right)$ | $a, b$ = sides of hexagons | $\text{mm}^3$ |
| 4 | Lateral Surface Area (General) | $L = \dfrac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2) \cdot l$ | $P_1, P_2$ = perimeters, $l$ = slant height | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 5 | Slant Height — Square Frustum | $l = \sqrt{h^2 + \left(\dfrac{a-b}{2}\right)^2}$ | $a, b, h$ as above | $\text{mm}$ |
| 6 | Slant Edge — Square Frustum | $l_e = \sqrt{h^2 + \left(\dfrac{a-b}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}$ | $a, b, h$ as above | $\text{mm}$ |
| 7 | Total Surface Area | $TSA = L + A_1 + A_2$ | All areas | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 8 | Cut-off Pyramid Height | $h_1 = \dfrac{b \cdot H}{a} = \dfrac{b \cdot h}{a - b}$ | Using similarity | $\text{mm}$ |
| 9 | Full Pyramid Height | $H = \dfrac{a \cdot h}{a - b}$ | Total height from apex | $\text{mm}$ |
| 10 | Axis Inclination (Oblique) | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{horizontal offset}}{h}$ | $\theta$ = axis tilt | degrees |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Warning:** Note that all absolute-value-style notations (none in this topic use $\vert \cdot \vert$, but be careful with $\sqrt{\cdot}$ arguments — they must always be **positive** real numbers).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Frustum Geometry |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Bridge abutments, stepped pyramid foundations, retaining wall cross-sections |
| **Architectural Design** | Sloped roof segments, atrium skylights, terraced building transitions |
| **Mechanical Design** | Hopper funnels, exhaust manifolds, gear housing transitions |
| **Manufacturing** | Sheet metal development for lamp shades, containers, transition ducts |
| **Surveying** | Volume computation of truncated earth mounds, embankments, borrow pits |

The ability to compute frustum volume is especially critical in **earthwork estimation** for road and dam construction, where the cross-section of cut/fill regions is rarely a full pyramid — it is almost always a **frustum**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General Volume Formula

We begin with the **principle of subtraction**: a frustum is a full pyramid minus the cut-off top pyramid.

**Given:**
- Full pyramid with base area $A_1$ and total height $H$
- Cut-off top pyramid with base area $A_2$ and height $h_1$
- Frustum height: $h = H - h_1$

**Step 1 — Write the volume of a full pyramid:**
$$V_{\text{full}} = \frac{1}{3} A_1 H$$

**Step 2 — Write the volume of the cut-off top pyramid:**
$$V_{\text{top}} = \frac{1}{3} A_2 h_1$$

**Step 3 — Subtract to get the frustum volume:**
$$V_{\text{frustum}} = \frac{1}{3} A_1 H - \frac{1}{3} A_2 h_1 = \frac{1}{3}(A_1 H - A_2 h_1)$$

**Step 4 — Use similarity to relate $h_1$ and $H$:**
Since the top polygon is similar to the base polygon, and the cutting plane is parallel to the base, the ratio of corresponding linear dimensions equals the ratio of heights:
$$\frac{b}{a} = \frac{h_1}{H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_1 = \frac{b}{a} H$$

**Step 5 — Use the area ratio (square of linear ratio for similar polygons):**
$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_2 = A_1 \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^2 = A_1 \left(\frac{h_1}{H}\right)^2$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $h_1$ into the frustum volume expression:**
$$V = \frac{1}{3} A_1 H - \frac{1}{3} \cdot A_1 \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{b}{a} H = \frac{1}{3} A_1 H \left[1 - \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^3\right]$$

**Step 7 — Factor using the algebraic identity** $1 - x^3 = (1 - x)(1 + x + x^2)$:
$$V = \frac{1}{3} A_1 H \left(1 - \frac{b}{a}\right)\left(1 + \frac{b}{a} + \frac{b^2}{a^2}\right)$$

**Step 8 — Note that** $H(1 - b/a) = H \cdot \dfrac{a-b}{a} = h$ (since $h = H - h_1 = H - bH/a$):
$$V = \frac{1}{3} A_1 \cdot \frac{h \cdot a}{a-b} \cdot \frac{a-b}{a}\left(1 + \frac{b}{a} + \frac{b^2}{a^2}\right)$$

Wait — let me re-derive cleanly. We have $H = \dfrac{ah}{a-b}$, so:
$$V = \frac{1}{3} A_1 \cdot \frac{ah}{a-b} \cdot \left(\frac{a-b}{a}\right)\left(1 + \frac{b}{a} + \frac{b^2}{a^2}\right)$$

**Step 9 — Cancel $a-b$ and $a$:**
$$V = \frac{h}{3} \cdot A_1 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{b}{a} + \frac{b^2}{a^2}\right)$$

**Step 10 — Express in terms of areas $A_1$ and $A_2$:** Recall $A_2 = A_1 (b/a)^2$, so $A_1 (b^2/a^2) = A_2$ and $A_1 (b/a) = \sqrt{A_1 A_2}$:
$$V = \frac{h}{3}\left(A_1 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2} + A_2\right)$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{frustum}} = \frac{h}{3}\left(A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}\right)}$$

---

### 3.2 Special Case: Volume of a Square Frustum

**Step 1 — Set** $A_1 = a^2$ and $A_2 = b^2$:
$$V = \frac{h}{3}\left(a^2 + b^2 + \sqrt{a^2 \cdot b^2}\right) = \frac{h}{3}\left(a^2 + b^2 + ab\right)$$

**Verification with limiting case:** When $b \to 0$, $V \to \frac{h}{3} a^2 = \frac{1}{3} a^2 h$ — which is the volume of a pyramid of base $a$ and height $h$. ✓

**Verification when** $b = a$: $V = \frac{h}{3}(3a^2) = a^2 h$ — which is a rectangular slab (frustum with no taper). ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Slant Height of a Square Frustum

**Geometry:** Consider the cross-section through the axis of a square frustum. We see a trapezoid with parallel sides $a$ and $b$, and height $h$. The slant height $l$ is the perpendicular distance between the parallel sides along the trapezoid's lateral edge.

**Step 1 — Identify the horizontal offset** between the top and bottom edges:
$$\text{Horizontal offset} = \frac{a - b}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Pythagorean theorem** to the right triangle formed by $h$, the offset, and $l$:
$$l^2 = h^2 + \left(\frac{a - b}{2}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{l = \sqrt{h^2 + \left(\frac{a - b}{2}\right)^2}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Slant Edge of a Square Frustum

**Step 1 — The slant edge** runs from a bottom vertex to the corresponding top vertex (diagonally across the face, not along the perpendicular from the midpoint of the edge).

**Step 2 — The horizontal offset between corresponding vertices** of bottom and top squares is the **half-diagonal difference**:
$$\text{Vertex offset} = \frac{\text{diagonal of bottom} - \text{diagonal of top}}{2} = \frac{a\sqrt{2} - b\sqrt{2}}{2} = \frac{(a-b)\sqrt{2}}{2} = \frac{a-b}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Pythagorean theorem:**
$$l_e^2 = h^2 + \left(\frac{a-b}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = h^2 + \frac{(a-b)^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{l_e = \sqrt{h^2 + \frac{(a-b)^2}{2}}}$$

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Square Frustum

**Given:** Base side $a = 50$ mm, top side $b = 30$ mm, frustum height $h = 60$ mm.

**Step 1 — Compute base and top areas:**
$$A_1 = a^2 = 50^2 = 2500 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_2 = b^2 = 30^2 = 900 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute volume using the square formula:**
$$V = \frac{60}{3}\left(2500 + 900 + 50 \cdot 30\right) = 20 \cdot (2500 + 900 + 1500)$$
$$V = 20 \cdot 4900 = 98000 \text{ mm}^3 = 98 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute slant height:**
$$l = \sqrt{60^2 + \left(\frac{50-30}{2}\right)^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 100} = \sqrt{3700} \approx 60.83 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute lateral surface area** (perimeters: $P_1 = 4 \times 50 = 200$ mm, $P_2 = 4 \times 30 = 120$ mm):
$$L = \frac{1}{2}(200 + 120) \cdot 60.83 = 160 \cdot 60.83 = 9732.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute total surface area:**
$$TSA = 9732.8 + 2500 + 900 = 13132.8 \text{ mm}^2 \approx 131.33 \text{ cm}^2$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Square vs. Hexagonal Frustum

| Parameter | Square Frustum | Hexagonal Frustum | Difference Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base area | $a^2$ | $\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} a^2$ | Hexagon area formula |
| Number of lateral faces | **4** trapezoids | **6** trapezoids | Equal to base side count |
| Perimeter | $4a$ | $6a$ | Sum of all side lengths |
| Volume formula | $\frac{h}{3}(a^2+b^2+ab)$ | $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cdot\frac{h}{3}(a^2+b^2+ab)$ | Area scaling |
| Apothem difference | $\frac{a-b}{2}$ | $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cdot\frac{a-b}{2}$ | Apothem of hexagon vs. square |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Geometric Decomposition of a Frustum

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the conceptual decomposition of a frustum into its parent and child pyramids.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Full Pyramid] --> B[Base Polygon Area A1]
    A --> C[Apex Point at Height H above base centroid]
    A --> D[Lateral Triangular Faces]

    E[Truncating Plane parallel to base] --> F[Position at height h1 from apex]
    E --> G[Intersects lateral faces]

    A -.cut by.-> E
    E --> H[Cut-off Top Pyramid]
    E --> I[Frustum of Pyramid]

    H --> H1[Base A2 smaller than A1]
    H --> H2[Height h1 from apex]
    H --> H3[Similar to parent pyramid]

    I --> I1[Bottom face: Base A1]
    I --> I2[Top face: Smaller polygon A2]
    I --> I3[Lateral faces: Trapezoids]
    I --> I4[Total height: h = H minus h1]

    H1 --> R[Similarity Ratio r = b over a = h1 over H]
    H2 --> R
    H3 --> R
    R --> J[Volume Frustum = V Full minus V Top]
    J --> K[V equals h over 3 times A1 plus A2 plus sqrt A1 A2]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Orthographic Projection Workflow (KTU Board Procedure)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Draw base polygon in Top View] --> P2[Step 2: Project vertices to Front View]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Mark full pyramid height H on axis]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Locate apex in Front View]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Project cutting plane line at height h above base]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Locate top polygon vertices in Top View using projectors]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Complete Top View with hidden lines for top polygon]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Complete Front View with visible trapezoidal faces]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Generate Side View by projecting 45 degree lines]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Dimension all views with mm units]
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic Visualisation — Cross-Sectional Geometry (Trapezoidal Profile)

The cross-section through the central axis of a **right square frustum** reveals a **symmetrical isosceles trapezoid**. Below is the Mermaid representation of this critical 2D profile, which is the basis for the slant height derivation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Bottom base of length a] --> B1[Left slanted edge of length l]
    A1 --> B2[Right slanted edge of length l]
    A1 --> A2[Perpendicular height h between bases]
    A2 --> A3[Top base of length b]
    B1 --> A3
    B2 --> A3
    A2 --> A4[Horizontal offset equals a minus b over 2 on each side]

    subgraph TrapezoidComponents["Cross-Section Components"]
        T1[Parallel side 1: Bottom a]
        T2[Parallel side 2: Top b]
        T3[Height: h]
        T4[Slant side: l = sqrt h squared plus a minus b over 2 squared]
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Visual Summary of Key Dimensions in 3D

| Feature | Location in 3D Frustum | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Bottom square | Base | Side **a** |
| Top square | Top face | Side **b** |
| Lateral edge | Slanted edges of trapezoidal faces | Slant edge $l_e$ |
| Mid-edge slant | From mid of bottom edge to mid of top edge | Slant height **l** |
| Axis | Centre of base to centre of top | Height **h** |
| Hidden edges | Inside top polygon in top view | Dashed lines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawing Convention (KTU / BIS):**
> - **Visible edges** → **Continuous thick lines** (≈ 0.6 mm)
> - **Hidden edges** → **Dashed lines** of equal weight
> - **Centre lines / axis** → **Chain thin lines** (long-dash-short-dash)
> - **Dimension lines** → **Thin continuous lines with arrows**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Define a *frustum of a pyramid*. With the help of a neat sketch, differentiate between a *right frustum* and an *oblique frustum*.

**Model Answer:**

A **frustum of a pyramid** is the portion of a pyramid that remains after a section parallel to the base cuts off the top portion. It is bounded by two similar, parallel polygonal faces (the larger base and the smaller top) and a series of trapezoidal lateral faces.

**Differentiation:**

| Feature | Right Frustum | Oblique Frustum |
|---|---|---|
| Axis orientation | Perpendicular to the base | Inclined to the base |
| Lateral faces | Symmetrical trapezoids | Asymmetrical trapezoids |
| Top polygon position | Centred above base centroid | Offset from base centroid |
| Top view | Top polygon concentric with base | Top polygon displaced (offset circle/polygon visible) |

**Sketch** (described in words): In a right frustum, the top polygon lies directly above the base polygon's centre. In an oblique frustum, the top polygon is shifted in one or two directions, making the slant edges on opposite sides unequal.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 1 for differentiation + 1 for sketch]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
State and explain the formula for the **volume of a frustum of a square pyramid** with base side *a*, top side *b*, and height *h*.

**Model Answer:**

$$\boxed{V = \frac{h}{3}\left(a^2 + b^2 + ab\right)}$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- $\frac{h}{3}$ → arises from the integration of cross-sectional area (pyramid family invariant)
- $a^2$ → area of the bottom square base
- $b^2$ → area of the top square face
- $ab$ → geometric mean term, equal to $\sqrt{a^2 \cdot b^2}$, capturing the average cross-section

**Limiting cases to verify:**
- If $b = 0$: $V = \frac{h}{3} a^2$ → volume of a pyramid of base $a$ and height $h$ ✓
- If $b = a$: $V = \frac{h}{3}(3a^2) = a^2 h$ → volume of a rectangular prism of base $a^2$ and height $h$ ✓

**[3 Marks: 1 for formula + 1 for explanation + 1 for verification]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**

A square pyramid of base side **50 mm** and axis height **70 mm** is resting on its base on the HP with all sides of the base parallel to the VP. It is cut by a horizontal cutting plane at a height of **45 mm** above the base. Draw the **front view, top view, and right side view** of the **remaining frustum**. Also calculate the **volume of the frustum** in cm³.

**Sub-parts:**
- **(a)** Construct the orthographic projections of the frustum. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** Calculate the volume of the frustum and its lateral surface area. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Construction Steps

**Given Data:**
- Base square: $a = 50$ mm, sides parallel to VP
- Full pyramid height: $H = 70$ mm
- Cutting plane height above base: $h = 45$ mm
- Top side length: $b = ?$ (must be calculated by similarity)

**Step 1 — Compute the top side length using similarity:**
The cut-off top pyramid has height $h_1 = H - h = 70 - 45 = 25$ mm.
By similarity: $\dfrac{b}{a} = \dfrac{h_1}{H}$
$$b = a \cdot \frac{h_1}{H} = 50 \cdot \frac{25}{70} = 50 \cdot \frac{5}{14} = \frac{250}{14} \approx 17.86 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Draw the Top View (Plan):**
- Draw a square of side 50 mm with its sides parallel/perpendicular to the XY line.
- Mark the centre O.
- Mark all four corners A, B, C, D (in sequence).
- Draw the centre line (chain line) through O.

**Step 3 — Draw the Front View (Elevation):**
- Project all four corners of the square up to a height of 70 mm.
- Locate the apex V directly above O at 70 mm height.
- Draw lines from V to the four corners A, B, C, D to form the full pyramid.
- Draw a horizontal cutting plane line at 45 mm above the base.
- The full pyramid outline in the front view is a triangle of base 50 mm and height 70 mm.

**Step 4 — Locate the Top Polygon:**
- The cutting plane intersects the four slant edges VA, VB, VC, VD at points A', B', C', D'.
- These intersection points are at 45 mm height (since the base is at 0 and apex is at 70 mm).
- Project A', B', C', D' down to the top view.
- Measure the projected distances to find the top square vertices.
- The top square has side $b \approx 17.86$ mm, centred on O (since the pyramid is a right pyramid).
- Draw the top square in the top view using dashed lines (since it is below the cutting plane in the front view and not directly visible from above without hidden lines).

**Step 5 — Finalise the Front View of the Frustum:**
- The frustum's front view is a **trapezoid** with parallel sides 50 mm (base) and 17.86 mm (top), height 45 mm.
- Draw it with thick continuous lines.

**Step 6 — Draw the Right Side View:**
- Project from both front and top views using 45° auxiliary lines.
- The side view is identical in shape to the front view (since the base is square and axis is vertical).
- Side view is also a trapezoid of base 50 mm, top 17.86 mm, height 45 mm.

**Step 7 — Dimension all views:**
- Mark $a = 50$ mm, $b \approx 17.86$ mm, $H = 70$ mm, $h = 45$ mm, $h_1 = 25$ mm on the views.

**[7 Marks breakdown: 2 for top view, 2 for front view, 2 for side view, 1 for dimensions]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Volume & Surface Area

**Step 1 — Compute areas:**
$$A_1 = a^2 = 50^2 = 2500 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_2 = b^2 = (17.86)^2 \approx 318.98 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply volume formula:**
$$V = \frac{h}{3}\left(A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}\right) = \frac{45}{3}\left(2500 + 318.98 + \sqrt{2500 \cdot 318.98}\right)$$

$$\sqrt{2500 \cdot 318.98} = \sqrt{797450} \approx 893.0 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$V = 15 \times (2500 + 318.98 + 893.0) = 15 \times 3711.98 \approx 55679.7 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 55679.7 \text{ mm}^3 = 55.68 \text{ cm}^3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute slant height:**
$$l = \sqrt{h^2 + \left(\frac{a-b}{2}\right)^2} = \sqrt{45^2 + \left(\frac{50-17.86}{2}\right)^2} = \sqrt{2025 + (16.07)^2}$$
$$l = \sqrt{2025 + 258.24} = \sqrt{2283.24} \approx 47.78 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute perimeters:**
$$P_1 = 4a = 4 \times 50 = 200 \text{ mm}, \quad P_2 = 4b = 4 \times 17.86 \approx 71.44 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute lateral surface area:**
$$L = \frac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2) \cdot l = \frac{1}{2}(200 + 71.44) \times 47.78 = 135.72 \times 47.78$$
$$\boxed{L \approx 6485.7 \text{ mm}^2 = 64.86 \text{ cm}^2}$$

**Step 6 — Total surface area:**
$$TSA = L + A_1 + A_2 = 6485.7 + 2500 + 318.98 = 9304.68 \text{ mm}^2 \approx 93.05 \text{ cm}^2$$

**[7 Marks breakdown: 2 for volume formula application, 2 for slant height, 2 for lateral surface area, 1 for total surface area]**

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]**

A **regular hexagonal pyramid** of base side **30 mm** and axis height **65 mm** rests on its base on the HP with two sides of the base parallel to the VP. A horizontal cutting plane at a height of **40 mm** above the base truncates the apex. Draw the orthographic projections (front view, top view, and side view) of the resulting frustum and determine its **volume** and **total surface area**.

**Sub-parts:**
- **(a)** Draw the three orthographic views with proper projection lines. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** Calculate the volume and total surface area of the frustum. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Construction Steps

**Step 1 — Draw the Top View (Hexagon):**
- Draw a regular hexagon of side 30 mm with two sides parallel to the XY reference line.
- Locate the six corners: A, B, C, D, E, F (going clockwise).
- Mark the centre O.

**Step 2 — Project to Front View:**
- Project the lowest and highest points of the hexagon (the points on the perpendicular bisector of the parallel sides) up to the XY line.
- Mark the base at 0 mm and the apex V at 65 mm above O.
- Draw slant lines from V to the two extreme vertices of the hexagon (which appear at the extreme left and right in the top view).
- The front view appears as a **triangle** with base equal to the apothem-doubled distance, height 65 mm.

**Step 3 — Apply the Cutting Plane:**
- Draw a horizontal line at 40 mm above the base in the front view.
- This line intersects the two slant lines of the front view at two points (call them A' and F', or whichever two appear at the extreme corners in the front view).
- The remaining frustum's front view is a **trapezoid** with base = full apothem-doubled distance and top = corresponding truncated width.

**Step 4 — Find the top hexagon side length:**
By similarity: $b = a \cdot \dfrac{h_1}{H} = 30 \cdot \dfrac{65-40}{65} = 30 \cdot \dfrac{25}{65} = 30 \cdot \dfrac{5}{13} \approx 11.54$ mm

**Step 5 — Project the top hexagon:**
- Project the intersection points of the cutting plane down to the top view.
- The top hexagon (side 11.54 mm) is centred at O, with the same orientation as the base.
- Draw it in dashed lines (hidden) in the top view since it lies below the top of the frustum.

**Step 6 — Draw the Right Side View:**
- Using 45° projectors from the top view, project to a vertical line on the right of the front view.
- The side view is also a trapezoid (with a different base width corresponding to the apothem-perpendicular dimension).

**Step 7 — Dimension and complete.**

**[7 Marks breakdown: 2 top view, 2 front view, 2 side view, 1 dimensions]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Calculations

**Step 1 — Compute areas using hexagon area formula** $A = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} s^2$:
$$A_1 = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot 30^2 = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot 900 = 1350\sqrt{3} \approx 2338.27 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_2 = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot 11.54^2 = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot 133.17 \approx 346.04 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the volume formula:**
$$V = \frac{h}{3}\left(A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}\right) = \frac{40}{3}(2338.27 + 346.04 + \sqrt{2338.27 \cdot 346.04})$$

$$\sqrt{2338.27 \cdot 346.04} = \sqrt{809078.2} \approx 899.49 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$V = \frac{40}{3}(2338.27 + 346.04 + 899.49) = \frac{40}{3} \times 3583.80$$
$$\boxed{V \approx 47784.0 \text{ mm}^3 = 47.78 \text{ cm}^3}$$

**Step 3 — Slant height of hexagonal frustum:**
For a regular hexagon, the **apothem** (centre-to-midpoint-of-side distance) is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} s$.
Horizontal offset of slant height: $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{a - b}{2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(a - b)$
$$l = \sqrt{h^2 + \left[\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(a-b)\right]^2} = \sqrt{40^2 + \left[\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(30-11.54)\right]^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{1600 + \left[\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot 18.46\right]^2} = \sqrt{1600 + [7.99]^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 63.88} \approx \sqrt{1663.88} \approx 40.79 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Lateral surface area:**
$$P_1 = 6 \times 30 = 180 \text{ mm}, \quad P_2 = 6 \times 11.54 \approx 69.24 \text{ mm}$$
$$L = \frac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2) \cdot l = \frac{1}{2}(180 + 69.24) \times 40.79 = 124.62 \times 40.79 \approx 5083.1 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Total surface area:**
$$TSA = L + A_1 + A_2 = 5083.1 + 2338.27 + 346.04 \approx 7767.4 \text{ mm}^2 = 77.67 \text{ cm}^2$$

**[7 Marks breakdown: 2 areas, 2 volume, 2 slant height and LSA, 1 TSA]**

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in Frustum Problems**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** on dimension lines → Lose up to **1 mark per view** (3 views = up to 3 marks).
> 2. **Not converting** $b$ correctly using similarity ratio. Always verify: $\dfrac{b}{a} = \dfrac{h_1}{H}$, NOT $\dfrac{b}{a} = \dfrac{h}{H}$.
> 3. **Mixing up slant height vs. slant edge.** Slant height $l$ goes from mid-edge to mid-edge; slant edge $l_e$ goes from vertex to vertex. KTU questions frequently ask for the **lateral surface area**, which requires **slant height**, not slant edge.
> 4. **Drawing hidden lines as solid** or vice versa. In the top view, the top polygon is *hidden* — must be drawn in **dashed lines**.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw the cutting plane line** in the front view (or drawing it as a thick line). Cutting plane lines should be **thin chain lines** (long-dash-short-dash) in standard convention.
> 6. **Not labelling** the apex V, the cutting plane height, and the side lengths $a$ and $b$ on the figure.
> 7. **Volume formula errors:** Writing $V = \frac{h}{3}(A_1 + A_2)$ — this is the **frustum of a cone** formula, NOT the pyramid! Always include the $\sqrt{A_1 A_2}$ term for pyramids.
> 8. **Rounding too early:** Carry at least 3 decimal places until the final answer, then round to 2 decimal places for the final value.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Frustum of Pyramid**

- [ ] **Definition:** A frustum is a pyramid with its top cut off by a plane parallel to the base.
- [ ] **Two parallel similar polygons** form the top and bottom faces; **trapezoidal faces** form the lateral boundary.
- [ ] **Similarity is the key:** $\dfrac{b}{a} = \dfrac{h_1}{H}$ — this single relation unlocks every dimension.
- [ ] **General volume formula:** $V = \dfrac{h}{3}\left(A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}\right)$ — must remember the **geometric mean term**.
- [ ] **Square frustum volume:** $V = \dfrac{h}{3}\left(a^2 + b^2 + ab\right)$.
- [ ] **Slant height of square frustum:** $l = \sqrt{h^2 + \left(\dfrac{a-b}{2}\right)^2}$ — uses the **half-difference** of sides.
- [ ] **Slant edge of square frustum:** $l_e = \sqrt{h^2 + \dfrac{(a-b)^2}{2}}$ — uses the **half-diagonal difference**.
- [ ] **Lateral surface area:** $L = \dfrac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2) \cdot l$ — always uses **slant height**, never slant edge.
- [ ] **Total surface area:** $TSA = L + A_1 + A_2$.
- [ ] **First angle projection** is the KTU standard; in the top view, the front of the object appears at the **bottom** of the view.
- [ ] **Hidden lines** (top polygon in top view) → **dashed thin lines**.
- [ ] **Visible edges** (slants and outer base) → **continuous thick lines**.
- [ ] **Centre/axis lines** → **chain lines** (long-dash-short-dash).
- [ ] **Always** write units (**mm**) on every dimension line.
- [ ] **Verify** with limiting cases: $b = 0$ should give a pyramid; $b = a$ should give a prism.
- [ ] **Construction sequence:** Top view first → Front view (projecting up) → Cutting plane → Locate top polygon → Side view (45° projectors) → Dimension.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frustum of Cone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Frustum of Cone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **FRUSTUM OF A CONE** — *A frustum of a cone (truncated cone) is the solid portion of a right circular cone that remains between its base and a plane parallel to the base, cutting across the cone at some distance from the apex.*

In strict engineering drawing parlance, a **frustum of a right circular cone** is generated when a right circular cone is sectioned by a plane **parallel to the circular base**, and the portion containing the base is retained while the smaller top portion (which is itself a smaller, similar cone) is discarded. The remaining solid has two parallel circular faces of *unequal diameter* — the **larger base** (radius $R$) at the bottom and the **smaller top circle** (radius $r$) at the top — connected by a **truncated lateral (slant) surface**.

The key terminology that KTU examiners expect in answers is:

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Larger base radius | $R$ | Radius of the original (full) cone's base |
| Smaller top radius | $r$ | Radius of the cut section parallel to the base |
| Height of frustum | $h_f$ | Perpendicular distance between the two parallel circular faces |
| Slant height of frustum | $l$ | The shortest distance along the lateral surface between the edges of the two bases |
| Height of original (full) cone | $H$ | Perpendicular distance from apex to the larger base |
| Height of small (cut-off) cone | $h_1$ | Perpendicular distance from apex to the cutting plane |
| Semi-vertical angle | $\alpha$ | Half the angle made by the slant edge with the axis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The frustum is **NOT a complete solid** in itself — it is fundamentally the *remainder* of a geometric subtraction. The full cone minus the small cut-off cone equals the frustum. This conceptual relationship is repeatedly tested in KTU valuation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real World Analogies

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Without a Handle"**
> Imagine a tall traffic cone (the kind placed on roads). Now slice off the pointed top with a perfectly horizontal knife cut, and throw away the pointed tip. What remains in your hand is a frustum of a cone. The flat bottom (where the cone was standing) is the *larger base*, and the new flat circular cut you just made is the *smaller top*.

Everyday examples that are physically frustums of cones:

- **A drinking tumbler (the wide-bottomed type)** — bottom is wider, top is narrower, lateral surface is slanting.
- **A traditional lampshade** (truncated conical type) — same geometry, smaller top opening for the bulb holder.
- **A flower pot** — wider at top, narrower at base (inverted frustum).
- **A drinking straw/beaker with sloping sides** in a chemistry lab.
- **A pail or bucket** with a tapering profile.

> [!NOTE]
> The single most useful mental model: **Frustum = Full Cone − Small Similar Cone (cut from the apex).** Every KTU problem on frustum begins from this conceptual fact.

## 1.3 Standard Geometric Constants & Assumptions

The following standards are assumed unless otherwise specified in the problem:

- The frustum is derived from a **right circular cone** (axis perpendicular to the base).
- The cutting plane is **parallel to the base**, ensuring the small cone removed is *similar* to the original.
- All measurements are in **millimetres (mm)** as per KTU drawing convention.
- The **axis of the frustum is vertical** in the front view of orthographic projection.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Construction of a Frustum — Logic Flow

The construction of a frustum rests on a chain of geometric facts. Each step depends on the previous one. KTU examiners award partial marks for *every* correctly identified relationship.

**Step 1 — Similarity of the cut-off cone to the original cone.**
When a cone of height $H$ and base radius $R$ is cut by a plane parallel to the base at a height $h_1$ from the apex, the removed top portion is a smaller cone that is **geometrically similar** to the original. The ratio of corresponding linear dimensions is constant:

$$
\frac{r}{R} = \frac{h_1}{H}
$$

**Why this works:** Two cones with parallel circular cross-sections and a common apex are similar by AA similarity (common apex angle + parallel cutting plane creates equal corresponding angles). The ratio of radii equals the ratio of any two corresponding linear measurements (heights, slant heights, diameters).

**Step 2 — The frustum height is the difference of the two cone heights.**
The frustum is the region between the cutting plane and the base. Therefore:

$$
h_f = H - h_1
$$

**Why:** The full cone height $H$ is measured apex to base. Removing the top $h_1$ leaves a vertical span of $(H - h_1)$ between the cut and the base.

**Step 3 — The slant height of the frustum.**
The slant height of the original cone is $L = \sqrt{R^2 + H^2}$, and that of the small removed cone is $l_1 = \sqrt{r^2 + h_1^2}$. The frustum's slant height is:

$$
l = L - l_1
$$

Alternatively, treating the frustum as a *truncated right triangle* (when viewed in the front view as a section through the axis):

$$
l = \sqrt{h_f^{\,2} + (R - r)^2}
$$

**Why both formulas work:** Subtraction works because slant edges are collinear (lying on the slant surface of the original cone). The Pythagorean form works because the frustum's slant height, vertical height, and radial difference $(R - r)$ form a right triangle in the axial section.

**Step 4 — Surface and volume properties (supporting knowledge).**
Although drawing courses focus on projection, the formulas for surface area and volume are occasionally asked in 2-mark questions:

$$
\text{Curved (lateral) surface area} = \pi (R + r)\, l
$$

$$
\text{Total surface area} = \pi (R + r)\, l + \pi R^2 + \pi r^2
$$

$$
\text{Volume of frustum} = \frac{\pi\, h_f}{3}\left(R^2 + R\,r + r^2\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The volume formula looks identical to the *truncated pyramid* formula, but with a coefficient of $\pi/3$ instead of $1/3$ for pyramids. This is because the *area of a circular cross-section* is $\pi r^2$, not $r^2$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise the following table. It is the **complete** formula universe for the topic "Frustum of Cone" at KTU level.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Ratio of similarity | $\dfrac{r}{R} = \dfrac{h_1}{H} = \dfrac{l_1}{L}$ | All in mm |
| 2 | Frustum height | $h_f = H - h_1$ | mm |
| 3 | Slant height of full cone | $L = \sqrt{R^2 + H^2}$ | mm |
| 4 | Slant height of small cone | $l_1 = \sqrt{r^2 + h_1^2}$ | mm |
| 5 | Slant height of frustum (form 1) | $l = L - l_1$ | mm |
| 6 | Slant height of frustum (form 2) | $l = \sqrt{h_f^{\,2} + (R - r)^2}$ | mm |
| 7 | Curved surface area (CSA) | $\text{CSA} = \pi (R + r)\, l$ | mm² |
| 8 | Total surface area (TSA) | $\text{TSA} = \pi (R + r)\, l + \pi R^2 + \pi r^2$ | mm² |
| 9 | Volume of frustum | $V = \dfrac{\pi\, h_f}{3}\left(R^2 + R\,r + r^2\right)$ | mm³ |
| 10 | Semi-vertical angle | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{R}{H} = \dfrac{r}{h_1}$ | radians or degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula 6** is the most important for *drawing* problems because it gives the slant height directly from the frustum's own dimensions $(h_f, R, r)$ — no need to know $H$ or $h_1$ separately. KTU problems often give you only the frustum's height and the two diameters.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The frustum of a cone is far more than an academic curiosity. KTU examiners occasionally frame 2-mark questions around applications:

- **Civil Engineering** — *transition pieces* in water supply pipelines where the diameter must change gradually to reduce head loss; *hopper bottoms* of storage silos; *cooling tower shells*.
- **Mechanical Engineering** — *tapered shafts*, *muzzle of a gun barrel*, *rocket nozzle divergent sections*, *exhaust manifolds*.
- **Architecture** — *dome bases* of mosques, *minarets*, *pyramidal frustums* (a similar idea but square base).
- **Manufacturing** — *cast iron pipe sections* with one end larger than the other for socket-and-spigot joints.

> [!TIP]
> The frustum is preferred over a *sudden step* in diameter because the **gradual transition** reduces turbulence, stress concentration, and flow losses. This is why every engineering student must understand its geometry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Slant Height of a Frustum (Form 2)

This is the derivation KTU most frequently asks, and the most common place students lose marks. We start from first principles.

**Given:**
- A right circular cone of base radius $R$ and vertical height $H$.
- A horizontal cutting plane at perpendicular distance $h_1$ from the apex.
- Top radius of frustum is $r$, frustum height is $h_f$.

**To Prove:**
$$
l = \sqrt{h_f^{\,2} + (R - r)^2}
$$

### Step 1 — Establish Similarity

The full cone (radius $R$, height $H$) and the small cut-off cone (radius $r$, height $h_1$) share a common apex and have parallel circular cross-sections. Therefore, by the **AA criterion of similarity**:

$$
\frac{r}{R} = \frac{h_1}{H}
$$

This ratio is a pure number, dimensionless.

### Step 2 — Express $h_1$ in Terms of $H$ and the Frustum Geometry

From the ratio:

$$
h_1 = \frac{r \cdot H}{R}
$$

The frustum height is, by definition:

$$
h_f = H - h_1
$$

### Step 3 — Express the Slant Height of the Full Cone

Using the Pythagorean theorem on the axial section (a right triangle with legs $R$ and $H$, hypotenuse being the slant height $L$):

$$
L = \sqrt{R^2 + H^2}
$$

### Step 4 — Express the Slant Height of the Small Cut-off Cone

$$
l_1 = \sqrt{r^2 + h_1^2}
$$

### Step 5 — Subtract to Get the Frustum's Slant Height

Because the slant generators of the small cone lie on the same line as those of the full cone, the frustum's slant height is:

$$
l = L - l_1
$$

### Step 6 — Alternative Direct Derivation (Preferred for KTU)

Consider the axial cross-section of the frustum. It is a **trapezoid** (isoceles) bounded by:
- Top edge: length $2r$ (diameter of top circle).
- Bottom edge: length $2R$ (diameter of base).
- Two slant sides each of length $l$ (slant height of frustum).
- Vertical distance between the two parallel edges: $h_f$.

Drop a perpendicular from the top edge to the bottom edge. The horizontal distance covered is $(R - r)$ (since the radii differ by $R - r$ on each side). The perpendicular forms a **right triangle** with:
- Vertical leg: $h_f$
- Horizontal leg: $R - r$
- Hypotenuse: $l$

By the Pythagorean theorem:

$$
l^2 = h_f^{\,2} + (R - r)^2
$$

Taking the positive square root (since $l$ is a physical length):

$$
\boxed{\,l = \sqrt{h_f^{\,2} + (R - r)^2}\,}
$$

This is the *cleanest and most direct* form. KTU examiners expect this derivation when only $h_f$, $R$, and $r$ are given.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Computing All Frustum Properties

**Problem:** A right circular cone of base diameter **60 mm** and height **70 mm** is cut by a plane parallel to the base at a height of **40 mm** from the apex. Determine: (a) the top radius of the frustum, (b) the frustum height, (c) the slant height of the frustum, and (d) the volume of the frustum.

**Given Data:**

$$
R = 30 \text{ mm}, \quad H = 70 \text{ mm}, \quad h_1 = 40 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step (a) — Top radius of the frustum:**

Using similarity:

$$
\frac{r}{R} = \frac{h_1}{H}
$$

$$
\frac{r}{30} = \frac{40}{70}
$$

$$
r = 30 \times \frac{40}{70} = \frac{1200}{70} = \frac{120}{7}
$$

$$
r \approx 17.143 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step (b) — Frustum height:**

$$
h_f = H - h_1 = 70 - 40 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step (c) — Slant height of the frustum (using Form 2):**

$$
l = \sqrt{h_f^{\,2} + (R - r)^2}
$$

$$
R - r = 30 - \frac{120}{7} = \frac{210 - 120}{7} = \frac{90}{7} \text{ mm} \approx 12.857 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
l = \sqrt{30^2 + \left(\frac{90}{7}\right)^2}
$$

$$
l = \sqrt{900 + \frac{8100}{49}}
$$

$$
l = \sqrt{\frac{44100 + 8100}{49}}
$$

$$
l = \sqrt{\frac{52200}{49}}
$$

$$
l = \frac{\sqrt{52200}}{7} = \frac{228.473\ldots}{7}
$$

$$
l \approx 32.639 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step (d) — Volume of the frustum:**

$$
V = \frac{\pi\, h_f}{3}\left(R^2 + R\,r + r^2\right)
$$

$$
V = \frac{\pi \times 30}{3}\left(30^2 + 30 \times \frac{120}{7} + \left(\frac{120}{7}\right)^2\right)
$$

$$
V = 10\pi\left(900 + \frac{3600}{7} + \frac{14400}{49}\right)
$$

Converting to a common denominator of 49:

$$
V = 10\pi\left(\frac{44100}{49} + \frac{25200}{49} + \frac{14400}{49}\right)
$$

$$
V = 10\pi \times \frac{83700}{49}
$$

$$
V = \frac{837000\,\pi}{49}
$$

$$
V \approx 53677.06 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V \approx 53.68 \text{ cm}^3}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Drawing-Based Frustum (Typical KTU)

**Problem:** A frustum of a cone has base diameter **80 mm**, top diameter **40 mm**, and height **60 mm**. Draw the front view, top view, and the isometric view of the frustum. Also calculate its slant height.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify dimensions:**

$$
D = 80 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 40 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
d = 40 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow r = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
h_f = 60 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate slant height:**

$$
l = \sqrt{h_f^{\,2} + (R - r)^2}
$$

$$
l = \sqrt{60^2 + (40 - 20)^2}
$$

$$
l = \sqrt{3600 + 400}
$$

$$
l = \sqrt{4000}
$$

$$
l = 20\sqrt{10} \text{ mm} \approx 63.246 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Construction of orthographic projections:**

The **front view** of a frustum resting on its base is a **trapezium** (specifically an isoceles trapezoid) with:
- Parallel sides: $80$ mm (bottom) and $40$ mm (top).
- Height: $60$ mm.
- Slant sides: each $63.246$ mm.

The **top view** is **two concentric circles**:
- Outer circle of radius $R = 40$ mm.
- Inner circle of radius $r = 20$ mm.

**Step 4 — For the isometric view:**
The frustum is placed with its axis vertical. The isometric projection uses the standard isometric axes (30° to horizontal) for the base circle and the top circle. Each circle is drawn as an ellipse with the major axis perpendicular to the axis of the cone and the minor axis along the direction of the cone's axis, scaled by the appropriate isometric scale factor.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Drawing Construction Sequence

The following table is the **master reference** for how a frustum is constructed in orthographic projection in KTU exam sheets.

| Step | Action in Front View (Elevation) | Action in Top View (Plan) |
|------|--------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Draw a centreline vertically. | Draw a centreline horizontally and vertically (cross). |
| 2 | Draw a horizontal line of length $2R$ (the base) at the bottom. | Draw a circle of radius $R$ with centre at the intersection of the two centrelines. |
| 3 | Mark the frustum height $h_f$ vertically above the base. | From the top view, project up to the front view using vertical projectors. |
| 4 | At the top, draw a horizontal line of length $2r$. | Draw a concentric inner circle of radius $r$ in the top view. |
| 5 | Join the ends of the two horizontal lines by two slant lines (these become the slant edges / generators). | The two concentric circles are visible in the top view (inner circle is visible if the frustum is hollow; otherwise hidden line). |
| 6 | The front view is a closed trapezium; hatch or dimension as required. | The top view shows the *true shape* of both circular ends. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **front view** of a frustum, you see an **isoceles trapezium**, NOT a triangle. The full cone would show as a triangle. The frustum is the trapezoidal *slice* of that triangle.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relationship Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram captures the *conceptual structure* of a frustum and its relationships to the full cone and the small cut-off cone.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Full Right Circular Cone"]:::root
    B["Height H, Base Radius R"]:::param
    C["Slant Height L = sqrt R sq + H sq"]:::formula
    D["Cutting Plane Parallel to Base"]:::action
    E["At height h1 from Apex"]:::action
    F["Small Similar Cone Removed"]:::part
    G["Height h1, Top Radius r"]:::param
    H["Slant Height l1 = sqrt r sq + h1 sq"]:::formula
    I["Frustum of Cone"]:::result
    J["Frustum Height hf = H minus h1"]:::formula
    K["Slant Height l = sqrt hf sq + R minus r sq"]:::formula
    L["Top Radius r = R times h1 by H"]:::formula
    M["Volume V = pi hf by 3 times R sq + Rr + r sq"]:::formula
    N["CSA = pi times R plus r times l"]:::formula

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    F --> H
    A --> I
    F -.-> I
    I --> J
    I --> K
    I --> L
    I --> M
    I --> N

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0a1929,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef param fill:#d9e7f5,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#0a1929
    classDef action fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#0a1929
    classDef part fill:#f5d9d9,stroke:#8b0000,color:#0a1929
    classDef formula fill:#d9f5d9,stroke:#006400,color:#0a1929
    classDef result fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#0a1929,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Orthographic Projection Construction Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the *step-by-step construction sequence* used by KTU students to draw the frustum in first-angle or third-angle projection.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Draw Reference Axes XY and Centre Lines"]:::step
    S2["Step 2: Front View - Draw Base Line of length 2R"]:::step
    S3["Step 3: Front View - Mark Height hf Vertically"]:::step
    S4["Step 4: Front View - Draw Top Line of length 2r"]:::step
    S5["Step 5: Front View - Join Ends to Form Trapezium"]:::step
    S6["Step 6: Top View - Draw Outer Circle Radius R"]:::step
    S7["Step 7: Top View - Draw Inner Circle Radius r"]:::step
    S8["Step 8: Project End Points to Top View"]:::step
    S9["Step 9: Dimension all Views as per KTU Convention"]:::step
    S10["Step 10: Add Title Block and Scale"]:::step

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    S1 --> S6
    S5 --> S8 --> S7
    S7 --> S9 --> S10

    classDef step fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#0a1929,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Isometric View Construction Logic

In KTU's Engineering Graphics and Computer Aided Drawing course, the isometric view of a frustum is constructed using the following logical steps (which we map as a flow diagram).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I1["Setup: Isometric Axes at 30 deg to Horizontal"]:::iso
    I2["Locate Centre of Base Circle on Origin"]:::iso
    I3["Draw Base Ellipse using Isometric Scale"]:::iso
    I4["Project Vertical Centreline Upward by hf"]:::iso
    I5["Locate Centre of Top Ellipse at Height hf"]:::iso
    I6["Draw Top Ellipse with Radius r using Isometric Scale"]:::iso
    I7["Draw Slant Tangent Lines from Base Edge to Top Edge"]:::iso
    I8["Complete the Visible Outline of Frustum"]:::iso
    I9["Darken Final Lines and Remove Construction Lines"]:::iso

    I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 --> I5 --> I6 --> I7 --> I8 --> I9

    classDef iso fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#0a1929,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Axial Section of Frustum (for clarity)

The following ASCII schematic depicts the axial section (a vertical cut through the axis) of a frustum. This is what a student should mentally visualise before drawing.

```
                  Top Circle (radius r)
                 ___________________
                /                   \
               /                     \      <-- Slant height l
              /                       \
             /                         \
            /___________________________\  <-- Base Circle (radius R)
            |                          |
            |    h_f (frustum height)  |
            |                          |
            ---------------------------XY Reference Line
```

In this axial section:
- The **trapezium** has top edge $2r$, bottom edge $2R$, vertical height $h_f$, and slant sides $l$.
- The two slant sides are **collinear** with the slant generators of the original full cone (which would be a triangle sitting on top of this trapezium).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define a frustum of a cone. State two real-world examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A frustum of a cone is the portion of a right circular cone that remains after its top is cut off by a plane parallel to the base, leaving a solid with two parallel circular faces of unequal radii ($R$ at the base, $r$ at the top) connected by a slanting lateral surface.

**Examples:** (i) A flower pot (wider at top, narrower at base — inverted frustum). (ii) A traffic cone with its pointed tip sliced off.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**The radii of the two ends of a frustum of a cone are $R$ and $r$, and the height is $h$. Derive the formula for its slant height.**

**Model Answer:**

The slant height $l$ of the frustum is found by considering the axial section. The section is an isoceles trapezium with parallel sides $2R$ and $2r$, vertical height $h$, and slant sides $l$. Dropping a perpendicular from the top edge to the bottom edge creates a right triangle with:
- Vertical leg $= h$
- Horizontal leg $= R - r$
- Hypotenuse $= l$

By the Pythagorean theorem:

$$
l = \sqrt{h^2 + (R - r)^2}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU's End Semester Examination, Module 4 questions are typically framed with internal choice. We provide two full alternative questions below — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`**

**(a) A right circular cone of base diameter $\mathbf{70}$ mm and height $\mathbf{80}$ mm is cut by a plane parallel to the base at a distance of $\mathbf{50}$ mm from the apex. Draw the front view, top view, and the isometric view of the remaining frustum. Calculate the slant height of the frustum. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Given data extraction:**

$$
D = 70 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 35 \text{ mm}, \quad H = 80 \text{ mm}, \quad h_1 = 50 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute top radius of frustum using similarity:**

$$
\frac{r}{R} = \frac{h_1}{H} \Rightarrow r = R \cdot \frac{h_1}{H} = 35 \times \frac{50}{80} = \frac{1750}{80} = 21.875 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute frustum height:**

$$
h_f = H - h_1 = 80 - 50 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute slant height of frustum:**

$$
l = \sqrt{h_f^{\,2} + (R - r)^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + (35 - 21.875)^2}
$$

$$
R - r = 13.125 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
l = \sqrt{900 + 172.2656} = \sqrt{1072.2656} \approx 32.74 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Construction sequence for orthographic views:**

- **Front view (elevation):** A trapezium with bottom edge $70$ mm, top edge $43.75$ mm (i.e., $2r = 43.75$ mm), and vertical height $30$ mm. Centreline drawn as a dash-dot line.
- **Top view (plan):** Two concentric circles — outer radius $35$ mm, inner radius $21.875$ mm. Centre at the origin, with horizontal and vertical centrelines.
- **Isometric view:** Draw the isometric axes at $30°$ to the horizontal. Construct the base ellipse (major axis $70$ mm perpendicular to the axis, minor axis $70 \times 0.5774 \approx 40.4$ mm along the axis), and the top ellipse at a vertical height of $30$ mm (minor axis $\approx 25.26$ mm). Join the tangent points of the two ellipses with slant lines.

**Dimensions to be marked:** Base diameter $70$ mm, top diameter $43.75$ mm, height $30$ mm, slant height $32.74$ mm.

---

**(b) A frustum of a cone has base radius $\mathbf{40}$ mm, top radius $\mathbf{20}$ mm, and height $\mathbf{50}$ mm. Find its (i) slant height, (ii) curved surface area, and (iii) volume. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**

$$
R = 40 \text{ mm}, \quad r = 20 \text{ mm}, \quad h_f = 50 \text{ mm}
$$

**(i) Slant height:**

$$
l = \sqrt{h_f^{\,2} + (R - r)^2} = \sqrt{50^2 + (40 - 20)^2} = \sqrt{2500 + 400} = \sqrt{2900} \approx 53.852 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Curved surface area:**

$$
\text{CSA} = \pi (R + r)\, l = \pi \times (40 + 20) \times 53.852 = \pi \times 60 \times 53.852
$$

$$
\text{CSA} = 60 \times 53.852 \times \pi \approx 3231.12 \times \pi \approx 10150.84 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Converting:

$$
\text{CSA} \approx 10150.84 \text{ mm}^2 \approx 101.51 \text{ cm}^2
$$

**(iii) Volume:**

$$
V = \frac{\pi\, h_f}{3}\left(R^2 + R\,r + r^2\right)
$$

$$
V = \frac{\pi \times 50}{3}\left(40^2 + 40 \times 20 + 20^2\right)
$$

$$
V = \frac{50\pi}{3}\left(1600 + 800 + 400\right) = \frac{50\pi}{3} \times 2800
$$

$$
V = \frac{140000\,\pi}{3} \approx 146607.66 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Converting:

$$
V \approx 146.61 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
l \approx 53.85 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{CSA} \approx 10150.84 \text{ mm}^2, \quad V \approx 146.61 \text{ cm}^3
$$

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`**

**(a) A cone of base diameter $\mathbf{50}$ mm and height $\mathbf{60}$ mm is cut by a horizontal plane at a height of $\mathbf{25}$ mm from the base. Draw the front view, top view, and the isometric view of the frustum formed (the portion between the cutting plane and the base). All dimensions are in mm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Given data:**

$$
D = 50 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 25 \text{ mm}, \quad H = 60 \text{ mm}, \quad h_f = 25 \text{ mm} \text{ (frustum height)}
$$

**Note:** Here the cutting plane is at $25$ mm from the **base** (not from the apex), so the frustum height is directly $h_f = 25$ mm.

**Step 2 — Compute the top radius (at the cutting plane):**

Height of the small cut-off cone from apex:

$$
h_1 = H - h_f = 60 - 25 = 35 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\frac{r}{R} = \frac{h_1}{H} \Rightarrow r = 25 \times \frac{35}{60} = \frac{875}{60} \approx 14.583 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Slant height of frustum (optional, for dimensioning):**

$$
l = \sqrt{25^2 + (25 - 14.583)^2} = \sqrt{625 + 108.51} = \sqrt{733.51} \approx 27.084 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Drawing sequence:**

- **Front view:** Trapezium with bottom $50$ mm, top $2 \times 14.583 = 29.167$ mm, height $25$ mm. Axis is the centreline.
- **Top view:** Two concentric circles, radius $25$ mm (outer) and $14.583$ mm (inner).
- **Isometric view:** Two concentric ellipses, joined by slant lines.

**Dimensions to mark:** All linear dimensions in mm, with units explicitly stated.

---

**(b) A frustum of a cone is formed by cutting a cone of base radius $\mathbf{30}$ mm and height $\mathbf{50}$ mm at a height of $\mathbf{30}$ mm from the apex. Calculate: (i) the top radius, (ii) the slant height of the frustum, and (iii) the curved surface area. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**

$$
R = 30 \text{ mm}, \quad H = 50 \text{ mm}, \quad h_1 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**(i) Top radius:**

$$
r = R \cdot \frac{h_1}{H} = 30 \times \frac{30}{50} = 30 \times 0.6 = 18 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Slant height of frustum:**

Frustum height:

$$
h_f = H - h_1 = 50 - 30 = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

Slant height:

$$
l = \sqrt{h_f^{\,2} + (R - r)^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + (30 - 18)^2} = \sqrt{400 + 144} = \sqrt{544}
$$

$$
l \approx 23.324 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Curved surface area:**

$$
\text{CSA} = \pi (R + r)\, l = \pi \times (30 + 18) \times 23.324
$$

$$
\text{CSA} = \pi \times 48 \times 23.324 = \pi \times 1119.55
$$

$$
\text{CSA} \approx 3516.99 \text{ mm}^2 \approx 35.17 \text{ cm}^2
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
r = 18 \text{ mm}, \quad l \approx 23.32 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{CSA} \approx 3516.99 \text{ mm}^2
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Frustum-of-Cone Problems — Examiner's Pitfall List:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the position of the cutting plane.** The plane can be specified as "at a height of $h_1$ from the apex" OR "at a height of $h_f$ from the base." These give **two different** frustum heights and **two different** top radii. Read the problem statement **carefully**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the units when marking dimensions.** A $70$ mm length in the drawing without the **"mm"** suffix costs a half-mark in KTU valuation. Always write the unit.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the full cone's slant height with the frustum's slant height.** The full cone's slant height $L$ is $\sqrt{R^2 + H^2}$. The frustum's slant height $l$ is $\sqrt{h_f^2 + (R - r)^2}$. These are **not** the same; the frustum's is always shorter (when the cone is truncated).
>
> 4. **Drawing the front view as a triangle instead of a trapezium.** The frustum's front view is an **isoceles trapezium**, not a triangle. Drawing it as a triangle is the single most common drawing error in KTU answer sheets.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to draw the centreline.** Every orthographic view in KTU must have the **axis / centreline** marked as a dash-dot line. Omitting it costs 1 mark per view.
>
> 6. **Mislabeling the top view.** The top view of a frustum is **two concentric circles**, not a single circle. Students sometimes draw only the outer circle and lose marks for the missing inner circle.
>
> 7. **Not using the similarity ratio correctly.** A common mistake: writing $r/R = H/h_1$ (inverted). The correct ratio is $r/R = h_1/H$ — the **smaller** radius is to the **larger** radius as the **smaller** height is to the **larger** height.
>
> 8. **Sign error in $(R - r)$.** Since $R > r$ always for a frustum cut from the apex side, $(R - r)$ is positive. If the frustum is inverted (apex cut off the base side), use $(r - R)$ — but the magnitude is the same.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Frustum of Cone**

- **Definition:** Frustum = Full Cone − Small Similar Cone (cut by a plane parallel to the base).
- **Three primary variables:** $R$ (base radius), $r$ (top radius), $h_f$ (frustum height).
- **Similarity ratio:** $r/R = h_1/H = l_1/L$ — the most fundamental relationship in the topic.
- **Slant height (best form):** $l = \sqrt{h_f^{\,2} + (R - r)^2}$ — depends only on frustum's own dimensions.
- **Slant height (alternative form):** $l = L - l_1$ — depends on the parent cone and the small cut-off cone.
- **Curved surface area:** $\text{CSA} = \pi (R + r)\, l$ — the product of mean circumference $\pi(R + r)$ and slant height $l$.
- **Total surface area:** Add the areas of the two circular ends: $\text{TSA} = \text{CSA} + \pi R^2 + \pi r^2$.
- **Volume:** $V = \dfrac{\pi h_f}{3}\left(R^2 + Rr + r^2\right)$ — the term $(R^2 + Rr + r^2)$ is the **sum of squares + cross-product**, not $(R + r)^2$ (which would be $R^2 + 2Rr + r^2$).
- **Front view shape:** Isoceles trapezium with parallel sides $2R$ and $2r$, vertical height $h_f$.
- **Top view shape:** Two concentric circles, radii $R$ and $r$.
- **Isometric view:** Two concentric ellipses joined by slant tangent lines.
- **Centreline:** Always draw a dash-dot centreline through the axis in both views.
- **Units:** Always state the unit (mm) in dimensioning, as per KTU convention.
- **Cutting plane location:** Note whether the problem gives $h_1$ (from apex) or $h_f$ (from base) — these are **different** quantities.
- **Real-world applications:** Transition pieces, hopper bottoms, lamp shades, flower pots, water pipelines, rocket nozzles.
- **Similar cones:** When the cutting plane is parallel to the base, the removed portion is a **similar** cone (same shape, smaller size), enabling all ratio-based calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sphere
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sphere

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Sphere** is a perfectly round three-dimensional solid of revolution generated by rotating a **semicircle** about its **diameter** (an axis passing through the center of the circle). Every point on the surface of the sphere is at an **equidistant** value (the radius) from a fixed interior point called the **center**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Sphere (Module 4: Solids):**
> The sphere is classified as a **double-curved solid** (non-polyhedral / non-prismatoidal solid). Unlike prisms, pyramids, cylinders, or cones, the sphere has *no straight edges, no vertices, and no flat faces*. It is bounded by a single continuous curved surface.

In the KTU 2024 Engineering Graphics framework, the sphere must be studied under the following learning scopes:

1. **Geometric Generation** — As a surface of revolution of a circle.
2. **Orthographic Projection** — All three principal views are identical circles.
3. **Sectioning by Cutting Planes** — Resulting sections are always circles (or ellipses when seen in projection).
4. **Development of Surface** — Approximated using **lunes (gore method)**.
5. **Intersection with Other Solids** — Sphere–Cylinder, Sphere–Cone intersections.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **cricket ball**, a **football**, a **soap bubble**, or the **planet Earth** as visualized on a globe. Each of these real-world objects is a sphere. The most intuitive property is this:

> *"If you stand at the exact center of a hollow sphere and throw a laser pointer in any direction, the laser hits the inner wall after traveling the **same distance** — the radius."*

This **uniform distance from the center to every point on the surface** is what mathematically defines a sphere. In contrast, in a cylinder, points on the flat circular ends are closer to the center than points on the curved side.

### 1.3 Fundamental Terms of a Sphere

| Term | Definition | Symbol / Unit |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Center** | Fixed interior point equidistant from all surface points | **O** |
| **Radius** | Constant distance from center to any surface point | **r** (mm) |
| **Diameter** | Maximum straight-line distance through the center | **D = 2r** (mm) |
| **Great Circle** | Section of sphere by a plane passing through the center | radius **r** |
| **Small Circle** | Section of sphere by a plane NOT passing through the center | radius **< r** |
| **Pole** | Endpoints of the axis of rotation (generating diameter) | **N, S** |
| **Meridian** | Any great circle passing through the two poles | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The sphere possesses **infinite planes of symmetry**, **infinite axes of symmetry**, and a **center of symmetry**. No other solid in the standard KTU 2024 syllabus has this degree of symmetry.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics

- **Total Surface Area:** $4\pi r^2$ (or $\pi d^2$)
- **Volume:** $\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$
- **Surface Area of Hemisphere (curved):** $2\pi r^2$
- **Total Surface Area of Hemisphere (curved + flat):** $3\pi r^2$
- **Volume of Hemisphere:** $\dfrac{2}{3}\pi r^3$
- **Circumference of Great Circle:** $2\pi r$
- **Area of Great Circle:** $\pi r^2$

> [!NOTE]
> A remarkable identity: the **Total Surface Area of a Sphere = 4 × Area of its Great Circle**. This means the curved skin of a sphere equals the area of four circles of the same radius laid flat.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Generation of a Sphere

A sphere is generated as a **surface of revolution**. The process of generation is fundamental for KTU board examination answers.

### Method 1: Rotation of a Semicircle about its Diameter

Consider a semicircle of radius **r** in a vertical plane. When this semicircle is rotated about its **diameter axis (AA')**, every point on the semicircular arc traces a **circle** in space. The locus of all these circles forms the spherical surface.

**Logical steps of generation:**

1. Draw a semicircle of radius **r** with its diameter **AA'** horizontal.
2. Fix the diameter **AA'** as the axis of rotation.
3. Rotate the semicircle by 360° about **AA'**.
4. The arc of the semicircle sweeps out the **entire spherical surface**.
5. The diameter **AA'** is the **axis of the sphere**; its endpoints are the **poles** of the sphere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked "How is a sphere generated?", always mention the two acceptable methods:
> - **Rotation of a semicircle about its diameter**, OR
> - **Translation of a circle (generator) along a circular path (directrix) of the same radius, with its plane always perpendicular to the directrix.**
> These two descriptions are mathematically equivalent.

### Method 2: Translation of a Circle Along a Circular Directrix

Alternatively, the sphere can be considered as the locus of a **circle (the generator)** of fixed radius **r** that moves such that:
- Its **center always lies on a circle of radius r** (the directrix) in a perpendicular plane.
- Its **plane remains perpendicular to the directrix plane**.
- Every point of the generating circle traces a meridian or parallel of the sphere.

## 2.2 Orthographic Projection Characteristics

Because the sphere looks the same from every direction (perfect rotational symmetry), its three principal orthographic views are **three identical circles** of diameter **D = 2r**, with their centers coinciding on the reference line.

| View | Shape | Diameter | Center Position |
|------|-------|----------|-----------------|
| **Top View (TV)** | Circle | D = 2r | On reference line XY |
| **Front View (FV)** | Circle | D = 2r | On reference line XY |
| **Side View (SV)** | Circle | D = 2r | On reference line XY |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All three views have the **same diameter (D)** and are **concentric** with the reference line. This is unique to the sphere and is a guaranteed KTU 2024 board question.

## 2.3 Sectioning of a Sphere — Critical Concept

When a sphere is cut by a **plane**, the resulting section is **always a circle** (true shape). However, what is *seen* in orthographic projection depends on the orientation of the cutting plane:

- **Cutting plane parallel to HP (horizontal):** True shape is a circle seen in **Top View (TV)**; in Front View it appears as a **straight line** parallel to XY.
- **Cutting plane parallel to VP (vertical, frontal):** True shape is a circle seen in **Front View (FV)**; in TV it appears as a **straight line** parallel to XY.
- **Cutting plane inclined to HP or VP (but not passing through center):** True shape is still a **circle**, but its projection appears as an **ellipse** in both TV and FV.

### The Unique Property of a Sphere

> [!NOTE]
> **Sections of a sphere are ALWAYS circles in true shape.** A plane passing through the center produces a **great circle (radius r)**. A plane not passing through the center produces a **small circle (radius < r)**. **No plane can produce a non-circular section from a sphere.** This is a unique property among all KTU 2024 Module 4 solids.

## 2.4 Development of a Sphere's Surface

A sphere's surface **cannot be unrolled into a flat plane** (it has positive Gaussian curvature throughout). Hence, the development is **approximate**, done by dividing the sphere into **lunes** (also called **gores** or **meridian strips**).

**Lune (Gore) Method:**

1. Divide the sphere (in FV) into a convenient number (typically 8, 12, or 16) of equal meridian lunes, like the segments of a peeled orange.
2. Each lune is bounded by two adjacent meridians.
3. The **arc length of each meridian = $\pi r$** (half the circumference of a great circle).
4. The **width of each lune at the equator = $\dfrac{2\pi r}{n}$** where n is the number of lunes.
5. The width tapers from this maximum at the equator to **0 at the poles** (since meridians converge at the poles).
6. Draw the lunes as approximate lens-shaped strips on the development plane.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Total Surface Area | $S = 4\pi r^2 = \pi d^2$ | mm² | All four sides of a cube of side r = $6r^2$; sphere has $4\pi r^2 \approx 12.57 r^2$ |
| 2 | Volume | $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ | mm³ | Same as the volume of a circumscribing cube minus a few corners |
| 3 | Curved Surface of Hemisphere | $CSA = 2\pi r^2$ | mm² | Exactly half the sphere's total area |
| 4 | Total Surface of Hemisphere | $TSA = 3\pi r^2$ | mm² | Curved area + flat circular base ($\pi r^2$) |
| 5 | Volume of Hemisphere | $V_h = \dfrac{2}{3}\pi r^3$ | mm³ | Exactly half the sphere's volume |
| 6 | Great Circle Circumference | $L = 2\pi r = \pi d$ | mm | Maximum distance around the sphere |
| 7 | Great Circle Area | $A_g = \pi r^2$ | mm² | Maximum cross-sectional area |
| 8 | Small Circle Area (cut at distance *h* from center) | $A_s = \pi (r^2 - h^2)$ | mm² | From Pythagoras: $r^2 = h^2 + \rho^2$ where $\rho$ is small-circle radius |
| 9 | Meridian Arc Length (pole to pole) | $L_m = \pi r$ | mm | Half the great-circle circumference |
| 10 | Lune Width at Equator (n lunes) | $w = \dfrac{2\pi r}{n}$ | mm | For 8 lunes: $w = \pi r \div 4$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure Vessels & Storage Tanks:** Spherical tanks provide the **maximum volume for minimum surface area**, minimizing material cost and heat loss. This is why LPG and LNG are often stored in spherical pressure vessels.
- **Bearing Balls & Ball Valves:** The sphere's perfect symmetry ensures uniform load distribution.
- **Optical Lenses:** Spherical surfaces form the basis of lenses and mirrors in cameras, telescopes, eyeglasses.
- **Architecture:** Domes of mosques, planetariums, and sports stadiums.
- **Aerospace:** Satellite fuel tanks are often spherical to handle uniform pressure.
- **CAD Modeling:** In SolidWorks, CATIA, and AutoCAD 3D, the sphere is a fundamental primitive, often used as a starting point for filleting, blending, and lofted surfaces.
