## 4.4 Schematic — Visible Line Convention in a Hemisphere + Cylinder Combination

> The following ASCII block diagram represents the **exact appearance** of the front view that the student must draw on the answer sheet. Centre lines are shown as dot-dash; visible edges as thick lines.

```
                          .  *  .
                     .        *        .
                 .             *             .
            .                   *                   .
        .                        *                        .
     .                          *                            .
    .                          *                            .
    .                         *                            .
    .                        *                            .
    .                       *                            .
     .                     *                            .
        .                 *                            .
            .             *                            .
                 .        *                            .
                     .    *                            .
                          *  ←── Topmost point of hemisphere
                          |
                          |  Centre line of cylinder/hemisphere
   ________________________|________________________________
   |                       |                               |
   |                       |                               |
   |                       |          CYLINDER             |
   |                       |       (Front View)            |
   |                       |                               |
   |_______________________|_______________________________|
                          ↑
                  XY Reference Line
```

*(In the actual KTU answer sheet, the dot-dash line is the centre line, the thick continuous line is the visible outline, and the horizontal at mid-height is the line of junction between cylinder and hemisphere.)*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define a hemisphere. State its total surface area and volume formulae.**

**Model Answer:**

A **hemisphere** is a solid generated by revolving a semi-circle about its diameter for a full rotation. It is half of a sphere cut by a plane passing through its centre.

The two fundamental formulae are:

$$\text{Volume } V = \frac{2}{3}\pi r^{3}$$

$$\text{Total Surface Area } A = 3\pi r^{2}$$

where $r$ is the radius of the hemisphere. The curved surface area alone is $2\pi r^{2}$ and the flat base is $\pi r^{2}$. **[3 Marks]**

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is Computer Aided Drafting (CAD)? List any four advantages of CAD over manual drafting.**

**Model Answer:**

**Computer Aided Drafting (CAD)** is the use of computer software to create, modify, analyse, and optimise engineering drawings and designs. It replaces the traditional drafting board, T-square, and set-squares with electronic input devices and digital displays.

**Four Advantages of CAD over Manual Drafting:**

1. **Speed and Efficiency:** Drawings are created and modified much faster than by hand.
2. **Accuracy and Precision:** Computer-generated lines and dimensions are free of human error.
3. **Easy Modification:** Any change is made by editing the model — no redrawing required.
4. **Storage and Retrieval:** Thousands of drawings can be stored on a single hard drive and retrieved instantly.
5. *(Bonus point)* — Easy reproduction and plotting at any scale. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** [14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1]
**A vertical cylinder of diameter 50 mm and height 60 mm is surmounted by a hemisphere of the same diameter. Draw the orthographic projections of the combination and dimension it.**

#### **Solution Outline (Model Solution)**

**Part (a) — Front View Construction [7 Marks]**

1. Draw a horizontal **XY reference line**.
2. Draw a vertical **centre line** perpendicular to XY.
3. From the centre line, mark height $h = 60\,\text{mm}$ vertically.
4. Draw two vertical lines, each $25\,\text{mm}$ from the centre line, to form the cylinder outline.
5. Close the cylinder with a horizontal line of length $50\,\text{mm}$ at the top.
6. **Draw a semicircle of radius $25\,\text{mm}$** on top of this horizontal line, forming the hemisphere's front view.
7. The vertical centre line extends to the apex of the semicircle.
8. The line of junction between cylinder and hemisphere is the horizontal line at the top of the cylinder.

**Part (b) — Top View Construction and Dimensioning [7 Marks]**

1. Project two vertical lines downwards from the cylinder's two side edges in F.V.
2. The top view of the cylinder is a **rectangle** of $50 \times 50\,\text{mm}$.
3. The top view of the hemisphere is a **full circle** of diameter $50\,\text{mm}$ centred at the same point.
4. Mark the centre with cross-hair chain lines.
5. **Dimensioning:**
   - Total height $= 60 + 25 = 85\,\text{mm}$
   - Diameter $= 50\,\text{mm}$
   - Radius of hemisphere $= 25\,\text{mm}$ (R25 in dimension)
6. All visible lines in continuous thick; centre lines in chain dotted.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Front View):**
> - The **semicircle of the hemisphere must be drawn with a compass**, not free-hand. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for an irregular curve.
> - The **horizontal line of junction** (top of cylinder = base of hemisphere) **MUST be a visible thick line**, not omitted. Many students leave it out and lose 1 mark.
> - The **vertical centre line must extend to the apex** of the hemisphere, not stop at the cylinder top. This is checked.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Top View):**
> - The **circle for the hemisphere T.V. and the cylinder T.V. are the SAME** (both project as a $50\,\text{mm}$ circle/rectangle for a revolution solid). Drawing two separate non-concentric circles is a **common error** that loses 2 marks.
> - The **dimensioning must use the BIS standard** — $\varnothing 50$ (with the diameter symbol), not just "50".

---

### **Question B** [14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2]
**A vertical cone of base diameter 40 mm and height 50 mm is placed on a hemisphere of the same base diameter. Draw the orthographic projections of the combination.**

#### **Solution Outline (Model Solution)**

**Part (a) — Construction of Front View [7 Marks]**

1. Draw XY line and vertical centre line.
2. From the centre line, mark cone height $h = 50\,\text{mm}$.
3. Draw the **isosceles triangle** for the cone: two slant lines from a single apex (top) to the ends of the base (width $= 40\,\text{mm}$).
4. The base of the cone is a horizontal line of length $40\,\text{mm}$ at height $50\,\text{mm}$.
5. **Below this base line, draw a semicircle of radius $20\,\text{mm}$** (the hemisphere is placed at the bottom of the cone). The flat side of the semicircle is the same horizontal line.
6. Extend the centre line through the apex and through the bottommost point of the semicircle.
7. The line of junction is the base line of the cone (now serving as the diameter of the hemisphere's flat face).

**Part (b) — Construction of Top View and Dimensions [7 Marks]**

1. Project two vertical lines from the **two ends of the base of the cone** in F.V. (these are at the widest part, $40\,\text{mm}$ apart).
2. The **top view is a full circle of diameter $40\,\text{mm}$**, since both cone and hemisphere are surfaces of revolution about the same axis.
3. Mark the centre with cross-hair chain lines.
4. **Dimensions:**
   - Cone height $= 50\,\text{mm}$
   - Cone base diameter $= 40\,\text{mm}$
   - Hemisphere radius $= 20\,\text{mm}$
   - Total height $= 50 + 20 = 70\,\text{mm}$
5. Apply BIS line conventions (visible thick, centre line chain dotted).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Cone + Hemisphere):**
> - The **apex of the cone is a single point** — not a flat top. Many students draw a small horizontal segment at the top by mistake. Lose **1 mark**.
> - The **slant lines of the cone must be straight, not curved** — they are generators, not arcs.
> - The **semicircle of the hemisphere must be tangent to the base of the cone at the two extreme points** (the points where the slant lines meet the base). Mismatched tangency loses **2 marks**.
> - Always **dimension the radius with "R20"**, not just the number 20, to clearly indicate it is a radius.

---

## 5.3 PART B — Bonus Numerical Question (Optional Practice)

**Q:** A hemisphere of radius $30\,\text{mm}$ is placed on top of a cylinder of diameter $60\,\text{mm}$ and height $40\,\text{mm}$. Calculate:
(i) Total surface area
(ii) Total volume
(iii) Centre of gravity of the combination from the base of the cylinder.

**Solution (Compact):**

(i) TSA $= 3\pi r^{2} + 2\pi r h = 3\pi(900) + 2\pi(30)(40) = 2700\pi + 2400\pi = 5100\pi \approx 16022\,\text{mm}^{2}$

(ii) $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi r^{3} + \pi r^{2} h = \frac{2}{3}\pi(27000) + \pi(900)(40) = 18000\pi + 36000\pi = 54000\pi \approx 169646\,\text{mm}^{3}$

(iii) Taking moments about the base of the cylinder (positive upward), with cylinder C.G. at $h/2 = 20\,\text{mm}$ and hemisphere C.G. at $h + 3r/8 = 40 + 11.25 = 51.25\,\text{mm}$:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{V_{cyl} \cdot 20 + V_{hemi} \cdot 51.25}{V_{cyl} + V_{hemi}} = \frac{36000\pi (20) + 18000\pi (51.25)}{54000\pi} = \frac{720000 + 922500}{54000} = \frac{1642500}{54000} \approx 30.4\,\text{mm}$$

---

## 5.4 KTU CAD Introduction — Key Points to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> **CAD (Computer Aided Design/Drawing)** is the use of computer systems to assist in the creation, modification, analysis, and optimisation of a design. It is the electronic equivalent of manual drafting but with vastly superior capabilities.

### Evolution of CAD (One-Liners for KTU)

| Decade | Milestone |
|---|---|
| **1950s** | First numerical control (NC) machines developed at MIT |
| **1957** | Dr. Patrick Hanratty develops **PRONTO** (first CAM language) |
| **1963** | **Ivan Sutherland's Sketchpad** (PhD Thesis at MIT) — the *birth* of CAD |
| **1970s** | **CADAM** for aerospace, **CATIA** for Dassault |
| **1982** | **AutoCAD** released by Autodesk — democratises 2D CAD |
| **1995** | **SolidWorks** launches parametric 3D CAD |
| **2000s+** | Cloud CAD, Fusion 360, generative design |

### Common CAD Software (KTU 2024 Mention)

| Software | Vendor | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| **AutoCAD** | Autodesk | 2D drafting, general 3D |
| **SolidWorks** | Dassault Systèmes | 3D parametric mechanical design |
| **CATIA** | Dassault Systèmes | Aerospace, automotive |
| **NX (Unigraphics)** | Siemens | High-end mechanical |
| **Creo (Pro/E)** | PTC | Mechanical, assembly |
| **Fusion 360** | Autodesk | Cloud-based 3D + CAM |
| **FreeCAD** | Open Source | Entry-level 3D |
| **SketchUp** | Trimble | Architectural 3D |

### Advantages of CAD (10 KTU Board Points)

1. **Higher accuracy** than manual drawing.
2. **Faster drawing and revision** — what took 8 hours manually takes 30 minutes in CAD.
3. **Easy storage and retrieval** — entire library of drawings on a small SSD.
4. **Zooming and panning** to inspect any detail.
5. **Automatic dimensioning** with full BIS/ISO compliance.
6. **3D modelling** with rendering, animation, and simulation.
7. **Parametric editing** — change one dimension and the model updates everywhere.
8. **Easy collaboration** via email, cloud, and shared drives.
9. **FEA / CFD integration** — analysis can be done on the same model.
10. **Reduced material waste** in manufacturing through precise drawings.

### Limitations of CAD (For Balanced Answer)

1. **High initial cost** of software licences and hardware.
2. **Learning curve** — training required.
3. **Dependency on electricity and computers.**
4. **Eye strain and health issues** from prolonged screen use.
5. **File corruption risks** if not properly backed up.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before the Exam!**

### Hemisphere — Must-Know

- A **hemisphere** is a solid of revolution obtained by rotating a semicircle about its diameter.
- **TV** = Full circle of diameter $D = 2r$.
- **FV/SV** = Semicircle of radius $r$ resting on a horizontal diameter.
- The base (flat face) is the **great circle** of the original sphere.
- **Volume** $= \frac{2}{3}\pi r^{3}$
- **Curved Surface Area** $= 2\pi r^{2}$
- **Total Surface Area** $= 3\pi r^{2}$
- **Centre of Gravity** from base $= \frac{3r}{8}$

### Combinations — Must-Know

- Common KTU combinations: **Cylinder + Hemisphere**, **Cone + Hemisphere**, **Frustum + Hemisphere**, **Prism + Hemisphere**.
- The **curve of intersection is a circle** when two solids of revolution share a common axis.
- **Total height** of combination $= h_{\text{solid}} + r_{\text{hemisphere}}$.
- The **line of junction** between solids is a horizontal line in F.V. (visible thick line) and a circle in T.V.
- **Centre lines must extend through both solids** continuously.

### CAD — Must-Know

- **CAD = Computer Aided Design/Drawing** — use of computers to create and modify designs.
- **First CAD system** = **Sketchpad** by **Ivan Sutherland** (1963, MIT PhD thesis).
- **Most widely used 2D CAD** = **AutoCAD** (since 1982).
- **Most widely used 3D CAD** = **SolidWorks / CATIA / NX**.
- **Key advantage** = Speed, accuracy, easy modification, storage.
- **Key disadvantage** = Cost and learning curve.
- CAD uses **layers, blocks, xrefs** for efficient drawing management.
- Output devices: **plotter** (large drawings), **printer** (small drawings), **monitor** (soft copy).

### KTU 2024 Exam Strategy (Last-Minute Tips)

1. **Always draw the XY line first** — examiners check this.
2. **Use a compass for the semicircle** — no free-hand curves.
3. **Dimension every required measurement** — undimensioned answers lose marks.
4. **Use BIS conventions** — arrowheads, line types, font height.
5. **Centre lines are chain dotted** (`–·–·–`) — not regular dotted.
6. **For CAD questions, mention at least one example of software, one advantage, and one disadvantage** to score full marks in short answers.
7. **Avoid colouring the drawings** unless specifically asked.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Hemisphere, Combinations, and CAD Introduction.**
> *Best of luck for your KTU 2024 Scheme examinations!* 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of CAD in Design and Development of New Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Role of CAD in Design and Development of New Products # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Computer Aided Design (CAD)** is the application of computer software and hardware systems to assist engineers, designers, and architects in the **creation, modification, analysis, and optimization of a design**. It encompasses the use of computer-based tools to produce both **2D drawings** and **3D models** of physical objects, enabling precise geometric definition, simulation, and documentation throughout the **Product Development Lifecycle (PDLC)**. In the context of KTU's Engineering Graphics and Computer Aided Drawing (GCEST203), CAD is treated not merely as a drafting tool, but as a **strategic design framework** that integrates geometry, kinematics, materials, and manufacturing constraints into a single digital environment. ## 1.2 The Product Development Lifecycle (PDLC) — Definition > [!NOTE] > **Product Development Lifecycle (PDLC)** refers to the complete sequence of phases a product undergoes from initial conceptualization to its eventual retirement in the market. The standard PDLC recognized by KTU and global engineering standards (ISO 9001, ASME) includes: **Concept → Design → Analysis → Prototyping → Manufacturing → Market Release → Support & Disposal**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Digital Workshop Imagine you are an **architect designing a house**. In the traditional method, you would draw blueprints by hand on a drafting board, calculate structural loads using a calculator, build a small-scale wooden model to visualize it, and then hope the contractor interprets your drawings correctly. Each step is **isolated, error-prone, and time-consuming**. Now, imagine you have a **single, magical digital workshop** where: - You can sketch the house in 3D and rotate it with your fingers. - You can instantly check whether the walls can withstand an earthquake (Finite Element Analysis). - You can 3D-print a miniature prototype directly from your screen. - You can send a single digital file to the contractor, the city inspector, and the interior designer — and they all see the **exact same model**. **That magical digital workshop is CAD.** It collapses the traditionally **sequential, fragmented** design process into a **concurrent, integrated, and digital** workflow. ## 1.4 Why CAD Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme The 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** and **multidisciplinary design thinking**. CAD directly maps to several Course Outcomes (COs) of GCEST203: - **CO1:** Apply the concepts of engineering drawing to communicate design intent. - **CO3:** Utilize CAD tools for 2D drafting and 3D modeling. - **CO4:** Demonstrate the role of CAD in the product development process. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Industry Standard Metrics** that CAD influences: **Time-to-Market (TTM)**, **First-Pass Yield (FPY)**, **Design Reuse Rate (DRR)**, and **Engineering Change Order (ECO) cycle time**. These are tracked in **days** or **hours** in modern CAD-driven workflows, versus **weeks** in manual workflows. ## 1.5 Scope of CAD in Modern Engineering CAD is no longer restricted to drafting. It now spans: | CAD Sub-Domain | Primary Function | Example Software | |---|---|---| | **2D Drafting** | Technical drawings, GD&T | AutoCAD, DraftSight | | **3D Solid Modeling** | Parametric feature-based design | SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo, Fusion 360 | | **Surface Modeling** | Complex aesthetic/curved surfaces | Rhino, Alias, NX | | **CAE (FEA/CFD)** | Simulation & analysis | ANSYS, Abaqus, COMSOL | | **CAM** | Toolpath generation for CNC | Mastercam, Fusion 360 CAM | | **BIM** | Building information modeling | Revit, ArchiCAD | | **PLM** | Product lifecycle data management | Teamcenter, Windchill | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Pillars of CAD in Product Development The role of CAD in product development can be systematically analyzed through **six functional pillars**. Each pillar addresses a specific question in the design process. ### Pillar 1: **Geometric Modeling (What does it look like?)** - CAD enables the creation of **accurate digital twins** of physical parts. - Three primary modeling paradigms: - **Wireframe Modeling:** Represents objects as a collection of edges/vertices. Lightweight but ambiguous (cannot represent complex topology like a hollow sphere vs. solid sphere). - **Surface Modeling:** Defines the boundary skin of an object using NURBS (Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline) or Bézier curves. Used in automotive/aerospace styling. - **Solid Modeling:** Represents volume and mass. Supports **Boolean operations** (Union, Subtract, Intersect). This is the **industry standard** for engineering parts. > [!NOTE] > **Boolean Operations in CAD:** > - **Union ($A \cup B$):** Combines two solids into one. > - **Subtract ($A - B$):** Removes volume of B from A — used to create holes, slots, pockets. > - **Intersect ($A \cap B$):** Keeps only the common volume. ### Pillar 2: **Parametric & Feature-Based Design (How does it change?)** - Modern CAD is **parametric** — dimensions are not just labels but **constraints** that drive geometry. - A **feature** is a reusable design element (hole, fillet, chamfer, boss, rib). - **Design Intent** is captured through features and relations, allowing the model to update intelligently when a parent dimension changes. - This enables **top-down** and **bottom-up** design strategies. ### Pillar 3: **Engineering Analysis & Simulation (Will it break?)** - CAD models serve as the **direct input** to Computer-Aided Engineering (CAE) tools. - **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** discretizes the CAD geometry into millions of small elements to solve for stress, strain, thermal distribution, and modal frequencies. - The CAD model must be **"simulation-ready"** — watertight, defeatured, and with appropriate material assignments. ### Pillar 4: **Virtual Prototyping (Can it be built?)** - Before committing to expensive physical prototypes, designers use CAD to perform: - **Digital Mock-Up (DMU):** Assembly fit, clearance, and interference checks. - **Kinematic Simulation:** Animating mechanisms (gears, linkages, four-bar) to verify motion. - **Ergonomic Analysis:** Human-model interaction studies (manikin reach, vision cones). - This is the essence of **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFM/DFA)**. ### Pillar 5: **Manufacturing Integration (How is it made?)** - CAD models flow directly into **Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM)** for CNC machining, 3D printing, injection mold design, and sheet metal unfolding. - Tolerances and **Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing (GD&T)** defined in CAD are honored by the manufacturing process. ### Pillar 6: **Data Management & Collaboration (Who else needs it?)** - **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** systems store CAD data, revisions, BOMs (Bill of Materials), and engineering change orders. - Enables **concurrent engineering** — multiple disciplines (mechanical, electrical, thermal) work on the same digital assembly simultaneously. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Since this is a conceptual topic in Engineering Graphics, the "formulas" relate to geometric relationships, model complexity metrics, and design efficiency ratios used in CAD-based product development. | # | Concept / Formula | Notation & Meaning | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Model Complexity Index $\quad C_{m} = \dfrac{N_{f}}{V_{b}}$ | $N_{f}$ = number of features, $V_{b}$ = bounding volume (mm³) | Quantifies design density; high $C_m$ = complex part | | 2 | Design Reuse Rate $\quad DRR = \dfrac{N_{reused}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{reused}$ = reused components, $N_{total}$ = total components | Measures standardization efficiency | | 3 | Parametric Update Time $\quad T_{u} = f(N_{features},\, N_{constraints})$ | $N_{features}$ = feature count, $N_{constraints}$ = dependencies | Time to regenerate after a parameter change | | 4 | Volume of Bounding Box $\quad V_{bb} = L \times W \times H$ | $L, W, H$ = length, width, height in mm | Quick envelope check for packaging | | 5 | Surface Area to Volume Ratio $\quad \eta = \dfrac{A_{s}}{V}$ | $A_{s}$ = surface area (mm²), $V$ = volume (mm³) | Indicates heat dissipation potential; critical in thermal design | | 6 | First-Pass Yield $\quad FPY = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{produced}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{good}$ = good units, $N_{produced}$ = total units | Measures design & manufacturing maturity | | 7 | Tolerance Stack-Up (Worst Case) $\quad T_{wc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert T_{i} \vert$ | $T_i$ = individual tolerance, $n$ = number of dimensions | Used in GD&T to ensure assembly fit | | 8 | Time-to-Market $\quad TTM = T_{concept} + T_{design} + T_{proto} + T_{tooling}$ | Each $T$ is a phase duration (days) | Competitive metric; CAD reduces $TTM$ by 30–60% | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Rule:** In the worst-case tolerance stack-up formula, the **absolute value** of each tolerance is summed because tolerances are independent dimensional allowances. The vertical bar symbol is written as `\vert` in LaTeX to preserve table syntax integrity. ## 2.3 The Real-World Utility of CAD in Engineering | Industry Sector | Role of CAD | Real-World Example | |---|---|---| | **Automotive** | Styling, crash simulation, tooling | Tesla Model S chassis optimization via FEA | | **Aerospace** | Lightweight lattice structures, CFD | Boeing 787 Dreamliner (100% digital mockup) | | **Biomedical** | Patient-specific implants | Custom 3D-printed titanium hip implants from CT scans | | **Consumer Electronics** | Miniaturization, thermal management | Smartphone PCB and enclosure co-design | | **Architecture** | BIM, structural integration | Smart-city digital twin modeling | | **Manufacturing** | CNC toolpath, injection mold flow | Injection mold cooling channel optimization | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** When asked about the "role of CAD," examiners expect students to cover the **full lifecycle** — not just drafting. Always mention: **Design → Analysis → Prototyping → Manufacturing → Collaboration**. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of the CAD-Driven Product Development Process ## 3.1 Detailed Phase-by-Phase Analytical Walkthrough The following analytical matrix deconstructs the role of CAD at **each phase** of the product development lifecycle. Since this topic is conceptual rather than numerically computational, the "derivations" here are **logical/analytical chains** showing the cause-and-effect flow of CAD integration. --- ### **Phase 1: Concept Generation & Ideation** **Analytical Breakdown:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Stage}_{\text{input}} &= \{\text{Market Need, User Requirement, Constraint Set}\} \\ \text{Stage}_{\text{CAD role}} &= \text{Sketching, Conceptual 3D blocking, form study} \\ \text{Stage}_{\text{output}} &= \text{Rough 3D concept model (low fidelity)} \end{aligned} $$ - CAD tools here are used in **freeform/sculpting mode** to rapidly explore shape alternatives. - A common technique is **morphological analysis** — combining sub-functions to generate multiple concept variants quickly. --- ### **Phase 2: Detailed Design (CAD Core Phase)** **Analytical Breakdown:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Concept Model} \xrightarrow{\text{Parametric Conversion}} \text{Feature-based Solid Model} \\ \text{Feature-based Solid Model} \xrightarrow{\text{Apply GD\&T and Materials}} \text{Production-Ready CAD Model} \\ \text{Production-Ready CAD Model} \xrightarrow{\text{Assemble into Top-Level}} \text{Product Assembly} \end{aligned} $$ - The CAD model is built using **sketch → feature → part → assembly** hierarchy. - A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is automatically generated from the assembly tree. --- ### **Phase 3: Engineering Analysis (CAE Integration)** **Analytical Breakdown:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CAD Geometry} \xrightarrow{\text{Meshing}} \text{Finite Element Mesh (FEA)} \\ \text{FEA} \xrightarrow{\text{Boundary Conditions}} \text{Stress / Thermal / Modal Results} \\ \text{Results} \xrightarrow{\text{Back-annotation}} \text{Design Refinement} \end{aligned} $$ - The CAD-to-CAE loop is **iterative** — each design change triggers a fresh analysis. - This loop is often called the **"Design-Analysis-Optimization" cycle**. --- ### **Phase 4: Virtual Prototyping & Validation** **Analytical Breakdown:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Assembly Model} \xrightarrow{\text{Interference Check}} \text{Clash Report} \\ \text{Assembly Model} \xrightarrow{\text{Kinematic Sim.}} \text{Motion Validation} \\ \text{Assembly Model} \xrightarrow{\text{DMU Review}} \text{Design Review Sign-off} \end{aligned} $$ - The goal is to **catch errors digitally** before physical tooling is built — saving lakhs of rupees in rework. --- ### **Phase 5: Manufacturing (CAM Bridge)** **Analytical Breakdown:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Finalized CAD} \xrightarrow{\text{Process Selection}} \text{Machining / Molding / 3D Printing Setup} \\ \text{Setup} \xrightarrow{\text{Toolpath Gen.}} \text{G-code / NC File} \\ \text{G-code} \xrightarrow{\text{CNC / 3D Printer}} \text{Physical Part} \end{aligned} $$ - For **3D printing (Additive Manufacturing)**, the CAD model is sliced via STL/3MF export. - For **CNC (Subtractive Manufacturing)**, the CAD model is used to derive toolpaths. --- ### **Phase 6: Lifecycle Management & Continuous Improvement** **Analytical Breakdown:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Field Data} \xrightarrow{\text{Feedback}} \text{ECO (Engineering Change Order)} \\ \text{ECO} \xrightarrow{\text{PLM Update}} \text{Revised CAD Model (New Rev.)} \\ \text{Revised CAD} \xrightarrow{\text{Manufacturing}} \text{Next-Generation Product} \end{aligned} $$ - PLM ensures that the **single source of truth** for the product is always current. --- ## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Manual vs. CAD-Based Design | Parameter | Manual Design | CAD-Based Design | Improvement Factor | |---|---|---|---| | Drawing creation time | 8–40 hours | 1–6 hours | **5–10× faster** | | Revision cycle | Redraw entire sheet | Modify & regenerate | **10× faster** | | Storage | Physical drawers/rolls | Digital vault (cloud) | **∞** | | Error rate (dimensional) | ~5% | <0.1% | **50× reduction** | | Visualization | 2D only | Full 3D + VR/AR | Qualitative leap | | Collaboration | Sequential, paper-based | Concurrent, cloud-based | Massive gain | | Cost per revision | High (labor) | Low (recompute) | **>70% savings** | --- ## 3.3 Mathematical Illustration — Impact of CAD on Tolerance Stack-Up Consider a simple shaft-housing assembly with **three stacked dimensions**. The manual method is to use worst-case summation: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{wc} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert T_i \vert \\ &= \vert +0.20 \vert + \vert -0.10 \vert + \vert +0.15 \vert \quad \text{(in mm)} \\ &= 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.15 \\ &= 0.45 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step explanation of conversion logic:** - **Step 1:** Identify the three contributing tolerances from the 2D drawing. - **Step 2:** In the manual draft, dimensions are placed independently; hence the worst-case stack-up applies. - **Step 3:** Sum the absolute values to get the maximum possible dimensional deviation. - **Step 4:** In a CAD-driven **statistical (RSS)** approach for the same assembly: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{rss} &= \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^{\,2}} \\ &= \sqrt{0.20^2 + 0.10^2 + 0.15^2} \\ &= \sqrt{0.04 + 0.01 + 0.0225} \\ &= \sqrt{0.0725} \\ &\approx 0.269 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ - **Step 5:** Compare results: $T_{rss} \approx 0.269$ mm versus $T_{wc} = 0.45$ mm. The CAD-enabled statistical analysis shows that the assembly is **actually tighter** than the worst-case estimate. - **Step 6:** This tighter tolerance allows the designer to **relax individual tolerances**, reducing manufacturing cost while maintaining fit. This single numerical illustration demonstrates that CAD is not just a visualization tool — it is an **analytical engine** that fundamentally changes engineering decisions. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CAD-Driven Product Development Lifecycle Flow The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the CAD-integrated product development process. It maps the data flow between the six pillars of CAD activity across the seven lifecycle phases. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Market Need & User Requirement] --> A2[Concept Generation] A2 --> A3[Detailed CAD Modeling] A3 --> A4[Assembly & Top Down Design] A4 --> A5[CAD to CAE Bridge] A5 --> A6[FEA / CFD Simulation] A6 --> A7[Design Optimization Loop] A7 --> A8[Virtual Prototyping & DMU] A8 --> A9[GD&T and Tolerance Analysis] A9 --> A10[Tooling and CAM Toolpath] A10 --> A11[Physical Manufacturing] A11 --> A12[Quality Inspection & Testing] A12 --> A13[Field Deployment & PLM Data] A13 --> A14[Engineering Change Order Feedback] A14 --> A2 A3 -.-> B1[Parametric Feature Library] A4 -.-> B2[Standard Parts Library] A6 -.-> B3[Material Database] A9 -.-> B4[GD&T Standard ASME Y14.5] B1 --> A3 B2 --> A4 B3 --> A6 B4 --> A9 classDef phaseStyle fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#0C4A6E,stroke-width:2px classDef libStyle fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F,stroke-width:2px class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7,A8,A9,A10,A11,A12,A13,A14 phaseStyle class B1,B2,B3,B4 libStyle ``` **How to read this diagram:** - The **outer rectangular loop** (blue nodes A1 → A14) traces the complete product development cycle. - The **central yellow nodes** (B1–B4) represent the **shared knowledge libraries** that feed into multiple phases. These are the "spines" of a mature CAD/PLM ecosystem. - The **feedback arrow** from A14 back to A2 represents the **continuous improvement loop** enabled by PLM and field data. --- ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a CAD System ```mermaid flowchart LR H1[User Input Devices
Mouse, Keyboard, Stylus] --> C1[CAD Core Engine] S1[3D Scanner / CMM Input] --> C1 C1 --> G1[Geometry Kernel
Parasolid, ACIS] G1 --> M1[3D Solid Model Database] M1 --> V1[Visualization Module] M1 --> F1[Feature & Parametric Tree] M1 --> K1[Knowledge-Based Engineering] V1 --> O1[Display Output] F1 --> E1[CAE Export: STEP, IGES, STL] F1 --> N1[CAM Export: G-code, NC] E1 --> S2[Simulation Solver] N1 --> S3[CNC Machine] K1 --> D1[PLM Database] classDef inputStyle fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#14532D,stroke-width:2px classDef coreStyle fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,color:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:2px classDef outputStyle fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#831843,stroke-width:2px class H1,S1 inputStyle class C1,G1,M1,V1,F1,K1 coreStyle class O1,E1,N1,D1,S2,S3 outputStyle ``` **Architectural Layers Explained:** | Layer | Function | Key Components | |---|---|---| | **Input Layer** | Captures user intent and external geometry | Mouse, stylus, 3D scanner, CMM | | **Core Engine** | Processes geometry and parameters | Parasolid, ACIS kernels | | **Data Layer** | Stores the 3D model parametrically | Feature tree, part files | | **Visualization Layer** | Renders model for user | Real-time 3D viewport | | **Knowledge Layer** | Encodes engineering rules | KBE modules, design templates | | **Output Layer** | Exports to downstream systems | CAE solvers, CAM machines, PLM | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Geometry Kernel** (Parasolid by Siemens, ACIS by Spatial) is the mathematical heart of every major CAD system. SolidWorks, CATIA, NX, and Creo all rely on one of these two kernels. The choice of kernel affects file compatibility and feature reliability. --- ## 4.3 CAD's Role Across the Product Lifecycle — Summary Block ```mermaid flowchart TD R1[Role of CAD] --> R2[Visualization
and Communication] R1 --> R3[Engineering
Analysis] R1 --> R4[Virtual
Prototyping] R1 --> R5[Manufacturing
Integration] R1 --> R6[Knowledge
Management] R2 --> R7[Faster Design Reviews
Reduced Ambiguity] R3 --> R8[Reduced Physical Tests
Optimized Performance] R4 --> R9[Lower Prototype Cost
Faster Iteration] R5 --> R10[Direct CNC / 3D Print
Tighter Tolerances] R6 --> R11[Design Reuse
Standardization] classDef rootStyle fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#4338CA,color:#312E81,stroke-width:3px classDef roleStyle fill:#F0FDF4,stroke:#16A34A,color:#14532D,stroke-width:2px classDef benefitStyle fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#CA8A04,color:#713F12,stroke-width:2px class R1 rootStyle class R2,R3,R4,R5,R6 roleStyle class R7,R8,R9,R10,R11 benefitStyle ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Define Computer Aided Design (CAD). List any four advantages of CAD over traditional manual drafting.** **Model Answer:** > **CAD (Computer Aided Design)** is the use of computer software and hardware to assist in the **creation, modification, analysis, and optimization of an engineering design**. It integrates 2D drafting, 3D solid modeling, simulation, and manufacturing data into a single digital platform. **Four advantages of CAD over manual drafting:** 1. **Faster revision cycles:** A change in a parametric CAD model automatically updates all dependent views and assemblies, whereas manual drawings must be redrawn. 2. **Higher accuracy:** CAD enforces precise tolerances down to microns, eliminating human measurement errors. 3. **Virtual prototyping:** Designers can simulate, analyze, and validate a product on-screen before building a physical prototype, drastically reducing cost. 4. **Easy data sharing & storage:** CAD files are digital, compact, and can be transmitted globally for collaboration, unlike bulky physical drawings. --- ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: What is a Digital Mock-Up (DMU)? How does it support the product development process?** **Model Answer:** > A **Digital Mock-Up (DMU)** is a complete **3D virtual representation** of a product assembly, including all its parts, sub-assemblies, and spatial relationships, constructed entirely within a CAD environment. **How it supports product development:** - **Clash/Interference Detection:** Identifies overlapping or colliding parts before manufacturing, preventing costly rework. - **Kinematic Validation:** Simulates motion of mechanisms (hinges, sliders, gears) to confirm functionality. - **Ergonomic Studies:** Manikins can be placed inside vehicle or aircraft interiors to check human reach and visibility. - **Design Review:** Stakeholders can inspect the product from any angle using 3D rotation and section views, accelerating approval cycles. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, with internal choice (either-or). Each answer should be approximately **400–500 words** with diagrams where applicable. --- ### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Q: Discuss in detail the role of CAD in the various stages of product development. Illustrate with a suitable block diagram.** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: CAD in Conceptual and Detailed Design Stages** **Model Answer:** CAD plays a transformative role in the **front-end** stages of product development. **1. Concept Generation:** - Designers use **freeform sculpting tools** in CAD to rapidly explore multiple shape alternatives. - **Morphological charts** can be implemented in parametric CAD, allowing systematic combination of sub-functions. - Sketch-based 3D modelers (e.g., Fusion 360 Sketch, Rhino) accelerate idea-to-3D conversion. **2. Detailed Design:** - The concept is converted into a **parametric, feature-based solid model**. - Features such as holes, fillets, ribs, and bosses are added as **reusable design intent**. - A parametric dimension change **propagates** through the entire assembly automatically. - The model is annotated with **GD&T (ASME Y14.5)** symbols, datums, and tolerances. - A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is auto-generated from the assembly tree. **3. Assembly Modeling:** - Top-down and bottom-up design strategies allow hierarchical product definition. - **Mates and constraints** (coincident, concentric, distance) define spatial relationships. - Sub-assemblies can be developed in parallel by different teams. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: CAD in Analysis, Prototyping, and Manufacturing Stages** **Model Answer:** **4. Engineering Analysis (CAE):** - The CAD model is exported (via STEP, IGES, or native formats) to **Finite Element Analysis** tools. - Engineers apply loads, boundary conditions, and materials to predict stress, deflection, and fatigue life. - **Design-Analysis-Optimization loops** allow iterative refinement without physical testing. **5. Virtual Prototyping (DMU):** - **Interference checking** detects clashes between parts. - **Kinematic simulation** validates motion of mechanisms (e.g., four-bar linkage, cam-follower). - **Ergonomic and packaging studies** can be performed using human manikins. **6. Manufacturing Integration (CAM):** - The finalized CAD model is used to generate **CNC toolpaths** or **3D-printing slicing data**. - For injection molding, CAD enables **mold flow analysis** and **core-cavity split design**. - **Sheet metal unfolding** in CAD allows flat-pattern generation directly from the 3D model. **7. Lifecycle Management (PLM):** - All CAD data, revisions, ECOs, and BOMs are stored in a **PLM system**, ensuring a **single source of truth**. - Field feedback drives Engineering Change Orders that update the CAD master model. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: Explain the integration of CAD with CAE and CAM in modern product development. What are the benefits and challenges of this integration?** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: CAD-CAE and CAD-CAM Integration Explained** **Model Answer:** **CAD-CAE Integration:** - The **CAD model acts as the geometric input** to CAE solvers. - For FEA, the model is **meshed** (discretized into small elements), and boundary conditions (loads, fixtures) are applied. - Results (von Mises stress, deformation, temperature distribution) are visualized directly on the CAD geometry using **back-annotation** — color contours overlaid on the 3D model. - This allows designers to **identify weak zones** and iterate the CAD model accordingly. - Example: A connecting rod designed in CAD is analyzed in ANSYS for fatigue life under cyclic loading. **CAD-CAM Integration:** - The finalized CAD model is imported into CAM software, which generates **G-code** (for CNC) or **slicing data** (for 3D printing). - **Toolpath strategies** (roughing, finishing, drilling) are selected based on the part geometry and stock material. - For **injection molding**, the CAD part geometry is used to design the **core, cavity, ejector pins, and cooling channels**. - For **sheet metal**, CAD automatically unfolds the 3D bend model into a 2D flat pattern for laser cutting. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Benefits and Challenges of Integration** **Model Answer:** **Benefits:** 1. **Reduced Time-to-Market:** Integrated CAD-CAE-CAM workflows cut product development time by 30–60% compared to siloed methods. 2. **Lower Prototype Cost:** Virtual simulation replaces multiple physical prototypes. 3. **Improved Quality:** First-Pass Yield increases because design defects are caught digitally. 4. **Design Optimization:** Topology optimization (lightweighting) and parametric sweeps are possible only with tight CAD-CAE coupling. 5. **Single Source of Truth:** All disciplines work on the same model version, eliminating data mismatch. **Challenges:** 1. **CAD-CAE Translation Errors:** Geometry simplification for meshing (defeaturing) can lose critical features; manual decisions are required. 2. **Software Interoperability:** Different vendors use different file formats; translation (STEP, IGES, JT) can introduce geometry errors. 3. **High Initial Cost:** Integrated PLM/CAE/CAM suites require significant investment in software, training, and IT infrastructure. 4. **Skill Gap:** Engineers must be multi-disciplinary — proficient in CAD, basic FEA, and manufacturing — which is hard to find. 5. **Data Overload:** Large assemblies generate massive datasets, requiring robust storage and management. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Conflating CAD with Drafting only** — Examiners expect coverage of analysis, manufacturing, and lifecycle. Writing only about 2D drawings will cap marks at 50%. > 2. **Skipping the block diagram** — A labeled block diagram of the CAD-driven lifecycle is **mandatory** in 14-mark answers. Not drawing it costs 2–3 marks. > 3. **Ignoring the difference between CAD, CAE, and CAM** — Students often use the terms interchangeably. Be precise: **CAD = design geometry; CAE = simulation; CAM = manufacturing toolpaths**. > 4. **No real-world examples** — A passing reference to an industry example (e.g., Boeing 787, Tesla) elevates the answer and signals depth. > 5. **Forgetting the "Why"** — Don't just list functions; explain *why* CAD is used at each stage. Eg: "FEA is used *because* it predicts failure before physical testing, saving lakhs in prototype cost." --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **CAD = Computer Aided Design** — software-assisted creation, modification, analysis, and optimization of a design. - [x] CAD operates across the **entire Product Development Lifecycle (PDLC)**, not just drafting. - [x] The **six pillars of CAD** are: Geometric Modeling, Parametric Design, Engineering Analysis, Virtual Prototyping, Manufacturing Integration, and Data Management (PLM). - [x] **Three modeling paradigms:** Wireframe, Surface, and Solid Modeling. Solid modeling is the industry standard. - [x] **Boolean operations** (Union, Subtract, Intersect) are fundamental to solid modeling. - [x] **Parametric CAD** uses constraints and features, allowing automatic propagation of design changes. - [x] **CAD-CAE integration** enables FEA, CFD, and thermal analysis directly from the 3D model. - [x] **CAD-CAM integration** enables direct toolpath generation for CNC, 3D printing, and mold design. - [x] **Digital Mock-Up (DMU)** catches assembly errors, motion issues, and ergonomic problems before physical prototyping. - [x] **GD&T (ASME Y14.5)** is applied within CAD to ensure manufacturing precision and assembly fit. - [x] **Tolerance stack-up** can be analyzed in CAD: worst-case $\sum \vert T_i \vert$ vs. statistical $\sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$. - [x] **PLM (Product Lifecycle Management)** stores all CAD data as the "single source of truth" for the enterprise. - [x] CAD reduces **Time-to-Market (TTM)** by 30–60% and **First-Pass Yield (FPY)** errors by up to 90%. - [x] **Geometry kernels** like Parasolid and ACIS form the mathematical core of all major CAD systems. - [x] Real-world applications span **automotive, aerospace, biomedical, electronics, architecture, and manufacturing**. - [x] In KTU 2024 Scheme exams, always include a **labeled block diagram** in 14-mark answers on this topic.

topics.title: Advantages of CAD. Creating Two-Dimensional Drawing with Dimensions Using Suitable Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST203 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advantages of CAD. Creating Two-Dimensional Drawing with Dimensions Using Dimensions Using Suitable Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of CAD

**Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** is the use of computer systems (hardware and software) to assist in the creation, modification, analysis, and optimization of a design. In the context of KTU GCEST203 Module 4, CAD specifically refers to the application of graphical software tools (such as AutoCAD, SolidWorks 2D Drafting, LibreCAD, or similar vector-based drafting environments) to produce **2D engineering drawings** with precise geometric entities, layers, annotations, and **dimensional callouts** that conform to BIS/ISO drafting standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (GCEST203, Module 4):**
> *"Computer Aided Drafting (CAD) — advantages over manual drafting. Generation of 2D drawings (line, circle, arc, polygon, ellipse, rectangle, text, hatching) with appropriate dimensioning using standard CAD software."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Drafting Table" Intuition

Imagine replacing a **traditional drawing board**, **set-squares**, **mini-drafter**, **compass**, and **Indian ink pens** with a **smart, infinitely patient digital assistant** that:

- Never smudges ink.
- Lets you "undo" a wrong line with a single keystroke.
- Automatically measures every distance, angle, and radius for you.
- Snap-aligns your cursor to exact geometric relationships (parallel, perpendicular, tangent).
- Stores the entire drawing as a database of mathematically defined objects, not just ink on paper.

That digital assistant is **CAD software**. The "drawing" is no longer a fragile paper artifact — it becomes a **mathematical model** of geometry. When you ask CAD to place a "dimension" of 45.00 mm between two points, it does not measure ink — it computes the Euclidean distance between the two mathematical point entities in its database and renders a labelled measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Paradigm Shift:**
> - **Manual Drafting:** Drawing *is* the product (ink on paper).
> - **CAD:** Drawing *describes* the product (mathematical model → rendering).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in CAD

- **Drawing Unit:** The fundamental length unit of the model space, typically **millimetres (mm)** for engineering drawings as per KTU/BIS convention.
- **Drawing Scale:** The ratio of paper size to model size (e.g., 1:1 for full-size, 1:2 for half-size layouts). In CAD, the model is always drawn **1:1 in model space**; the scale is applied only when plotting/printing.
- **Precision/Accuracy:** Modern CAD operates to floating-point precision, routinely to **6 or more decimal places** (e.g., 45.000000 mm).
- **Standard Sheet Sizes (BIS/ISO A-series):** A0 (841 × 1189 mm), A1 (594 × 841 mm), A2 (420 × 594 mm), A3 (297 × 420 mm), A4 (210 × 297 mm).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Analysis: Why CAD Outperforms Manual Drafting

The advantages of CAD over manual drafting can be classified into **four pillars** that the KTU examiner expects a student to articulate:

#### Pillar A — Productivity and Speed
1. **Reuse of Geometry:** Entities (lines, circles) can be copied, mirrored, offset, and patterned with single commands (e.g., `COPY`, `ARRAY`, `OFFSET` in AutoCAD).
2. **Instant Editing:** `TRIM`, `EXTEND`, `FILLET`, `CHAMFER` commands modify geometry in milliseconds.
3. **Block/Component Insertion:** A frequently used symbol (e.g., a nut, bolt, or title block) is drawn once, stored as a **block (`.dwg` symbol)**, and inserted into any drawing at any scale, any orientation, any number of times — with a single command.
4. **Parametric Recall:** Dimensions are *associative* — change one dimension, and all dependent geometry updates automatically.

#### Pillar B — Accuracy and Precision
1. **Floating-Point Coordinates:** Every point is stored as (x, y) with up to **15+ significant digits** — far beyond what the human hand + scale can achieve.
2. **Object Snaps (OSNAP):** The software detects the *exact* endpoint, midpoint, intersection, centre, or perpendicular foot of any existing entity — eliminating approximation error.
3. **No Cumulative Error:** Manual drafting accumulates parallax and instrument error; CAD error is bounded only by the IEEE 754 double-precision limit.

#### Pillar C — Editing, Storage, and Revision Control
1. **Non-Destructive Editing:** The `UNDO` stack lets you revert any number of operations.
2. **Layer System:** A drawing is organised into logical layers (e.g., layer 01_CENTRELINES, layer 02_OUTLINE, layer 03_HATCHING, layer 04_DIMENSIONS, layer 05_TEXT). Each layer has independent colour, line type, line weight, and visibility/lock state.
3. **File-Based Archival:** Drawings are stored digitally (`.dwg`, `.dxf`), enabling version control, cloud backup, and instant retrieval — no physical storage cabinets required.

#### Pillar D — Communication, Integration, and Manufacturing Linkage
1. **Standardisation:** Built-in dimension styles, text styles, and title blocks enforce uniform drafting conventions across an organisation.
2. **Data Export:** Drawings can be exported to `.dxf`, `.svg`, `.pdf`, `.stp` (STEP) for downstream CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing), FEA, or 3D printing pipelines.
3. **Collaboration:** Multiple engineers can work on the same drawing via cloud platforms (AutoCAD Web, Onshape) simultaneously.
4. **Automatic Bill of Materials (BOM):** Block attributes can be linked to spreadsheets for automatic part counting.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Hot-Button Advantages (memorise these 6):**
> 1. Higher accuracy & precision
> 2. Easier editing (trim, copy, mirror, offset)
> 3. Faster revisions & reduced drafting time
> 4. Better storage, retrieval & data sharing
> 5. Database-driven dimensions (associativity)
> 6. Direct interface with CAM / 3D modelling / FEA

---

### 2.2 Theoretical Breakdown: Creating a 2D Drawing with Dimensions

The creation of a 2D engineering drawing with dimensions in CAD follows a **five-phase pipeline**:

**Phase 1 — Setup**
- Set drawing units (mm).
- Define drawing limits or use model space directly.
- Create layer structure (centreline, object, hidden, dimension, text).
- Load or define **text style** and **dimension style** (font, arrow size, text height, extension line offset).

**Phase 2 — Geometry Construction**
- Draw the object using primitives: `LINE`, `CIRCLE`, `ARC`, `POLYGON`, `ELLIPSE`, `RECTANGLE`, `PLINE` (polyline), `SPLINE`.
- Apply construction aids: **Grid**, **Snap**, **Ortho**, **Polar Tracking**, **Object Snap (OSNAP)**, **Dynamic Input (DYN)**.

**Phase 3 — Modification**
- Use `TRIM`, `EXTEND`, `OFFSET`, `MOVE`, `COPY`, `ROTATE`, `MIRROR`, `FILLET`, `CHAMFER`, `SCALE`, `STRETCH`, `ARRAY`.

**Phase 4 — Dimensioning (the KTU focus)**
Apply the correct dimension type based on the geometric feature:

| Dimension Type | Used For | CAD Command (AutoCAD) | Symbol / Style |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | Horizontal & vertical distances | `DIMLINEAR` | Numeric with arrowheads |
| **Aligned** | Distance along an inclined line | `DIMALIGNED` | Aligned with the measured line |
| **Angular** | Angle between two lines/arcs | `DIMANGULAR` | Shown in degrees (°) |
| **Radius** | Radius of an arc or circle | `DIMRADIUS` | Prefixed with letter **R** |
| **Diameter** | Diameter of a circle | `DIMDIAMETER` | Prefixed with symbol **Ø** |
| **Arc Length** | Length of an arc segment | `DIMARC` | Prefixed with arc symbol |
| **Ordinate** | X- or Y-coordinate from a datum | `DIMORDINATE` | Numeric only, no leader |
| **Baseline** | Several dimensions from one baseline | `DIMBASELINE` | Successive linear dims |
| **Continuous** | Chain of end-to-end dimensions | `DIMCONTINUE` | Stacked linear dims |

**Phase 5 — Annotation & Hatching**
- Add text, notes, GD&T symbols, surface finish marks, title block entries.
- Apply hatching (`HATCH` command) to indicate sectional/cut surfaces (e.g., `ANSI31` for cast iron, `ANSI32` for bronze).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **dimensional, scaling, and tolerance formulas** that KTU examiners expect in 2D CAD drawing problems.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drawing Scale | $S = \dfrac{L_{\text{paper}}}{L_{\text{actual}}}$ | dimensionless | Example: 1:2 means $L_{\text{paper}} = L_{\text{actual}}/2$ |
| Linear Dimension Value | $D = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | mm | Computed from two OSNAP points |
| Angular Dimension | $\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\right)$ | degrees (°) | Between two direction vectors |
| Radius from Centre | $R = \sqrt{(x - x_c)^2 + (y - y_c)^2}$ | mm | Centre $(x_c, y_c)$ to point $(x, y)$ |
| Circle Diameter | $\varnothing = 2R$ | mm | Always prefix with $\varnothing$ symbol |
| Arc Length | $L_{arc} = R \cdot \theta_{rad}$ | mm | $\theta_{rad} = \theta_{deg} \cdot \pi/180$ |
| Area of a Circle | $A = \pi R^2$ | mm² | Used in CAD `AREA` / `MASSPROP` commands |
| Perimeter of Polygon | $P = n \cdot s$ | mm | $n$ sides, $s$ = side length |
| Tolerance (BIS) | $T = \text{Upper Limit} - \text{Lower Limit}$ | mm | Bilateral: $\pm T/2$ |
| Paper Size Selection | Choose next larger standard size | mm | A0 > A1 > A2 > A3 > A4 |
| Text Height (KTU norm) | $h = 2.5 \,\text{mm}$ (annotations) | mm | Title block: $h = 5 \,\text{mm}$ to $7 \,\text{mm}$ |
| Arrowhead Size | $a = 2.5 \,\text{mm}$ (filled) | mm | Per BIS SP 46:2003 |

**Critical note for tables:** Since markdown tables cannot contain the `|` character (it would break column delimiters), the absolute-value or magnitude operator is intentionally omitted in formula cells above; in written answers use $\vert x \vert$ notation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Manufacturing drawings of machine parts (shafts, gears, brackets) with GD&T.
- **Civil Engineering:** Site plans, floor plans, elevation views with linear/angular dimensions.
- **Electrical Engineering:** PCB layout, wiring diagrams, panel layouts.
- **Architecture:** Plan, elevation, section drawings with material hatching and area callouts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Worked Examples

> Since this topic is **procedural and drafting-oriented** rather than formula-derivation-oriented, the analytical depth is delivered through **exhaustive step-by-step procedures** for executing a complete CAD drawing task, and a **workout example** showing how dimensional values are computed from underlying geometry.

### 3.1 Procedure A — Creating a 2D Drawing from Scratch (AutoCAD Convention)

**Goal:** Draw a rectangular plate of 80 mm × 50 mm with four corner holes of $\varnothing$ 10 mm, centred 10 mm from each edge, then dimension it fully.

#### Step 1 — Initialise the Drawing
- Open AutoCAD → New → select `acadiso.dwt` template.
- Set units: type `UNITS` → Decimal → Millimetres → 2 decimal places of precision.

#### Step 2 — Build the Layer Table
- Open the **Layer Properties Manager** (`LA` command).
- Create the following layers:

| Layer Name | Colour | Line Type | Line Weight | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 01_CENTER | Red | CENTER | 0.25 mm | Centre lines of holes |
| 02_OUTLINE | White/Black | Continuous | 0.50 mm | Object boundary |
| 03_HIDDEN | Yellow | HIDDEN | 0.25 mm | Hidden features |
| 04_DIM | Cyan | Continuous | 0.25 mm | Dimensions |
| 05_TEXT | Green | Continuous | 0.25 mm | Notes & title block |
| 06_HATCH | Magenta | Continuous | 0.25 mm | Sectional hatching |

Set current layer to `02_OUTLINE`.

#### Step 3 — Draw the Outline Rectangle
- Activate `RECTANGLE` command.
- Specify first corner at coordinate $(0, 0)$.
- Specify other corner at $(80, 50)$.
- **Result:** A rectangle of width $80$ mm and height $50$ mm is generated with four corner points at $(0,0)$, $(80,0)$, $(80,50)$, and $(0,50)$.

#### Step 4 — Locate the Hole Centres
- Each hole is **10 mm** in from each edge → centre coordinates:
  - Bottom-left hole: $(10, 10)$
  - Bottom-right hole: $(70, 10)$
  - Top-right hole: $(70, 40)$
  - Top-left hole: $(10, 40)$
- Switch to layer `01_CENTER`.
- Activate `CIRCLE` command. For each centre point, use OSNAP endpoint or direct coordinate entry.

#### Step 5 — Draw the Holes
- Switch to layer `02_OUTLINE`.
- Activate `CIRCLE` with centre $(10, 10)$ and radius $5$ mm (diameter $\varnothing = 10$ mm). Repeat for the other three centres.

#### Step 6 — Add Centre Lines
- On layer `01_CENTER`, draw centre lines through each hole:
  - For hole at $(10, 10)$: vertical line from $(10, 3)$ to $(10, 17)$; horizontal line from $(3, 10)$ to $(17, 10)$.
  - Use `LINE` command with OSNAP centre override.

#### Step 7 — Set Up a Dimension Style
- Type `DIMSTYLE` → New → name it `KTU_STD`.
- Set Text height = $2.5$ mm, Arrow size = $2.5$ mm, Extension line offset = $1.0$ mm, Extension line overshoot = $1.25$ mm.
- Set Primary units → Precision = $0.00$ (two decimal places).
- Set this style as Current.

#### Step 8 — Apply Dimensions
- **Linear (overall):** `DIMLINEAR` → select bottom edge of rectangle → place dimension below, showing $80$ mm. Repeat for left edge → $50$ mm.
- **Linear (hole to edge):** `DIMLINEAR` → click hole centre $(10, 10)$ and left edge → $10$ mm. Repeat for hole $(10, 10)$ to bottom edge → $10$ mm. Mirror for right and top.
- **Diameter:** `DIMDIAMETER` → click each circle → the software auto-inserts $\varnothing 10$ with a leader.

#### Step 9 — Hatch Sectional Views (if required)
- Type `HATCH` → pick internal point of any section → choose pattern `ANSI31` → set scale = $1.0$ → press Enter.

#### Step 10 — Save and Plot
- Save as `plate_v1.dwg`.
- For printout, set layout → page setup → choose A4 sheet → plot scale 1:1 → preview → plot.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Workout — Computing Dimensional Values

**Problem:** A 2D triangle has vertices at $A(0, 0)$, $B(60, 0)$, and $C(20, 40)$ in mm. The CAD software must dimension (a) the side length $AB$, (b) the side length $BC$, (c) the angle at $A$, and (d) the radius of the inscribed circle.

#### (a) Side Length $AB$ — Linear / Aligned Dimension

$$
AB = \sqrt{(x_B - x_A)^2 + (y_B - y_A)^2}
$$

Substituting $A(0, 0)$ and $B(60, 0)$:

$$
AB = \sqrt{(60 - 0)^2 + (0 - 0)^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 0} = \sqrt{3600}
$$

$$
\boxed{AB = 60.00 \text{ mm}}
$$

**CAD command:** `DIMLINEAR` (horizontal alignment, since $y_A = y_B$).

#### (b) Side Length $BC$ — Aligned Dimension

$$
BC = \sqrt{(x_C - x_B)^2 + (y_C - y_B)^2}
$$

Substituting $B(60, 0)$ and $C(20, 40)$:

$$
BC = \sqrt{(20 - 60)^2 + (40 - 0)^2} = \sqrt{(-40)^2 + (40)^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 1600}
$$

$$
BC = \sqrt{3200} = 40 \cdot \sqrt{2} \approx 56.5685
$$

$$
\boxed{BC \approx 56.57 \text{ mm}}
$$

**CAD command:** `DIMALIGNED` (dimension aligned with the line $BC$).

#### (c) Angle at Vertex $A$ — Angular Dimension

The angle at $A$ is the angle between vector $\vec{AB}$ and vector $\vec{AC}$.

Vector $\vec{AB} = (60 - 0, 0 - 0) = (60, 0)$.

Vector $\vec{AC} = (20 - 0, 40 - 0) = (20, 40)$.

Using the dot-product formula:

$$
\cos(\angle BAC) = \frac{\vec{AB} \cdot \vec{AC}}{\vert \vec{AB} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{AC} \vert}
$$

Compute dot product:

$$
\vec{AB} \cdot \vec{AC} = (60)(20) + (0)(40) = 1200
$$

Compute magnitudes:

$$
\vert \vec{AB} \vert = \sqrt{60^2 + 0^2} = 60
$$

$$
\vert \vec{AC} \vert = \sqrt{20^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{400 + 1600} = \sqrt{2000} = 20\sqrt{5}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\cos(\angle BAC) = \frac{1200}{60 \cdot 20\sqrt{5}} = \frac{1200}{1200\sqrt{5}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}
$$

$$
\angle BAC = \arccos\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right) \approx \arccos(0.4472)
$$

$$
\boxed{\angle BAC \approx 63.43^\circ}
$$

**CAD command:** `DIMANGULAR` → select line $AB$ then line $AC$ → place arc dimension inside the angle.

#### (d) Inscribed Circle Radius — Derivation Step-by-Step

The inradius $r$ of a triangle with side lengths $a, b, c$ and semi-perimeter $s$ is:

$$
r = \frac{\text{Area}}{s}
$$

We already have $a = BC = 40\sqrt{2}$, $b = CA$, $c = AB = 60$.

Compute $b = CA$:

$$
CA = \sqrt{(20 - 0)^2 + (40 - 0)^2} = \sqrt{400 + 1600} = \sqrt{2000} = 20\sqrt{5} \approx 44.7214 \text{ mm}
$$

Sum of sides:

$$
a + b + c = 40\sqrt{2} + 20\sqrt{5} + 60 \approx 56.5685 + 44.7214 + 60 = 161.2899
$$

Semi-perimeter:

$$
s = \frac{a + b + c}{2} \approx \frac{161.2899}{2} = 80.6449 \text{ mm}
$$

Area of triangle (using cross-product of vectors from $A$):

$$
\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert \vec{AB} \times \vec{AC} \vert = \frac{1}{2} \vert (60)(40) - (0)(20) \vert = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2400 = 1200 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Inradius:

$$
r = \frac{1200}{80.6449} \approx 14.8803
$$

$$
\boxed{r \approx 14.88 \text{ mm}}
$$

**CAD command:** `CIRCLE` → centre at in-centre (intersection of angle bisectors) → radius $14.88$ mm.

**Verification of in-centre coordinates** (using weighted formula $I = (a \cdot A + b \cdot B + c \cdot C) / (a + b + c)$):

$$
I_x = \frac{40\sqrt{2} \cdot 0 + 20\sqrt{5} \cdot 60 + 60 \cdot 20}{40\sqrt{2} + 20\sqrt{5} + 60} = \frac{1200\sqrt{5} + 1200}{161.2899}
$$

Numerically:

$$
I_x \approx \frac{2683.28 + 1200}{161.2899} \approx \frac{3883.28}{161.2899} \approx 24.08
$$

$$
I_y = \frac{40\sqrt{2} \cdot 0 + 20\sqrt{5} \cdot 0 + 60 \cdot 40}{161.2899} = \frac{2400}{161.2899} \approx 14.88
$$

Hence the in-centre is at approximately $(24.08, 14.88)$ mm. The CAD command `CIRCLE` with these centre coordinates and radius $14.88$ mm completes the inscribed circle.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Dimensioning Best Practices (BIS SP 46:2003 / ISO 129)

| Feature | Correct Practice | Common Mistake | Marks Lost If Wrong |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear dimension of a horizontal edge | Dimension placed *below* the view | Dimension placed *above* the view | 1 mark |
| Diameter of a circle | $\varnothing$ symbol prefixed, value in mm | Plain numeric value "10" | 1 mark |
| Radius of an arc | $R$ symbol prefixed | Plain numeric value | 1 mark |
| Centre lines | Extend 2–3 mm beyond circle | Flush with circle boundary | 0.5 mark |
| Extension lines | Gap of 1–2 mm from object | Touching the object outline | 0.5 mark |
| Arrowheads | Touching extension lines | Arrowheads oversized (≥ 3 mm) | 0.5 mark |
| Hatching | At 45° to principal axis | Random angle, inconsistent spacing | 1 mark |
| Hidden lines | Short dashes, equal gap | Dashes too long or too short | 0.5 mark |

flowchart TD

    F2 --> G1[Phase 4 Apply Dimensions]

    G1 --> G3[DIMALIGNED for inclined lines]

### 4.3 Comparative Flow — Manual Drafting vs CAD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Manual Drafting Workflow] --> R2[Mount Drawing Sheet on Board]
    R2 --> R3[Measure and Mark with Scale]
    R3 --> R4[Draw with Pencil and Instruments]

    R5 --> R6[Hand Letter Dimensions]

    S1[CAD Workflow] --> S2[Open Software and Set Units]

```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Dimension Type to Apply

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Identify Geometric Feature to Dimension] --> T2{Is it a straight line distance}
    T2 -->|Yes horizontal or vertical| T3[Use DIMLINEAR]
    T2 -->|Yes inclined line| T4[Use DIMALIGNED]
    T2 -->|No it is an angle| T5[Use DIMANGULAR]
    T2 -->|No it is a circle| T6{Is the value greater than semicircle}
    T6 -->|Yes| T7[Use DIMDIAMETER prefix with diameter symbol]
    T6 -->|No| T8[Use DIMRADIUS prefix with R]
    T2 -->|No it is an arc length| T9[Use DIMARC]
    T2 -->|No it is a coordinate from datum| T10[Use DIMORDINATE]
    T2 -->|No it is a chain of dimensions| T11{From a common baseline}
    T11 -->|Yes| T12[Use DIMBASELINE]
    T11 -->|No end to end chain| T13[Use DIMCONTINUE]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. List any six advantages of Computer-Aided Drafting (CAD) over manual drafting.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**
1. **Higher accuracy and precision** — dimensions are computed to floating-point accuracy rather than measured by hand.
2. **Faster drafting and editing** — commands like COPY, MIRROR, OFFSET, and ARRAY allow reuse of geometry in seconds.
3. **Easy revisions and modifications** — the UNDO/REDO stack and associative dimensions allow non-destructive editing.
4. **Better storage and retrieval** — drawings are stored digitally (`.dwg` / `.dxf` files) and can be retrieved instantly.
5. **Standardisation** — dimension styles, text styles, and title blocks enforce uniform drafting standards across an organisation.
6. **Integration with CAM and 3D modelling** — CAD models can be directly exported to manufacturing, FEA, and 3D-printing pipelines.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q2. Distinguish between a Linear Dimension and an Aligned Dimension in CAD. When is each used?** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Linear Dimension | Aligned Dimension |
|---|---|---|
| Orientation | Always horizontal or vertical (aligned with the UCS axes) | Always parallel to the measured line/object |
| Use case | Dimensions on horizontal/vertical edges of a view | Dimensions on inclined or slanted edges |
| AutoCAD command | `DIMLINEAR` | `DIMALIGNED` |
| Extension lines | Perpendicular to the dimension line | Perpendicular to the *inclined* dimension line |
| Example | Width $80$ mm of a horizontal rectangle | Hypotenuse length of a right triangle |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In dimensioning questions, students **lose 2 marks** if they omit the **diameter symbol (Ø)** before a circle dimension, or the **letter "R"** before a radius dimension.
> - Students **lose 1 mark** if extension lines touch the object outline (they should leave a 1–2 mm gap).
> - Students **lose 1 mark** if centre lines are not extended 2–3 mm beyond the circle.
> - Students **lose 1 mark** if the dimension text is placed *on top of* the object instead of outside.
> - Always state the **CAD command name** (e.g., `DIMLINEAR`, `HATCH`) — examiners award a separate 1 mark for naming the command.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain **eight advantages** of CAD over manual drafting, with a brief justification for each. [7 Marks]

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **step-by-step procedure** to create a 2D drawing of a hexagonal nut (across-flats $30$ mm, $\varnothing$ of central hole $12$ mm) with full **linear, diameter, and angular dimensioning** using any standard CAD software. State the CAD commands used at each step. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Eight Advantages of CAD:**

1. **Precision and Accuracy** — Coordinates are stored to 15+ significant digits, eliminating instrument error inherent in manual drafting.
2. **Speed of Drafting** — A single `ARRAY` command can replicate a feature 20 times instantly; manual drafting would require 20 individual measurements and ink lines.
3. **Easy Editing** — `TRIM`, `EXTEND`, `STRETCH`, and `OFFSET` allow instant modification; manual erasing damages the sheet.
4. **Associative Dimensions** — Changing a base dimension automatically updates all dependent geometry — impossible in manual drafting.
5. **Layer Management** — Logical separation of centre lines, outlines, hidden lines, dimensions, hatching, and text enables instant visibility toggling.
6. **Reusability via Blocks** — A standardised title block, bolt, or symbol is created once and inserted in any drawing.
7. **Digital Storage and Sharing** — `.dwg` files occupy kilobytes, can be emailed, version-controlled, and cloud-backed; manual sheets require physical storage and couriers.
8. **Direct CAM/3D Linkage** — The same CAD model can be exported to STL for 3D printing, to STEP for CAM toolpaths, and to DXF for laser cutting — eliminating re-drafting.

**(b) Step-by-Step CAD Procedure for Hexagonal Nut:**

| Step | Action | CAD Command (AutoCAD) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open a new drawing and set units to **mm** with 0.00 precision | `UNITS` |
| 2 | Create layers: `01_CENTER`, `02_OUTLINE`, `04_DIM`, `05_TEXT` | `LA` |
| 3 | Compute the across-corners distance: $D_{corners} = \dfrac{2 \cdot AF}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 30}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 34.64$ mm | Calculator |
| 4 | Draw the regular hexagon inscribed in a circle of radius $17.32$ mm (i.e., $D_{corners}/2$) centred at origin | `POLYGON` → 6 sides → inscribed |
| 5 | Draw a circle of $\varnothing 12$ mm at the origin for the central hole | `CIRCLE` → radius 6 |
| 6 | Add centre lines extending 2–3 mm beyond the hole and across two opposite vertices of the hexagon | `LINE` + OSNAP |
| 7 | Set up a dimension style with text height $2.5$ mm, arrow size $2.5$ mm | `DIMSTYLE` |
| 8 | Apply **across-flats dimension** ($30$ mm) using `DIMLINEAR` between two parallel hexagon edges | `DIMLINEAR` |
| 9 | Apply **diameter dimension** ($\varnothing 12$) on the central hole | `DIMDIAMETER` |
| 10 | Apply **angular dimension** ($120°$) at any one vertex of the hexagon using `DIMANGULAR` | `DIMANGULAR` |
| 11 | Save as `hex_nut.dwg` and plot at 1:1 scale on A4 sheet | `PLOT` |

**Supporting Math (Step 3 derivation):**

For a regular hexagon with across-flats $AF$, the across-corners distance $D$ is:

$$
D = \frac{2 \cdot AF}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

Substituting $AF = 30$ mm:

$$
D = \frac{2 \cdot 30}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{60}{1.7321} \approx 34.641 \text{ mm}
$$

Hence the circumscribed circle radius is $R = D/2 \approx 17.32$ mm. The hexagon can be drawn with the `POLYGON` command by selecting "Circumscribed about circle" mode and specifying centre $(0,0)$ and radius $17.32$ mm.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **dimensioning workflow** in CAD with a labelled block diagram. List the **nine types of dimensions** available in CAD software with their respective commands. [7 Marks]

**(b)** A rectangular plate of size $120$ mm $\times$ $80$ mm has **four equal circular slots** of diameter $\varnothing 20$ mm, located at the midpoints of the four edges. The plate also has a central hole of $\varnothing 30$ mm. Using CAD commands, describe the complete procedure to create this drawing and apply **all relevant dimensions** (linear, aligned, diameter, and radius if applicable). [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Dimensioning Workflow & Dimension Types:**

The dimensioning workflow in CAD is:

1. **Set up Dimension Style** (`DIMSTYLE`) — define text height, arrow size, extension line offset, primary unit precision, and decimal separator.
2. **Switch to Dimension Layer** — for clean layer management.
3. **Apply Dimension Command** — pick the two extension-line origins (OSNAP endpoints) and place the dimension line.
4. **Verify Placement** — ensure arrows do not overlap the object and text is readable.
5. **Edit Dimension if needed** — `DIMEDIT`, `DIMTEDIT` to move text; `DIMREASSOCIATE` to re-link to new geometry.

**Nine Types of CAD Dimensions:**

| # | Dimension Type | AutoCAD Command |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear (horizontal/vertical) | `DIMLINEAR` |
| 2 | Aligned (parallel to line) | `DIMALIGNED` |
| 3 | Angular (between two lines) | `DIMANGULAR` |
| 4 | Radius (R) | `DIMRADIUS` |
| 5 | Diameter ($\varnothing$) | `DIMDIAMETER` |
| 6 | Arc Length | `DIMARC` |
| 7 | Ordinate (datum coordinate) | `DIMORDINATE` |
| 8 | Baseline (multiple from one line) | `DIMBASELINE` |
| 9 | Continuous (chain, end-to-end) | `DIMCONTINUE` |

**(b) Step-by-Step Procedure for the Plate with Slots:**

**Step 1 — Draw the outer rectangle** using `RECTANGLE` from $(0, 0)$ to $(120, 80)$.

**Step 2 — Locate the four edge midpoints** (slot centres):
- Bottom edge: $(60, 0)$
- Top edge: $(60, 80)$
- Left edge: $(0, 40)$
- Right edge: $(120, 40)$

**Step 3 — Draw the slots** (since they are full circular holes at the edge midpoints, they touch the edge; if they are slots *along* the edge, draw a `RECTANGLE` of $20$ mm width $\times 60$ mm length centred on the midpoint). For the question as stated, draw four circles of $\varnothing 20$ mm at the four midpoints using the `CIRCLE` command with radius $10$ mm.

**Step 4 — Draw the central hole** using `CIRCLE` at $(60, 40)$ with radius $15$ mm ($\varnothing 30$ mm).

**Step 5 — Add centre lines** on layer `01_CENTER`:
- For each of the four edge holes: vertical/horizontal line through the centre, extending 3 mm beyond the hole boundary.
- For the central hole: both a horizontal and vertical centre line spanning the full plate.

**Step 6 — Apply dimensions** on layer `04_DIM`:
- Overall plate size: `DIMLINEAR` on bottom edge → $120$ mm; on left edge → $80$ mm.
- Slot-to-edge distance: `DIMLINEAR` from slot centre to nearest edge → $10$ mm (radius offset).
- Slot diameter: `DIMDIAMETER` on each of the four edge holes → $\varnothing 20$.
- Central hole diameter: `DIMDIAMETER` on the central hole → $\varnothing 30$.
- Slot pitch (centre-to-centre): `DIMLINEAR` between bottom-slot centre $(60, 0)$ and top-slot centre $(60, 80)$ → $80$ mm.

**Step 7 — Save and Plot:**
- Save as `plate_with_slots.dwg`.
- Use `PLOT` to print on A3 sheet, scale 1:1.

**Supporting Math:**

The total distance from the bottom edge to the top edge of the plate equals $80$ mm. The slot diameter is $20$ mm, so the slot extends $10$ mm above the bottom edge and $10$ mm below the top edge. Hence the distance between the centres of the bottom and top slots is:

$$
d_{centres} = 80 - 10 - 10 = 60 \text{ mm}
$$

Wait — this assumes the slot is *half-in / half-out* of the edge. If the slot is a *full circle tangent to the edge*, then the centre of the slot is exactly on the edge (10 mm radius), so:

$$
d_{centres} = 80 - 10 + 10 = 80 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(slot centres at } y = 0 \text{ and } y = 80 \text{)}
$$

The horizontal pitch between the left and right slots is $120$ mm (slot centres at $x = 0$ and $x = 120$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Flash-Card for KTU GCEST203 Module 4:**

- [ ] **CAD definition:** Computer-Aided Design/Drafting — use of software to create, modify, and document 2D/3D designs.
- [ ] **Six core advantages** to memorise: (1) precision, (2) speed, (3) easy editing, (4) digital storage, (5) standardisation, (6) CAM/3D integration.
- [ ] **Model space vs Paper space:** Always draw at 1:1 in model space; apply scale only at plot time.
- [ ] **Layer system** is mandatory for any non-trivial drawing: separate layers for centre, outline, hidden, dimension, text, hatch.
- [ ] **Dimension style** must be set *before* applying any dimension — text height $2.5$ mm, arrow size $2.5$ mm are the KTU defaults.
- [ ] **Nine dimension types** (memorise command + symbol): Linear, Aligned, Angular, Radius (`R`), Diameter (`Ø`), Arc Length, Ordinate, Baseline, Continuous.
- [ ] **Always prefix** $\varnothing$ before a circle dimension and `R` before an arc dimension — forgetting this loses 1 mark.
- [ ] **Extension lines** must have a 1–2 mm gap from the object; arrows must touch the extension lines; text must be placed *outside* the object body.
- [ ] **Centre lines** must extend 2–3 mm beyond the circle; use the `CENTER` linetype (long-short-long-short pattern).
- [ ] **Hatching** of sectioned surfaces uses 45° lines; standard patterns are `ANSI31` (cast iron), `ANSI32` (bronze), `ANSI37` (aluminium).
- [ ] **For a regular hexagon** with across-flats $AF$, the across-corners distance is $D = 2AF/\sqrt{3} \approx 1.1547 \cdot AF$.
- [ ] **Linear distance formula** for two points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$: $D = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$.
- [ ] **CAD is a database, not a picture** — the model is a set of mathematically defined entities; this is why associative dimensioning is possible.
- [ ] **Always mention the CAD command** (`LINE`, `CIRCLE`, `DIMLINEAR`, `HATCH`, etc.) in your KTU answer — examiners award marks for naming the correct command.
